topics.title: Food Rheological Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Food Rheological Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Rheology?

The word **Rheology** originates from the Greek word *rheos*, meaning *"to flow."* Formally, rheology is the science of the **deformation and flow of matter** under the influence of an applied force (stress).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Steiner, 2024 Scheme):**
> *Rheology is the branch of physics/mechanics that deals with the study of the flow of matter, primarily in the liquid and soft-solid states, and the relationship between applied force, deformation, and time.*

## 1.2 What is Food Rheology?

Food rheology is the specific application of rheological principles to **food materials**. Most foods are neither pure solids nor pure liquids—they exist in a complex intermediate state.

**Food Rheology = Study of the deformation and flow behavior of food materials under applied forces, emphasizing structure–texture relationships.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is it critical for Food Technology?**
> - **Quality Control:** Sensory perception of "mouthfeel," "thickness," and "spreadability" is fundamentally a rheological experience.
> - **Process Engineering:** Design of pipes, pumps, mixers, extruders, and heat exchangers requires accurate viscosity data.
> - **Product Development:** Creating new textures (e.g., pourable yogurts, squeezable ketchups, sliceable cheese) is a rheological design problem.
> - **Shelf-life Prediction:** Syneresis, gel collapse, and sedimentation are all rheological instability phenomena.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Friends in Your Kitchen"

Imagine three friends in your kitchen:

| Food Item | Real Behavior | Rheological Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Water / Cooking Oil** | Flows the same regardless of how hard you push it | **Newtonian Fluid** |
| **Tomato Ketchup** | Hard to push at first, then suddenly flows when squeezed | **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning) Fluid** |
| **Toothpaste / Mayonnaise** | Won't flow until you apply sufficient force | **Yield-stress (Plastic) Fluid** |
| **Bread Dough / Cheese** | Stretches a bit when pulled, then springs back | **Viscoelastic Solid** |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** If you tilt a glass of water, it splashes. If you tilt a spoon of honey, it *flows* slowly. If you tilt a spoon of peanut butter, it *deforms* but does not flow. The "How easily does it deform and flow?" question is exactly what Food Rheology answers.

## 1.4 Fundamental Quantities Every Food Engineer Must Know

- **Force (F):** A push or pull, measured in **Newtons (N)**.
- **Area (A):** The surface over which force acts, measured in **m²**.
- **Stress (σ or τ):** Force per unit area → measured in **Pascals (Pa = N/m²)**.
- **Strain (γ or ε):** Relative deformation (change in shape divided by original shape) → dimensionless.
- **Shear:** A type of deformation where parallel layers slide over one another.
- **Time (t):** The hidden fourth dimension. Some foods "remember" their history; others don't.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Confusing Pair:**
> - **Stress (τ)** ≠ **Pressure (P)**. Pressure acts *normal* to the surface; shear stress acts *tangential* to the surface.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars: Stress and Strain

### 2.1.1 Shear Stress (τ)

When a force $F$ is applied **parallel** to a surface of area $A$, the resulting shear stress is:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

Units: **N/m² = Pa (Pascal)**. In CGS, the unit is **dyne/cm²**, where **1 Pa = 10 dyne/cm²**.

### 2.1.2 Shear Strain (γ)

For a fluid layer of thickness $y$, when the top surface is displaced by a distance $dx$ relative to the bottom:

$$\gamma = \frac{dx}{dy}$$

It is dimensionless (radians or unitless). For small deformations, the angle of deformation (in radians) is itself the shear strain.

### 2.1.3 Shear Rate ( $\dot{\gamma}$ or $\gamma^{\cdot}$ )

The **rate** at which strain changes with time:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{dx}{dy}\right) = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

Units: **reciprocal seconds (s⁻¹)**.

## 2.2 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Mother Equation of Rheology

Sir Isaac Newton postulated that for a "perfect" fluid, the shear stress is directly proportional to the shear rate. The constant of proportionality is the **viscosity (η or μ)**.

$$\boxed{\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa)
- $\eta$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = Shear rate (s⁻¹)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Hypothesis Restated:** The resistance to flow is *intrinsic* to the fluid (depends on the fluid, not on how hard you stir). A fluid that obeys this is called a **Newtonian fluid** (water, milk, refined oils, air, sugar solutions, dilute juices).

## 2.3 Dynamic vs. Kinematic Viscosity

The **kinematic viscosity (ν)** is the dynamic viscosity divided by the density of the fluid:

$$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho}$$

Units: **m²/s** (or Stokes in CGS: **1 m²/s = 10⁴ Stokes = 10⁶ centiStokes (cSt)**).

> [!TIP]
> **Why two viscosities?** Dynamic viscosity is useful in *force-balance* problems (e.g., pressure drop in pipes). Kinematic viscosity is useful in *gravity-driven* flow problems (e.g., sedimentation, Stokes' law for particles in a liquid).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | SI Units | Typical Food Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = F/A$ | Definition of shear stress | Pa | Pump pressure calculations |
| 2 | $\gamma = dx/dy$ | Definition of shear strain | dimensionless | Texture analysis |
| 3 | $\dot{\gamma} = d\gamma/dt = dv/dy$ | Shear rate | s⁻¹ | Stirring, mixing, pipe flow |
| 4 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | Pa | Newtonian fluids (water, oil) |
| 5 | $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Definition of dynamic viscosity | Pa·s | Viscometer readings |
| 6 | $\nu = \eta / \rho$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s | Gravity settling, sedimentation |
| 7 | $\dot{\gamma}_{pipe} = 8v/D$ | Wall shear rate in pipe | s⁻¹ | Pipe sizing in dairy plants |
| 8 | $Re = \rho v D / \eta$ | Reynolds number (laminar vs turbulent) | dimensionless | Flow regime in food processing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notational Note (KTU Valuation Tip):**
> Some textbooks (and examiners) use $\mu$ instead of $\eta$ for viscosity, and $\sigma$ instead of $\tau$ for shear stress. Always define your symbol in the *first line* of the answer to avoid losing marks.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Real-World Significance

The equation $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ is the **starting equation for almost every unit operation in Food Engineering**:

1. **Pipe Flow:** Pressure drop $\Delta P = \frac{32 \eta L v}{D^2}$ (Hagen-Poiseuille equation) — used to size pumps in fruit juice lines.
2. **Heat Exchanger Design:** The convective heat transfer coefficient depends on the Reynolds number, which uses $\eta$.
3. **Mixing Power:** Power number $N_p = f(Re)$; you need $\eta$ to size a mixer motor for a starch slurry.
4. **Sensory Texture:** "Perceived thickness" of a soup correlates with $\eta$ measured at $\dot{\gamma} \approx 50$ s⁻¹ (oral shear rate).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If a food follows Newton's law, *one number* (η) is enough to describe its flow behavior. If it doesn't, you need a *function* or a *model* — and that is where Non-Newtonian food rheology begins (next module).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shear Stress from First Principles

Consider a fluid confined between two parallel plates.

- **Bottom plate:** stationary, fixed at $y = 0$.
- **Top plate:** moving with velocity $v$ at $y = h$.
- **Area of plates:** $A$.

A force $F$ is applied on the top plate parallel to the surface. Because the fluid sticks to both plates (no-slip condition), a velocity gradient develops.

**Step 1 — Express the velocity profile.**
For a Newtonian fluid in laminar flow, the velocity varies linearly with height (Couette flow):

$$v(y) = v \cdot \left(\frac{y}{h}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain the shear rate.**
The shear rate is the gradient of velocity in the y-direction:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\left[v \cdot \frac{y}{h}\right] = \frac{v}{h}$$

This is a **constant** throughout the fluid — a characteristic of simple shear.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's law of viscosity.**
The shear stress at any point is:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \eta \cdot \frac{v}{h}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to force per unit area.**
Since $\tau = F/A$, the force required to move the top plate is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \eta \cdot A \cdot \frac{v}{h}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{F = \eta \cdot A \cdot \frac{v}{h}}$$

> This is the fundamental relationship used in **rotational viscometers** (cone-and-plate, parallel plate) to measure η.

## 3.2 Derivation: Wall Shear Rate in a Pipe

Consider a fluid flowing through a circular pipe of diameter $D$ with an average velocity $v$. The fluid at the wall experiences the maximum shear rate.

**Step 1 — Set up the force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$.**
The pressure force on the cross-section must balance the shear force on the cylindrical surface:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

**Step 2 — Solve for shear stress as a function of radius.**

$$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

**Step 3 — At the wall ($r = D/2$), the wall shear stress is:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L}$$

**Step 4 — For a Newtonian fluid, the velocity profile is parabolic (Poiseuille flow):**

$$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} \left(\frac{D^2}{4} - r^2\right)$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate $v(r)$ with respect to $r$ and evaluate at the wall to get the wall shear rate:**

$$\frac{dv}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} \cdot 2r = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 \eta L}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \left\vert \frac{dv}{dr} \right\vert_{r=D/2} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 \eta L} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the volumetric flow rate $Q$ relationship.**
For a circular pipe, $Q = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} v$ and the result is the famous **Rabinowitsch-Mooney equation in its simplest form**:

$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{8v}{D}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Food Engineering:**
> When designing a ketchup filling line, the average velocity in the pipe is known. You *must* convert this to wall shear rate using this formula before selecting the right viscosity value from a rheometer's non-Newtonian data table.

## 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between Kinematic and Dynamic Viscosity

**Step 1 — Dynamic viscosity η** is a measure of the fluid's internal resistance to shear, independent of its mass.

**Step 2 — Kinematic viscosity ν** is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density, capturing the fluid's resistance to flow under gravity (a body force).

**Step 3 — Definition:**

$$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Unit verification:**

$$[\nu] = \frac{[\eta]}{[\rho]} = \frac{\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{s} / \text{m}^2}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}/\text{s}^2 \cdot \text{s} / \text{m}^2}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

**Step 5 — Concluding remark.** Both viscosities convey the same "thickness" information; the choice depends on whether the dominant force is *inertial* (use ν) or *viscous* (use η).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem)

**Problem:** A Newtonian sauce has a dynamic viscosity of **0.5 Pa·s** and a density of **1100 kg/m³**. It flows through a pipe of diameter **5 cm** at an average velocity of **0.4 m/s**. Calculate (a) the kinematic viscosity, (b) the wall shear rate, and (c) the wall shear stress.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Kinematic viscosity.

$$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho} = \frac{0.5}{1100} = 4.545 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Part (b):** Wall shear rate.

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{8v}{D} = \frac{8 \times 0.4}{0.05} = 64 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Part (c):** Wall shear stress (Newton's law).

$$\tau_w = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}_w = 0.5 \times 64 = 32 \text{ Pa}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Check:** Always carry units in every line. A 14-mark question often allocates 1–2 marks for *correct unit conversion*, so this is free marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Food Rheological Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Material Response to Applied Force] --> B{Mechanism of Response}
    B -->|Flows irreversibly| C[Purely Viscous Behavior]
    B -->|Deforms reversibly| D[Purely Elastic Behavior]
    B -->|Both flow and recover| E[Viscoelastic Behavior]
    B -->|Flows only after threshold| F[Plastic Behavior]

    C --> C1[Newtonian Fluids: Water, Milk, Refined Oils]
    C --> C2[Non-Newtonian Fluids: Ketchup, Honey, Starch Slurry]

    D --> D1[Hookean Solids: Brittle Foods, Hard Cheese]

    E --> E1[Viscoelastic Liquids: Dough, Yogurt, Fondant]
    E --> E2[Viscoelastic Solids: Bread Crumb, Gelatin Gel]

    F --> F1[Bingham Plastics: Toothpaste, Margarine]
    F --> F2[Yield Stress Fluids: Mayonnaise, Tomato Paste]

    C1 --> G1[Defined by viscosity eta - single constant]
    C2 --> G2[Defined by models: Power-law, Herschel-Bulkley]
    E1 --> G3[Defined by relaxation modulus G and time]
    F1 --> G4[Defined by yield stress tau_0 and plastic viscosity]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Rheological Measurement Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Food Sample] --> B[Selected Geometry]
    B --> B1[Concentric Cylinder]
    B --> B2[Cone and Plate]
    B --> B3[Parallel Plate]
    B --> B4[Capillary Tube]

    B1 --> C[Apply Controlled Shear]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C

    C --> D[Measure Torque and Speed]
    D --> E[Compute Shear Stress tau]
    D --> F[Compute Shear Rate gamma_dot]

    E --> G[Plot Rheogram: tau vs gamma_dot]
    F --> G

    G --> H{Model Fit}
    H --> H1[Newton: tau = eta times gamma_dot]
    H --> H2[Power Law: tau = K times gamma_dot to the n]
    H --> H3[Bingham: tau = tau_0 plus eta_pl times gamma_dot]

    H1 --> I[Report: Viscosity eta]
    H2 --> J[Report: Consistency K, Index n]
    H3 --> K[Report: Yield Stress tau_0, Plastic Viscosity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram is in Block form:** A physical schematic of a concentric cylinder requires fine geometric detail (gap dimensions, bob length) that is impossible to render accurately in a flowchart. The functional architecture above clearly maps the *interactions* between sample, instrument, calculation, and final rheological parameter — which is what Module 1 (and the KTU 2024 Scheme) emphasizes.

## 4.3 Force-Deformation-Time Triangle (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Force[Applied Force] -->|generates| Stress[Stress tau]
    Deformation[Geometric Deformation] -->|generates| Strain[Strain gamma]
    Time[Elapsed Time] -->|modulates| Rate[Strain Rate gamma_dot]

    Stress -->|acts on| Material[Food Material]
    Strain -->|occurs in| Material
    Rate -->|develops in| Material

    Material -->|measured via| Response[Rheological Response]
    Response --> Viscosity[Viscosity eta]
    Response --> Modulus[Modulus G]
    Response --> Yield[Yield Stress tau_0]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
Define **Food Rheology**. List any **four** industrial applications of rheology in food processing.

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

*Food rheology is the science that deals with the study of deformation and flow behavior of food materials under the influence of applied force or stress, emphasizing the relationship between structure, composition, and perceived texture.*

*Four industrial applications:*
1. *Design of pumps, pipes, and mixers for fluid foods (e.g., fruit juice, milk) — uses viscosity data.*
2. *Textural control of products like yogurt, cheese, and sauces — correlated with consumer acceptance.*
3. *Extrusion cooking of cereals and snacks — uses viscosity to predict dough behavior.*
4. *Quality control of chocolate tempering and confectionery flow — ensures coating uniformity.*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
Differentiate clearly between **dynamic viscosity** and **kinematic viscosity**. State their **SI units**.

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

| Property | Dynamic Viscosity (η) | Kinematic Viscosity (ν) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Internal resistance to flow under applied shear force | Resistance to flow under gravity (body force) |
| Formula | $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | $\nu = \eta / \rho$ |
| SI Unit | Pa·s (Pascal-second) | m²/s (metre squared per second) |
| CGS Unit | Poise (P); 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s | Stokes (St); 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s |
| Typical Use | Pressure drop, pump power | Sedimentation, gravity settling |

*Dynamic viscosity is a property of the fluid alone, while kinematic viscosity incorporates the fluid's density, making it useful when body forces (like gravity) dominate the flow.*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **shear stress** in a Newtonian fluid confined between two parallel plates, starting from Newton's law of viscosity. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Newtonian fruit juice with viscosity **2.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** flows through a smooth pipe of diameter **4 cm** at a flow rate of **2 L/min**. Calculate (i) the average velocity, (ii) the wall shear rate, and (iii) the wall shear stress. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define the system.** Two parallel plates separated by a gap $h$. Bottom plate fixed; top plate moves with velocity $v$ under applied force $F$. [1 Mark for diagram/description]

**Step 2 — Assumptions:**
- Laminar, steady flow.
- No-slip at both plates.
- Incompressible fluid.
- Isothermal conditions.
- Newtonian behavior.

[1 Mark for assumptions]

**Step 3 — Velocity profile** (Couette flow):

$$v(y) = v \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

**Step 4 — Shear rate:**

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{v}{h}$$

[1 Mark]

**Step 5 — Newton's law of viscosity:**

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \eta \cdot \frac{v}{h}$$

[1 Mark]

**Step 6 — Convert to force:**

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \eta \cdot A \cdot \frac{v}{h}$$

[1 Mark for the final boxed equation]

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation:** This equation is the working principle of rotational viscometers; by controlling $v$ and measuring $F$, η can be back-calculated. [1 Mark for interpretation]

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $\eta = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $D = 4 \text{ cm} = 0.04$ m
- $Q = 2$ L/min $= \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s $= 3.333 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of pipe:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.04)^2}{4} = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

[1 Mark]

**Step 2 — (i) Average velocity:**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{-5}}{1.257 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0265 \text{ m/s}$$

[1 Mark]

**Step 3 — (ii) Wall shear rate:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{8v}{D} = \frac{8 \times 0.0265}{0.04} = 5.3 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

[2 Marks]

**Step 4 — (iii) Wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_w = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}_w = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.3 = 0.01325 \text{ Pa}$$

[2 Marks]

**Step 5 — Units check and concluding remark.** Velocity ~ 0.027 m/s is a typical low-flow beverage line; the corresponding τ is very small, indicating near-Newtonian, easily-pumpable juice.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with proper units: **(i)** Shear stress, **(ii)** Shear strain, **(iii)** Shear rate, **(iv)** Dynamic viscosity, **(v)** Kinematic viscosity, **(vi)** Reynolds number, **(vii)** Power-law index. **(7 Marks — 1 mark each)**

**(b)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **concept of a rheogram** for a Newtonian fluid. How does it differ from the rheogram of a **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** fluid? Give **two food examples** for each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

1. **Shear stress (τ):** Force per unit area applied tangentially to a fluid layer. **Unit: Pa (N/m²).** [1]
2. **Shear strain (γ):** Ratio of displacement to the perpendicular distance over which it occurs. **Unit: dimensionless (radians).** [1]
3. **Shear rate (γ̇):** Time derivative of shear strain; also equal to the velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction. **Unit: s⁻¹.** [1]
4. **Dynamic viscosity (η):** Ratio of shear stress to shear rate for a Newtonian fluid; a measure of internal friction. **Unit: Pa·s.** [1]
5. **Kinematic viscosity (ν):** Ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density. **Unit: m²/s.** [1]
6. **Reynolds number (Re):** Dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces, $Re = \rho v D / \eta$, used to determine flow regime. **Unit: dimensionless.** [1]
7. **Power-law index (n):** Exponent in the Ostwald-de Waele equation $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$; n = 1 for Newtonian, n < 1 for pseudoplastic, n > 1 for dilatant. **Unit: dimensionless.** [1]

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Rheogram definition.** A rheogram (or flow curve) is a plot of shear stress $\tau$ on the y-axis versus shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ on the x-axis. [1 Mark]

**Step 2 — Newtonian Rheogram.** For a Newtonian fluid, the plot is a **straight line passing through the origin** with slope equal to η:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

[1 Mark for description + 1 Mark for diagram description]

**Step 3 — Pseudoplastic Rheogram.** For a pseudoplastic fluid, viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate. The plot is a **curve concave to the shear-rate axis**, often described by the power-law model $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ with $n < 1$. The "apparent viscosity" is not constant. [2 Marks]

**Step 4 — Food Examples.**
- *Newtonian:* water, milk, refined sunflower oil, dilute fruit juice, honey (approximately). [1 Mark]
- *Pseudoplastic:* tomato ketchup, banana puree, cooked starch paste, salad dressing, molten chocolate. [1 Mark]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the origin:** Many students draw the Newtonian line but *not through the origin*. The line **must pass through (0,0)** because τ = 0 when γ̇ = 0. Loss of 1 mark guaranteed.
> 2. **Confusing "apparent viscosity" with "true viscosity":** In pseudoplastic fluids, the slope of the secant from origin to a point is *not* a constant. The viscosity at *each* shear rate is the *apparent* viscosity. Examiners love to test this distinction.
> 3. **Unit mistakes:** Viscosity in Pa·s, not "kg/m·s" (which is dimensionally wrong). Reynolds number has *no units* — writing "Re = 1200 m/s" loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram:** Even for a 7-mark part-(a) definition question, a tiny sketch differentiates a 6-mark answer from a 7-mark answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Rheology** = Science of deformation and flow of matter (from Greek *rheos* = to flow).
- [x] **Food Rheology** = Application to foods; bridges sensory texture and engineering design.
- [x] **Stress (τ)** = Force per area in tangential direction → **Pa (N/m²)**.
- [x] **Strain (γ)** = Relative deformation → **dimensionless** (radians).
- [x] **Shear rate (γ̇)** = $dv/dy$ → **s⁻¹**.
- [x] **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ — the foundational equation.
- [x] **Newtonian fluids:** τ vs γ̇ is a straight line through the origin. Examples: water, milk, refined oils.
- [x] **Dynamic viscosity η** → **Pa·s**; **Kinematic viscosity ν = η/ρ** → **m²/s**.
- [x] **Wall shear rate in pipe:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = 8v / D$ — essential for pipe design.
- [x] **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho v D / \eta$ — classifies laminar vs. turbulent flow.
- [x] **Foods are mostly Non-Newtonian**, but Module 1 builds the Newtonian foundation first.
- [x] **Common KTU traps:** τ vs P (tangential vs normal), μ vs η, missing units, line must pass through origin.
- [x] **Practical applications:** Pipe sizing, pump selection, mixer design, texture engineering, extrusion, sensory correlation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Types of Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Various Types of Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Stress?

In the context of **Food Rheology**, stress ($\sigma$) is the fundamental measure of the internal distribution of forces within a deformable food material, expressed as the force applied per unit area. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the applied force ($F$) to the cross-sectional area ($A$) over which the force acts.

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

The standard unit of stress in the **SI system** is the **Pascal (Pa)**, where **1 Pa = 1 N/m²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** Stress is a **second-order tensor** quantity (a 3×3 matrix), not a simple scalar. It is defined at every point inside a continuous material and varies from point to point in food systems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine squeezing a ball of **wheat dough** between your palms. The force you apply travels through the dough in different directions:
- Some force acts **perpendicular** (normal) to a small imagined plane inside the dough → this creates **normal stress**.
- Some force acts **tangentially** (parallel) to that plane, trying to slide one layer of dough over another → this creates **shear stress**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for KTU Students:** Think of stress as the "internal traffic controller" inside a food material. Just as traffic police direct cars flowing in different directions on a road, stress coordinates the internal molecular response (spreading, flowing, deforming) of food when external forces (mixing, extrusion, chewing) are applied.

### 1.3 Why Stress Matters in Food Rheology

Foods are **viscoelastic** materials — they behave partly like solids (elastic) and partly like liquids (viscous). The **type** of stress a food experiences determines its rheological response:
- **Hard cheese** resists **normal stress** but yields under sustained shear.
- **Tomato ketchup** is almost immobile at rest but flows easily under **shear stress**.
- **Bread dough** exhibits both **shear and normal stresses** during kneading and proofing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** Classification of stress is the foundation for understanding **simple shear, elongational flow, and steady shear measurements** covered in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Stress Tensor — Mathematical Foundation

At any infinitesimal point inside a food material, a complete description of the internal forces requires a **3×3 stress tensor** $\sigma_{ij}$:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zx} & \sigma_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- **Diagonal terms** ($\sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{yy}, \sigma_{zz}$) → **Normal stresses** (perpendicular to faces)
- **Off-diagonal terms** ($\sigma_{xy}, \sigma_{xz}, \sigma_{yx}, \sigma_{yz}, \sigma_{zx}, \sigma_{zy}$) → **Shear stresses** (parallel to faces)

**Why the tensor form?** Because stress depends on:
1. The **direction** of the outward normal of the plane on which it acts.
2. The **direction** of the force component on that plane.

A single scalar cannot capture this directional dependence.

### 2.2 Three Primary Types of Stress

#### A. Normal Stress ($\sigma_n$)
The component of force that acts **perpendicular (90°)** to a chosen surface within the material. Two sub-types exist:
- **Tensile Normal Stress (Positive):** Tries to **pull apart** the material.
  - *Food Example:* Stretching melted mozzarella cheese.
- **Compressive Normal Stress (Negative):** Tries to **push together** the material.
  - *Food Example:* Compressing a chocolate bar between plates.

#### B. Shear Stress ($\tau$ or $\sigma_{ij}$ where $i \neq j$)
The component of force that acts **tangentially (parallel)** to a surface. It attempts to **slide** one layer of the material over an adjacent layer.
- *Food Example:* Spreading butter on toast; mixing cake batter.

#### C. Bulk (Volumetric / Hydrostatic) Stress ($\sigma_v$)
A normal stress applied **uniformly from all directions**, causing a change in **volume** without a change in shape.
- *Food Example:* Deep-sea pressure on a sealed can of tuna.

### 2.3 Engineering Sub-Types Relevant to Food Processing

| Sub-Type | Symbol | Description | Food Processing Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniaxial Tensile Stress** | $\sigma_{11}$ | Tension along one axis only | Tensile testing of pasta strands |
| **Uniaxial Compressive Stress** | $\sigma_{11}$ | Compression along one axis only | Texture analysis of biscuits |
| **Pure Shear Stress** | $\tau$ | Tangential stress with no normal component | Lubricated squeezing flows |
| **Simple Shear Stress** | $\tau$ | Linear velocity gradient between plates | Capillary rheometry of chocolate |
| **Biaxial Extensional Stress** | $\sigma_B$ | Equal stretching in two perpendicular directions | Film-blowing of edible packaging |
| **Yield Stress** | $\sigma_y$ | Minimum stress required to initiate flow | Pumping of yogurt from a tube |
| **Oscillatory Shear Stress** | $\tau(t) = \tau_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | Time-dependent sinusoidal stress | Dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) of gels |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Yield stress is a **critical** concept in food rheology. Many sauces, pastes, and semi-solid foods (e.g., mayonnaise, ketchup, peanut butter) will not flow until the applied stress exceeds their **yield stress**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A$ | General stress definition (Pa) |
| 2 | $\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zx} & \sigma_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$ | Full 3D stress tensor (9 components) |
| 3 | $\sigma_n = \vec{n} \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \vec{n}$ | Normal stress on a plane with unit normal $\vec{n}$ |
| 4 | $\tau = \vec{t} \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \vec{n}$ | Shear stress on a plane (traction vector decomposition) |
| 5 | $I_1 = \sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz}$ | First stress invariant (trace) |
| 6 | $J_2 = \frac{1}{2} s_{ij} s_{ij}$ | Second deviatoric stress invariant (yield criteria) |
| 7 | $\sigma_v = -\frac{1}{3}(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz})$ | Bulk (volumetric) stress |
| 8 | $\sigma_{m} = \frac{1}{3}(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz})$ | Mean (hydrostatic) stress |
| 9 | $\sigma_{y} = \tau_0$ | Yield stress (Bingham model) |
| 10 | $\tau_{xy} = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Newtonian shear stress (where $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In industrial food design, knowledge of stress types allows engineers to:
> - Select appropriate **pumps and extruders** for viscous foods.
> - Design **chewing and swallowing models** for texture perception.
> - Predict **structural failure** of gel-based desserts under packaging stress.

### 2.5 Symmetry Property of the Stress Tensor

For most food materials (assuming no body couples or moment stresses):

$$\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$$

This **symmetry** reduces the independent stress components from 9 to **6**:
- 3 normal stresses ($\sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{yy}, \sigma_{zz}$)
- 3 shear stresses ($\sigma_{xy}, \sigma_{yz}, \sigma_{xz}$)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stress Vector on an Arbitrary Plane (Cauchy's Stress Theorem)

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between the stress tensor and the traction (force per unit area) acting on an arbitrary plane inside a food material.

**Step 1 — Consider an Infinitesimal Tetrahedron**

Take an infinitesimal tetrahedron of food material with three faces aligned with the coordinate planes and a fourth inclined face with outward unit normal $\vec{n} = (n_x, n_y, n_z)$.

**Step 2 — Apply Force Equilibrium**

For the tetrahedron in static equilibrium, the sum of forces on all four faces must be zero:

$$\vec{T}^{(\vec{n})} \, dA + \vec{T}^{(-x)} \, dA_x + \vec{T}^{(-y)} \, dA_y + \vec{T}^{(-z)} \, dA_z = 0$$

where $dA_x = n_x dA$, $dA_y = n_y dA$, $dA_z = n_z dA$ are the projected areas.

**Step 3 — Recognize the Opposite-Face Stress Relations**

By Newton's third law, stress on the $-x$ face is the negative of stress on the $+x$ face:

$$\vec{T}^{(-x)} = -\vec{T}^{(+x)} = -\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} \\ \sigma_{yx} \\ \sigma_{zx} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify**

$$\vec{T}^{(\vec{n})} \, dA - \sigma_{xx} n_x dA - \sigma_{yx} n_y dA - \sigma_{zx} n_z dA = 0$$

Dividing through by $dA$:

$$\vec{T}^{(\vec{n})} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} n_x + \sigma_{xy} n_y + \sigma_{xz} n_z \\ \sigma_{yx} n_x + \sigma_{yy} n_y + \sigma_{yz} n_z \\ \sigma_{zx} n_x + \sigma_{zy} n_y + \sigma_{zz} n_z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Express in Compact Tensor Form (Cauchy's Formula)**

$$\boxed{\vec{T}^{(\vec{n})} = \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \vec{n}}$$

**Step 6 — Decompose the Traction into Normal and Shear Components**

The traction vector $\vec{T}^{(\vec{n})}$ is split into:
- A component **along** $\vec{n}$: $T_n = \vec{T}^{(\vec{n})} \cdot \vec{n}$ (the **normal stress**)
- A component **perpendicular** to $\vec{n}$: $T_s = \sqrt{\vert \vec{T}^{(\vec{n})} \vert^2 - T_n^2}$ (the **shear stress** magnitude)

Thus:
$$\sigma_n = T_n = \vec{n} \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \vec{n} = \sigma_{ij} \, n_i \, n_j$$

$$\tau = \sqrt{T_i T_i - \sigma_n^2}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Principal Stresses (Eigenvalues of the Stress Tensor)

**Step 1 — Definition**

Principal stresses are the **normal stresses** acting on planes where the **shear stress vanishes**. Mathematically, on a principal plane, the traction vector is parallel to the normal:

$$\boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \vec{n} = \lambda \vec{n}$$

where $\lambda$ is the principal stress (an eigenvalue).

**Step 2 — Form the Characteristic Equation**

$$\det(\boldsymbol{\sigma} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$$

$$\begin{vmatrix} \sigma_{xx} - \lambda & \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} - \lambda & \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zx} & \sigma_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Determinant**

$$\lambda^3 - I_1 \lambda^2 + I_2 \lambda - I_3 = 0$$

where the **stress invariants** are:
$$I_1 = \sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz}$$
$$I_2 = \sigma_{xx}\sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{yy}\sigma_{zz} + \sigma_{zz}\sigma_{xx} - \sigma_{xy}^2 - \sigma_{yz}^2 - \sigma_{zx}^2$$
$$I_3 = \det(\boldsymbol{\sigma})$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Three Eigenvalues**

The three real roots $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$ are the **principal stresses**, conventionally ordered $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$.

**Step 5 — Engineering Use**

The maximum shear stress in a material occurs on a plane oriented at **45°** between the maximum and minimum principal stresses:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** For food packaging and texture failure analysis, $\tau_{\max}$ predicts where a brittle food (e.g., a cracker) will fracture under load.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

A cylindrical sample of cheese is subjected to a uniaxial compressive force of **200 N** on its top face. The cylindrical face has diameter **4 cm**. Compute the axial stress.

**Given:**
- $F = 200$ N
- $d = 4$ cm = $0.04$ m

**Step 1 — Compute Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.04)^2}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Stress**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{200}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.592 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 159.2 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Sign Convention**

Since the force is **compressive**, by KTU convention:

$$\sigma_{zz} = -159.2 \text{ kPa}$$

All other components of the stress tensor are zero for uniaxial loading:

$$\boldsymbol{\sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -159.2 \text{ kPa} \end{bmatrix}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Mechanical Stress in Foods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MECHANICAL STRESS IN FOODS"] --> B["NORMAL STRESS"]
    A --> C["SHEAR STRESS"]
    A --> D["BULK STRESS"]

    B --> B1["TENSILE sigma greater than 0"]
    B --> B2["COMPRESSIVE sigma less than 0"]
    B1 --> B1a["Example: stretching mozzarella"]
    B2 --> B2a["Example: compressing biscuit"]

    C --> C1["SIMPLE SHEAR tau equals eta gamma dot"]
    C --> C2["OSCILLATORY SHEAR tau equals tau0 sin omega t"]
    C --> C3["YIELD STRESS sigma y minimum flow threshold"]
    C1 --> C1a["Example: ketchup flow in pipe"]
    C2 --> C2a["Example: DMA of food gels"]
    C3 --> C3a["Example: yogurt tube extrusion"]

    D --> D1["HYDROSTATIC uniform pressure"]
    D --> D2["DEVIATORIC shape change only"]
    D1 --> D1a["Example: canned food pressurization"]
    D2 --> D2a["Example: dough kneading deformation"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Stress Tensor Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Stress Tensor sigma 3x3 matrix"] --> D["Decomposition"]
    D --> ISO["Isotropic Part pI where p is mean stress"]
    D --> DEV["Deviatoric Part s 3x3 matrix"]
    ISO --> V["Volumetric Change Response"]
    DEV --> SH["Shape Change Response"]

    style S fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style ISO fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff
    style DEV fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff
    style V fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
    style SH fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Material Response Mapping (Food-Type to Stress-Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F["FOOD TYPE"] --> M1["MAYONNAISE"]
    F --> M2["BREAD DOUGH"]
    F --> M3["CHOCOLATE BAR"]
    F --> M4["CARROT STICK"]

    M1 --> R1["YIELD STRESS + SHEAR FLOW"]
    M2 --> R2["ELONGATIONAL + SHEAR STRESS"]
    M3 --> R3["FRACTURE UNDER TENSILE STRESS"]
    M4 --> R4["CRUSH UNDER COMPRESSIVE STRESS"]

    style F fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style R1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style R2 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style R3 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style R4 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "shear stress" and state its SI unit. Give one food-processing example where shear stress is the dominant loading mode.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Shear stress** is defined as the component of force per unit area acting **tangentially (parallel)** to a surface within a material, attempting to slide adjacent layers relative to one another.

$$\tau = \frac{F_{\text{tangential}}}{A} \quad [\text{SI unit: Pascal, Pa}]$$

**Food Example:** Flow of molten chocolate through a pipeline during confectionery manufacturing. The chocolate is sheared between the stationary pipe wall and the moving central layers, making shear stress the dominant loading.

> **Mark Split:** Definition (1) + Formula & Unit (1) + Example (1)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between normal stress and shear stress with a suitable diagram. State the sign convention used for tensile and compressive normal stresses.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Feature | Normal Stress ($\sigma_n$) | Shear Stress ($\tau$) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of force | Perpendicular to surface | Parallel to surface |
| Effect on material | Volume change / direct stretch | Shape change / layer sliding |
| Sign convention (KTU) | Tensile: positive (+); Compressive: negative (−) | No sign (always positive magnitude) |

**Sign Convention:** As per KTU/Mohr's circle convention, **tensile normal stress is taken as positive** and **compressive normal stress is taken as negative**. Shear stresses are reported as magnitudes.

> **Mark Split:** Definition (1) + Tabular differences (1) + Sign convention (1)

---

### PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Derive **Cauchy's stress theorem** and show that the traction vector on an inclined plane in a food material can be expressed as $\vec{T}^{(n)} = \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \vec{n}$.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A spherical food product (e.g., a meatball of diameter **5 cm**) is subjected to a uniform external pressure of **250 kPa** from all sides (e.g., during retort sterilization). Calculate the **bulk (volumetric) stress**, the **mean stress**, and the **maximum shear stress** experienced by the product. Assume isotropic loading.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Derivation of Cauchy's Stress Theorem (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider an infinitesimal tetrahedron with three faces coincident with the coordinate planes ($xy$, $yz$, $xz$) and the fourth (inclined) face having outward unit normal $\vec{n} = (n_x, n_y, n_z)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Let $dA$ be the area of the inclined face. The projected areas of the three coordinate faces are:
$$dA_x = n_x dA, \quad dA_y = n_y dA, \quad dA_z = n_z dA$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply **static equilibrium** of forces on the tetrahedron:
$$\vec{T}^{(n)} dA + \vec{T}^{(-x)} dA_x + \vec{T}^{(-y)} dA_y + \vec{T}^{(-z)} dA_z = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Use Newton's third law ($\vec{T}^{(-x)} = -\vec{T}^{(x)}$, etc.) and substitute the projected areas:
$$\vec{T}^{(n)} = \vec{T}^{(x)} n_x + \vec{T}^{(y)} n_y + \vec{T}^{(z)} n_z$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Recognize that the stress on a coordinate face is the corresponding column of the stress tensor:
$$\vec{T}^{(x)} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} \\ \sigma_{yx} \\ \sigma_{zx} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \vec{T}^{(y)} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xy} \\ \sigma_{yy} \\ \sigma_{zy} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \vec{T}^{(z)} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xz} \\ \sigma_{yz} \\ \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Combine into the compact form:
$$\boxed{\vec{T}^{(n)} = \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \vec{n}}$$

This is **Cauchy's stress theorem**, the foundational equation of stress analysis in continuous media, including all food materials.

---

#### (b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Spherical meatball diameter $d = 5$ cm
- Uniform external pressure $P = 250$ kPa applied isotropically

**Step 1 — Identify the Stress State (1 mark)**

For **isotropic (hydrostatic) loading**, the stress tensor is:
$$\boldsymbol{\sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} -P & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -P & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -P \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -250 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -250 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -250 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kPa}$$

(Compressive → negative sign per KTU convention.)

**Step 2 — Compute Bulk (Volumetric) Stress (2 marks)**

The bulk stress is the negative of the trace of the stress tensor divided by 3:
$$\sigma_v = -\frac{1}{3}(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz}) = -\frac{1}{3}(-250 - 250 - 250)$$
$$\boxed{\sigma_v = +250 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Mean Stress (2 marks)**

$$\sigma_m = \frac{1}{3}(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz}) = \frac{1}{3}(-750)$$
$$\boxed{\sigma_m = -250 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Maximum Shear Stress (2 marks)**

For pure hydrostatic loading, $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = -250$ kPa, hence:
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{-250 - (-250)}{2}$$
$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = 0 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Physical Interpretation (Bonus):** Under purely hydrostatic pressure, no shear stress develops — the meatball is uniformly compressed with no shape distortion, only volume reduction. This is why canned foods in retort sterilization do not undergo shear-induced textural damage from pressure alone.

> **Mark Distribution for (b):** Stress state (1) + Bulk (2) + Mean (2) + Max shear (2) = 7

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank 2024]*

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With the help of a neat sketch, explain the difference between **uniaxial tensile stress** and **simple shear stress**. Tabulate the stress tensor for each loading condition.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A sample of wheat dough is subjected to a **simple shear test** in a parallel-plate rheometer. The top plate (radius **2 cm**) is moved with a force of **5 N** while the bottom plate is fixed. The gap between plates is **1 mm**, and the top plate velocity is **2 mm/s**. Compute the **shear stress**, **shear rate**, and **apparent viscosity** of the dough.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Uniaxial Tensile vs Simple Shear Stress (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Concept Definitions (2 marks)**

- **Uniaxial Tensile Stress:** A pulling force is applied along a **single axis** (say, the $z$-axis). The material is stretched in that direction and contracts in the transverse directions (Poisson effect). The two transverse normal stresses are zero.
- **Simple Shear Stress:** A tangential force is applied to the top face of the material, causing it to deform such that layers slide over one another in a linear-velocity-gradient manner. No normal force is applied.

**Step 2 — Stress Tensor Comparison (3 marks)**

| Loading Type | Stress Tensor $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ | Sketch Description |
|---|---|---|
| Uniaxial Tensile (along $z$) | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$ where $\sigma_{zz} > 0$ | Rod pulled at both ends |
| Simple Shear ($xy$-plane) | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & \tau_{xy} & 0 \\ \tau_{xy} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | Block with displaced top plate |

**Step 3 — Key Differences (2 marks)**

| Property | Uniaxial Tensile | Simple Shear |
|---|---|---|
| Off-diagonal terms | Zero | Non-zero ($\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$) |
| Volume change | Yes (Poisson contraction) | Negligible (incompressible) |
| Food example | Tensile test of pasta | Spreading of butter |

---

#### (b) Numerical Problem on Dough Shear (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Plate radius $R = 2$ cm = $0.02$ m
- Shear force $F = 5$ N
- Gap $h = 1$ mm = $0.001$ m
- Top plate velocity $V = 2$ mm/s = $0.002$ m/s

**Step 1 — Compute Plate Area (1 mark)**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (0.02)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Shear Stress (2 marks)**

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{5}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 3979 \text{ Pa} \approx 3.98 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Shear Rate (2 marks)**

For a parallel-plate rheometer:
$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{V}{h} = \frac{0.002}{0.001} = 2 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Apparent Viscosity (2 marks)**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{3979}{2} = 1989.5 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{app}} \approx 1990 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}}$$

> **Mark Distribution for (b):** Area (1) + Shear stress (2) + Shear rate (2) + Viscosity (2) = 7

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Forgetting to mark compressive stresses as **negative** (loses 1 mark per occurrence in numerical problems).
> 2. **Unit Conversion Mistakes:** Mixing cm with m, N with kN — always convert to SI base units (Pa, m, s) before calculation.
> 3. **Confusing Bulk Stress with Mean Stress:** $\sigma_v = -\sigma_m$ in sign. Mark examiners will deduct ½ mark for mixing them up.
> 4. **Tensor Symmetry:** Writing $\sigma_{ij} \neq \sigma_{ji}$ — this is incorrect for food materials under standard conditions; you lose marks for stating the wrong symmetry property.
> 5. **Misapplying Yield Stress:** Yield stress applies only in **shear loading**, not in pure hydrostatic pressure. A food under uniform pressure does **not** "yield" in the rheological sense.
> 6. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** Even a correct numerical value without units (Pa, kPa) loses ½ mark under strict KTU valuation.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Stress is a second-order tensor** with **9 components** in 3D; **6 independent** due to symmetry ($\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$).
- ✅ **Three primary types:** **Normal stress** (perpendicular), **Shear stress** (parallel), **Bulk stress** (uniform, volumetric).
- ✅ **Sign convention (KTU):** Tensile = positive, Compressive = negative.
- ✅ **Cauchy's stress theorem:** $\vec{T}^{(n)} = \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \vec{n}$ — the foundational equation of stress analysis.
- ✅ **Principal stresses** are eigenvalues of the stress tensor where shear vanishes; **maximum shear stress** occurs at 45° between $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$.
- ✅ **Yield stress** is critical for **semi-solid foods** (ketchup, yogurt, mayonnaise) — flow initiates only when applied stress exceeds $\sigma_y$.
- ✅ **Mean stress** $\sigma_m = \frac{1}{3}(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz})$ governs **volumetric** (bulk) response.
- ✅ **Deviatoric stress** $s_{ij} = \sigma_{ij} - \sigma_m \delta_{ij}$ governs **shape** (shear) response.
- ✅ **Unit:** Always **Pascal (Pa = N/m²)** in SI; convert to **kPa or MPa** for industrial magnitudes.
- ✅ **Food processing links:** Pumping → shear stress; Extrusion → elongational stress; Packaging → compressive/bulk stress; Texture testing → mixed stress states.
- ✅ **Newtonian shear relation:** $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ — fundamental to all rheometers and pipe-flow calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Rheology** (the science of flow and deformation of food matter), **Strain** is a dimensionless measure of the relative deformation of a material body in response to an applied stress. Formally, strain quantifies the *geometric change* in the configuration of a material element relative to its *reference (undeformed) configuration*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> Strain is the ratio of the change in dimension (length, angle, or volume) of a material element to its original dimension. It is a pure (dimensionless) number and is denoted by the Greek letter **$\varepsilon$** (for normal/tensile strain) or **$\gamma$** (for shear strain).

### Mathematical Symbolism

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

Where:
- $L_0$ = original length of the material element
- $L$ = deformed length
- $\Delta L$ = change in length

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stretching a piece of **pizza dough** while preparing a base. Before you pull it, it is 20 cm wide. After you spin and stretch it, it becomes 35 cm wide.

- The *change* in width is $35 - 20 = 15$ cm.
- The *strain* is not 15 cm — that would be a length! Instead, strain is the **change divided by how big it was to start with**:
  $\varepsilon = 15 / 20 = 0.75$ (or 75%).

So strain answers the question: *"By what fraction of its original size has the material changed?"*

> [!TIP]
> **Why "dimensionless"?** Because we divide a length (cm) by another length (cm), the units cancel out. Strain is **unitless**. It can be reported as a pure number (0.75) or as a percentage (75%).

### Real Food Examples

- **Cheese stretching (Mozzarella)**: undergoes very high strains (often > 100%) before tearing.
- **Bread dough sheeting**: typical rolling strains are 0.3–0.5.
- **Apple skin deformation** during a bite: small strain (< 0.05), often modeled as "small strain" elastic behavior.
- **Yogurt spooning**: experiences large shear strains that accumulate with each scoop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain vs. Deformation:** Deformation is the *absolute* change (in cm, m³, etc.). Strain is the *normalized* change — it lets us compare how much a tiny piece of spice and a large apple have each "given way" to the same applied force.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Strain** $\varepsilon$ or $\gamma$ — **dimensionless**, often expressed as a percentage or in **strain units** (e.g., $\text{mm/mm}$).
- **Strain rate** $\dot{\varepsilon}$ or $\dot{\gamma}$ — has units of **$\text{s}^{-1}$** (reciprocal seconds).
- Typical shear strain rate in food processing:
  - Chewing: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10$ to $100 \text{ s}^{-1}$
  - Pumping: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 100$ to $1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$
  - High-pressure homogenization: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^4$ to $10^6 \text{ s}^{-1}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Strain in Food Rheology

The syllabus distinguishes strain into several operational categories. Each is measured differently and arises in different food-processing scenarios.

### A. Normal (Tensile / Compressive) Strain

A linear deformation along one axis. Positive $\varepsilon$ = tension (pulling), negative $\varepsilon$ = compression (squashing).

$$\varepsilon_n = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

**Food examples:** Stretching of dough sheets, tensile testing of cheese strings, compression of biscuits.

### B. Shear Strain ($\gamma$)

The change in angle (in radians) between two originally perpendicular material line elements.

$$\gamma = \tan(\theta) \approx \theta \text{ (for small angles)}$$

**Food examples:** Spreadability of butter, flow of ketchup in a tube, extrusion of pasta dough.

### C. Volumetric (Bulk) Strain

The relative change in volume of a material element under hydrostatic pressure.

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0}$$

**Food examples:** Cellular expansion in bread during fermentation/oven rise, cavitation in ultrasound-treated liquids.

## 2.2 Engineering Strain vs. True Strain (Logarithmic / Hencky Strain)

This is a **KTU high-yield topic** because food materials routinely experience **large deformations** (strain > 5–10%), where the small-strain assumption breaks down.

### Engineering Strain
$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

Assumes the denominator is fixed at the *original* length. Acceptable for small strains.

### True (Logarithmic / Hencky) Strain
$$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

The denominator is updated continuously as the material deforms. **This is the strain measure used in food rheology for large deformations**, such as dough sheeting and extensional viscosity testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hencky strain dominates in food rheology:**
> 1. It is **additive in successive deformations** ($\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + ...$), unlike engineering strain.
> 2. It correctly handles finite (large) rotations and stretches.
> 3. It is the natural conjugate variable to **true stress** in viscoelastic constitutive models (e.g., the Lodge network model for bread dough).

### Comparison at $\varepsilon_{eng} = 1.0$ (100% extension):

$$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$$

So a 100% engineering strain corresponds to ~69.3% true strain — a substantial difference for tensile testing of mozzarella or dough.

## 2.3 The Strain Tensor (3D Generalization)

For a full 3D description, strain is represented by a **second-order symmetric tensor** $E_{ij}$ (or $\varepsilon_{ij}$):

$$E_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} & \varepsilon_{xy} & \varepsilon_{xz} \\ \varepsilon_{yx} & \varepsilon_{yy} & \varepsilon_{yz} \\ \varepsilon_{zx} & \varepsilon_{zy} & \varepsilon_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- **Diagonal elements** $\varepsilon_{xx}, \varepsilon_{yy}, \varepsilon_{zz}$ = normal strains along the three coordinate axes.
- **Off-diagonal elements** $\varepsilon_{xy} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_{xy}$, etc. = half the engineering shear strains (symmetric tensor convention).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — print this for revision.**

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Used For |
|---|----------|--------|---------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Engineering normal strain | $\varepsilon$ | $\dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$ | dimensionless | Small deformations |
| 2 | True (Hencky) normal strain | $\varepsilon_H$ | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)$ | dimensionless | Large deformations, dough, cheese |
| 3 | Shear strain | $\gamma$ | $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ | radians (dimensionless) | Spreading, flow |
| 4 | Shear strain rate | $\dot{\gamma}$ | $\dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Viscosity measurement |
| 5 | Volumetric strain | $\varepsilon_v$ | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V_0}$ | dimensionless | Expansion, fermentation |
| 6 | Cauchy (infinitesimal) strain tensor | $E_{ij}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$ | dimensionless | Small-strain elasticity |
| 7 | Green–St. Venant strain tensor | $E^{GSV}_{ij}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial x_k}{\partial X_i}\dfrac{\partial x_k}{\partial X_j} - \delta_{ij}\right)$ | dimensionless | Finite (large) strain |
| 8 | Hencky (Almansi–Euler) strain | $e^H_{ij}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(C_{ij})$ | dimensionless | Conjugate to true stress |

Where:
- $u_i$ = displacement components
- $x_i$ = current (deformed) coordinates
- $X_i$ = reference (undeformed) coordinates
- $C_{ij}$ = right Cauchy–Green deformation tensor
- $\delta_{ij}$ = Kronecker delta (=1 if $i=j$, else 0)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Condition for KTU:**
> In the **small strain regime** ($\varepsilon \ll 1$, typically $< 0.01$ or 1%):
> $$\varepsilon_{true} \approx \varepsilon_{eng}$$
> The two definitions converge and are interchangeable. Always check the magnitude of strain first.

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Food Engineering

1. **Process design** — knowing the strain helps set roller gaps, extruder die sizes, and mixer blade clearances.
2. **Texture prediction** — failure strain of crispy foods (chips, biscuits) determines crispness perception.
3. **Quality control** — dough must reach a target strain window for proper gluten network development.
4. **Sensory science** — perceived firmness correlates with both stress and strain thresholds in the oral cavity.
5. **Shelf-life modeling** — strain-based creep tests reveal long-term softening of bread or hardening of chocolate bloom.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Engineering Normal Strain from First Principles

**Statement:** Show that for a uniform uniaxial extension, engineering strain is the change in length divided by the original length.

**Step 1.** Consider a cylindrical food sample of original length $L_0$. Apply a tensile force $F$ along its axis.

**Step 2.** The sample elongates to a new length $L = L_0 + \Delta L$.

**Step 3.** By definition, the **engineering normal strain** is the fractional change in length:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{final length} - \text{original length}}{\text{original length}}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute the symbols:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

**Step 5.** Equivalent forms (algebraically identical):
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L}{L_0} - 1$$

**Result:** $\varepsilon$ is a pure number (length / length). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: True (Hencky) Strain by Integration

**Statement:** Derive the logarithmic strain by accumulating infinitesimal strain increments over a finite deformation.

**Step 1.** Start with an infinitesimal strain increment:
$$d\varepsilon = \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 2.** Integrate from the initial length $L_0$ to the final length $L$:
$$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate the integral:
$$\varepsilon_{true} = \big[\ln L\big]_{L_0}^{L} = \ln L - \ln L_0$$

**Step 4.** Apply the logarithm rule $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$:
$$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

**Step 5.** Express in terms of engineering strain by substituting $L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$:
$$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Result:** For small $\varepsilon_{eng}$, use the Taylor expansion $\ln(1+x) \approx x - \dfrac{x^2}{2} + ...$, confirming that for $\varepsilon_{eng} \ll 1$, $\varepsilon_{true} \approx \varepsilon_{eng}$. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additivity proof (why Hencky is preferred for sequential deformations):**
> Suppose a food sample is first stretched from $L_0 \to L_1$, then from $L_1 \to L_2$.
> Total Hencky strain:
> $$\varepsilon_{total} = \ln(L_2/L_0) = \ln(L_2/L_1) + \ln(L_1/L_0) = \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_1$$
> Engineering strain would give:
> $$\varepsilon_{eng,total} = \frac{L_2 - L_0}{L_0} \neq \frac{L_1 - L_0}{L_0} + \frac{L_2 - L_1}{L_1}$$
> This is why **Hencky strain is the natural state variable** for food processes involving repeated or continuous deformation (mixing, kneading, chewing).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shear Strain from Angular Displacement

**Statement:** For simple shear, derive $\gamma = \tan\theta$ and show that $\gamma \approx \theta$ for small strains.

**Step 1.** Consider a square element of side $L_0$ in a fluid under simple shear (e.g., ketchup smeared on a plate).

**Step 2.** The top surface displaces horizontally by $\Delta x$ relative to the bottom.

**Step 3.** The originally right angle at the corner is deformed by an angle $\theta$ such that:
$$\tan\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{\Delta x}{L_0}$$

**Step 4.** By definition, the **engineering shear strain** equals this ratio:
$$\gamma = \tan\theta = \frac{\Delta x}{L_0}$$

**Step 5.** For small angles (in radians), $\tan\theta \approx \theta$:
$$\gamma \approx \theta \quad \text{(valid for } \gamma \ll 1\text{)}$$

**Result:** The shear strain is also dimensionless, but it is measured in **radians** (a pure number) when expressed as an angle. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Infinitesimal Strain Tensor (Cauchy)

**Statement:** Show that the linearized strain tensor components are $E_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$.

**Step 1.** Define the displacement field $\vec{u}$ that maps a point from its reference position $\vec{X}$ to its current position $\vec{x}$:
$$\vec{x} = \vec{X} + \vec{u}(\vec{X}, t)$$

**Step 2.** The **deformation gradient** tensor is:
$$F_{ij} = \frac{\partial x_i}{\partial X_j} = \delta_{ij} + \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial X_j}$$

**Step 3.** The right Cauchy–Green deformation tensor is $C = F^T F$. Its components:
$$C_{ij} = F_{ki}F_{kj} = \left(\delta_{ki} + \frac{\partial u_k}{\partial X_i}\right)\left(\delta_{kj} + \frac{\partial u_k}{\partial X_j}\right)$$

**Step 4.** Expand and retain only first-order terms in displacement gradients (small-strain assumption):
$$C_{ij} \approx \delta_{ij} + \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial X_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial X_i}$$

**Step 5.** The Green–St. Venant strain tensor is $E_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}(C_{ij} - \delta_{ij})$. Substituting:
$$E_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial X_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial X_i}\right)$$

**Step 6.** In the small-strain limit, $X_j \approx x_j$, so we can replace the reference coordinate with the current coordinate. The **Cauchy (infinitesimal) strain tensor** is:
$$E_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$$

**Result:** The diagonal terms $E_{ii} = \dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_i}$ recover the normal strain, and the off-diagonal terms $E_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_{ij}$ are half the engineering shear strains. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Strain-Regime Selection for Common Foods

> [!TIP]
> Use this decision matrix to choose the correct strain measure in lab reports and exam problems.

| Food Material | Typical Strain Range | Regime | Recommended Strain Measure |
|---------------|----------------------|--------|----------------------------|
| Apple flesh (bite) | 0.01 – 0.05 | Small | Engineering |
| Bread crumb (compression) | 0.05 – 0.20 | Moderate | Engineering (acceptable) |
| Pizza dough (sheeting) | 0.3 – 1.5 | Large | True / Hencky |
| Mozzarella (stretch) | 1.0 – 3.0 | Very large | True / Hencky |
| Fermenting bread dough | 0.5 – 2.0 (volumetric) | Large | True volumetric |
| Chocolate (tempering flow) | 0.01 – 0.10 (shear) | Small–moderate | Engineering shear |
| Wheat gluten (extensional) | 1.0 – 5.0 | Very large | Hencky |
| Yogurt (spooning shear) | 0.1 – 1.0 | Moderate | Engineering or Hencky |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Taxonomy of Strain in Food Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strain in Food Rheology]:::root --> B[Normal Strain]
    A --> C[Shear Strain]
    A --> D[Volumetric Strain]
    A --> E[Strain Rate]

    B --> B1[Tensile Strain - Pulling]
    B --> B2[Compressive Strain - Squashing]
    B --> B3[Engineering Form]
    B --> B4[True Hencky Form]

    C --> C1[Simple Shear]
    C --> C2[Pure Shear]
    C --> C3[Engineering gamma]
    C --> C4[Shear Rate gamma dot]

    D --> D1[Hydrostatic Compression]
    D --> D2[Bulk Expansion - Bread Rise]
    D --> D3[Cavitation Bubbles]

    E --> E1[Strain Rate Tensor]
    E --> E2[Velocity Gradient]
    E --> E3[Time Derivative of Strain]

    B1 --> F1[Example: Stretching Mozzarella]
    B2 --> F2[Example: Biting an Apple]
    B3 --> G1[Small Strain Regime]
    B4 --> G2[Large Strain Regime]

    C1 --> F3[Example: Spreading Butter]
    C2 --> F4[Example: Calendering Dough]
    C4 --> F5[Example: Pipe Flow of Ketchup]

    D2 --> F6[Example: Bread Proofing]
    D3 --> F7[Example: Ultrasonication of Milk]

    G1 --> H1[Use Cauchy Tensor]
    G2 --> H2[Use Hencky Tensor]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef main fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef food fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef special fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef tensor fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class A root
    class B,C,D,E,B1,B2,B3,B4,C1,C2,C3,C4,D1,D2,D3,E1,E2,E3 main
    class F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F6,F7 food
    class G1,G2 special
    class H1,H2 tensor
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Choosing the Correct Strain Measure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Start: Identify Process]:::start --> Q1{Strain Magnitude?}:::decision
    Q1 -->|Less than 0.01| R1[Small Strain Regime]:::regime
    Q1 -->|0.01 to 0.10| R2[Moderate Strain Regime]:::regime
    Q1 -->|Greater than 0.10| R3[Large Strain Regime]:::regime

    R1 --> X1[Use Engineering Strain]:::choice
    R2 --> X2[Use Engineering Strain - acceptable]:::choice
    R3 --> X3[Use Hencky True Strain]:::choice

    X1 --> Y1[Formula: dL over L0]:::formula
    X2 --> Y2[Formula: dL over L0]:::formula
    X3 --> Y3[Formula: ln L over L0]:::formula

    Y1 --> Z1[Examples: Apple Bite, Chocolate Flow]:::example
    Y2 --> Z2[Examples: Bread Compression]:::example
    Y3 --> Z3[Examples: Dough Sheeting, Mozzarella Stretch]:::example
```
