<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Poisson's Ratio (ν or μ)** is a fundamental dimensionless elastic constant that characterizes the lateral (transverse) strain response of a material to an applied axial (longitudinal) strain. In the context of **Food Rheology**, it is the negative ratio of lateral (transverse) strain to axial (longitudinal) strain when a food material is subjected to a uniaxial mechanical load within its elastic limit.

Mathematically, the formal definition is expressed as:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

For isotropic, homogeneous food materials (e.g., gels, cheese, fruits, meat), Poisson's ratio is a **material property** and lies theoretically within the bounds:

$$-1.0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, Poisson's Ratio is studied as a critical *elastic constant* governing the three-dimensional mechanical behavior of food materials under small deformations. It links the **Young's Modulus (E)**, **Shear Modulus (G)**, and **Bulk Modulus (K)** through fundamental relationships, making it indispensable for texture profile analysis and food product design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are squeezing a **soft rubber ball** (or a piece of *paneer / tofu*) between your palms:
- Your palms push the ball along the vertical (axial) direction → the ball becomes **shorter vertically**.
- Simultaneously, the ball **bulges outward horizontally** → it becomes **wider laterally**.

This is the classic **Poisson Effect**. The ratio between how much it bulges sideways versus how much it compresses vertically is the Poisson's Ratio.

**Real-world food examples:**
- **Most foods (cheese, apple flesh, bread crumb):** When compressed, they bulge sideways → ν is **positive** (typically 0.2 – 0.49).
- **Some engineered foods / auxetic foods:** When compressed, they become *narrower* sideways → ν is **negative** (rare but observed in certain starch-based foams and tendons/meat fibers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A higher Poisson's ratio means the food "spreads" more sideways when compressed. This directly affects **texture perception**, **cutting force**, and **mouthfeel**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following key metrics are essential for Poisson Ratio Evaluation in foods:

- **Poisson's Ratio (ν):** dimensionless, range **−1.0 to 0.5**
- **Young's Modulus (E):** units of **Pa (Pascal)** or **kPa / MPa** for foods
- **Shear Modulus (G):** units of **Pa**
- **Bulk Modulus (K):** units of **Pa**
- **Strain (ε):** dimensionless (m/m)
- **Universal isotropic relation:** **E = 2G(1 + ν) = 3K(1 − 2ν)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The Poisson Effect arises from the **conservation of volume and the tensor nature of stress-strain** in 3D elastic bodies. The logic unfolds as follows:

1. **Step 1 — Apply Uniaxial Load:** A food sample (cylindrical, e.g., a carrot or cheese cylinder) is subjected to a uniaxial compressive force σ_axial along one axis (say, the z-axis).
2. **Step 2 — Generate Axial Strain:** The sample deforms in the loaded direction, producing axial strain ε_axial = ΔL / L₀.
3. **Step 3 — Generate Lateral Strain:** Due to internal molecular/network rearrangement, the sample deforms in the perpendicular (transverse) directions, producing lateral strain ε_lateral = Δd / d₀.
4. **Step 4 — Compute the Ratio:** Take the negative ratio of lateral to axial strain, yielding ν.
5. **Step 5 — Verify Linearity:** For ν to be a *constant material property*, the stress-strain response must be in the **linear elastic regime** (small strain, typically < 1–2% for most foods).
6. **Step 6 — Cross-Validation:** Use the relationship E = 2G(1+ν) to validate ν via independent shear and tensile/compression tests.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following high-yield formulas are **must-know** for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Bounds |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | Fundamental definition of Poisson's ratio | dimensionless, $-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$ |
| 2 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Relates Young's modulus to Shear modulus | E, G in Pa |
| 3 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Relates Young's modulus to Bulk modulus | E, K in Pa |
| 4 | $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | Relates G to K and ν | G, K in Pa |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\nu}{1 - 2\nu} = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{6K + 2G}$ | Rearranged form for ν from K and G | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)}$ | Direct computation of ν from K and G | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial}$ | Lateral strain from axial strain | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\sigma_{lateral} = \dfrac{\nu}{1 - \nu} \sigma_{axial}$ | Lateral stress (confined compression) | Pa |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

Poisson's ratio evaluation is critical in the following food engineering applications:

- **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA):** Determines how foods deform under the teeth/mimicked mastication.
- **Food Extrusion Design:** Affects die swell, product shape retention.
- **Cutting and Slicing Operations:** Influences energy required and fracture mechanics.
- **Packaging Design:** Predicts stress transfer from package to food product.
- **3D Food Printing:** Governs layer adhesion and dimensional stability of printed constructs.
- **Process Modeling (FEM):** Essential input for finite element analysis of baking, drying, frying.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Generalized Hooke's Law to Poisson's Ratio Definition

The generalized 3D Hooke's Law for an isotropic, linearly elastic material is:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)]$$

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Apply a uniaxial stress σ_z along the z-axis only. This means σ_x = σ_y = 0.

Substituting into the equations:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[0 - \nu(\sigma_z)] = -\frac{\nu \sigma_z}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}[0 - \nu(\sigma_z)] = -\frac{\nu \sigma_z}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_z - \nu(0)] = \frac{\sigma_z}{E}$$

**Step 2:** The axial strain is ε_z (the strain in the direction of applied stress) and the lateral strain is ε_x = ε_y (perpendicular to the applied stress).

**Step 3:** Form the ratio of lateral to axial strain:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = \frac{\varepsilon_x}{\varepsilon_z} = \frac{-\nu \sigma_z / E}{\sigma_z / E}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify (E and σ_z cancel):

$$\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\nu$$

**Step 5:** Solve for ν by introducing the negative sign to make ν positive for normal materials:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

This is the **fundamental definition of Poisson's ratio** — a result that emerges directly from the symmetric form of Hooke's Law in 3D.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deriving E = 2G(1+ν)

**Step 1:** Start with the relationship between shear modulus G, Young's modulus E, and Poisson's ratio. Consider a pure shear state where shear stress τ_xy produces shear strain γ_xy = τ_xy / G.

**Step 2:** Transform the principal stresses from the pure shear state. The principal stresses are σ_1 = +τ, σ_2 = −τ, σ_3 = 0.

**Step 3:** Using Hooke's Law for the principal strain in direction 1:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)] = \frac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau + 0)] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** The shear strain γ_xy relates to principal strains as:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} - \frac{-\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** But by definition, γ_xy = τ / G. Therefore:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel τ and solve for E:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deriving E = 3K(1 − 2ν)

**Step 1:** Apply hydrostatic pressure p to the material: σ_x = σ_y = σ_z = −p.

**Step 2:** The bulk modulus is defined as K = −p / ε_v, where ε_v is volumetric strain: ε_v = ε_x + ε_y + ε_z.

**Step 3:** Using Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[-p - \nu(-p - p)] = \frac{-p + 2\nu p}{E} = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** By symmetry, ε_x = ε_y = ε_z, so:

$$\varepsilon_v = 3\varepsilon_x = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into K = −p/ε_v:

$$K = \frac{-p}{-3p(1 - 2\nu)/E} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for E:

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Theoretical Bounds of Poisson's Ratio

**Upper bound (ν = 0.5):**

Setting K → ∞ (incompressible material, e.g., water, oils, soft gels):

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} \to \infty \implies 1 - 2\nu \to 0 \implies \nu = 0.5$$

**Lower bound (ν = −1.0):**

Setting G → 0 (auxetic limit, no shear resistance):

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} \to 0 \implies 1 + \nu \to 0 \implies \nu = -1.0$$

**Standard range for most foods:** **0.2 ≤ ν ≤ 0.49**

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A cylindrical sample of *cheddar cheese* (height 20 mm, diameter 15 mm) is subjected to a uniaxial compression test. The axial force produces an axial strain of ε_z = −0.015 (−1.5%) and a measured lateral expansion of 0.045 mm. Compute the Poisson's ratio.

**Given:**
- L₀ = 20 mm, d₀ = 15 mm
- ε_axial = −0.015 (compression is negative)
- Δd = +0.045 mm (lateral expansion)

**Step 1:** Calculate lateral strain:

$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} = \frac{0.045}{15} = 0.003$$

**Step 2:** Apply Poisson's ratio formula:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{0.003}{-0.015} = 0.20$$

**Step 3:** Interpret: ν = 0.20 is a typical value for semi-solid cheese, indicating moderate lateral expansion under compression.

> [!NOTE]
> This type of numerical problem is **highly probable** in KTU Part A (3-mark) or as a sub-part in Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 3.6 Typical Poisson's Ratio Values for Common Foods

| Food Material | Approximate ν | Reference State |
|---------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Water | 0.5 | Incompressible limit |
| Apple flesh (raw) | 0.30 – 0.40 | Fresh, room temp |
| Carrot (raw) | 0.30 – 0.45 | Fresh |
| Cheddar cheese | 0.20 – 0.35 | Aged, room temp |
| Bread crumb | 0.10 – 0.30 | Cellular solid |
| Potato (raw) | 0.35 – 0.45 | Fresh |
| Meat (beef) | 0.40 – 0.49 | Along fiber |
| Gellan gum gel | 0.45 – 0.49 | Hydrogel |
| Auxetic starch foam | −0.10 to −0.30 | Engineered |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Uniaxial Compression Test Setup for Poisson Ratio Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Cylindrical Food Sample] --> B[Measure Initial Dimensions]
    B --> B1["L0 = initial height"]
    B --> B2["d0 = initial diameter"]
    B1 --> C[Place on Texture Analyzer]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Apply Uniaxial Compressive Force]
    D --> E[Record Axial Deformation]
    E --> E1["Delta L = change in height"]
    E1 --> F[Record Lateral Deformation]
    F --> F1["Delta d = change in diameter"]
    F1 --> G["Compute epsilon_axial = Delta L / L0"]
    G --> H["Compute epsilon_lateral = Delta d / d0"]
    H --> I["Compute nu = - epsilon_lateral / epsilon_axial"]
    I --> J{Is nu in valid range?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Valid Poisson Ratio]
    J -->|No| L[Re-check strain measurement]
    K --> M[Cross-validate with E and G]
    M --> N[End: Final nu value]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Relationships Between Elastic Constants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Young's Modulus E] -->|E = 2G times 1 plus nu| B[Shear Modulus G]
    A -->|E = 3K times 1 minus 2nu| C[Bulk Modulus K]
    B -->|G = 3K times 1 minus 2nu divided by 2 times 1 plus nu| C
    A --> D[Poisson's Ratio nu]
    B --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E[All Four Constants Define Isotropic Linear Elastic Food Material]
    F[Uniaxial Test] --> A
    G[Pure Shear Test] --> B
    H[Hydrostatic Pressure Test] --> C
    I[Strain Ratio Test] --> D
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Poisson Effect Visualization (Logic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Applied Force: F arrow down] --> P2[Axial Compression: sample shortens]
    P2 --> P3[Lateral Expansion: sample bulges outward]
    P3 --> P4{Which food material?}
    P4 -->|Most foods| P5[nu positive: 0.2 to 0.5]
    P4 -->|Auxetic engineered| P6[nu negative: -0.1 to -0.3]
    P4 -->|Incompressible| P7[nu equals 0.5: water oils]
    P5 --> P8[Normal Poisson Behavior]
    P6 --> P9[Counterintuitive: shrinks sideways when compressed]
    P7 --> P10[Pure volume preservation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]
**Define Poisson's ratio. State its theoretical limits and explain its physical significance in food texture evaluation.**

**Model Answer:**

Poisson's ratio (ν) is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to axial strain in a material subjected to uniaxial loading within the elastic limit:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

It is **dimensionless** and has theoretical limits of **−1.0 ≤ ν ≤ 0.5**.

**Physical significance in food texture:**
- Indicates how a food material deforms sideways when compressed axially.
- For most foods, ν lies between **0.2 and 0.49**.
- A higher ν (closer to 0.5) indicates a more **incompressible** food (e.g., water-rich foods, gels).
- A lower ν indicates the food deforms more in the loading direction than laterally (e.g., porous bread, cellular foods).
- Critical for **TPA (Texture Profile Analysis)**, **finite element modeling** of food processes, and designing **cutting/slicing operations**.

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**A cylindrical sample of food gel (height 25 mm, diameter 20 mm) is compressed to a height of 24.5 mm, and the diameter increases to 20.05 mm. Calculate the Poisson's ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- L₀ = 25 mm, d₀ = 20 mm
- L = 24.5 mm, d = 20.05 mm

**Step 1:** Compute axial strain:

$$\varepsilon_{axial} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{24.5 - 25}{25} = -0.02$$

**Step 2:** Compute lateral strain:

$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = \frac{d - d_0}{d_0} = \frac{20.05 - 20}{20} = 0.0025$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{0.0025}{-0.02} = 0.125$$

**Answer:** ν = 0.125, indicating moderate lateral expansion. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Young's modulus (E), Shear modulus (G), and Poisson's ratio (ν) for an isotropic linearly elastic food material. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a food material, the Young's modulus is E = 500 kPa and the Shear modulus is G = 180 kPa. Calculate:
   (i) The Poisson's ratio.
   (ii) The Bulk modulus.
   (iii) Comment on the compressibility of the food material based on the calculated ν. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of E = 2G(1+ν):**

Consider an element under pure shear stress τ_xy. The principal stresses are σ₁ = +τ and σ₂ = −τ (and σ₃ = 0).

Using 3D Hooke's law:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_2 - \nu\sigma_1] = \frac{-\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

The shear strain is γ_xy = ε₁ − ε₂:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

By definition of shear modulus, γ_xy = τ/G, so:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**(i) Poisson's ratio:**

From E = 2G(1+ν), solve for ν:

$$\nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1 = \frac{500}{2 \times 180} - 1 = \frac{500}{360} - 1 = 1.389 - 1 = 0.389$$

**(ii) Bulk modulus:**

From E = 3K(1−2ν), solve for K:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{500}{3(1 - 2 \times 0.389)} = \frac{500}{3 \times 0.222} = \frac{500}{0.666}$$

$$K = 750.75 \text{ kPa}$$

**(iii) Comment on compressibility:**

Since ν = 0.389, the food material exhibits **moderate incompressibility** (close to the 0.5 limit). This indicates the material is largely **water-rich** and resists volumetric change. It will **bulge significantly laterally** when compressed. Suitable for soft gel-like foods (e.g., pudding, jelly, soft cheese). The high K value confirms strong resistance to hydrostatic compression.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat schematic the **uniaxial compression test** procedure used for experimental evaluation of Poisson's ratio in a food material. List the assumptions and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A carrot sample (cylindrical, L₀ = 30 mm, d₀ = 18 mm) is tested. Under a uniaxial compressive load, the axial strain is measured as ε_z = −0.008 and lateral strain ε_x = +0.0032. Calculate:
   (i) Poisson's ratio.
   (ii) If E = 2.5 MPa for carrot, calculate the Shear modulus G and Bulk modulus K. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Uniaxial Compression Test Procedure:**

1. **Sample Preparation:** A cylindrical food sample is cut with uniform dimensions. The ends must be **parallel and smooth** to ensure uniform stress distribution. Lubrication (e.g., silicone oil) is applied at contact surfaces to minimize **barreling effects** and friction at platen interfaces.

2. **Initial Measurement:** Record the initial height L₀ and initial diameter d₀ using digital calipers (±0.01 mm accuracy).

3. **Mounting:** Place the sample on the lower platen of a **texture analyzer** or universal testing machine (UTM) equipped with a load cell of appropriate capacity (typically 5 N to 500 N for foods).

4. **Loading:** Apply a uniaxial compressive force at a constant crosshead speed (typically 0.1–10 mm/min) while recording force F and displacement ΔL.

5. **Strain Measurement:** Axial strain is computed from displacement data. Lateral strain is measured either by:
   - **Contact methods:** Mounting lateral extensometers or LVDTs.
   - **Non-contact methods:** Digital image correlation (DIC) or video extensometry.

6. **Calculation:**

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{(\Delta d / d_0)}{(\Delta L / L_0)}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Material is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
- Deformation is within the **linear elastic range**.
- **Uniform stress** distribution (no barreling).
- Frictionless contact at platens.

**Limitations:**
- Friction at platen-sample interface causes non-uniform deformation.
- Difficult to maintain linearity for highly viscoelastic foods.
- Anisotropy in fibrous foods (meat, asparagus) makes single ν insufficient.
- End effects and sample geometry imperfections introduce errors.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**(i) Poisson's ratio:**

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{0.0032}{-0.008} = 0.40$$

**(ii) Shear modulus and Bulk modulus:**

Shear modulus from E = 2G(1+ν):

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{2.5 \text{ MPa}}{2(1 + 0.40)} = \frac{2.5}{2.80} = 0.893 \text{ MPa}$$

Bulk modulus from E = 3K(1−2ν):

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{2.5}{3(1 - 0.80)} = \frac{2.5}{0.60} = 4.167 \text{ MPa}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Many students forget the **negative sign** in the Poisson's ratio formula. Remember: axial strain is **negative** (compression) and lateral strain is **positive** (expansion) for normal materials. The negative signs **cancel**, giving a positive ν.
> 2. **Units Mismatch:** E, G, K must be in **consistent units** (all Pa, or all kPa, or all MPa) before substitution. Mixing units is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Range Check:** If your computed ν falls **outside [−1, 0.5]**, immediately re-check your strain measurements or sign convention.
> 4. **Formula Confusion:** Do **not** confuse E = 2G(1+ν) with E = 3K(1−2ν). The first uses **shear**, the second uses **bulk**.
> 5. **Incompressibility Assumption Trap:** Setting ν = 0.5 simplifies E = 3K(1−2ν) to a division-by-zero. Always state that K → ∞ for incompressible materials.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ⭐ **Definition:** ν = −(ε_lateral / ε_axial); dimensionless.
- ⭐ **Theoretical bounds:** −1.0 ≤ ν ≤ 0.5; most foods: 0.2 to 0.49.
- ⭐ **Key Formula 1:** E = 2G(1 + ν)
- ⭐ **Key Formula 2:** E = 3K(1 − 2ν)
- ⭐ **Key Formula 3:** ν = E/(2G) − 1
- ⭐ **Key Formula 4:** K = E / [3(1 − 2ν)]
- ⭐ **Incompressible limit:** ν → 0.5; K → ∞; applies to water, oils, soft gels.
- ⭐ **Auxetic materials:** ν < 0; rare in foods; engineered starch foams.
- ⭐ **Experimental method:** Uniaxial compression with simultaneous lateral strain measurement (DIC, LVDT, or video extensometry).
- ⭐ **Assumptions:** Isotropy, homogeneity, linear elasticity, small strain (< 2%).
- ⭐ **Sign convention:** Compression strain is negative; expansion strain is positive.
- ⭐ **Common food values to memorize:** Apple (≈ 0.35), Cheese (≈ 0.25), Meat (≈ 0.45), Bread (≈ 0.15–0.25), Water (0.5).
- ⭐ **Anisotropic foods:** Meat, fish, asparagus, pineapple — require **multiple Poisson's ratios** (ν_12, ν_13, ν_23) since properties differ along/across fibers.
- ⭐ **Viscoelastic caveat:** For viscoelastic foods (most real foods), ν is **time-dependent** and frequency-dependent — dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) may be required.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical State of a Food Material
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical State of a Food Material

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Rheology**, the **Physical State of a Food Material** refers to the structural configuration and phase condition (solid, liquid, gas, or intermediate colloidal/gel state) under which a food substance exists at a given temperature, pressure, moisture content, and composition. It directly dictates how the material responds to an applied **stress ($\sigma$)**, **strain ($\gamma$)**, or **strain rate ($\dot{\gamma}$)** — the fundamental variables of rheological measurement.

Mathematically, the physical state defines the constitutive relationship:

$$\sigma = f(\gamma, \dot{\gamma}, t)$$

where $t$ is time. Foods rarely exist as ideal solids or ideal liquids; most are **viscoelastic**, occupying an intermediate spectrum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The physical state of a food material is the *first determining factor* in selecting the appropriate rheological model (Hookean, Newtonian, Non-Newtonian, or Viscoelastic).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a chocolate bar in three situations:
1. **Cold (5 °C)** — it is a hard, brittle **solid** (snap it → it breaks, little flow).
2. **Warm (35 °C, body temperature)** — it becomes a soft, **viscoelastic paste** (it bends, slowly flows, and remembers its shape partially).
3. **Molten (45 °C)** — it pours like a **viscous liquid** (it flows continuously and has no memory of shape).

> This single example shows that the *same* food material can occupy multiple physical states depending on thermal and mechanical history. **Temperature**, **water activity ($a_w$)**, and **composition** are the master switches that flip a food between these states.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range in Foods | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear Stress | $\sigma$ | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{6}$ | Pa |
| Shear Strain | $\gamma$ | $10^{-4}$ to 10 | dimensionless |
| Shear Rate | $\dot{\gamma}$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{3}$ | s$^{-1}$ |
| Glass Transition Temp. | $T_g$ | $-135$ to $200$ | °C |
| Melting Point | $T_m$ | $-50$ to $300$ | °C |
| Water Activity | $a_w$ | $0.0$ to $1.0$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Newton's Constant of Viscosity ($\eta$)** for water at 20 °C is **$\eta = 1.002$ mPa·s**, often used as the calibration standard in food rheometers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Physical States of Foods

Foods are classified based on their **mechanical response** to applied stress. The two ideal end-members are:

#### 2.1.1 Ideal (Hookean) Solid
- Stores energy elastically.
- Stress is proportional to strain, independent of strain rate and time.
- Modeled by **Hooke's Law**:

$$\sigma = G \cdot \gamma$$

where $G$ is the **Shear Modulus (Pa)**.

- Example: A frozen ice cube, a hard candy.

#### 2.1.2 Ideal (Newtonian) Liquid
- Dissipates energy viscously (as heat).
- Stress is proportional to strain rate, independent of strain and time.
- Modeled by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

where $\eta$ is the **Dynamic Viscosity (Pa·s)**.

- Example: Water, vegetable oil, honey (at moderate shear rates).

#### 2.1.3 Viscoelastic Material (Real Foods)
The vast majority of foods — bread dough, cheese, yogurt, ketchup, melted chocolate, jam, butter — are **viscoelastic**, meaning they exhibit *both* elastic (solid-like) and viscous (liquid-like) behavior simultaneously.

The general linear viscoelastic constitutive equation is a **hereditary integral**:

$$\sigma(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} G(t-\tau) \, \dot{\gamma}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

- $G(t-\tau)$ is the **relaxation modulus**.
- The stress at time $t$ depends on the entire deformation *history*.

### 2.2 Phase/State Categories of Foods

| Physical State | Structural Character | Rheological Behavior | Food Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **True Solid (Crystalline)** | Ordered 3-D lattice | Brittle / Elastic (Hookean) | Ice, sugar crystals, salt |
| **Amorphous Glassy** | Disordered, frozen | Hard, brittle, $E \approx 10^{9}$ Pa | Hard candy, freeze-dried fruit |
| **Amorphous Rubbery** | Disordered, mobile | Soft, flexible, $E \approx 10^{6}$ Pa | Bread crust, mozzarella, gum |
| **Gel (Polymer/Swollen)** | 3-D network trapping water | Yield stress, viscoelastic | Agar, gelatin, pectin jelly |
| **Paste / Dough** | Concentrated dispersion | Highly viscoelastic, non-Newtonian | Peanut butter, chocolate, dough |
| **Emulsion** | Oil + water phases | Viscoelastic, often shear-thinning | Mayonnaise, milk, butter |
| **Foam** | Gas in liquid/solid matrix | Non-Newtonian, often plastic | Whipped cream, bread, meringue |
| **Suspension** | Solid particles in liquid | Shear-thinning, may yield | Orange juice, chocolate milk |
| **True Liquid** | Molecular fluid | Newtonian | Water, oils, syrups |

### 2.3 The Five Physical States of Amorphous Foods (State Diagram Concept)

For amorphous (non-crystalline) foods, **temperature** and **water content** determine five sub-states:

1. **Glassy State** — molecules immobile; material is hard and brittle.
2. **Rubbery State** — molecular motion increases; soft, flexible.
3. **Viscous Flow / Liquid State** — long-range molecular flow.
4. **Rubbery–Glassy Transition** — occurs at **$T_g$** (Glass Transition Temperature).
5. **Crystalline / Melted** — when temperature exceeds $T_m$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$T_g$ is a KTU-favorite concept.** Above $T_g$, an amorphous food becomes rubbery; below, it is glassy. The **$T_g$ depends strongly on water content** — adding water *plasticizes* the food and lowers $T_g$, per the **Gordon–Taylor equation** (see Section 3).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute-value bars and conditional dividers are written as `\vert` to preserve the markdown table structure.

| # | Equation | Name / Law | Application in Foods | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = G \cdot \gamma$ | Hooke's Law | Ideal elastic solid | Pa |
| 2 | $\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's Law | Ideal viscous liquid | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele) | Pseudoplastic ($n<1$), dilatant ($n>1$) | Pa |
| 4 | $\sigma = \sigma_y + \eta_{pl} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Bingham Model | Yield-stress foods (ketchup, mustard) | Pa |
| 5 | $\sigma - \sigma_y = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Herschel–Bulkley | Most real yield-stress foods | Pa |
| 6 | $\sigma = \eta_{\infty} \cdot \dot{\gamma} + \dfrac{(\eta_0 - \eta_{\infty})\dot{\gamma}}{1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^{m}}$ | Cross Model | Shear-thinning liquid foods | Pa |
| 7 | $G(t) = G_{\infty} + \sum_{i} G_i \, e^{-t/\lambda_i}$ | Generalized Maxwell | Viscoelastic relaxation | Pa |
| 8 | $T_g = \dfrac{w_1 T_{g1} + k \, w_2 T_{g2}}{w_1 + k \, w_2}$ | Gordon–Taylor | Predicting $T_g$ of mixed foods | K |
| 9 | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Water Activity Definition | Shelf-life, microbial safety | dimensionless |
| 10 | $D = \dfrac{\sigma}{3 \eta}$ | Trouton Ratio (Extensional) | Dough, melt flow | s$^{-1}$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Design:** Selecting pumps (for liquids) vs extruders (for doughs) vs mixers (for pastes) — the physical state dictates the equipment.
- **Texture Engineering:** Cheese is a *gel* → firmness is controlled by the casein network density. Bread is a *foam* → texture is controlled by gas-cell wall thickness.
- **Sensory Science:** The "mouthfeel" of a food is a direct perceptual correlate of its physical state.
- **Shelf-Life Prediction:** Foods stored *below* $T_g$ are glassy and stable; those *above* $T_g$ undergo stickiness, caking, and crystallization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Generalized Hookean Stress–Strain Relation for a Solid Food

We start from the definition of the **shear modulus** as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain in the linear elastic limit.

**Step 1: Define shear stress.**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$$

where $F$ is the tangential force (N) applied over a contact area $A$ (m$^2$).

**Step 2: Define shear strain.**

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{h} = \tan\theta$$

where $\Delta x$ is the lateral displacement (m) and $h$ is the height of the sample (m).

**Step 3: State Hooke's empirical law for small deformations.**

In the linear elastic regime, stress is directly proportional to strain:

$$\sigma \propto \gamma$$

**Step 4: Introduce the proportionality constant — the shear modulus $G$.**

$$\sigma = G \cdot \gamma$$

**Step 5: Derive the relationship to Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$.**

For isotropic elastic solids, the elastic constants are related by:

$$G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

For incompressible food materials, $\nu \approx 0.5$, giving:

$$G \approx \dfrac{E}{3}$$

> This is why many food texts use the **elastification factor $E/3$** to convert between tensile and shear measurements on soft solids like cheese or butter.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Newtonian Viscosity Law from Kinetic Theory

**Step 1: Newton's postulate.**

For a fluid confined between two parallel plates separated by distance $h$, with the top plate moving at velocity $U$:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{U}{h}$$

**Step 2: Define the coefficient of dynamic viscosity.**

The shear stress required to sustain a steady velocity gradient is proportional to $\dot{\gamma}$:

$$\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{U}{h}$$

**Step 3: Combine with the definition of kinematic viscosity.**

$$\nu = \dfrac{\eta}{\rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the density (kg/m$^3$). Kinematic viscosity has units of m$^2$/s.

**Step 4: Generalize to non-Newtonian fluids (Power Law).**

If the proportionality is not constant, replace $\eta$ with an apparent viscosity $\eta_{ap}$:

$$\eta_{ap} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$

This yields the **Ostwald–de Waele Power Law**:

$$\sigma = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

where $K$ is the **consistency index (Pa·s$^n$)** and $n$ is the **flow behavior index (dimensionless)**.

> [!NOTE]
> - $n = 1$ → Newtonian.
> - $n < 1$ → **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** — most foods (ketchup, blood, molten chocolate).
> - $n > 1$ → **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** — rare; e.g., highly concentrated starch suspensions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: The Gordon–Taylor Equation for $T_g$ of a Moist Food

**Step 1: Start with the rule of mixtures for a binary system of solids (mass fraction $w_1$) and water (mass fraction $w_2 = 1 - w_1$).**

The glass transition temperature $T_g$ of the mixture lies between $T_{g1}$ (anhydrous solid) and $T_{g2}$ (water = 135 K).

**Step 2: Apply the empirical Gordon–Taylor weighting:**

$$T_g = \dfrac{w_1 T_{g1} + k \, w_2 T_{g2}}{w_1 + k \, w_2}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to solve for $w_2$ (the water content).**

$$T_g (w_1 + k w_2) = w_1 T_{g1} + k w_2 T_{g2}$$

$$T_g w_1 + T_g k w_2 = w_1 T_{g1} + k w_2 T_{g2}$$

$$T_g w_1 - w_1 T_{g1} = k w_2 T_{g2} - T_g k w_2$$

$$w_1 (T_g - T_{g1}) = k w_2 (T_{g2} - T_g)$$

$$w_2 = \dfrac{w_1 (T_g - T_{g1})}{k (T_{g2} - T_g)}$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example (KTU-style).**

> A freeze-dried strawberry has $T_{g1} = 340$ K and $k = 5.0$. The water's $T_{g2} = 135$ K. At water content $w_2 = 0.10$ (10%), find the new $T_g$.

Using the Gordon–Taylor equation with $w_1 = 0.90$:

$$T_g = \dfrac{(0.90)(340) + (5.0)(0.10)(135)}{0.90 + (5.0)(0.10)}$$

$$T_g = \dfrac{306 + 67.5}{0.90 + 0.50} = \dfrac{373.5}{1.40}$$

$$T_g = 266.8 \text{ K} \approx -6.4 \text{ °C}$$

> **Interpretation:** Adding 10% water *plastically lowered* the glass transition from 67 °C to −6 °C — a 73 K drop. This is why crispy foods (chips, cookies) become chewy when they absorb moisture: they rise *above* their $T_g$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Identifying the Physical State from Rheological Data

| Observed Response | Likely Physical State | Suggested Model | KTU-Expected Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\sigma$ independent of $\dot{\gamma}$ until yield, then linear | Plastic solid | Bingham | Mustard, toothpaste |
| $\sigma$ independent of $\dot{\gamma}$ until yield, then power-law | Yield-stress fluid | Herschel–Bulkley | Ketchup, tomato paste |
| $\sigma \propto \dot{\gamma}$ linearly | Newtonian liquid | Newton's Law | Water, milk, oil |
| $\sigma \propto \dot{\gamma}^{\,0.3}$ | Strongly shear-thinning | Power Law ($n \approx 0.3$) | Applesauce, banana puree |
| Stress decays exponentially at constant strain | Viscoelastic solid | Maxwell | Bread, cheese |
| Strain increases linearly with time under load | Viscoelastic liquid | Kelvin–Voigt + dashpot | Yogurt, sour cream |
| $G' > G''$ (storage > loss), both flat | Weak gel | Structured fluid | Gelatin, low-fat cheese |
| $G' < G''$, both flat | Viscous solution | Dilute polymer | Dilute gum solution |
| $G' = G''$ crossover | Gel point | Winter–Chambon criterion | Critical gelling concentration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Physical States

The following diagram maps the decision logic a rheologist (or a KTU examiner) uses to assign a food material to its correct physical state based on observed mechanical behavior.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FOOD MATERIAL"] --> B["Apply Small Shear Stress"]
    B --> C{"Is Strain Recoverable After Load Removed?"}
    C -- "YES Fully" --> D["ELASTIC DOMINANT"]
    C -- "PARTIAL" --> E["VISCOELASTIC DOMINANT"]
    C -- "NO Fully" --> F["VISCOUS DOMINANT"]

    D --> D1["Hookean Solid"]
    D1 --> D1a["Crystalline Solid"]
    D1 --> D1b["Amorphous Glassy State"]
    D1 --> D1c["Amorphous Rubbery State"]

    E --> E1["Viscoelastic Material"]
    E1 --> E1a["Gel"]
    E1 --> E1b["Paste or Dough"]
    E1 --> E1c["Emulsion"]
    E1 --> E1d["Foam"]
    E1 --> E1e["Suspension"]

    F --> F1["Viscous Fluid"]
    F1 --> F1a["Newtonian"]
    F1 --> F1b["Pseudoplastic n less than 1"]
    F1 --> F1c["Dilatant n greater than 1"]
    F1 --> F1d["Bingham Plastic"]
    F1 --> F1e["Herschel Bulkley"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: The Five Amorphous States of a Food

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["GLASSY STATE"] -- "T rises above Tg" --> S2["RUBBERY STATE"]
    S2 -- "T rises above Tm" --> S3["VISCOUS LIQUID"]
    S3 -- "Cool rapidly quench" --> S1
    S2 -- "Cool slowly crystallize" --> S4["CRYSTALLINE STATE"]
    S4 -- "T rises above Tm" --> S3
    S2 -- "Water added plasticization" --> S5["GEL NETWORK STATE"]
    S5 -- "Water removed drying" --> S2

    S1 -.- N1["Hard Brittle E approx 10^9 Pa"]
    S2 -.- N2["Soft Flexible E approx 10^6 Pa"]
    S3 -.- N3["Flows Continuously E approx 10^3 Pa"]
    S4 -.- N4["Ordered Lattice Sharp Melting"]
    S5 -.- N5["3D Network Traps Water"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Rheological Test Method Selection by Physical State

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Determine Physical State"] --> Q{"Food Sample"}
    Q -- "Solid" --> R["Use Oscillatory Test Small Amplitude"]
    Q -- "Soft Solid or Gel" --> S["Use Creep Test or Oscillatory"]
    Q -- "Liquid" --> T["Use Rotational Flow Test"]
    Q -- "Powder" --> U["Use Compression or Bending Test"]

    R --> R1["Measure G Prime and G Double Prime"]
    S --> S1["Measure J t and Recovery"]
    T --> T1["Measure Viscosity vs Shear Rate"]
    U --> U1["Measure Young Modulus E"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of a Shear Deformation Test

Although the rheometer is a physical instrument, its functional architecture can be represented as a block diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Top Plate Moves at Velocity U"] --> A2["Shear Rate dot gamma equals U over h"]
    A2 --> A3["Force Sensor Measures F"]
    A3 --> A4["Stress sigma equals F over A"]
    A4 --> A5["Compute Apparent Viscosity eta ap equals sigma over dot gamma"]
    A5 --> A6["Plot Flow Curve log sigma vs log dot gamma"]
    A6 --> A7["Fit Power Law or Herschel Bulkley Model"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the physical state of a food material. List the four principal physical states relevant to food rheology.**

**Model Answer:**

The physical state of a food material describes its phase configuration and structural arrangement (solid, liquid, gas, or intermediate colloidal/gel state) that governs how it deforms and flows under applied stress.

The four principal states are:
1. **True Solid** — Hookean, energy-storing (e.g., ice).
2. **Viscous Liquid** — Newtonian, energy-dissipating (e.g., water, oil).
3. **Viscoelastic Solid** — stores and dissipates energy (e.g., bread, cheese).
4. **Viscoelastic Liquid** — flows over time but shows elastic recoil (e.g., yogurt, ketchup).

> Marks: 1 (definition) + 2 (classification with examples) = **3 marks**.

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State Hooke's Law and Newton's Law of Viscosity. Give one food example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hooke's Law (Ideal Solid):**
$$\sigma = G \cdot \gamma$$
Stress is proportional to strain; the constant $G$ is the **shear modulus (Pa)**. *Example:* a hard candy or a frozen fruit bar.

**Newton's Law of Viscosity (Ideal Liquid):**
$$\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$
Stress is proportional to strain rate; the constant $\eta$ is the **dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)**. *Example:* refined vegetable oil at room temperature.

> Marks: 1 (each law with formula) + 0.5 (example each) = **3 marks**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the physical states of food materials with suitable examples. Explain, with equations, the constitutive laws that govern the ideal solid and ideal liquid states. Discuss why most real foods deviate from these ideal laws.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A chocolate sauce exhibits non-Newtonian flow. Using the **Power Law model**, calculate the apparent viscosity at a shear rate of $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$ given that the consistency index $K = 12.5 \text{ Pa·s}^{0.4}$ and the flow behavior index $n = 0.4$. Comment on the flow behavior and the physical state of the sauce.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice).** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **five physical states of amorphous foods** with a state-diagram concept. How does water content (moisture) affect the glass transition temperature $T_g$? Write the **Gordon–Taylor equation** and describe its significance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A freeze-dried mango slice has anhydrous $T_{g1} = 328 \text{ K}$ and Gordon–Taylor constant $k = 4.2$. The $T_{g2}$ of pure water is $135 \text{ K}$. If the moisture content is 8% (w.b.), determine the new $T_g$ in °C. State two practical implications of your result for the storage of dried fruits.

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Model Answer:**

| Physical State | Equation | Constant | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal Solid | $\sigma = G \gamma$ | $G$ = Shear Modulus | Ice, hard candy |
| Ideal Liquid | $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta$ = Viscosity | Water, vegetable oil |
| Viscoelastic Solid | $\sigma = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $G$ and $\eta$ combined | Bread, cheese |
| Yield-Stress Fluid | $\sigma = \sigma_y + \eta_{pl} \dot{\gamma}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress | Ketchup, mustard |
| Emulsion/Foam/Gel | $\sigma = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $K$, $n$ | Mayonnaise, whipped cream |

> **Why real foods deviate:** Real foods are *composite, multi-phase, and time-dependent*. They contain water, biopolymers, fats, sugars, and air — all of which interact. Their internal structure changes with deformation (thixotropy, aging) and with temperature ($T_g$ transitions). Hence neither Hooke's nor Newton's law alone is sufficient.

> Marks distribution: 3 (classification) + 2 (equations) + 2 (deviation reasoning) = **7 marks**.

**(b) Model Answer:**

Given:
- $K = 12.5 \text{ Pa·s}^{0.4}$
- $n = 0.4$
- $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$

**Step 1: Apply the Power Law.**

$$\sigma = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n} = 12.5 \cdot (50)^{0.4}$$

**Step 2: Compute $(50)^{0.4}$.**

$$(50)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 50} = e^{0.4 \cdot 3.912} = e^{1.5648} \approx 4.78$$

**Step 3: Compute shear stress.**

$$\sigma = 12.5 \cdot 4.78 = 59.75 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4: Compute apparent viscosity.**

$$\eta_{ap} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\dot{\gamma}} = \dfrac{59.75}{50} = 1.195 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Alternatively**, using the direct formula $\eta_{ap} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$:

$$\eta_{ap} = 12.5 \cdot (50)^{0.4 - 1} = 12.5 \cdot (50)^{-0.6}$$

$$(50)^{-0.6} = \dfrac{1}{(50)^{0.6}} = \dfrac{1}{10.99} \approx 0.0910$$

$$\eta_{ap} = 12.5 \cdot 0.0910 = 1.137 \text{ Pa·s}$$

> **Comment:** Since $n = 0.4 < 1$, the chocolate sauce is **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**. Its viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate. The physical state is a **viscoelastic concentrated dispersion** — a non-Newtonian yield-stress fluid in the Herschel–Bulkley category.

> Marks: 1 (formula) + 3 (calculation steps) + 2 (interpretation) + 1 (units) = **7 marks**.

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Model Answer:**

The five physical states of amorphous foods (on a temperature axis at constant water content) are:

1. **Glassy State** — molecules are frozen in disordered positions; the material is hard and brittle (modulus $\approx 10^9$ Pa).
2. **Rubbery State** — molecular mobility increases; soft, flexible, leathery.
3. **Viscous Flow State** — long-range molecular motion; material flows.
4. **Crystalline State** — ordered, sharp melting.
5. **Gel State** — a 3-D polymer network trapping water (cross-linked polymer + solvent).

The **glass transition temperature $T_g$** separates glassy and rubbery states. Water acts as a **plasticizer** — it increases molecular mobility and therefore *lowers* $T_g$.

**Gordon–Taylor equation:**

$$T_g = \dfrac{w_1 T_{g1} + k \, w_2 T_{g2}}{w_1 + k \, w_2}$$

- $w_1, w_2$ = mass fractions of solids and water.
- $T_{g1}, T_{g2}$ = glass transition temperatures of solids and water.
- $k$ = Gordon–Taylor constant (empirical).

**Significance:** Predicts the stability window for storage of amorphous foods. Storage temperature must remain well *below* $T_g$ to maintain crispness and prevent caking, stickiness, and collapse.

> Marks: 2 (states) + 2 (water effect) + 2 (equation + significance) + 1 (diagram mention) = **7 marks**.

**(b) Model Answer:**

Given:
- $T_{g1} = 328 \text{ K}$
- $T_{g2} = 135 \text{ K}$
- $k = 4.2$
- Moisture content = 8% w.b. → $w_2 = 0.08$, $w_1 = 0.92$

**Step 1: Substitute into the Gordon–Taylor equation.**

$$T_g = \dfrac{(0.92)(328) + (4.2)(0.135)(0.08)}{0.92 + (4.2)(0.08)}$$

> *Note: Be careful with the moisture fraction. The question states 8% w.b., so $w_2 = 0.08$.*

**Step 2: Compute the numerator.**

$$(0.92)(328) = 301.76$$

$$(4.2)(0.135)(0.08) = 0.04536$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 301.76 + 0.04536 = 301.805$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator.**

$$(4.2)(0.08) = 0.336$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.92 + 0.336 = 1.256$$

**Step 4: Final calculation.**

$$T_g = \dfrac{301.805}{1.256} = 240.29 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5: Convert to °C.**

$$T_g = 240.29 - 273.15 = -32.86 \text{ °C}$$

**Practical Implications:**

1. **Storage temperature** must be kept well below $-32.86$ °C, which is impractical for ordinary storage. The mango slice must therefore be protected from moisture pickup to keep $T_g$ high.
2. **Packaging design:** Use **moisture-impermeable** laminates (aluminium foil, metallised films) to prevent plasticization by ambient humidity, which would otherwise cause the slice to collapse, become sticky, and lose crispness.

> Marks: 1 (formula setup) + 2 (numerical computation) + 1 (unit conversion) + 2 (practical implications) + 1 (correct rounding & sign) = **7 marks**.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize:**
> 1. **Forgetting units.** A value of $\eta_{ap} = 1.19$ without "Pa·s" loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $T_g$ and $T_m$.** $T_g$ is for *amorphous* foods; $T_m$ is for *crystalline* foods. Examiners will deduct marks for confusing them.
> 3. **Confusing pseudoplastic and dilatant.** $n < 1$ = pseudoplastic (most foods). $n > 1$ = dilatant (rare). Mixing these up is a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Moisture basis errors.** 8% w.b. ≠ 8% d.b. (dry basis). On a dry basis, 8% w.b. becomes $0.08 / 0.92 = 8.7\%$ d.b. Always read carefully.
> 5. **Skipping the physical-state comment.** Numerical answers without a *qualitative* discussion of what the result means for the food's physical state lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Physical State of a Food Material**

- ☐ The **physical state** of a food is determined by composition, water content ($a_w$, moisture), temperature, and stress history.
- ☐ Foods are classified as **solids, liquids, gases, gels, pastes, emulsions, foams, and suspensions**.
- ☐ **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = G \gamma$ — for ideal elastic solids; $G$ = shear modulus.
- ☐ **Newton's Law:** $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ — for ideal viscous liquids; $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity.
- ☐ Most foods are **viscoelastic** — they store *and* dissipate energy.
- ☐ **Amorphous foods** exist in 5 states: glassy, rubbery, viscous flow, crystalline, gel.
- ☐ The **glass transition temperature $T_g$** separates the glassy and rubbery states.
- ☐ **Water is a plasticizer** — it *lowers* $T_g$, per the **Gordon–Taylor equation**.
- ☐ **Power Law:** $\sigma = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$. Pseudoplastic if $n < 1$; dilatant if $n > 1$; Newtonian if $n = 1$.
- ☐ **Bingham Plastic:** has a yield stress $\sigma_y$ before flow begins (ketchup, mustard).
- ☐ **Herschel–Bulkley:** combines yield stress with power-law post-yield flow — the *most common* model for real food pastes.
- ☐ **Apparent viscosity** $\eta_{ap} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ — depends on shear rate for non-Newtonian fluids.
- ☐ **Crossover point** ($G' = G''$) defines the **gel point** — critical for sol–gel transition foods (pectin, agar, starch).
- ☐ Always **state units** (Pa, Pa·s, s$^{-1}$, K or °C) in numerical answers.
- ☐ **Interpretation is mandatory:** a numerical answer without a qualitative comment on the food's physical state is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Various Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Flow in Food Rheology

In the context of **Food Rheology**, **flow** refers to the continuous, irreversible deformation of a food material under the influence of an applied shear stress. Unlike elastic deformation, flow involves the perpetual rearrangement of molecules or particles within the food matrix, resulting in permanent shape change. The mathematical description of flow is governed by the relationship between **shear stress (τ)** and **shear rate (γ̇)**, which is captured by the rheological constitutive equation of the material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The classification of "Various Flows" establishes the foundational vocabulary required for Module 2 (Viscometry) and Module 3 (Dynamic Rheological Testing). Mastering this topic is mandatory for solving numerical problems on viscosity measurement and food texture design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honey vs. Water" Intuition

Imagine you tip a glass of **water** and a jar of **honey** simultaneously. The water gushes out almost instantly, doubling its flow speed when you tilt harder. The honey, on the other hand, creeps out slowly, and surprisingly, the harder you push (squeeze the jar), the easier it seems to flow. This everyday phenomenon is the essence of **various flow behaviors**:

- **Water** represents **Newtonian flow** — predictable, linear, constant viscosity.
- **Honey** represents a **non-Newtonian flow** — viscosity changes with applied force.
- **Tomato ketchup** is even more peculiar: it refuses to move until you shake the bottle hard enough (a **yield stress**), then it flows freely.
- **Whipped cream** becomes thinner as you whip it (shear thinning) and may rebuild thickness at rest (**thixotropy**).

This intuitive picture forms the basis of how food engineers classify fluids for processing, sensory perception, and quality control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Shear Rate (γ̇):** The rate of change of velocity (dv) with respect to perpendicular distance (dy). Standard unit: **s⁻¹** (reciprocal seconds). The typical range in food processing spans **1 to 10,000 s⁻¹**.

## 1.3 The Two Major Classification Axes

Food flows are classified along two independent axes:

| Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|------|--------|--------|
| **Time-dependence** | Time-Independent (steady-state) | Time-Dependent |
| **Elastic Recovery** | Purely Viscous | Viscoelastic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Time-independent flows** reach a constant shear stress for a given shear rate almost immediately. **Time-dependent flows** exhibit shear stress that evolves with the duration of shearing at constant shear rate.

## 1.4 Master Classification Chart of Various Flows

1. **Newtonian Flow** (ideal viscous)
2. **Non-Newtonian Flows**:
   - **Time-Independent Non-Newtonian**:
     - Shear-Thinning (Pseudoplastic)
     - Shear-Thickening (Dilatant)
     - Yield-Stress Fluids:
       - Bingham Plastic
       - Casson Plastic
       - Herschel–Bulkley
   - **Time-Dependent Non-Newtonian**:
     - Thixotropic
     - Rheopectic (Negative thixotropy)
3. **Viscoelastic Flows** (briefly introduced in Module 1)

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newtonian Flow — The Baseline

A fluid is **Newtonian** when the shear stress is **directly proportional** to the shear rate, with the proportionality constant being the **viscosity (η)**. The fluid element has no memory of its deformation history.

**Governing Equation:**

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

Where:
- **τ** = Shear stress (Pa)
- **η** = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- **γ̇** = Shear rate (s⁻¹)

**Key Properties:**
- Viscosity is **constant** with respect to shear rate, temperature (at fixed T), and time.
- On a τ vs. γ̇ plot, the curve is a **straight line passing through the origin**.
- On a η vs. γ̇ plot, the curve is a **horizontal straight line**.

**Food Examples:** Water, milk, fruit juices (clarified), refined oils, sugar solutions, dilute suspensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Note:** The viscosity of Newtonian fluids depends **only on temperature and composition**, not on the kinematic history of the flow. Examiners often test this fundamental property.

## 2.2 Non-Newtonian Time-Independent Flows

### 2.2.1 Shear-Thinning (Pseudoplastic) Flow

The apparent viscosity **decreases** with increasing shear rate. The fluid becomes "thinner" when stirred or pumped faster.

**Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Model:**

$$\tau = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^n$$

Where:
- **K** = Consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- **n** = Flow behavior index (dimensionless)
- For pseudoplastic: **0 < n < 1**

**Apparent viscosity:**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^{n-1}$$

**Food Examples:** Tomato ketchup, honey, fruit purees, polymer solutions, blood, most salad dressings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Shear-thinning behavior is exploited in pipeline design (lower pumping energy at high flow rates) and in sensory design (pourable but cohesive on the spoon).

### 2.2.2 Shear-Thickening (Dilatant) Flow

The apparent viscosity **increases** with increasing shear rate. The fluid becomes "thicker" when agitated.

**Power-Law Model (same form, different n):**

$$\tau = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^n \quad \text{with} \quad n > 1$$

**Food Examples:** Concentrated starch suspensions, wet sand, some high-solids corn starch dispersions, certain cocoa suspensions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *n < 1* (pseudoplastic) with *n > 1* (dilatant). Memorize: **n less than 1 → thinning; n greater than 1 → thickening**. The case *n = 1* recovers Newtonian flow.

### 2.2.3 Bingham Plastic Flow

A solid-like material that **does not flow** until the applied stress exceeds a critical value called the **yield stress (τ₀)**. Above τ₀, it flows with a **constant plastic viscosity (η_p)**.

**Bingham Equation:**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma} \quad \text{for} \quad \tau > \tau_0$$

If τ < τ₀, the material behaves as a rigid solid (γ̇ = 0).

**Food Examples:** Toothpaste, margarine, tomato paste (high concentration), peanut butter, mayonnaises with high internal structure.

### 2.2.4 Casson Plastic Flow

A refinement of the Bingham model that captures the **square-root stress–shear rate relationship** observed in many food suspensions. It was specifically developed for **chocolate melts** by Malcolm Casson (1959).

**Casson Equation:**

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$

Where:
- **τ₀** = Casson yield stress (Pa)
- **η_∞** = Casson infinite-shear viscosity (Pa·s)

**Food Examples:** Chocolate (melted), blood, tomato sauce, printing inks, some fruit pulps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The Casson model is the **industry standard for chocolate rheology**. Questions on chocolate flow in KTU exams almost exclusively use the Casson equation.

### 2.2.5 Herschel–Bulkley Flow

The most general three-parameter model: combines a yield stress with a power-law shear response. It subsumes Bingham (n = 1) and Casson (special case) as limiting cases.

**Herschel–Bulkley Equation:**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^n \quad \text{for} \quad \tau > \tau_0$$

Where:
- **τ₀** = Yield stress (Pa)
- **K** = Consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- **n** = Flow behavior index (dimensionless)

**Special cases:**
- If τ₀ = 0, reduces to **Power-Law model**.
- If n = 1, reduces to **Bingham model**.
- If n = 0.5 and special K–η relation, reduces to **Casson model**.

**Food Examples:** Yogurt, mustard, dough, processed cheese, structured emulsions.

## 2.3 Non-Newtonian Time-Dependent Flows

### 2.3.1 Thixotropic Flow

The apparent viscosity **decreases** with time under **constant** shear rate, and **recovers** gradually when shear is removed. The structural breakdown is **reversible**.

**Visual signature:** A **hysteresis loop** on the τ vs. γ̇ plot, with the **up-curve lying below the down-curve**.

**Food Examples:** Ketchup (thixotropy allows it to pour after shaking), whipped toppings, gels, yogurt, some honey varieties.

**Structural Model (Simplified):**

$$\tau(t) = \eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma} + [\tau_0 - \eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}] \cdot e^{-t/\lambda}$$

Where **λ** is the structural breakdown time constant (s).

### 2.3.2 Rheopectic (Negative Thixotropic) Flow

The apparent viscosity **increases** with time under constant shear rate. The **up-curve lies above the down-curve** on a hysteresis plot. This is rare in foods.

**Food Examples:** Some gypsum pastes, certain protein solutions, printer's ink, some bentonite suspensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — Thixo vs. Rheo:** "**Thixo**" comes from Greek *thixis* (touch) — viscosity changes upon **touching/shearing**. "**Rheo**" comes from *rheos* (flow) — viscosity builds up with **continued flow**. Thixotropy = thinning with time; Rheopexy = thickening with time.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Model | Equation | Parameters | Typical Food | n range / τ₀ condition |
|-------|----------|------------|--------------|------------------------|
| **Newtonian** | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | η | Water, milk, oil | Always linear through origin |
| **Power-Law (Pseudoplastic)** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | K, n | Ketchup, honey | $0 < n < 1$ |
| **Power-Law (Dilatant)** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | K, n | Starch suspensions | $n > 1$ |
| **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | τ₀, η_p | Toothpaste, peanut butter | $\tau_0 > 0$, $n = 1$ |
| **Casson Plastic** | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ | τ₀, η_∞ | Chocolate, blood | $\tau_0 > 0$, $n = 0.5$ |
| **Herschel–Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | τ₀, K, n | Yogurt, dough | $\tau_0 > 0$, any n |
| **Thixotropic** | $\tau(t) = \eta_\infty \dot{\gamma} + (\tau_0 - \eta_\infty \dot{\gamma})e^{-t/\lambda}$ | τ₀, η_∞, λ | Ketchup, gels | Time-dependent |
| **Rheopectic** | Viscosity increases with time | — | Rare, some pastes | Time-dependent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** Consistency index **K** has units of **Pa·sⁿ** (not Pa·s) because the power-law exponent n is non-unity. Many students lose marks for writing "Pa·s" universally. **Apparent viscosity** is always expressed in **Pa·s**, regardless of the model.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipe-line design** (Hagen–Poiseuille flow): shear rate depends on flow rate; power-law index determines pressure drop.
- **Pump selection:** Shear-thinning fluids require lower pumping power than Newtonian at the same flow rate.
- **Sensory texture:** Mouthfeel correlates with viscosity at shear rates of **10 to 100 s⁻¹**.
- **Quality control:** Chocolate must meet **Casson yield stress ≤ 10 Pa** and **Casson viscosity ≤ 2.5 Pa·s** per international standards (ICA).
- **Process design:** Spray drying atomizers operate at **10⁴ to 10⁵ s⁻¹** — pseudoplasticity is critical.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Apparent Viscosity from Power-Law Model

**Step 1 — Starting Equation (Power-Law Constitutive Law):**

$$\tau = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^n$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Apparent Viscosity:**

By definition, the apparent viscosity at any shear rate equals the ratio of shear stress to shear rate:

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the Power-Law Expression:**

Replace τ in the numerator with the right-hand side of the power-law equation:

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^n}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Simplification:**

Combine the two shear-rate terms using the law of exponents $\left( \frac{x^a}{x^b} = x^{a-b} \right)$:

$$\eta_{app} = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^{n-1}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

- If $n = 1$ (Newtonian): $\eta_{app} = K = \eta$ → constant viscosity recovered.
- If $n < 1$ (pseudoplastic): $n - 1 < 0$ → as γ̇ increases, the exponent becomes more negative, and $\eta_{app}$ **decreases**.
- If $n > 1$ (dilatant): $n - 1 > 0$ → as γ̇ increases, $\eta_{app}$ **increases**.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **single most-tested mathematical manipulation** in KTU Module 1 numerical questions. Practice it until you can reproduce it without referring to notes.

## 3.2 Derivation: Recovery of Newtonian Limit from Herschel–Bulkley

**Starting Point — Herschel–Bulkley Equation:**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^n$$

**Apply the constraint $\tau_0 = 0$:**

When the yield stress vanishes, the material flows under arbitrarily small stress:

$$\tau = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^n$$

**Further apply the constraint $n = 1$:**

Setting the flow index to unity makes the equation linear in shear rate:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Compare with the Newtonian constitutive law:**

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Identification:** It is clear that $K = \eta$, so the Herschel–Bulkley model reduces exactly to the Newtonian model under the double constraint $(\tau_0 = 0, n = 1)$. The Bingham model is obtained with $\tau_0 > 0, n = 1$.

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> **Problem:** A tomato ketchup sample is tested in a rotational viscometer at 25 °C. The following data are obtained:
> - At γ̇ = 10 s⁻¹, τ = 18 Pa
> - At γ̇ = 100 s⁻¹, τ = 95 Pa
>
> Assuming the fluid obeys the power-law model, calculate the consistency index (K) and flow behavior index (n). Then determine the apparent viscosity at γ̇ = 50 s⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Write the model:**

$$\tau = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^n$$

**Step 2 — Form two equations:**

$$18 = K \cdot (10)^n \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$95 = K \cdot (100)^n \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Divide (ii) by (i) to eliminate K:**

$$\frac{95}{18} = \frac{K \cdot (100)^n}{K \cdot (10)^n}$$

$$\frac{95}{18} = \left(\frac{100}{10}\right)^n = (10)^n$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{95}{18}\right) = n \cdot \ln(10)$$

$$n = \frac{\ln(5.2778)}{\ln(10)} = \frac{1.6639}{2.3026} = 0.7226$$

**Step 5 — Substitute n back into (i) to find K:**

$$18 = K \cdot (10)^{0.7226}$$

$$10^{0.7226} = 5.2778$$

$$K = \frac{18}{5.2778} = 3.411 \ \text{Pa·s}^{0.7226}$$

**Step 6 — Compute apparent viscosity at γ̇ = 50 s⁻¹:**

$$\eta_{app} = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^{n-1} = 3.411 \cdot (50)^{(0.7226 - 1)}$$

$$\eta_{app} = 3.411 \cdot (50)^{-0.2774}$$

$$(50)^{-0.2774} = \frac{1}{50^{0.2774}} = \frac{1}{3.008} = 0.3324$$

$$\eta_{app} = 3.411 \times 0.3324 = 1.134 \ \text{Pa·s}$$

**Final Answer (Boxed):**
- $K = 3.41 \ \text{Pa·s}^{0.7226}$
- $n = 0.723$ (confirms pseudoplastic since $0 < n < 1$)
- $\eta_{app}(50 \ s^{-1}) = 1.13 \ \text{Pa·s}$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Choose the Right Model

**Step 1 — Plot the raw τ vs. γ̇ data on linear axes.**
- If the line passes through the origin AND is straight → **Newtonian**.
- If the line passes through the origin AND is curved (concave-down) → **Pseudoplastic**.
- If the line passes through the origin AND is concave-up → **Dilatant**.

**Step 2 — Re-examine whether the curve passes through the origin.**
- If not, the fluid has a **yield stress**. Move to Step 3.

**Step 3 — Apply a yield-stress model.**
- If the post-yield region is linear → **Bingham**.
- If the post-yield region follows a square-root law → **Casson**.
- If the post-yield region is curved with unknown exponent → **Herschel–Bulkley**.

**Step 4 — Check for time effects.**
- If the shear stress drifts downward at constant γ̇ → **Thixotropic**.
- If the shear stress drifts upward at constant γ̇ → **Rheopectic**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision matrix is the **exact algorithm** that KTU board examiners follow when constructing model answers. Rehearse it before the exam.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Rheological Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Shear Stress tau applied] --> DET{Rheological Detector}
    DET --> CLASS1[Constitutive Equation Selector]
    CLASS1 --> M1[Newtonian: tau equals eta gamma dot]
    CLASS1 --> M2[Non-Newtonian Time-Independent]
    CLASS1 --> M3[Non-Newtonian Time-Dependent]

    M3 --> M3A[Thixotropic: structural breakdown]
    M3 --> M3B[Rheopectic: structural build-up]
    M1 --> OUT1[Output: Constant Viscosity]
```
