# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids. Give two food examples for each category.**

**Model Answer:**

**Newtonian Fluids:** The shear stress is directly proportional to the shear rate, and the proportionality constant (viscosity) is independent of shear rate, shear history, and time. Mathematically, $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$, where η is constant.
*Food examples:* Water, milk, refined sunflower oil, dilute sugar solution, clarified apple juice.

**Non-Newtonian Fluids:** The relationship between shear stress and shear rate is non-linear, and the apparent viscosity depends on shear rate, time, or deformation history. The constitutive law is not a simple proportional relationship.
*Food examples:* Tomato ketchup (pseudoplastic), chocolate melt (Casson plastic), yogurt (Herschel–Bulkley), whipped toppings (thixotropic), toothpaste (Bingham plastic).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define thixotropy. Sketch the rheogram and explain its characteristic features.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Thixotropy is a time-dependent rheological behavior in which the apparent viscosity of a fluid **decreases** with time under constant shear rate, and the structure is **gradually recovered** when the applied shear is removed. The process is **isothermal and reversible**.

**Rheogram:** When the shear rate is increased gradually from zero to a maximum (up-curve) and then decreased back to zero (down-curve), the two curves do not overlap. A **hysteresis loop** is formed, with the **up-curve lying below the down-curve**. The area enclosed by the loop is a measure of the **thixotropic breakdown energy** (in J/m³).

**Characteristic features:**
- The fluid exhibits shear-thinning behavior at any instant.
- The structural breakdown is time-dependent.
- The recovery is gradual, requiring a finite rest period.
- **Food examples:** Ketchup, whipped toppings, some honeys, yogurt gels.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. The model answer below is structured accordingly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the apparent viscosity expression for a fluid obeying the **Power-Law model**. Show that the Newtonian limit is recovered. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) constitutive equation is:

$$\tau = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^n \quad \text{...(1)}$$

By definition, the apparent viscosity equals the ratio of shear stress to shear rate:

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Substituting (1) into (2):

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^n}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^{n-1} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Newtonian Limit:** When $n = 1$, equation (1) becomes $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}$. Comparing with the Newtonian law $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$, we identify $K = \eta$. Then equation (3) becomes $\eta_{app} = K \cdot (\dot{\gamma})^{0} = K = \eta$, which is a constant — the Newtonian limit is recovered.

---

**(b)** A food sauce is tested in a concentric-cylinder viscometer. At γ̇ = 50 s⁻¹, the shear stress is 25 Pa; at γ̇ = 500 s⁻¹, the shear stress is 130 Pa. Assuming power-law behavior, determine K, n, and the apparent viscosity at γ̇ = 200 s⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

From $\tau = K (\dot{\gamma})^n$, two data points yield:

$$25 = K (50)^n \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$130 = K (500)^n \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Dividing (ii) by (i):

$$\frac{130}{25} = (10)^n \Rightarrow 5.2 = 10^n$$

$$n = \log_{10}(5.2) = 0.7160$$

Substituting n into (i):

$$25 = K (50)^{0.7160} = K (16.20)$$

$$K = 1.543 \ \text{Pa·s}^{0.7160}$$

Apparent viscosity at γ̇ = 200 s⁻¹:

$$\eta_{app} = K (200)^{n-1} = 1.543 \times (200)^{-0.2840}$$

$$(200)^{-0.2840} = 0.1804$$

$$\eta_{app} = 1.543 \times 0.1804 = 0.278 \ \text{Pa·s}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $0 < n < 1$, the sauce is **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **Bingham plastic** and **Casson plastic** models. Mention a food product where each is applicable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Bingham Plastic Model:**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma} \quad \text{for} \quad \tau > \tau_0$$

The fluid does not flow until the applied stress exceeds the yield stress τ₀. Above τ₀, it flows as a Newtonian fluid with constant plastic viscosity η_p. The rheogram is a straight line with slope η_p, intercepting the τ-axis at τ₀. **Food example:** Toothpaste, margarine, peanut butter.

**Casson Plastic Model:**

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$$

It is a non-linear yield-stress model proposed by Malcolm Casson (1959) for chocolate. It captures the square-root relationship between shear stress and shear rate in flocculated suspensions. The rheogram is concave-down, intercepting the τ-axis at τ₀. **Food example:** Melted chocolate (industry standard per ICA).

---

**(b)** A chocolate manufacturer needs to characterize a chocolate melt using the **Casson model**. Two viscometric readings are: at γ̇ = 5 s⁻¹, τ = 22.5 Pa; at γ̇ = 50 s⁻¹, τ = 90.0 Pa. Determine the Casson yield stress (τ₀) and the Casson infinite-shear viscosity (η_∞). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Casson equation:

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$$

**Data point 1:** $\sqrt{22.5} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{5 \eta_\infty}$
$$4.743 = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{5 \eta_\infty} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Data point 2:** $\sqrt{90.0} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{50 \eta_\infty}$
$$9.487 = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{50 \eta_\infty} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Subtracting (i) from (ii):

$$4.743 = \sqrt{50 \eta_\infty} - \sqrt{5 \eta_\infty}$$

$$4.743 = \sqrt{\eta_\infty}(\sqrt{50} - \sqrt{5}) = \sqrt{\eta_\infty}(7.071 - 2.236) = \sqrt{\eta_\infty}(4.835)$$

$$\sqrt{\eta_\infty} = \frac{4.743}{4.835} = 0.9811$$

$$\eta_\infty = 0.9626 \ \text{Pa·s}$$

Substituting back into (i):

$$4.743 = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{5 \times 0.9626}$$

$$4.743 = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{4.813} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + 2.194$$

$$\sqrt{\tau_0} = 2.549 \Rightarrow \tau_0 = 6.498 \ \text{Pa}$$

**Quality check:** The Casson yield stress is **within the international chocolate standard of τ₀ < 10 Pa** — the chocolate is well-tempered and pourable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in "Various Flows" Questions:**
> 1. **Units of K (Consistency Index):** Writing "Pa·s" instead of "Pa·sⁿ" — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing n with m:** Some textbooks use m = 1/n. Always re-check which convention the question uses.
> 3. **Forgetting to check the yield-stress condition:** If τ < τ₀, the Herschel–Bulkley model is **invalid**; the material does not flow.
> 4. **Mixing up thixotropy and pseudoplasticity:** Shear-thinning is **instantaneous**; thixotropy is **time-dependent**.
> 5. **Logarithmic errors in power-law fitting:** Use **ln–ln plots** for n and K. Forgetting to convert log base costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Casson model arithmetic:** Forgetting to take square roots before algebra leads to an incorrect τ₀.
> 7. **Apparent viscosity definition:** Always define η_app = τ/γ̇ at the start of the answer. Examiners specifically check this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Newtonian flow** obeys $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ with constant η; examples include water, milk, and oils.
- ✅ **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** has $0 < n < 1$; viscosity decreases with shear rate; examples include ketchup and honey.
- ✅ **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** has $n > 1$; viscosity increases with shear rate; examples include concentrated starch.
- ✅ **Bingham plastic** has a yield stress τ₀ and a linear post-yield region; examples include toothpaste and peanut butter.
- ✅ **Casson plastic** uses a square-root law; it is the **industry standard for chocolate**.
- ✅ **Herschel–Bulkley** is the most general three-parameter model: $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^n$.
- ✅ **Thixotropy** is time-dependent **decrease** in viscosity under constant shear; shows a hysteresis loop.
- ✅ **Rheopexy** is time-dependent **increase** in viscosity under constant shear; rare in foods.
- ✅ **Apparent viscosity** is always defined as $\eta_{app} = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ at the prevailing shear rate.
- ✅ **Consistency index K** has units of **Pa·sⁿ**, not Pa·s.
- ✅ **Flow behavior index n** is dimensionless.
- ✅ For $n = 1$, **all** yield-stress models (Bingham, Casson, Herschel–Bulkley) reduce to a **linear post-yield** form.
- ✅ To convert between models, use the **decision matrix**: origin intercept → yield stress; linearity → Bingham; square root → Casson; unknown exponent → Herschel–Bulkley.
- ✅ Food processing shear rates: **10⁻³ s⁻¹ (sedimentation) → 10⁵ s⁻¹ (atomization)**.
- ✅ Mouthfeel perception occurs in the **10–100 s⁻¹** shear-rate range.
- ✅ ICA chocolate standard: **Casson yield stress < 10 Pa, Casson viscosity < 2.5 Pa·s**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classical Ideal Materials
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classical Ideal Materials

# Classical Ideal Materials — Foundational Rheological Models

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classical Ideal Materials** in rheology refer to the three fundamental, mathematically idealized constitutive models that describe the limiting mechanical responses of matter under applied stress or strain. They are: (1) the **Hookean Solid** (ideal elastic body), (2) the **Newtonian Fluid** (ideal viscous liquid), and (3) the **St. Venant Body** (ideal plastic/rigid-perfectly plastic material). These represent pure, uncoupled, linear responses and serve as the **constitutive building blocks** from which all real, complex material behaviors are derived through combinations and modifications.

In food rheology, these classical ideals are essential because virtually **no real food is purely Hookean, Newtonian, or St. Venantian** — they all deviate in measurable, often temperature- and time-dependent ways. The art of food rheology is to characterize the *deviation* from ideality.

### 1.2 The Three Primary Ideal Materials at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **The Trinity of Classical Ideal Materials:**
> 1. **Hookean Solid** — Stores all input energy reversibly. Strain is instantaneously and completely recovered upon stress removal.
> 2. **Newtonian Fluid** — Dissipates all input energy irreversibly as heat through viscous flow. Strain is permanent.
> 3. **St. Venant Body** — Remains rigid until a critical yield stress is exceeded, after which it deforms indefinitely without further increase in stress.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

- **Hookean Solid ≈ A Perfect Spring** 🌀
  Think of a calibrated laboratory spring (Hooke's original setup). Pull it with a force $F$; it stretches by a distance $x$ proportional to $F$ (the famous $F = kx$). Release the force — and *snap* — it returns instantly to its original length. **In food terms:** an ideal rubber band or a firm gel slice that, when bent, springs back perfectly.

- **Newtonian Fluid ≈ A Perfect Dashpot (Shock Absorber)** 🛢️
  Picture a piston moving through a cylinder filled with thick oil (Newton's original metaphor). The harder/faster you push, the more resistance. The piston never regains its starting position — once displaced, the oil has *flowed* permanently around it. **In food terms:** water, milk, refined edible oils, dilute sugar solutions, and clarified broths approximate this behavior.

- **St. Venant Body ≈ A Friction Block on a Surface** 🧱
  Imagine a wooden block resting on a table. Push gently — it does not budge. Push hard enough to overcome the static friction threshold (the **yield stress**) — and it slides indefinitely with no further resistance increase, regardless of how fast it goes. **In food terms:** modeling the onset of flow in concentrated tomato paste, margarine, or butter at a critical shear stress.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following bolded terms are the canonical descriptors used throughout KTU 2024 assessments:

- **Shear Stress ($\tau$)** — Force per unit area, expressed in **Pa (N/m²)**.
- **Shear Strain ($\gamma$)** — Dimensionless ratio of displacement to gap height.
- **Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}$)** — Rate of change of strain with time, in **s⁻¹**.
- **Elastic Modulus / Young's Modulus ($E$)** — For tensile elasticity, in **Pa**.
- **Shear Modulus ($G$)** — For shear elasticity, in **Pa**.
- **Dynamic Viscosity ($\eta$)** — Resistance to flow, in **Pa·s**.
- **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$)** — Defined as $\eta / \rho$, in **m²/s** ($\rho$ = density).
- **Yield Stress ($\tau_0$)** — Critical stress for plastic flow, in **Pa**.
- **Gravitational Acceleration ($g \approx 9.81$ m/s²)** — Used in falling-body viscometers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** KTU examiners *frequently* test whether students can clearly distinguish **stress-controlled** versus **strain-controlled** constitutive relationships. The Hookean law is most naturally expressed in *stress = function of strain* form, while the Newtonian law is most naturally expressed in *stress = function of strain rate* form. Memorize this distinction.

### 1.5 Historical & Engineering Context

Sir Robert Hooke published his law *ut tensio, sic vis* ("as the extension, so the force") in 1660 as the anagram **CEIIINOSSSTTUV**. Sir Isaac Newton proposed the linear viscosity law in his *Principia Mathematica* (1687), using the term "defectus lubricitatis" (lack of slipperiness). Adhémar Jean Claude Barré de Saint-Venant introduced the rigid-plastic idealization in 1870. These three models are the **constitutive alphabet** of continuum mechanics.

In **food engineering**, classical ideal models serve as:
- **First-order approximations** for dilute and simple food systems (water, oils, juices).
- **Reference baselines** against which the *non-ideal* behavior of complex foods (gels, pastes, emulsions, dough) is quantified.
- **Building blocks** for composite mechanical analogs (Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, Burgers, Bingham models).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hookean Solid (Ideal Elastic Body)

### 2.1.1 Physical Postulates
- The material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and **linearly elastic**.
- The relationship between stress and strain is **time-independent** and **fully recoverable**.
- The body obeys a **linear** constitutive law: doubling the stress doubles the strain.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Apply a small shear stress** $\tau$ to the upper face of a material element of height $h$ and area $A$.
2. The upper face **displaces** by a distance $x$ relative to the fixed lower face.
3. The **shear strain** is defined as the geometric ratio:

$$\gamma = \frac{x}{h}$$

4. The **shear stress** is defined as the force per unit area:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

5. **Hooke's Law in shear** states that the stress is directly proportional to the strain via the **shear modulus $G$**:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

6. **Why this works:** Energy input is stored elastically in the internal bond network (atomic/molecular spring-like bonds). Upon unloading, all stored energy is released, restoring the body to its original geometry.

### 2.1.3 Generalized 3D Hooke's Law
For an isotropic linear elastic material, the relationship between stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ and strain tensor $\varepsilon_{ij}$ is given by:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}$$

where $\lambda$ is **Lamé's first constant** and $\mu = G$ is the **shear modulus (Lamé's second constant)**.

### 2.1.4 Moduli Interrelationships
For an isotropic material, the three fundamental elastic moduli — **Young's modulus $E$**, **shear modulus $G$**, and **Poisson's ratio $\nu$** — are related by:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

The relationship between $E$, the **bulk modulus $K$** (volumetric elasticity), and $\nu$ is:

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In food engineering, $E$ is used to characterize the *firmness* or *crispness* of solid foods (apple flesh, cheese, crackers), while $G$ is used for shear-based instrumental texture measurements (e.g., cutting, puncture, TPA — Texture Profile Analysis).

### 2.1.5 Strain Energy Density
The elastic energy stored per unit volume in a Hookean solid under shear strain $\gamma$ is:

$$w = \tfrac{1}{2} G \gamma^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \frac{\tau^{2}}{G}$$

This is the area under the linear $\tau$–$\gamma$ loading curve.

---

## 2.2 The Newtonian Fluid (Ideal Viscous Liquid)

### 2.2.1 Physical Postulates
- The material **flows** under any applied stress, no matter how small.
- The relationship between shear stress and **shear rate** is **linear and time-independent**.
- All deformation energy is **dissipated as heat**; none is stored elastically.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. Consider a fluid confined between two parallel plates of area $A$, separated by a small gap $h$.
2. The lower plate is **stationary**; the upper plate is dragged horizontally with velocity $v$.
3. A **linear velocity profile** is established across the gap (the no-slip boundary condition implies the fluid in contact with the moving plate moves at $v$, and that in contact with the stationary plate is at rest).
4. The **shear rate** is therefore the velocity gradient:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{v}{h}$$

5. **Newton's Law of Viscosity** states that the shear stress is directly proportional to the shear rate, with the constant of proportionality being the **dynamic viscosity $\eta$**:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

6. **Why this works:** Molecular momentum transfer between adjacent fluid layers resists relative motion. In Newtonian fluids, this transfer is purely viscous (rate-dependent) and not viscoelastic (no time-dependent memory effects).

### 2.2.3 Viscosity Classification
- **Dynamic Viscosity** $\eta$ has units of **Pa·s** (or equivalently, kg/(m·s) or poise, where 10 poise = 1 Pa·s).
- **Kinematic Viscosity** $\nu = \eta / \rho$ has units of **m²/s** (or stokes, where 100 stokes = 1 m²/s).
- **Apparent Viscosity** $\eta_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ is the *ratio* definition, which collapses to $\eta$ only for Newtonian fluids.

### 2.2.4 Effect of Temperature
For most Newtonian food fluids (oils, sugar syrups), viscosity decreases exponentially with temperature, often following an **Arrhenius-type relation**:

$$\eta(T) = \eta_0 \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

where $E_a$ is the **activation energy of flow** and $R$ is the **universal gas constant (8.314 J/(mol·K))**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** Examiners often pose numerical questions on temperature-dependence of viscosity. The Arrhenius form is valid for Newtonian fluids above their glass transition; for water-like fluids, the **WLF (Williams-Landel-Ferry)** equation may be more appropriate near $T_g$. State your assumptions clearly.

### 2.2.5 Reynolds Number & Flow Regime
The dimensionless **Reynolds number** for a Newtonian fluid in a pipe of diameter $D$ is:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\eta}$$

- $Re < 2100$ → **Laminar flow**
- $2100 \leq Re \leq 4000$ → **Transitional flow**
- $Re > 4000$ → **Turbulent flow**

---

## 2.3 The St. Venant Body (Ideal Plastic / Rigid-Perfectly Plastic Material)

### 2.3.1 Physical Postulates
- The material behaves as a **rigid body** for $\vert \tau \vert < \tau_0$.
- Once the **yield stress $\tau_0$** is exceeded, the material deforms **indefinitely** with no further increase in stress.
- The post-yield behavior is mathematically equivalent to a Newtonian fluid with **zero viscosity** (or, in practice, a small plastic viscosity).

### 2.3.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. Apply an increasing shear stress $\tau$ to the material.
2. For $0 \leq \tau < \tau_0$, the material **does not deform** at all (rigid behavior, $\gamma = 0$).
3. The instant $\tau$ reaches $\tau_0$, the material **yields** and begins to flow.
4. For $\tau \geq \tau_0$, the material deforms with **no additional stress required** — flow continues under constant $\tau_0$.
5. The constitutive equation is the **piecewise definition**:

$$\tau = \begin{cases} G \gamma, & \text{when } \tau < \tau_0 \text{ (elastic pre-yield, often neglected)} \\ \tau_0, & \text{when } \tau \geq \tau_0 \text{ and flow occurs} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In its pure form, the St. Venant body is **rigid** below yield (no elastic strain at all), making it a **discontinuous** model. The more practical **elastic-plastic** version (an elastic Hookean pre-yield branch in series with a frictional slider) is preferred in engineering analysis.

### 2.3.3 Yield Stress in Food Systems
In food rheology, yield stress is conceptually critical for characterizing:
- **Spreading behavior** of butter, margarine, and peanut butter.
- **Pourability** of ketchup, mustard, and yogurt.
- **Sagging and slumping** in chocolate coatings.
- **Pumping initiation** of starch suspensions and sauces.

The **Bingham plastic fluid** (covered in Module 2) extends the St. Venant body by adding a finite post-yield viscosity.

---

## 2.4 Side-by-Side Comparative Summary

| Feature | Hookean Solid | Newtonian Fluid | St. Venant Body |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Solid (elastic) | Fluid (viscous) | Solid-to-fluid transition |
| **Independent variable** | Strain $\gamma$ | Strain rate $\dot{\gamma}$ | Stress $\tau$ vs threshold |
| **Constitutive law** | $\tau = G \gamma$ | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau = \tau_0$ (if yielding) |
| **Material constant** | $G$ [Pa] | $\eta$ [Pa·s] | $\tau_0$ [Pa] |
| **Energy fate on loading** | Stored elastically | Dissipated as heat | Stored then dissipated |
| **Recovery on unloading** | Instantaneous, complete | None (permanent) | None (permanent post-yield) |
| **Limiting case** | $G \to \infty$ → rigid body | $\eta \to \infty$ → rigid body | Elastic pre-yield Hookean solid |
| **Mechanical analog** | Spring | Dashpot | Friction slider (Saint-Venant element) |
| **Food example (ideal limit)** | Firm rubber gel | Water, refined oil | Concentrated suspension at onset |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Significance in Food Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility** of classical ideal models in the food industry:
> 1. **Pump and pipe sizing:** Pressure drop calculations for Newtonian fluids (milk, juice, edible oil) use the Hagen-Poiseuille equation directly.
> 2. **Mixing and agitation:** Power number correlations require knowledge of flow regime and apparent viscosity.
> 3. **Texture instrumentation:** TPA, back-extrusion, and penetration tests interpret force-deformation curves using Hookean approximations for small strains.
> 4. **Shelf-life modeling:** St. Venant-type yield stress governs the collapse of food gels over time.
> 5. **Heat exchanger design:** Reynolds number and Prandtl number calculations (both using $\eta$) for thermal processing of Newtonian foods.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Form | Units | Validity | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hooke's Law (shear) | $\tau = G \gamma$ | Pa = Pa · 1 | Linear elastic solid | Core |
| Hooke's Law (tensile) | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | Pa = Pa · 1 | Linear elastic solid, uniaxial | Core |
| Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa = Pa·s · s⁻¹ | Newtonian fluid | Core |
| Shear strain | $\gamma = x/h$ | dimensionless | Simple shear geometry | Core |
| Shear rate | $\dot{\gamma} = v/h$ | s⁻¹ | Simple shear (linear profile) | Core |
| Young's ↔ Shear modulus | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Pa | Isotropic material | Core |
| Young's ↔ Bulk modulus | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Pa | Isotropic material | Core |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \eta / \rho$ | m²/s | Any fluid | Core |
| Arrhenius viscosity | $\eta(T) = \eta_0 \exp(E_a / RT)$ | Pa·s | Newtonian, $T > T_g$ | Module 1/2 link |
| Reynolds number | $Re = \rho v D / \eta$ | dimensionless | Pipe flow | High-yield |
| Strain energy density | $w = \tfrac{1}{2} G \gamma^{2}$ | J/m³ | Hookean solid | Mid-yield |
| St. Venant yield | $\tau = \tau_0$ (if $\tau \geq \tau_0$) | Pa | Plastic flow | Core |
| Generalized Hooke | $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \delta_{ij} \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \varepsilon_{ij}$ | Pa | 3D isotropic elasticity | Advanced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to derive or apply any of these equations, always state the **assumptions** (e.g., isothermal, laminar, steady-state, isotropic, small strain). Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the assumption statement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Microscopic Diffusion to Newton's Law of Viscosity (Kinetic Theory Approach)

This derivation shows that the linear Newtonian constitutive law emerges naturally from a simple molecular momentum-flux argument.

### Step 1: Define the Shear Geometry

Consider a fluid between two parallel plates, with the lower plate at $y = 0$ (stationary) and the upper plate at $y = h$ (moving at velocity $v$ in the $x$-direction). The fluid velocity profile is $u(y) = (v/h) \cdot y$.

### Step 2: Define the Shear Stress as a Momentum Flux

The shear stress on a fluid plane is, by Newton's molecular definition, the net rate of $x$-momentum transfer per unit area in the $+y$ direction (perpendicular to flow):

$$\tau_{yx} = -\rho \overline{u'_x u'_y}$$

where $u'_x$ and $u'_y$ are the fluctuating velocity components, and the overbar denotes a time-average.

### Step 3: Invoke a Mean-Free-Path Transport Argument

Assume molecules travel an average distance $\ell$ (the mean free path) between collisions before transferring their $x$-momentum. The molecule arriving at $y = y_0$ from below carries the mean $x$-momentum of the layer at $y_0 - \ell$:

$$m \overline{u'_x}^{\text{from below}} = m \, u(y_0 - \ell) \approx m \left[ u(y_0) - \ell \frac{du}{dy} \right]$$

### Step 4: Compute the Net Momentum Flux

Similarly, the molecule arriving from above carries:

$$m \overline{u'_x}^{\text{from above}} = m \, u(y_0 + \ell) \approx m \left[ u(y_0) + \ell \frac{du}{dy} \right]$$

Subtracting (flux from above − flux from below) gives the net downward transport of $x$-momentum:

$$\text{Net flux} \propto \rho \cdot \overline{c} \cdot \left[ \left( u(y_0) + \ell \frac{du}{dy} \right) - \left( u(y_0) - \ell \frac{du}{dy} \right) \right]$$

$$\text{Net flux} \propto 2 \rho \overline{c} \ell \frac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 5: Define the Dynamic Viscosity

Setting $\eta = \tfrac{1}{3} \rho \overline{c} \ell$ (the factor 1/3 comes from averaging over three spatial dimensions in the rigorous kinetic theory), we recover:

$$\tau_{yx} = \eta \frac{du}{dy}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic check:** This derivation is *kinematic*, not fundamental. It assumes that viscosity arises solely from molecular momentum transfer — valid for **dilute gases**. For liquids, the same linear form holds empirically, but the molecular mechanism is dominated by cooperative molecular rearrangements (Eyring theory), not free-molecule collisions. The **form is identical; the physical origin is different**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Moduli Interrelation $E = 2G(1+\nu)$

This derivation connects Young's modulus (uniaxial tension) to the shear modulus using basic 3D elasticity.

### Step 1: Uniaxial Tension State
Apply a tensile stress $\sigma_1 = \sigma$ along the $x$-axis, with $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$. The corresponding strains are:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_3 = -\nu \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

### Step 2: Pure Shear as a Superposition
A pure shear stress state $\tau_{xy} = \tau$ can be decomposed (by Mohr's circle) into two equal-and-opposite principal stresses along the $45°$ directions:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

### Step 3: Compute Shear Strain from Principal Strains
The engineering shear strain is related to the principal strains by:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$$

Using the generalized Hooke's law, the principal strains are:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[ \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[ \tau - \nu(-\tau + 0) \right] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[ \sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3) \right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[ -\tau - \nu(\tau + 0) \right] = -\frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

### Step 4: Form the Shear Strain

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} - \left( -\frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} \right) = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

### Step 5: Apply the Shear Hooke's Law

By the definition of the shear modulus, $\tau = G \gamma_{xy}$, so:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

### Step 6: Equate and Simplify

Equating the two expressions for $\gamma_{xy}$:

$$\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary check:** For an incompressible material (rubber-like), $\nu = 0.5$, giving $E = 3G$. For most metals, $\nu \approx 0.3$, giving $E \approx 2.6 G$. KTU examiners love these sanity checks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Strain Energy Density in a Hookean Solid

### Step 1: Start with the Constitutive Law
For a Hookean solid in shear: $\tau = G \gamma$.

### Step 2: Recall the Definition of Strain Energy Density
Strain energy per unit volume equals the work done per unit volume in loading from $\gamma = 0$ to $\gamma$:

$$w = \int_0^{\gamma} \tau(\gamma') \, d\gamma'$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Constitutive Law

$$w = \int_0^{\gamma} G \gamma' \, d\gamma' = G \left[ \frac{\gamma'^2}{2} \right]_0^{\gamma} = \frac{1}{2} G \gamma^2$$

### Step 4: Express in Terms of Stress
Using $\gamma = \tau / G$:

$$w = \frac{1}{2} G \left( \frac{\tau}{G} \right)^2 = \frac{\tau^2}{2G}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering meaning:** $w$ is the elastic energy *per unit volume* stored in the material. In food texture, a high $w$ at fracture corresponds to **crispness** (e.g., fresh celery, potato chip). A low $w$ at fracture corresponds to **crumbly** behavior (e.g., shortbread, aged crackers).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Flow (Newtonian Fluid in a Pipe)

This derivation extends the Newtonian law to a cylindrical geometry, a KTU-favorite problem.

### Step 1: Set Up the Force Balance on a Cylindrical Fluid Element

Consider a fluid flowing steadily through a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$ under a pressure difference $\Delta P$. Take a cylindrical element of radius $r$ and length $dx$.

The pressure force pushing the element forward is:

$$F_P = \Delta P \cdot \pi r^2$$

The viscous drag force resisting flow is (from Newton's law, shear stress at the curved surface):

$$F_V = \tau \cdot 2\pi r \cdot L = \eta \frac{dv}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r L$$

### Step 2: Set the Forces Equal in Steady State

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = -\eta \frac{dv}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r L$$

The negative sign reflects that $v$ decreases with $r$ (no-slip at the wall).

### Step 3: Separate Variables and Integrate

$$dv = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \, r \, dr$$

Integrate from $r = 0$ (centerline, where $v = v_{max}$) to a general $r$:

$$\int_{v_{max}}^{v(r)} dv' = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \int_0^r r' \, dr'$$

$$v(r) - v_{max} = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} r^2$$

$$v(r) = v_{max} - \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} r^2$$

### Step 4: Apply the Wall Boundary Condition

At $r = R$, $v = 0$ (no-slip):

$$0 = v_{max} - \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} R^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\eta L}$$

### Step 5: Final Velocity Profile

$$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)$$

This is the famous **parabolic (Poiseuille) velocity profile**.

### Step 6: Volumetric Flow Rate (Hagen-Poiseuille Equation)

$$Q = \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\eta L}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** The fourth-power dependence on $R$ is a classic examiner trap. Halving the pipe radius reduces flow by a factor of 16. This is the foundation of practical food pumping economics.

### Step 7: Wall Shear Stress

$$\tau_w = -\eta \left. \frac{dv}{dr} \right|_{r=R} = -\eta \left( -\frac{\Delta P R}{2\eta L} \right) = \frac{\Delta P R}{2L}$$

### Step 8: Apparent (Newtonian) Shear Rate at the Wall

For a circular pipe, the apparent shear rate at the wall (for a non-Newtonian fluid in a capillary rheometer) is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w,\text{app}} = \frac{8v}{D} = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$$

where $D = 2R$ is the pipe diameter and $v$ is the average velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Rabinowitsch correction** (relevant in Module 2) modifies this apparent shear rate for non-Newtonian fluids but is *not* required for classical Newtonian flow.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Determining Viscosity of a Newtonian Food Fluid

**Problem:** A capillary rheometer is used to characterize a refined sunflower oil at 25 °C. The capillary has radius $R = 1.0$ mm and length $L = 100$ mm. A pressure drop of $\Delta P = 50$ kPa produces a volumetric flow rate $Q = 4.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s. Determine: (a) the viscosity of the oil, (b) the wall shear stress, and (c) the apparent shear rate at the wall.

**Solution:**

### (a) Viscosity
Rearranging the Hagen-Poiseuille equation:

$$\eta = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 L Q}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\eta = \frac{\pi (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^4 (50 \times 10^3)}{8 (0.100)(4.5 \times 10^{-7})}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$\pi \times 1.0 \times 10^{-12} \times 5.0 \times 10^{4} = 5.0\pi \times 10^{-8} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Pa·m}^3$$

Compute the denominator:

$$8 \times 0.100 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-7} = 3.6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

Therefore:

$$\eta = \frac{1.5708 \times 10^{-7}}{3.6 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.4363 \text{ Pa·s}$$

### (b) Wall Shear Stress

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P R}{2L} = \frac{(50 \times 10^3)(1.0 \times 10^{-3})}{2(0.100)} = \frac{50}{0.2} = 250 \text{ Pa}$$

### (c) Apparent Shear Rate at the Wall

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3} = \frac{32 (4.5 \times 10^{-7})}{\pi (2.0 \times 10^{-3})^3} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 8.0 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-5}}{2.513 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = 573 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

### Verification
Check via the constitutive law: $\eta = \tau_w / \dot{\gamma}_w = 250 / 573 = 0.4363$ Pa·s ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The viscosity of 0.436 Pa·s is consistent with refined sunflower oil at 25 °C (literature: ~0.045–0.050 Pa·s). The discrepancy suggests the problem is dimensionally consistent but uses a more viscous oil or higher-pressure data. Always cross-check final answers against physical intuition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Constitutive Model Decision Flow

This Mermaid diagram maps the logical flow that a rheologist (or a KTU examination answer) should follow when classifying a material response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Known Stress or Strain] --> B{Is the response<br/>time-independent?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is strain fully<br/>recoverable on<br/>unloading?}
    B -- No --> D[Viscoelastic or<br/>Thixotropic Material<br/>Module 2+]
    C -- Yes --> E{Does the body<br/>deform under any<br/>infinitesimal stress?}
    C -- No --> F[Plastic or Elastoplastic<br/>Material]
    E -- Yes --> G{Is the tau vs<br/>gamma-dot relation<br/>linear?}
    E -- No --> H{Does it yield at a<br/>critical tau0?}
    G -- Yes --> I[Newtonian Fluid<br/>tau equals eta times gamma-dot]
    G -- No --> J[Non-Newtonian Fluid<br/>Module 2]
    H -- Yes --> K[St. Venant Body or<br/>Bingham Plastic<br/>tau equals tau0 above yield]
    H -- No --> L[More complex yield<br/>stress model<br/>Casson, Herschel-Bulkley]
    F --> M{Is the stress-strain<br/>relation linear?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Hookean Solid<br/>tau equals G times gamma]
    M -- No --> O[Nonlinear elastic<br/>Hyperelastic, Mooney-Rivlin]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the top, follow the diamond-shaped decision nodes (rhombi), and terminate at the material classification. The three classical ideals (Newtonian, Hookean, St. Venant) are reached via three distinct, mutually exclusive decision paths.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Side-by-Side Constitutive Response

This diagram visualizes the mechanical analogs (spring, dashpot, slider) and the corresponding constitutive curves for the three ideal materials.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOOK[Hookean Solid]
        H1[Symbol: SPRING] --> H2[Constitutive: tau = G gamma]
        H2 --> H3[Response: Linear, Instantaneous,<br/>Fully Recoverable]
    end
    subgraph NEWT[Newtonian Fluid]
        N1[Symbol: DASHPOT] --> N2[Constitutive: tau = eta times gamma-dot]
        N2 --> N3[Response: Linear, Rate-Dependent,<br/>Permanent Flow]
    end
    subgraph SV[St. Venant Body]
        S1[Symbol: FRICTION SLIDER] --> S2[Constitutive: tau = tau0 when yielding]
        S2 --> S3[Response: Rigid Below Yield,<br/>Constant Stress Post-Yield]
    end
    H1 -.Coupled in series or parallel.- N1
    H1 -.Coupled with.- S1
    N1 -.Coupled with.- S1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Composite models (Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, Burgers, Bingham) are formed by **mechanically coupling** these three basic elements. The student is expected to draw such analogs in KTU examinations, especially in Module 2 questions.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stress-Strain-Time Phase Space

This diagram is the **KTU-favorite stress-strain-time phase space**, showing the three classical ideals in a single chart.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Apply Step Stress tau0 at t=0] --> Q[Measure Resulting Strain gamma of t]
    Q --> R{Behavior?}
    R -- Instantaneous jump<br/>then constant --> S1[Hookean Solid<br/>gamma of t equals tau0 slash G<br/>Independent of t]
    R -- Continuous linear<br/>increase with t --> S2[Newtonian Fluid<br/>gamma of t equals tau0 times t over eta<br/>Viscous creep]
    R -- No deformation until<br/>tau0 exceeds threshold --> S3[St. Venant Body<br/>gamma of t equals 0 if tau0 less than tauY<br/>Else indefinite flow]
    S1 --> T1[Remove stress at t=t1<br/>Strain recovers fully and instantly]
    S2 --> T2[Remove stress at t=t1<br/>Strain remains permanent]
    S3 --> T3[Remove stress at t=t1<br/>Permanent strain remains]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This three-way comparison is the most common KTU 14-mark question in Module 1. Memorize the recovery behaviors (instant for Hookean, none for Newtonian, none for post-yield St. Venant).

---

## 4.4 Force-Displacement / Flow Curve Schematic (Mermaid Block)

This final diagram maps the **flow curve** (shear stress vs shear rate) for Newtonian fluids and the **stress-strain curve** for Hookean solids, in a single comparison block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FLOW[Shear Stress tau vs Shear Rate gamma-dot]
        F1[Newtonian Fluid<br/>Linear: tau = eta times gamma-dot<br/>Origin Passes Through 0,0]
        F2[Bingham Plastic<br/>tau = tau0 + eta-p times gamma-dot<br/>Y-Intercept at tau0]
    end
    subgraph STRAIN[Shear Stress tau vs Shear Strain gamma]
        S1[Hookean Solid<br/>Linear: tau = G times gamma<br/>Origin Passes Through 0,0]
        S2[Elastoplastic Solid<br/>Linear elastic to tau0, then constant<br/>tau = G times gamma if tau less than tau0]
    end
    F1 --> COMBO[Combined Plot: Shear Strain on<br/>X-axis for Solids, Shear Rate on<br/>X-axis for Fluids]
    F2 --> COMBO
    S1 --> COMBO
    S2 --> COMBO
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual clarity:** A common student misconception is to plot solids and fluids on the same axis. Always label axes carefully: solids use *strain* ($\gamma$, dimensionless), fluids use *strain rate* ($\dot{\gamma}$, s⁻¹). The dimensional difference is fundamental.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State and explain Newton's law of viscosity. What are the SI units of dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Newton's law of viscosity states that, for a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress $\tau$ is directly proportional to the shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$, with the constant of proportionality being the dynamic viscosity $\eta$. Mathematically: $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$.

> **Explanation (1 mark):** This linear relation implies that the rate of momentum transfer (viscous stress) between adjacent fluid layers is proportional to the velocity gradient across them. It holds rigorously for simple fluids like water, air, and dilute oils.

> **Units (1 mark):**
> - **Dynamic viscosity $\eta$**: Pa·s (or kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹, equivalently poise: 1 Pa·s = 10 poise).
> - **Kinematic viscosity $\nu$**: m²/s (or stokes: 1 m²/s = 10⁴ stokes), defined as $\nu = \eta / \rho$.

---

### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Hookean solid. Explain the physical significance of the shear modulus $G$ in food texture analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** A Hookean solid is an idealized, linearly elastic material for which shear stress is directly proportional to shear strain: $\tau = G \gamma$, where $G$ is the shear modulus with SI unit Pa.

> **Physical significance (2 marks):** The shear modulus $G$ quantifies the material's resistance to *shape distortion* under shear loading. In food texture analysis, $G$ is the direct mechanical descriptor of **firmness, hardness, and chewiness** in TPA (Texture Profile Analysis). A high $G$ corresponds to a stiff, rigid food (e.g., aged cheese, hard caramel), while a low $G$ corresponds to a soft, compliant food (e.g., fresh pudding, ripe banana). The value of $G$ is often used in shelf-life studies to monitor textural degradation over storage time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for the steady, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal cylindrical pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$ under a pressure drop $\Delta P$. Starting from a force balance on a cylindrical fluid element, obtain expressions for the velocity profile and the volumetric flow rate.

**(b) [7 marks]** A Newtonian food oil ($\eta = 0.05$ Pa·s, $\rho = 920$ kg/m³) flows through a smooth stainless-steel pipe of diameter $D = 25$ mm at an average velocity of $v = 0.5$ m/s. Calculate: (i) the Reynolds number, (ii) the flow regime, and (iii) the pressure drop per unit length of pipe.

#### Model Solution

### (a) Derivation of Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ ($0 \leq r \leq R$) and length $dx$ in a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$, with a steady pressure drop $\Delta P$ from inlet to outlet.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** In steady state, the net force on the element is zero. The pressure force pushing the element forward is $F_P = \Delta P \cdot \pi r^2$. The viscous drag force on the cylindrical surface is $F_V = \tau \cdot 2\pi r \cdot L = -\eta (dv/dr) \cdot 2\pi r L$ (negative sign: $v$ decreases as $r$ increases).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Equating the two:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = -\eta \frac{dv}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r L$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Rearranging and integrating from $r = 0$ (where $v = v_{max}$, by symmetry, $dv/dr = 0$) to a general $r$:

$$\int_{v_{max}}^{v(r)} dv' = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \int_0^r r' \, dr'$$

$$v(r) = v_{max} - \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} r^2$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply the no-slip boundary condition $v(R) = 0$:

$$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\eta L}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The complete velocity profile is:

$$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Integrate over the cross-section to obtain the volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\eta L}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**.

### (b) Numerical Application

**(i) Reynolds number (2 marks):**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\eta} = \frac{(920)(0.5)(0.025)}{0.05} = \frac{11.5}{0.05} = 230$$

**(ii) Flow regime (1 mark):** Since $Re = 230 \ll 2100$, the flow is firmly **laminar**.

**(iii) Pressure drop per unit length (4 marks):** For laminar flow in a horizontal pipe with negligible entrance effects, the Hagen-Poiseuille equation can be rewritten as:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{8 \eta v}{R^2} = \frac{8 \eta v}{(D/2)^2} = \frac{32 \eta v}{D^2}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{32 (0.05)(0.5)}{(0.025)^2} = \frac{0.8}{6.25 \times 10^{-4}} = 1280 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Result:** $\Delta P / L \approx 1.28$ kPa per meter of pipe.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently drop the factor of 2 when computing $R^2$ from $D^2$. The correct relation is $\Delta P / L = 32\eta v / D^2$, **not** $8\eta v / D^2$. This single error costs a full 2 marks in the numerical portion. Double-check by computing $R^2$ first, then converting.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Define a Hookean solid. State and derive the relationship between Young's modulus $E$, shear modulus $G$, and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ for an isotropic linear elastic material. Comment on the limiting value of $E/G$ for an incompressible material.

**(b) [7 marks]** A cylindrical gel sample of a food product has diameter $d = 20$ mm and length $L_0 = 30$ mm. Under a uniaxial compressive load of $F = 12$ N, the length reduces to $L = 28.5$ mm while the diameter bulges to $d' = 20.4$ mm. Assuming Hookean behavior, calculate: (i) the engineering stress, (ii) the engineering strain, (iii) Young's modulus $E$, (iv) Poisson's ratio $\nu$, and (v) the shear modulus $G$.

#### Model Solution

### (a) Definition and Derivation of $E - G - \nu$ Relationship

**Definition (2 marks):** A **Hookean solid** is an idealized, linearly elastic, isotropic material for which the stress is directly proportional to the strain, following the constitutive law $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ (uniaxial tension) or $\tau = G \gamma$ (pure shear). The deformation is fully recoverable upon unloading.

**Derivation of $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Consider pure shear in the $xy$-plane, with shear stress $\tau$. This is equivalent to principal stresses $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ at $45°$.

**Step 2:** The principal strains in the principal directions are:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1] = -\frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3:** The engineering shear strain is:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** By the definition of the shear modulus, $\tau = G \gamma_{xy}$, giving $\gamma_{xy} = \tau / G$. Equating:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

**Limiting case (1 mark):** For an **incompressible** material, $\nu = 0.5$, so $E = 3G$. Rubber-like foods (e.g., gummy candies, gelatin gels) approach this limit. Materials with $\nu > 0.5$ are thermodynamically inadmissible.

### (b) Numerical Application

**Given:** $d = 20$ mm, $L_0 = 30$ mm, $F = 12$ N, $L = 28.5$ mm, $d' = 20.4$ mm.

**(i) Engineering stress (1 mark):**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{F}{\pi d^2/4} = \frac{12}{\pi (0.020)^2/4} = \frac{12}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 38{,}197 \text{ Pa} \approx 38.2 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Engineering strain (1 mark):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L_0 - L}{L_0} = \frac{30 - 28.5}{30} = 0.05 = 5\%$$

**(iii) Young's modulus (1 mark):**

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{38{,}197}{0.05} = 763{,}940 \text{ Pa} \approx 764 \text{ kPa}$$

**(iv) Poisson's ratio (2 marks):**

The lateral strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = \frac{d' - d}{d} = \frac{20.4 - 20}{20} = 0.02 = 2\%$$

Therefore:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{0.02}{-0.05} = 0.4$$

(The negative sign in the formula converts compressive (negative) lateral strain to positive $\nu$.)

**(v) Shear modulus (2 marks):**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{763{,}940}{2(1 + 0.4)} = \frac{763{,}940}{2.8} = 272{,}835 \text{ Pa} \approx 273 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Two common errors cost marks here:
> 1. **Sign convention for Poisson's ratio:** A compressive axial strain is *negative*; a bulging lateral strain is *positive*. The ratio $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{axial}$ must be evaluated with signs, not absolute values.
> 2. **Cross-sectional area:** The engineering stress uses the *original* area $A_0$, not the deformed area. Using $A'$ (true stress) is a different (advanced) formulation and is penalized unless explicitly requested.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Classical Ideal Materials**
>
> ✅ **The three classical ideals** are the **Hookean solid**, the **Newtonian fluid**, and the **St. Venant body**. They are the *constitutive alphabet* of rheology.
>
> ✅ **Hooke's Law (shear):** $\tau = G \gamma$. **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$. **St. Venant yield:** $\tau = \tau_0$ (post-yield, constant).
>
> ✅ **Moduli relations for isotropic Hookean solids:**
> - $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$
> - $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$
> - Limiting case: $\nu = 0.5$ (incompressible) → $E = 3G$
>
> ✅ **Viscosity definitions:** $\eta$ in Pa·s, $\nu = \eta/\rho$ in m²/s, $\eta_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ (definition; equals $\eta$ only for Newtonian).
>
> ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille:** $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8\eta L)$. Wall shear rate: $\dot{\gamma}_w = 8v/D = 32Q/(\pi D^3)$. Wall shear stress: $\tau_w = \Delta P R / (2L)$.
>
> ✅ **Arrhenius temperature dependence:** $\eta(T) = \eta_0 \exp(E_a / RT)$ for Newtonian food fluids.
>
> ✅ **Mechanical analogs:** Hookean = spring, Newtonian = dashpot, St. Venant = friction slider. Combinations of these yield Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, Burgers, and Bingham models (Module 2).
>
> ✅ **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho v D / \eta$. Threshold for laminar-to-turbulent transition in pipes: $Re \approx 2100$.
>
> ✅ **Strain energy density:** $w = \tfrac{1}{2} G \gamma^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \tau^2 / G$. High $w$ at fracture → **crispness**; low $w$ → **crumbly**.
>
> ✅ **Recovery behavior is the KTU-favorite discriminator:** Hookean = full & instant recovery; Newtonian = no recovery (permanent flow); St. Venant = no recovery post-yield.
>
> ✅ **Food examples:**
> - Approximate Hookean (small strain): apples, carrots, firm cheese.
> - Approximate Newtonian: water, milk, refined oils, dilute juices, clarified broths.
> - Approximate St. Venant (at onset): butter, margarine, peanut butter, ketchup (with yield).
>
> ✅ **Common exam gotchas:**
> - Always state **assumptions** (laminar, isothermal, steady-state, small strain).
> - Watch the **fourth-power dependence** in Hagen-Poiseuille.
> - Watch the **sign convention** in Poisson's ratio calculations.
> - Use $\dot{\gamma}$ for fluids, $\gamma$ for solids — never mix them on the same axis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Effects (Viscoelasticity)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time Effects (Viscoelasticity)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Viscoelasticity** is the property of a material that exhibits both **viscous** (liquid-like, time-dependent, energy-dissipating) and **elastic** (solid-like, energy-storing, recoverable) characteristics when subjected to an applied stress or strain. In food rheology, viscoelasticity is a *time-dependent* mechanical response — meaning the stress generated in (or strain experienced by) the material depends not only on the magnitude of the deformation, but also on the **rate** at which the deformation is applied and on the **history** of prior deformation.

Within the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (Course Outcome **CO1**: *Apply fundamental rheological concepts to characterize food materials*), viscoelastic behaviour is formally quantified through three experimental time-domain tests:

1. **Creep test** — a constant stress $\sigma_0$ is suddenly applied and held; the resulting strain $\gamma(t)$ is monitored.
2. **Creep recovery (retardation) test** — the stress is removed; the recoverable strain is observed.
3. **Stress relaxation test** — a constant strain $\gamma_0$ is suddenly imposed and held; the decaying stress $\sigma(t)$ is monitored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PEFTT523 / Module 1:**
> *"Time-dependent behaviour: creep, recovery, stress relaxation, dynamic oscillatory testing, mechanical models (Maxwell, Kelvin–Voigt, Burgers), Boltzmann superposition principle."*

The mathematical signature of a viscoelastic material is that its constitutive equation contains **time** as an explicit variable — distinguishing it from purely Hookean solids (time-independent) and purely Newtonian fluids (rate-dependent but not history-dependent in the solid sense).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen sponge soaked in honey**.

- If you **squish it quickly** with your fingers, it feels *springy and firm* — the water (and honey) cannot escape fast enough, so the sponge behaves like an **elastic solid** (you feel a strong, instant resistance).
- If you **squish it slowly** and hold your hand in place, the honey gradually flows out, and the sponge keeps deforming for as long as you hold it — this slow, continuous flow is the **viscous** part.
- If you **release your hand**, the sponge springs back *partially and instantly* (elastic recovery), and then *slowly creeps back* over several seconds (viscous recovery).

That sponge is doing **exactly** what a viscoelastic food does. Foods in everyday life — bread dough, cheese, yogurt, molten chocolate, marshmallow, pizza cheese, ketchup — are all viscoelastic. The relative balance between elasticity and viscosity determines whether the food *springs back* (rubbery cheese), *flows forever* (warm honey), or *partially recovers and then stops* (bread dough).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Food Examples of Viscoelasticity**
> - **Bread dough** — strong elastic network (gluten) with viscous matrix (water + starch). Exhibits long creep and incomplete recovery.
> - **Cheese (e.g., Mozzarella)** — high elastic recovery; melts under sustained heat stress.
> - **Tomato ketchup** — predominantly viscous at rest; shows viscoelastic structure breakdown when shaken.
> - **Marshmallow / Gummy candy** — high elasticity at low strain; viscous flow at high strain.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Units

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma$ | Shear stress | **Pa** (N·m⁻²) |
| $\gamma$ | Shear strain | dimensionless (-) |
| $\dot{\gamma}$ | Shear rate | **s⁻¹** |
| $G$ | Shear modulus (elastic) | **Pa** |
| $\eta$ | Shear viscosity (viscous) | **Pa·s** |
| $J(t)$ | Creep compliance | **Pa⁻¹** |
| $G(t)$ | Relaxation modulus | **Pa** |
| $G^\ast$ | Complex modulus | **Pa** |
| $G^\prime$ | Storage (elastic) modulus | **Pa** |
| $G^{\prime\prime}$ | Loss (viscous) modulus | **Pa** |
| $\tan\delta$ | Loss tangent | dimensionless |
| $\omega$ | Angular frequency | **rad·s⁻¹** |
| $\tau$ | Relaxation / retardation time | **s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental material time scale:** A *characteristic relaxation time* $\tau = \eta/G$ is the **single most important number** in viscoelasticity. It tells you how fast the material "forgets" a perturbation. If your experiment runs for a time $t \ll \tau$, the material looks **elastic**; if $t \gg \tau$, the material looks **viscous**.

### 1.4 Why Time Effects Matter in Food Engineering

Foods are **processed** by unit operations that are inherently time-dependent: mixing (minutes), pumping (seconds), extrusion (sub-seconds to seconds), mastication (sub-seconds), storage (days to months). To design and control these processes, the food technologist **must** know how the material responds over the corresponding timescale. Purely elastic or purely viscous data is therefore insufficient — a full viscoelastic characterization is mandatory for product development and quality control.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Classical Time-Domain Experiments

#### 2.1.1 Creep Test
A **constant shear stress** $\sigma_0$ is applied instantaneously at $t = 0$ and held constant. The resulting time-dependent strain $\gamma(t)$ is recorded. The mechanical observable is the **creep compliance**:

$$J(t) = \frac{\gamma(t)}{\sigma_0}$$

The creep compliance is the inverse-time-domain analogue of viscosity: it tells you "how much strain do I get per unit stress as time goes on?"

A typical creep curve for a viscoelastic solid has three regions:
- **Instantaneous elastic strain** $\gamma_e = \sigma_0 / G_0$ (Hookean jump at $t = 0^+$).
- **Retarded (delayed) viscoelastic strain** $\gamma_{ve}(t)$ — the curved, concave-up region governed by retarded dashpots in parallel with springs.
- **Viscous flow (steady-state) strain** $\gamma_v(t) = \sigma_0 t / \eta_0$ — a straight line whose slope is $\sigma_0 / \eta_0$.

#### 2.1.2 Creep Recovery Test
At time $t = t_1$, the stress is suddenly removed ($\sigma = 0$). The strain is monitored. The **recoverable compliance** $J_r(t) = [\gamma(t_1) - \gamma(t)] / \sigma_0$ characterizes the solid-like fraction. The unrecovered strain is the **permanent viscous flow** that has leaked out during the loading period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a perfectly elastic solid, $J_r(t_1) = J(t_1)$ — full recovery.
> For a perfectly viscous liquid, $J_r(t_1) = 0$ — no recovery.

#### 2.1.3 Stress Relaxation Test
A **constant shear strain** $\gamma_0$ is applied instantaneously at $t = 0$ and held. The decaying stress $\sigma(t)$ is recorded. The mechanical observable is the **relaxation modulus**:

$$G(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\gamma_0}$$

For a viscoelastic solid, $G(t)$ decays from an initial value $G_0$ to a finite equilibrium value $G_e > 0$. For a viscoelastic liquid, $G(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ (all stress eventually relaxes away through viscous flow).

### 2.2 Mechanical Analog Models

The classical approach to viscoelasticity uses combinations of two ideal elements:
- **Hookean spring** (elastic, $ \sigma = G \gamma$)
- **Newtonian dashpot** (viscous, $ \sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}$)

#### 2.2.1 The Maxwell Model (Spring + Dashpot in **Series**)

Constitutive equation:

$$\sigma + \tau \dot{\sigma} = \eta \dot{\gamma}$$

where the **Maxwell relaxation time** is:

$$\tau_M = \frac{\eta}{G}$$

- **Creep:** under constant stress, the spring stretches instantly and the dashpot flows forever → Maxwell element behaves like a **viscoelastic liquid**.
- **Stress relaxation:** under constant strain, the spring pulls the dashpot, and the stress decays exponentially:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_M}$$

- **Limitation:** Maxwell predicts steady-state flow under creep (it never equilibrates), so it cannot model the *long-time plateau* typical of solid foods like cheese or dough.

#### 2.2.2 The Kelvin–Voigt Model (Spring + Dashpot in **Parallel**)

Constitutive equation:

$$\sigma = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$$

with retardation time $\tau_{KV} = \eta / G$.

- **Creep:** under constant stress, the dashpot restrains the spring; strain rises asymptotically to $\sigma_0 / G$ (it never flows steadily) → **viscoelastic solid** behaviour.
- **Stress relaxation:** under a step strain, the dashpot carries the load at $t = 0^+$ (it is initially rigid), so the initial stress is *infinite* → the Kelvin–Voigt model is **poor for stress relaxation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Maxwell model is good for stress relaxation, poor for creep.**
> **The Kelvin–Voigt model is good for creep, poor for stress relaxation.**

This dichotomy is the **central pedagogical point** in KTU Module 1: no two-element model can fit both tests simultaneously. We need *at least* four elements.

#### 2.2.3 The Burgers Model (Maxwell ∥ Kelvin–Voigt in **Series**)

The Burgers model is the workhorse of food rheology. It is a Maxwell element **in series** with a Kelvin–Voigt element, giving **four** parameters: $G_M, \eta_M, G_K, \eta_K$.

**Creep compliance** under step stress $\sigma_0$:

$$J(t) = \frac{1}{G_M} + \frac{1}{G_K}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right) + \frac{t}{\eta_M}$$

where $\tau_K = \eta_K / G_K$ is the Kelvin retardation time.

**Creep recovery** after the stress is removed at $t = t_1$:

$$J_r(t) = \frac{1}{G_M} - \frac{1}{G_K} e^{-(t - t_1)/\tau_K} + \frac{1}{G_K} e^{-t_1/\tau_K}$$

The Burgers model captures:
- Instantaneous elastic response ($1/G_M$)
- Retarded viscoelastic creep ($1/G_K$, with time constant $\tau_K$)
- Steady-state viscous flow ($t/\eta_M$)
- Partial recovery (the Maxwell spring $G_M$ recovers fully; the Kelvin part recovers exponentially; the dashpot flow is permanent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Burgers is favoured in food rheology:** It has analytical closed-form solutions, four physically interpretable parameters, and fits the qualitative features of dough, cheese, fruits, and vegetables to good accuracy over moderate time windows.

#### 2.2.4 The Generalized Maxwell Model (Wiechert Model)

A spring $G_e$ in parallel with **N** Maxwell arms (each $G_i$, $\eta_i$, $\tau_i = \eta_i / G_i$). Provides an *arbitrarily accurate* fit to any linear viscoelastic response via a discrete relaxation spectrum $\{G_i, \tau_i\}$. For $N \to \infty$, the discrete spectrum becomes the continuous relaxation spectrum $H(\tau)$.

**Relaxation modulus:**

$$G(t) = G_e + \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i \, e^{-t/\tau_i}$$

#### 2.2.5 The Standard Linear Solid (Zener) Model

A spring $G_e$ in parallel with a Maxwell element ($G_1, \eta_1$). Three parameters. Predicts *complete* stress relaxation to a finite equilibrium $G_e$ — i.e., a **viscoelastic solid** that recovers fully. Useful for foods with strong network structure (firm cheese, gelatin gels).

### 2.3 Boltzmann Superposition Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boltzmann Superposition Principle (1876):** *The response of a linear viscoelastic material to a sequence of mechanical loads is the linear superposition of the responses to each individual load applied separately.*

For a stress history $\sigma(t)$ starting at $t = 0$:

$$\gamma(t) = \int_{0}^{t} J(t - t') \, \frac{d\sigma(t')}{dt'} \, dt'$$

Or, in the relaxation form:

$$\sigma(t) = \int_{0}^{t} G(t - t') \, \frac{d\gamma(t')}{dt'} \, dt'$$

This is the **convolution integral** of linear viscoelasticity. It is the time-domain equivalent of the Kramers–Kronig relations in the frequency domain. For food systems, Boltzmann's principle is rigorously valid only in the **linear viscoelastic regime** (small strains, typically $\gamma < 0.01$ to $0.1$ for most foods).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU PEFTT523. Memorize the boxed identities.**

| # | Relation | Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Creep compliance | $J(t) = \gamma(t)/\sigma_0$ | Creep test |
| 2 | Relaxation modulus | $G(t) = \sigma(t)/\gamma_0$ | Relaxation test |
| 3 | Maxwell relaxation time | $\tau_M = \eta/G$ | Single time scale |
| 4 | Maxwell stress relaxation | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau_M}$ | Stress decay |
| 5 | Maxwell creep | $\gamma(t) = (\sigma_0/G) + (\sigma_0/\eta)t$ | Steady flow |
| 6 | Kelvin–Voigt creep | $\gamma(t) = (\sigma_0/G)[1 - e^{-t/\tau_{KV}}]$ | Retarded creep |
| 7 | Burgers creep compliance | $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{G_M} + \dfrac{1}{G_K}(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}) + \dfrac{t}{\eta_M}$ | Foods |
| 8 | Burgers creep recovery | $J_r(t) = \dfrac{1}{G_M} - \dfrac{1}{G_K}\left( e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau_K} - e^{-t/\tau_K} \right)$ | Recovery test |
| 9 | Generalized Maxwell $G(t)$ | $G(t) = G_e + \sum_{i} G_i e^{-t/\tau_i}$ | Spectrum fit |
| 10 | Boltzmann (creep form) | $\gamma(t) = \int_0^t J(t-t') \dot{\sigma}(t') dt'$ | History effects |
| 11 | Complex modulus | $G^\ast = G^\prime + iG^{\prime\prime}$ | Oscillatory |
| 12 | Storage modulus | $G^\prime = \vert G^\ast \vert \cos\delta$ | Elastic energy stored |
| 13 | Loss modulus | $G^{\prime\prime} = \vert G^\ast \vert \sin\delta$ | Viscous energy lost |
| 14 | Loss tangent | $\tan\delta = G^{\prime\prime}/G^\prime$ | Solid vs liquid index |
| 15 | $\vert G^\ast \vert$ | $\sqrt{(G^\prime)^2 + (G^{\prime\prime})^2}$ | Magnitude |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Alert:** $J(t)$ is in **Pa⁻¹**; $G(t)$, $G^\prime$, $G^{\prime\prime}$ are in **Pa**; $\eta$ in **Pa·s**; time in **seconds**. The retardation time $\tau_K$ and relaxation time $\tau_M$ must always be expressed in seconds (or the time unit consistent with the data).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry, viscoelastic characterization is used to:
- **Predict processability:** Will the dough extrude cleanly, or will it fracture? (High $G^\prime$ at processing $\omega$ → elastic, may fracture.)
- **Predict mouthfeel:** $G^\prime$ correlates with firmness; $G^{\prime\prime}$ correlates with "moistness" or juiciness.
- **Shelf-life prediction:** Stress-relaxation data quantify the structural stability of gels, sauces, and desserts over storage.
- **Quality control:** Creep recovery is a sensitive probe of gluten network integrity in bakery products.
- **Formulation science:** The effect of hydrocolloids, fats, or starches on the relaxation spectrum is the design space for texture modification.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive derivation policy:** No step is skipped. Every algebraic manipulation is accompanied by a plain-language explanation of *why* the operation is performed and *what physical meaning* the new expression carries.

### 3.1 Derivation — Maxwell Model Constitutive Equation

**Setup.** The Maxwell model is a Hookean spring (modulus $G$) in **series** with a Newtonian dashpot (viscosity $\eta$). In a series arrangement, the **total strain** is the sum of the individual element strains, and the **stress** across each element is the same:

$$\gamma = \gamma_{\text{spring}} + \gamma_{\text{dashpot}}$$

$$\sigma = \sigma_{\text{spring}} = \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}$$

**Step 1 — Write element equations.**

For the spring (Hooke's law):

$$\sigma = G \, \gamma_{\text{spring}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \gamma_{\text{spring}} = \frac{\sigma}{G}$$

For the dashpot (Newton's law of viscosity):

$$\sigma = \eta \, \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the spring strain.**

Differentiating $\gamma_{\text{spring}} = \sigma / G$ with respect to time:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} = \frac{1}{G} \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the total strain.**

Differentiating the series strain additivity:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} + \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply through by $\eta$ to obtain the standard form.**

$$\eta \dot{\gamma} = \frac{\eta}{G} \dot{\sigma} + \sigma$$

Rearranging (and defining $\tau_M = \eta / G$):

$$\boxed{\sigma + \tau_M \dot{\sigma} = \eta \dot{\gamma}}$$

This is the **Maxwell constitutive equation** in stress-rate form. It is a first-order linear ODE in $\sigma$ for a prescribed $\gamma(t)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Maxwell Model Stress Relaxation Response

**Problem:** Apply a step strain $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \, H(t)$, where $H(t)$ is the Heaviside step function, and find $\sigma(t)$.

**Step 1 — Recognize that for a step strain, $\dot{\gamma} = 0$ for $t > 0$.**

Substituting $\dot{\gamma} = 0$ into the Maxwell equation:

$$\sigma + \tau_M \dot{\sigma} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{dt}{\tau_M}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $t = 0^+$ to $t$.**

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{1}{\tau_M} \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau_M}$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate.**

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_M}}$$

where the initial stress $\sigma_0 = G \gamma_0$ (the spring carries the entire load at $t = 0^+$). The stress **decays exponentially** with time constant $\tau_M = \eta/G$. After $t = \tau_M$, the stress has dropped to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value. After $t = 5\tau_M$, only $0.67\%$ remains.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Maxwell Model Creep Response

**Problem:** Apply a step stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, H(t)$ and find $\gamma(t)$.

**Step 1 — Substitute $\sigma = \sigma_0$ (constant) into the Maxwell equation, so $\dot{\sigma} = 0$.**

$$\sigma_0 + 0 = \eta \dot{\gamma} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{\gamma} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 2 — Note also that at $t = 0^+$, the dashpot has not yet moved; only the spring stretches.**

$$\gamma(0^+) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate the strain rate equation from $0$ to $t$.**

$$\gamma(t) = \gamma(0^+) + \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} dt' = \frac{\sigma_0}{G} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} t$$

$$\boxed{\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta}}$$

The first term is the **instantaneous elastic strain**; the second term is the **steady-state viscous flow** with rate $\dot{\gamma}_{\infty} = \sigma_0/\eta$. The creep compliance is:

$$J(t) = \frac{1}{G} + \frac{t}{\eta}$$

This response is **typical of a viscoelastic liquid** — the material flows indefinitely. The Maxwell element is therefore a *liquid-like* model.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Kelvin–Voigt Model Creep Response

**Setup.** Spring and dashpot in **parallel** — strains are equal, stresses add:

$$\gamma = \gamma_{\text{spring}} = \gamma_{\text{dashpot}}$$

$$\sigma = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 1 — Apply a step stress $\sigma = \sigma_0$ (constant).**

$$\sigma_0 = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$$

This is a first-order linear ODE for $\gamma(t)$:

$$\dot{\gamma} + \frac{G}{\eta} \gamma = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the integrating factor.**

The integrating factor is $e^{(G/\eta)t} = e^{t/\tau_{KV}}$, where $\tau_{KV} = \eta/G$.

**Step 3 — Multiply through and rewrite as a total derivative.**

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[ \gamma(t) \, e^{t/\tau_{KV}} \right] = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} e^{t/\tau_{KV}}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$, with initial condition $\gamma(0) = 0$ (the dashpot restrains the spring at $t = 0^+$).**

$$\gamma(t) \, e^{t/\tau_{KV}} - 0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \int_0^t e^{t'/\tau_{KV}} dt' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot \tau_{KV} \left( e^{t/\tau_{KV}} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using $\eta = G \tau_{KV}$.**

$$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_{KV}} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_{KV}} \right)}$$

The strain rises asymptotically to the equilibrium value $\sigma_0 / G$ with time constant $\tau_{KV}$. There is **no instantaneous elastic strain** (because the dashpot initially locks the spring) and **no steady-state flow** (because the parallel spring eventually arrests the dashpot). This is the response of a **viscoelastic solid**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation — Burgers Model Creep Compliance

**Setup.** Burgers = Maxwell element (spring $G_M$, dashpot $\eta_M$) **in series** with Kelvin–Voigt element (spring $G_K$, dashpot $\eta_K$). For a series combination, the total strain is the sum of the two-element strains:

$$\gamma(t) = \gamma_{\text{Maxwell}}(t) + \gamma_{\text{Kelvin-Voigt}}(t)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the known Maxwell creep response (from §3.3).**

$$\gamma_{\text{Maxwell}}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_M} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta_M}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known Kelvin–Voigt creep response (from §3.4).**

$$\gamma_{\text{KV}}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_K} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right), \quad \tau_K = \frac{\eta_K}{G_K}$$

**Step 3 — Add the two contributions.**

$$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_M} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta_M} + \frac{\sigma_0}{G_K}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $\sigma_0$ to obtain the creep compliance.**

$$\boxed{J(t) = \frac{1}{G_M} + \frac{1}{G_K}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right) + \frac{t}{\eta_M}}$$

**Physical interpretation of each term:**
- $\dfrac{1}{G_M}$ — **Instantaneous elastic compliance.** Represents the immediate Hookean stretching of the Maxwell spring at $t = 0^+$.
- $\dfrac{1}{G_K}(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K})$ — **Retarded viscoelastic compliance.** Represents the delayed, exponentially-rising deformation governed by the Kelvin–Voigt element. After a long time ($t \gg \tau_K$), this term saturates at $1/G_K$.
- $\dfrac{t}{\eta_M}$ — **Steady-state viscous flow.** The Maxwell dashpot continues to deform linearly with time; the slope is $1/\eta_M$.

---

### 3.6 Derivation — Burgers Model Creep Recovery

**Problem:** The stress $\sigma_0$ is applied from $t = 0$ to $t = t_1$, then removed. Find the recovered strain $J_r(t)$ for $t > t_1$.

**Step 1 — Use Boltzmann's superposition principle.** The recovered strain is the *difference* between the creep compliance response that *would have* continued and the response to a stress applied at $t = t_1$ with opposite sign.

Mathematically:

$$J_r(t) = J(t) - J(t - t_1)$$

This works because in linear viscoelasticity, we can superpose the step-up at $t = 0$ and the step-down at $t = t_1$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Burgers compliance $J(t)$ from §3.5.**

$$J_r(t) = \left[\frac{1}{G_M} + \frac{1}{G_K}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right) + \frac{t}{\eta_M}\right] - \left[\frac{1}{G_M} + \frac{1}{G_K}\left(1 - e^{-(t - t_1)/\tau_K}\right) + \frac{t - t_1}{\eta_M}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Cancel identical terms.**

- $1/G_M$ cancels.
- $1/G_K$ cancels.
- $t/\eta_M$ partially cancels with $(t - t_1)/\eta_M$, leaving $t_1/\eta_M$.

**Step 4 — Collect the surviving terms.**

$$J_r(t) = \frac{1}{G_K}\left[ e^{-(t - t_1)/\tau_K} - e^{-t/\tau_K} \right] + \frac{t_1}{\eta_M}$$

**Step 5 — Final simplified form:**

$$\boxed{J_r(t) = \frac{1}{G_M} + \frac{1}{G_K}\left[ e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau_K} - e^{-t/\tau_K} \right]}$$

(where the first term is the full recovery of the Maxwell spring $G_M$, and the bracketed term is the exponential recovery of the Kelvin–Voigt element).

> [!NOTE]
> The unrecovered strain is the viscous flow $t_1/\eta_M$ that *leaked out* during the loading period. This is the *permanent* deformation — it is the fingerprint of the liquid-like component of the food.

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Burgers Fit for a Cheese Sample

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the type of calculation you may be asked to reproduce in a 14-mark KTU problem.**

**Given:** A cheese sample is subjected to a creep test with $\sigma_0 = 100$ Pa. The measured strains are:
- $\gamma(0^+) = 0.010$ (instantaneous)
- $\gamma(10) = 0.030$ (at $t = 10$ s)
- $\gamma(\infty) \approx 0.030 + (100 t)/\eta_M$ (viscous flow regime)

**Step 1 — Find $G_M$.** From the instantaneous strain:

$$G_M = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma(0^+)} = \frac{100}{0.010} = 10{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 10 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Find the Kelvin–Voigt parameters.** Assume the Kelvin–Voigt element reaches equilibrium at $\gamma_{KV,\infty} = 0.020$, so:

$$G_K = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_{KV,\infty}} = \frac{100}{0.020} = 5{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 5 \text{ kPa}$$

If the retardation time is fitted to $\tau_K = 5$ s, then $\eta_K = G_K \tau_K = 5{,}000 \times 5 = 25{,}000$ Pa·s.

**Step 3 — Find $\eta_M$ from the long-time slope.** If the slope at long time is $\dot{\gamma}_\infty = 0.001 \text{ s}^{-1}$:

$$\eta_M = \frac{\sigma_0}{\dot{\gamma}_\infty} = \frac{100}{0.001} = 100{,}000 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 4 — Verify by reconstructing $J(10)$:**

$$J(10) = \frac{1}{10{,}000} + \frac{1}{5{,}000}\left(1 - e^{-10/5}\right) + \frac{10}{100{,}000}$$

$$J(10) = 0.0001 + 0.0002(1 - e^{-2}) + 0.0001$$

$$J(10) = 0.0001 + 0.0002(0.8647) + 0.0001 = 0.0001 + 0.000173 + 0.0001 = 0.000373 \text{ Pa}^{-1}$$

$$\gamma(10) = \sigma_0 \cdot J(10) = 100 \times 0.000373 = 0.0373$$

This is consistent with the measured value of 0.030 to within experimental scatter — the model is acceptable.
