## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanical Element Schematics (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below maps the **structural architecture** of the four most important viscoelastic models used in Module 1. Each node is a functional element; the arrows indicate the *series* (strain additive) and *parallel* (stress additive) connections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigmaIn([Applied Stress sigma0]) --> maxwellBlock
    sigmaIn --> kvBlock
    sigmaIn --> burgersBlock
    sigmaIn --> zenerBlock

    subgraph maxwellBlock["MAXWELL MODEL: Viscoelastic LIQUID"]
        direction LR
        S1["Spring G_M"] ---|series| D1["Dashpot eta_M"]
    end

    subgraph kvBlock["KELVIN-VOIGT MODEL: Viscoelastic SOLID"]
        direction LR
        S2["Spring G_K"] ===|parallel| D2["Dashpot eta_K"]
    end

    subgraph burgersBlock["BURGERS MODEL: 4-Parameter Food Model"]
        direction LR
        maxArm["Maxwell Arm: G_M in series with eta_M"] -->|series| kvArm["Kelvin-Voigt Arm: G_K parallel eta_K"]
    end

    subgraph zenerBlock["STANDARD LINEAR SOLID: Zener Model"]
        direction LR
        S3["Equilibrium Spring G_e"] ===|parallel| maxArm2["Maxwell Arm: G_1 in series with eta_1"]
    end

    maxwellBlock --> out1([Creep: flow forever])
    kvBlock --> out2([Creep: asymptote to sigma0/G])
    burgersBlock --> out3([Creep: 4-feature curve])
    zenerBlock --> out4([Stress relaxes to G_e > 0])
```

### 4.2 Creep–Recovery Curve Anatomy (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["t = 0: Step stress applied"] --> B["Instantaneous elastic strain gamma_e = sigma0 / G_M"]
    B --> C["Region II: Retarded viscoelastic strain governed by Kelvin-Voigt"]
    C --> D["Region III: Steady-state viscous flow with rate sigma0 / eta_M"]
    D --> E["t = t1: Stress removed"]
    E --> F["Instantaneous elastic recovery of G_M"]
    F --> G["Exponential viscoelastic recovery with time constant tau_K"]
    G --> H["Permanent viscous strain t1 / eta_M: LEAKAGE"]
    H --> I([End: equilibrium recoverable strain])
```

### 4.3 Stress Relaxation Curve Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["t = 0+: Step strain imposed gamma_0"] --> P1["Initial stress sigma_0 = G_e * gamma_0"]
    P1 --> P2["Region I: Fast initial decay from high G_1 modes"]
    P2 --> P3["Region II: Slow exponential decay with time constants tau_i = eta_i / G_i"]
    P3 --> P4["Long-time plateau: G_infinity = G_e for solids, or G_infinity = 0 for liquids"]
    P4 --> P5([End: residual stress signature])
```

### 4.4 Mapping Models to Food Types (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FoodType{Food Type and Texture}
    FoodType -->|Hard cheese, gelatin gel| Z["Standard Linear Solid - full recovery"]
    FoodType -->|Bread dough, mozzarella, yogurt| B["Burgers - 4-parameter fit"]
    FoodType -->|Polymer melt, warm honey| M["Maxwell - liquid-like flow"]
    FoodType -->|Cross-linked rubber at short time| K["Kelvin-Voigt - solid-like retardation"]

    Z --> R1["Recovery is complete. G_infinity finite."]
    B --> R2["Partial recovery. Permanent viscous strain t1/eta_M."]
    M --> R3["No recovery. Linear flow forever."]
    K --> R4["Full recovery, but no instantaneous elasticity."]
```

### 4.5 Boltzmann Superposition: Block Diagram of History Effects

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph history["Past Stress History"]
        H1["Step at t1"] --> H2["Step at t2"] --> H3["Step at t3"] --> Hn["Step at tn"]
    end

    history --> kernel["Convolution with J or G"]

    kernel --> R1["Response to stress 1 at t1"]
    kernel --> R2["Response to stress 2 at t2"]
    kernel --> R3["Response to stress 3 at t3"]
    kernel --> Rn["Response to stress n at tn"]

    R1 --> sum["Linear superposition: sum of all responses"]
    R2 --> sum
    R3 --> sum
    Rn --> sum
    sum --> output([Total strain gamma or stress sigma at time t])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters:** The convolution structure of linear viscoelasticity means that the *entire past* of the material influences its *current* response. For a food technologist, this implies that **pre-shear history** (mixing, pumping, resting) of a dough or sauce *must* be controlled if reproducible rheological measurements are to be obtained. The "rest time" between sample loading and test initiation is therefore a critical experimental variable.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define viscoelasticity. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the creep and recovery behaviour of a viscoelastic food material.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Viscoelasticity** is the time-dependent mechanical response of a material that simultaneously exhibits both **elastic** (solid-like, energy-storing) and **viscous** (liquid-like, energy-dissipating) behaviour.

**Sketch description (to be drawn in the answer):**
- A step stress $\sigma_0$ is applied at $t = 0$ and held until $t = t_1$.
- The strain response shows three regions: (i) **instantaneous elastic jump** of magnitude $\sigma_0 / G_M$, (ii) **retarded (curved) viscoelastic strain** governed by the Kelvin–Voigt element, and (iii) **steady-state viscous flow** with constant slope $\sigma_0 / \eta_M$.
- At $t = t_1$, the stress is removed. The strain shows (i) **instantaneous elastic recovery** of the Maxwell spring, (ii) **exponential viscoelastic recovery** of the Kelvin–Voigt element with time constant $\tau_K$, and (iii) a **permanent residual strain** $t_1/\eta_M$ that does not recover — the viscous leakage.

**Example food:** Bread dough shows all four regions clearly under a 100-Pa creep load for 60 s, followed by a 120-s recovery.

*(Examiner marking note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sketch, 1 mark for the three-region identification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare and contrast the Maxwell and Kelvin–Voigt models. State one limitation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Maxwell Model | Kelvin–Voigt Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Element arrangement** | Spring and dashpot in **series** | Spring and dashpot in **parallel** |
| **Constitutive equation** | $\sigma + \tau_M \dot{\sigma} = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\sigma = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$ |
| **Creep response** | Instantaneous strain + steady flow (liquid-like) | Asymptotic rise to $\sigma_0/G$ (solid-like) |
| **Stress relaxation** | Exponential decay (good fit) | Initial infinite stress (poor fit) |
| **Limitation** | Cannot model viscoelastic solids (no equilibrium modulus) | Cannot model instantaneous elasticity (dashpot locks spring) |
| **Best suited for** | Viscoelastic liquids, polymer melts | Cross-linked rubbers, gels, short-time solid food behaviour |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking tip:** A comparison table is the most examiner-friendly way to present this answer in 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the constitutive equation for the **Maxwell model**. Starting from this equation, obtain the expression for the **stress relaxation** response under a step strain $\gamma_0$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **creep compliance** of the **Burgers model**, identifying the physical meaning of each term in your final expression. Show that this model predicts **partial recovery** with a permanent residual strain. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Maxwell constitutive equation and stress relaxation**

*Derivation of the constitutive equation:*
The Maxwell model is a spring (modulus $G$) in series with a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$). In series, strains add and stresses are equal:

$$\gamma = \gamma_s + \gamma_d, \quad \sigma = \sigma_s = \sigma_d$$

The element equations are $\sigma = G \gamma_s$ and $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}_d$. Differentiating the spring relation gives $\dot{\gamma}_s = \dot{\sigma}/G$, while the dashpot gives $\dot{\gamma}_d = \sigma/\eta$. Substituting into the strain additivity $\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}_s + \dot{\gamma}_d$:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

Multiplying by $\eta$ and rearranging, with $\tau_M = \eta / G$:

$$\boxed{\sigma + \tau_M \dot{\sigma} = \eta \dot{\gamma}}$$

*Derivation of stress relaxation:*
Apply a step strain $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 H(t)$. For $t > 0$, $\dot{\gamma} = 0$. The constitutive equation reduces to:

$$\sigma + \tau_M \dot{\sigma} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\frac{\sigma}{\tau_M}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $t = 0^+$ (where $\sigma_0 = G \gamma_0$) to $t$:

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{1}{\tau_M}\int_0^t dt' \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_M}$$

The stress decays exponentially with characteristic time $\tau_M = \eta/G$. At $t = \tau_M$, $\sigma = \sigma_0/e \approx 0.368 \sigma_0$.

**Part (b) — Burgers model creep compliance**

The Burgers model is a Maxwell element ($G_M, \eta_M$) in series with a Kelvin–Voigt element ($G_K, \eta_K$). For a series combination, total strain is the sum of element strains. Under a step stress $\sigma_0$:

- Maxwell creep: $\gamma_M(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{G_M} + \dfrac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta_M}$
- Kelvin–Voigt creep: $\gamma_{KV}(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{G_K}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right)$, with $\tau_K = \eta_K/G_K$

Adding:

$$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_M} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta_M} + \frac{\sigma_0}{G_K}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right)$$

Dividing by $\sigma_0$:

$$\boxed{J(t) = \frac{1}{G_M} + \frac{1}{G_K}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right) + \frac{t}{\eta_M}}$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $1/G_M$: **instantaneous elastic compliance** (Hookean jump at $t = 0^+$)
- $(1/G_K)(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K})$: **retarded viscoelastic compliance** (delayed elastic response, saturates at $1/G_K$)
- $t/\eta_M$: **steady-state viscous flow** (linear in $t$ with rate $\sigma_0/\eta_M$)

**Partial recovery demonstration:**
When the stress is removed at $t = t_1$, the *recoverable* compliance is $J_r(t) = J(t) - J(t - t_1)$:

$$J_r(t) = \frac{1}{G_M} + \frac{1}{G_K}\left[e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau_K} - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right]$$

The term $1/G_M$ recovers fully; the bracketed Kelvin–Voigt term recovers exponentially. However, the viscous term $t_1/\eta_M$ remains as a **permanent residual strain** — this is the *unrecoverable* part. Hence the recovery is **partial**, not complete.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **Boltzmann superposition principle** as applied to linear viscoelastic food systems. Mention the conditions under which it is valid. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **bread dough** sample is loaded in creep with $\sigma_0 = 80$ Pa. The Burgers model parameters fitted to the data are:
$G_M = 2.5 \times 10^3$ Pa, $G_K = 1.0 \times 10^3$ Pa, $\eta_M = 5.0 \times 10^4$ Pa·s, $\tau_K = 8$ s.

Compute:
1. The instantaneous elastic strain.
2. The steady-state viscous strain rate.
3. The strain at $t = 10$ s.
4. The Kelvin–Voigt retardation viscosity $\eta_K$.
5. The permanent residual strain if the stress is removed at $t = 30$ s. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Boltzmann Superposition Principle**

The Boltzmann superposition principle states that **the mechanical response of a linear viscoelastic material to a sequence of applied loads is the linear superposition of the responses to each individual load applied separately.**

Mathematically, for a stress history starting at $t = 0$:

$$\gamma(t) = \int_{0}^{t} J(t - t') \, \frac{d\sigma(t')}{dt'} \, dt'$$

Equivalently, in relaxation form:

$$\sigma(t) = \int_{0}^{t} G(t - t') \, \frac{d\gamma(t')}{dt'} \, dt'$$

**Sketch (to be drawn):** A plot of stress vs. time showing two (or three) step stresses applied at different times, with the corresponding strain responses drawn as superposed creep curves.

**Conditions of validity:**
- The material must be in the **linear viscoelastic regime** (typically $\gamma < 0.01$ to $0.1$ for foods).
- The material response must be **time-invariant** — the constitutive properties do not change during the test (no curing, no drying, no structural breakdown).
- **Loading must be small** enough that superposition holds (e.g., dough at strains below the strain-hardening threshold).

**Application to foods:** Useful for predicting how a sauce or dough will respond to a complex process history (pumping → holding → extrusion).

**Part (b) — Numerical Burgers computation**

**Given:** $\sigma_0 = 80$ Pa, $G_M = 2500$ Pa, $G_K = 1000$ Pa, $\eta_M = 5.0 \times 10^4$ Pa·s, $\tau_K = 8$ s.

**(1) Instantaneous elastic strain:**

$$\gamma_e = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_M} = \frac{80}{2500} = 0.032 \quad (\text{dimensionless, or 3.2\% strain})$$

**(2) Steady-state viscous strain rate:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_{\infty} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M} = \frac{80}{5.0 \times 10^4} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(3) Strain at $t = 10$ s:**

First, $\eta_K = G_K \tau_K = 1000 \times 8 = 8000$ Pa·s.

$$J(10) = \frac{1}{2500} + \frac{1}{1000}\left(1 - e^{-10/8}\right) + \frac{10}{5.0 \times 10^4}$$

$$J(10) = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-3}(1 - e^{-1.25}) + 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$J(10) = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-3}(1 - 0.2865) + 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$J(10) = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} + 7.135 \times 10^{-4} + 2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.3135 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$$

$$\gamma(10) = \sigma_0 \cdot J(10) = 80 \times 1.3135 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1051 \quad (\approx 10.5\%)$$

**(4) Kelvin–Voigt retardation viscosity:**

$$\eta_K = G_K \tau_K = 1000 \times 8 = 8.0 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**(5) Permanent residual strain after unloading at $t_1 = 30$ s:**

$$\gamma_{\text{perm}} = \frac{\sigma_0 \cdot t_1}{\eta_M} = \frac{80 \times 30}{5.0 \times 10^4} = 4.8 \times 10^{-2} = 0.048 \quad (4.8\%)$$

**Interpretation:** The dough retains 4.8% permanent strain after 30 s of loading — this is the viscous "leakage" characteristic of dough and indicates its tendency to flow irreversibly under sustained stress. In contrast, a cross-linked gel (e.g., gelatin) would have $\eta_M \to \infty$ and hence zero permanent strain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Viscoelasticity Problems**
> 1. **Units trap (–1 mark):** Forgetting that $J(t)$ is in Pa⁻¹, not Pa. Writing $\gamma = \sigma \cdot G(t)$ instead of $\gamma = \sigma \cdot J(t)$ costs 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Series vs. parallel confusion (–2 marks):** Mixing up which element is in series and which is in parallel in the Burgers model. **Memory aid:** "In **series**, the strain is shared (sum), stress is common. In **parallel**, the stress is shared (sum), strain is common."
> 3. **Skipping the constitutive equation (–2 marks):** Going directly to the creep response without deriving or stating the constitutive equation. KTU examiners explicitly award marks for the derivation in Part (a).
> 4. **Forgetting the physical interpretation (–1 mark):** Stating the formula but not identifying what each term means physically. Always add the "physical meaning" sentence for each term — this is a guaranteed mark.
> 5. **In Boltzmann problems, not stating the linearity assumption (–1 mark):** "Linear" is the operative word. Always add: "valid in the linear viscoelastic regime (small strains)."

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Month KTU Revision: Memorize These 20 Bullets**

- [x] **Viscoelasticity** = simultaneous viscous + elastic response, time-dependent.
- [x] **Three classical time-domain tests:** creep, creep recovery, stress relaxation.
- [x] **Creep compliance** $J(t) = \gamma(t)/\sigma_0$, units **Pa⁻¹**.
- [x] **Relaxation modulus** $G(t) = \sigma(t)/\gamma_0$, units **Pa**.
- [x] **Maxwell model** (S + D in series): viscoelastic **liquid**; exponential stress decay $\sigma = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau_M}$.
- [x] **Kelvin–Voigt model** (S + D in parallel): viscoelastic **solid**; asymptotic creep $\gamma = (\sigma_0/G)(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{KV}})$.
- [x] **Burgers model** = Maxwell **in series** with Kelvin–Voigt; four parameters $G_M, \eta_M, G_K, \eta_K$.
- [x] **Burgers creep compliance** has three terms: instantaneous elastic + retarded viscoelastic + steady viscous flow.
- [x] **Burgers recovery** is *partial*; the permanent strain is $t_1/\eta_M$ (viscous leakage).
- [x] **Standard Linear Solid (Zener)**: spring $G_e$ in parallel with one Maxwell arm; full recovery to $G_e > 0$.
- [x] **Generalized Maxwell** uses $N$ arms to fit any linear viscoelastic response (discrete spectrum).
- [x] **Boltzmann superposition**: linear response = sum of responses to individual loads; valid only in the **linear** regime.
- [x] **Convolution integral form:** $\gamma(t) = \int_0^t J(t-t') \dot{\sigma}(t') dt'$.
- [x] **Characteristic time** $\tau = \eta/G$: if $t \ll \tau$ → elastic; if $t \gg \tau$ → viscous.
- [x] **Storage modulus** $G'$ = elastic energy stored per cycle; **loss modulus** $G''$ = viscous energy dissipated.
- [x] **Loss tangent** $\tan\delta = G''/G'$. If $\tan\delta < 1$ → solid-like ($G' > G''$); if $\tan\delta > 1$ → liquid-like.
- [x] **Complex modulus** $G^* = G' + iG''$ with magnitude $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}$.
- [x] **Food examples:** dough (Burgers), cheese (Standard Linear Solid), honey (Maxwell), gelatin gel (Kelvin–Voigt or Zener).
- [x] **Pre-shear history matters:** wait a consistent rest time before testing; record loading history.
- [x] **Boltzmann requires small strains:** typically $\gamma < 1\%$ for doughs, $\gamma < 10\%$ for fluid gels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip (Module 1):** When a question gives you a Burgers fit, the examiner expects you to (a) write the compliance equation, (b) compute numerical values, (c) interpret each term physically, and (d) predict the recovery behaviour. **All four steps are required for full 14 marks.** Skipping interpretation is the #1 reason for losing 2–3 marks on a perfectly calculated answer.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rheological Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rheological Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Rheological Equations?

**Rheological Equations** are mathematical relationships (constitutive equations) that describe how a material **deforms** or **flows** when subjected to an applied force (stress). In the context of food rheology, these equations quantitatively relate the **shear stress (τ)** experienced by a food material to the **shear rate (γ̇)** or **shear strain (γ)** imposed on it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A *constitutive rheological equation* is a phenomenological or mechanistic mathematical model that uniquely defines the relationship between stress, strain, and time-rate of strain for a given material, allowing classification of its flow behaviour as either **Newtonian**, **Non-Newtonian (time-independent)**, **Viscoelastic (time-dependent)**, or **Viscoplastic**.

The study of these equations is foundational because it allows food engineers to:
- Predict **pumpability** of fluids (e.g., milk, fruit juice, sauces).
- Design **pipe networks** for food processing.
- Control **texture** and **mouthfeel** of products.
- Optimize **mixing, extrusion**, and **packaging** operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Resistance to Flow" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Hose & Honey" Model**
> 
> Imagine you are squeezing honey out of a bottle versus pouring water from a jug:
> - **Water** flows easily the moment you tilt the jug. The more you tilt (more force/stress), the faster it flows. The relationship is **linear and immediate** → this is a *Newtonian fluid*.
> - **Honey** resists flow. Even when you apply force, it starts slowly. If you apply *more* force, the flow suddenly speeds up. The stress required to start flow is non-zero → this is *viscoplastic behaviour* (e.g., ketchup, tomato paste).
> - **Toothpaste** won't flow at all until you squeeze hard enough, then it flows easily — a perfect *Bingham Plastic*.
> - **Silly Putty / Bread dough** behaves like a solid when you press it slowly, but flows like a liquid if you pull it quickly → this is *viscoelastic* (time-dependent).

In every case above, an **equation** mathematically captures the "how much force produces how much flow" relationship.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Quantities in Rheology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Variables** (must be memorized):
> 1. **Shear Stress (τ)** — Force per unit area applied *parallel* to the material surface. **Units: Pa (N/m²)**.
> 2. **Shear Rate (γ̇)** — Velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction. **Units: s⁻¹ (or 1/s)**.
> 3. **Viscosity (η)** — The material's internal resistance to flow. **Units: Pa·s** (Newtonian definition: η = τ / γ̇).

These three variables form the foundation upon which **every** rheological equation in this module is constructed.

## 1.4 Why Rheological Equations Matter in Food Engineering

- **Process Design**: Calculating pressure drops in pipelines transporting chocolate, cream, or fruit pulp.
- **Quality Control**: Ensuring consistent texture in yogurt, ice cream, and sauces batch-to-batch.
- **Sensory Science**: Correlating instrumental rheological data with human mouthfeel perception.
- **Shelf-life Prediction**: Tracking structural breakdown of gels and emulsions over storage time.
- **Energy Efficiency**: Estimating pumping power requirements in food manufacturing plants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Rheological Behaviour

Rheological equations are classified based on how τ relates to γ̇, and whether the response depends on **time** or **deformation history**.

### A. Time-Independent (Steady Shear) Models

> [!NOTE]
> These models describe materials whose shear stress depends **only** on the instantaneous shear rate — no memory effect.

#### 1. Newtonian Model
The simplest rheological equation — a straight line through the origin.

$$
\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

- **η (eta)** = constant viscosity (independent of shear rate)
- Examples: water, milk, fruit juice, vegetable oil, honey (at low concentrations)

#### 2. Power Law Model (Ostwald–de Waele Model)
The most widely used non-Newtonian model in the food industry.

$$
\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}
$$

- **K** = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ) — measure of fluid thickness
- **n** = flow behaviour index (dimensionless)
- If **n = 1** → reduces to Newtonian (K = η)
- If **n < 1** → *shear-thinning* (pseudoplastic) — most foods (ketchup, yogurt)
- If **n > 1** → *shear-thickening* (dilatant) — starch suspensions, wet sand

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apparent Viscosity for Power Law fluids**:
> $$\eta_{\text{app}} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$
> This is **not constant** — it changes with shear rate.

#### 3. Bingham Plastic Model
Combines a yield stress with Newtonian flow above that yield.

$$
\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma} \quad \text{for } \tau > \tau_{0}
$$

- **τ₀** = yield stress (Pa) — minimum stress to initiate flow
- **ηₚ** = plastic viscosity (Pa·s)
- Examples: toothpaste, margarine, tomato paste (at low concentrations)

#### 4. Herschel–Bulkley Model
The most general three-parameter model combining yield stress + power-law flow.

$$
\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}
$$

- Reduces to **Bingham Plastic** when n = 1
- Reduces to **Power Law** when τ₀ = 0
- Reduces to **Newtonian** when τ₀ = 0 and n = 1
- Examples: many structured foods — chocolate, mayonnaise, dough

#### 5. Casson Model
A specialized two-parameter model popular for food systems.

$$
\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

- **η∞** = infinite-shear viscosity
- Particularly accurate for **chocolate melts** and **tomato ketchup** (an ISO standard for chocolate)

#### 6. Cross Model
A four-parameter model capturing both low-shear and high-shear Newtonian plateaus.

$$
\dfrac{\eta - \eta_{\infty}}{\eta_{0} - \eta_{\infty}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (K \cdot \dot{\gamma})^{m}}
$$

- **η₀** = zero-shear viscosity
- **η∞** = infinite-shear viscosity
- **K** = consistency parameter
- **m** = rate index

#### 7. Carreau Model
Mathematically similar to Cross, often used for polymer melts and concentrated foods.

$$
\dfrac{\eta - \eta_{\infty}}{\eta_{0} - \eta_{\infty}} = \left[\dfrac{1}{1 + (\lambda \cdot \dot{\gamma})^{2}}\right]^{\,s}
$$

- **λ** = time constant (relaxation time)
- **s** = power-law index

### B. Time-Dependent Models

#### 8. Thixotropy Model (Structural Breakdown)
Shear stress decreases with time at constant shear rate.

$$
\tau = (\tau_{0} - \tau_{\infty}) \cdot e^{-k t} + \eta_{\infty} \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

- Examples: gelatin, yogurt (after shearing), bentonite suspensions

#### 9. Rheopexy Model (Structural Build-up)
Shear stress increases with time at constant shear rate (rare in foods, more common in lubricants).

### C. Viscoelastic Models (Time-Dependent Response with Recovery)

> [!NOTE]
> Viscoelastic materials exhibit **both** viscous (flow) and elastic (recovery) behaviour.

#### 10. Hooke's Law (Purely Elastic Solid)
$$
\tau = G \cdot \gamma
$$

- **G** = elastic (shear) modulus (Pa)
- **γ** = shear strain (dimensionless)

#### 11. Newton's Law (Purely Viscous Liquid)
Already covered: τ = η·γ̇

#### 12. Maxwell Model (Spring + Dashpot in Series)
Models stress relaxation at constant strain.

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \dfrac{\tau}{\eta}
$$

- **Relaxation time**: λ = η / G
- Applicable for: dough, polymer solutions, melted cheese

#### 13. Kelvin–Voigt Model (Spring + Dashpot in Parallel)
Models creep behaviour at constant stress.

$$
\tau = G \cdot \gamma + \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

#### 14. Burgers Model (Maxwell + Kelvin–Voigt in Series)
Four-parameter model capturing creep + recovery.

$$
\gamma(t) = \dfrac{\tau_{0}}{G_{1}} + \dfrac{\tau_{0} \cdot t}{\eta_{1}} + \dfrac{\tau_{0}}{G_{2}}\left(1 - e^{-t \, G_{2}/\eta_{2}}\right)
$$

- Most complete model for bread dough rheology.

### D. Dynamic (Oscillatory) Rheology Equation

For sinusoidal testing:

$$
\tau(t) = \tau_{0} \, \sin(\omega t + \delta)
$$
$$
\gamma(t) = \gamma_{0} \, \sin(\omega t)
$$

- **ω** = angular frequency (rad/s)
- **δ** = phase shift angle (0° for purely elastic, 90° for purely viscous)
- **Complex modulus**:
$$
G^{*} = G' + i G'' = \dfrac{\tau_{0}}{\gamma_{0}} \, e^{i\delta}
$$
- **G'** = storage modulus (elastic), **G''** = loss modulus (viscous)
- **tan δ** = G'' / G' (loss tangent)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE** — Memorize all entries below for KTU 2024 ESE.

| Model | Equation | Parameters | Limiting Cases |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Newtonian | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta$ | Special case of Power Law (n=1, K=η) |
| Power Law | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | $K$, $n$ | Newtonian if $n=1$ |
| Bingham Plastic | $\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_{0}$, $\eta_{p}$ | Newtonian if $\tau_{0}=0$ |
| Herschel–Bulkley | $\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | $\tau_{0}$, $K$, $n$ | Unifies Power Law + Bingham |
| Casson | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty} \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\tau_{0}$, $\eta_{\infty}$ | Standard for chocolate |
| Cross | $(\eta-\eta_{\infty})/(\eta_{0}-\eta_{\infty}) = 1/[1+(K\dot{\gamma})^{m}]$ | $\eta_{0}$, $\eta_{\infty}$, $K$, $m$ | Captures both plateaus |
| Carreau | $(\eta-\eta_{\infty})/(\eta_{0}-\eta_{\infty}) = [1+(\lambda\dot{\gamma})^{2}]^{s}$ | $\eta_{0}$, $\eta_{\infty}$, $\lambda$, $s$ | Polymer melt standard |
| Maxwell | $\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\tau}/G + \tau/\eta$ | $G$, $\eta$ | Stress relaxation |
| Kelvin–Voigt | $\tau = G\gamma + \eta\dot{\gamma}$ | $G$, $\eta$ | Creep recovery |
| Burgers | $\gamma(t) = \tau_{0}/G_{1} + \tau_{0}t/\eta_{1} + (\tau_{0}/G_{2})(1-e^{-tG_{2}/\eta_{2}})$ | $G_{1}$, $\eta_{1}$, $G_{2}$, $\eta_{2}$ | Full dough creep |
| Hooke's Law | $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ | $G$ | Purely elastic |
| Dynamic | $G^{*}=G'+iG''$; $\tan\delta=G''/G'$ | $G'$, $G''$, $\delta$ | Oscillatory testing |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline Design (Hagen–Poiseuille generalized for non-Newtonian)**:
$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{4K}{D} \left(\dfrac{8V}{D}\right)^{n}
$$
- **Pumping Power Calculation**:
$$
P = Q \cdot \Delta P
$$
- **Chocolate Tempering (Casson model)** → controls viscosity for moulding and enrobing.
- **Yogurt Texture (Herschel–Bulkley)** → predicts spoon-ability and pumpability.
- **Bread Dough Fermentation (Burgers model)** → predicts gas-cell wall relaxation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Law Apparent Viscosity

**Given**: The Power Law equation
$$
\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}
$$

**Find**: An explicit expression for apparent viscosity $\eta_{\text{app}} = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$.

**Step 1**: Start with the Newtonian definition of viscosity.

By definition, for *any* fluid (Newtonian or not), viscosity is the ratio of shear stress to shear rate.

$$
\eta_{\text{app}} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the Power Law expression for τ.

Replace the numerator with the Power Law constitutive equation.

$$
\eta_{\text{app}} = \dfrac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}}{\dot{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 3**: Simplify the exponent algebraically.

Using the law of exponents, $\dot{\gamma}^{\,n} / \dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$.

$$
\eta_{\text{app}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}
$$

**Step 4**: Verify limiting cases.

- If $n = 1$: $\eta_{\text{app}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,0} = K$ → constant (Newtonian recovered).
- If $n < 1$: $\eta_{\text{app}}$ decreases as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases → **shear-thinning** confirmed.
- If $n > 1$: $\eta_{\text{app}}$ increases as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases → **shear-thickening** confirmed.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hagen–Poiseuille Equation Generalized for Power Law Fluids

**Given**: A cylindrical pipe of diameter $D$ and length $L$ carrying a power-law fluid at average velocity $V$. We must derive the pressure drop $\Delta P / L$.

**Step 1**: Start from the general force balance on a cylindrical fluid element.

For steady, fully-developed laminar flow in a pipe, equilibrium between pressure force and viscous force gives:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot \pi r^{2} = -\tau \cdot 2 \pi r
$$

**Step 2**: Solve for the local shear stress.

Divide both sides by $2 \pi r$ and rearrange.

$$
\tau = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2L} \cdot r
$$

The negative sign indicates τ is opposite to the flow direction; taking magnitudes:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}
$$

**Step 3**: Equate to the Power Law model.

Substitute $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n} = K \cdot (-dr/dy)^{n}$. With the substitution $y = D/2 - r$, we have $dy = -dr$, so:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L} = K \left(-\dfrac{dv}{dr}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 4**: Solve for the velocity gradient.

$$
-\dfrac{dv}{dr} = \left(\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n}
$$

**Step 5**: Integrate with boundary condition $v = 0$ at $r = R = D/2$.

$$
v(r) = \int_{r}^{R} \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2LK}\right)^{1/n} r^{1/n} dr
$$

**Step 6**: Apply the binomial identity for $1/n \ne 1$.

Using $\int r^{1/n} dr = \dfrac{r^{1/n + 1}}{1/n + 1}$:

$$
v(r) = \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2LK}\right)^{1/n} \dfrac{n}{n+1}\left(R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n}\right)
$$

**Step 7**: Integrate to obtain mean velocity $V = Q/A = (2/R^{2})\int_{0}^{R} v(r) \, r \, dr$.

After completing the radial integration (standard but lengthy algebra), the final result is:

$$
V = \dfrac{n}{3n+1} \left(\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 8**: Solve for $\Delta P / L$ in terms of mean velocity $V$.

Rearranging to isolate the pressure gradient:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{4K}{D} \left(\dfrac{8V}{D}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 9**: Verify Newtonian case ($n = 1$, $K = \eta$):

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{4 \eta}{D} \cdot \dfrac{8V}{D} = \dfrac{32 \eta V}{D^{2}}
$$

This matches the classical Hagen–Poiseuille equation ✓.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maxwell Model Relaxation Behaviour

**Given**: A Maxwell element (spring $G$ in series with dashpot $\eta$) is subjected to an instantaneous constant strain $\gamma_0$.

**Step 1**: Write the Maxwell constitutive equation.

The total strain rate is the sum of elastic and viscous strain rates (series arrangement):

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{\text{total}} = \dot{\gamma}_{\text{elastic}} + \dot{\gamma}_{\text{viscous}} = \dfrac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \dfrac{\tau}{\eta}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply the constraint of constant strain.

For a stress relaxation test, $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0$ for all $t \ge 0$, so $\dot{\gamma} = 0$.

$$
0 = \dfrac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \dfrac{\tau}{\eta}
$$

**Step 3**: Rearrange into a first-order linear ODE.

$$
\dfrac{d\tau}{dt} = -\dfrac{G}{\eta} \, \tau
$$

**Step 4**: Solve by separation of variables.

$$
\int \dfrac{d\tau}{\tau} = -\dfrac{G}{\eta} \int dt
$$

**Step 5**: Integrate both sides.

$$
\ln(\tau) = -\dfrac{G}{\eta} t + C
$$

**Step 6**: Exponentiate.

$$
\tau(t) = A \, e^{-(G/\eta) t}
$$

**Step 7**: Apply initial condition $t = 0 \Rightarrow \tau(0) = G \gamma_0$ (only the spring responds instantly).

$$
A = G \gamma_0
$$

**Step 8**: Final form of the Maxwell relaxation modulus.

$$
\tau(t) = G \gamma_{0} \, e^{-t/\lambda} \quad \text{where } \lambda = \dfrac{\eta}{G}
$$

**Step 9**: Define the relaxation modulus $G(t)$.

Since $G(t) = \tau(t)/\gamma_0$:

$$
G(t) = G \, e^{-t/\lambda}
$$

This **exponential decay** is the hallmark fingerprint of a Maxwell fluid in stress relaxation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Model Selection Guide for Food Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this decision matrix to choose the correct rheological model during KTU exam problems.

| Food System | Dominant Model | Why? | Typical Parameters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Water, milk, fruit juice | Newtonian | No structure to break | $\eta \approx 1$ mPa·s |
| Vegetable oil, honey | Newtonian | Simple molecular fluid | $\eta = 0.1$ – $10$ Pa·s |
| Ketchup, yogurt | Power Law ($n<1$) | Shear-thinning from floc breakdown | $K \sim 10$–$50$ Pa·sⁿ |
| Chocolate (molten) | Casson | Yield stress + shear-thinning | ISO standard |
| Tomato paste (concentrated) | Herschel–Bulkley | Yield stress + power-law flow | $\tau_0 \sim 10$ Pa |
| Toothpaste, margarine | Bingham Plastic | Linear flow after yield | $\tau_0 \sim 50$–$200$ Pa |
| Bread dough | Burgers | Creep + recovery | 4 parameters |
| Gelatin gel, pudding | Kelvin–Voigt | Elastic recovery dominant | $G$ large, $\eta$ small |
| Ice cream (molten) | Carreau | Two Newtonian plateaus | $\lambda$ captures structure |
| Wheat flour batter | Cross | Wide shear rate range | 4 parameters |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic A: Classification Tree of Rheological Equations

This Mermaid block represents the **hierarchical taxonomy** of constitutive models covered in Module 1.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNodeA[Rheological Equations]
    startNodeA --> branchTimeA[Time-Independent]
    startNodeA --> branchTimeB[Time-Dependent]
    startNodeA --> branchOscA[Oscillatory Dynamic]

    branchTimeA --> newtonNode[Newtonian: tau equals eta times gamma dot]
    branchTimeA --> nonNewNode[Non-Newtonian]
    nonNewNode --> viscNode[Viscosity varies with shear rate]
    viscNode --> shearThinNode[Shear Thinning: n less than 1]
    viscNode --> shearThickNode[Shear Thickening: n greater than 1]

    branchTimeA --> viscopNode[Viscoplastic: has yield stress]
    viscopNode --> binghamNode[Bingham Plastic: linear after yield]
    viscopNode --> hbNode[Herschel Bulkley: power law after yield]
    viscopNode --> cassonNode[Casson: chocolate standard]

    branchTimeA --> genNode[Generalised]
    genNode --> crossNode[Cross: two plateaus]
    genNode --> carreauNode[Carreau: polymer melts]

    branchTimeB --> viscoNodeA[Viscoelastic]
    viscoNodeA --> maxNode[Maxwell: stress relaxation]
    viscoNodeA --> kvNode[Kelvin Voigt: creep recovery]
    viscoNodeA --> burgerNode[Burgers: dough creep]

    branchTimeB --> thixoNode[Thixotropic: structure breaks with time]
    branchTimeB --> rheoNode[Rheopectic: structure builds with time]

    branchOscA --> oscEq[G star equals G prime plus i G double prime]
    oscEq --> storageNode[Storage G prime: elastic]
    oscEq --> lossNode[Loss G double prime: viscous]
    oscEq --> tanDNode[Loss Tangent tan delta]
```

## 4.2 Schematic B: Mechanical Analogues (Spring–Dashpot Representations)

This Mermaid diagram maps **mechanical analogue elements** to their corresponding **mathematical equations**.

```mermaid
graph LR
    hookeNode[HOOKE SPRING]
    hookeNode --> hookeEq[tau equals G times gamma]
    hookeNode --> hookeProp[Purely Elastic Solid]

    newtNode[NEWTON DASH POT]
    newtNode --> newtEq[tau equals eta times gamma dot]
    newtNode --> newtProp[Purely Viscous Liquid]

    maxArr[ARRANGEMENT: SERIES]
    maxArr --> maxModel[Maxwell Model]
    maxModel --> maxEq[gamma dot total equals tau dot over G plus tau over eta]
    maxModel --> maxApp[Stress relaxation tests]

    kvArr[ARRANGEMENT: PARALLEL]
    kvArr --> kvModel[Kelvin Voigt Model]
    kvModel --> kvEq[tau equals G gamma plus eta gamma dot]
    kvModel --> kvApp[Creep recovery tests]

    burgerArr[ARRANGEMENT: MAXWELL PLUS KELVIN VOIGT IN SERIES]
    burgerArr --> burgerModel[Burgers Model]
    burgerModel --> burgerEq[gamma of t equals four parameter equation]
    burgerModel --> burgerApp[Bread dough and wheat flour]
```

## 4.3 Schematic C: Flow Curve Block Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

This Mermaid flow depicts how raw rheological data flows through the constitutive modelling pipeline — a block-level functional architecture rather than a physical sketch.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rawInput[Raw Data: tau and gamma dot from rheometer]
    rawInput --> step1[Step 1: Plot tau vs gamma dot]
    step1 --> step2{Step 2: Linear through origin?}
    step2 --> yesNewt[Yes: Newtonian Model]
    step2 --> noNewt[No: Proceed to Non Newtonian]
    noNewt --> step3{Step 3: Yield stress present?}
    step3 --> noYield[No: Power Law Fit]
    step3 --> yesYield[Yes: Viscoplastic]
    yesYield --> step4{Step 4: Linear or curved above yield?}
    step4 --> linearAbove[Linear: Bingham]
    step4 --> curvedAbove[Curved: Herschel Bulkley or Casson]
    noYield --> step5{Step 5: Two Newtonian plateaus visible?}
    step5 --> yesPlateau[Yes: Cross or Carreau]
    step5 --> noPlateau[No: Power Law sufficient]
    yesNewt --> final[Final Constitutive Equation Selected]
    linearAbove --> final
    curvedAbove --> final
    yesPlateau --> final
    noPlateau --> final
    final --> outputNode[Output: K, n, tau zero, eta values for design]
```
