# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]
**Differentiate clearly between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids. Give two food examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Newtonian | Non-Newtonian |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Equation | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ (linear) | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ or other (non-linear) |
| Viscosity | Constant, independent of $\dot{\gamma}$ | Apparent viscosity varies with $\dot{\gamma}$ |
| Origin | Passes through zero | May or may not pass through zero |
| Food examples | Water, milk, vegetable oil | Ketchup, yogurt, chocolate, bread dough |

*(1 mark for table structure, 1 mark for correct equations, 1 mark for food examples)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]
**What is yield stress? Write the constitutive equation of a Bingham plastic material and explain the physical significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Yield stress** ($\tau_0$) is the **minimum shear stress** that must be exceeded before a material begins to flow. Below $\tau_0$, the material behaves as a rigid solid; above $\tau_0$, it flows like a liquid.

**Bingham Plastic Equation**:
$$
\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

- $\tau_0$ (Pa) — **yield stress**: minimum stress to initiate flow (e.g., ketchup must be tapped before pouring).
- $\eta_{p}$ (Pa·s) — **plastic viscosity**: resistance to flow once the material is moving.
- The plot of $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a straight line intersecting the $\tau$-axis at $\tau_0$.

*(1 mark yield stress definition, 1 mark equation, 1 mark physical significance of each term)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Module 1 Pattern, Model Question A]**

**(a)** Derive the generalized Hagen–Poiseuille equation for the flow of a Power Law fluid through a circular pipe. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tomato sauce is being pumped through a 5 cm diameter stainless steel pipe at a mean velocity of 0.5 m/s. The sauce follows the Power Law model with $K = 12$ Pa·sⁿ and $n = 0.45$. Calculate:
- (i) The pressure drop per metre length of pipe. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The apparent viscosity at the wall. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The shear stress at the wall. **(1 mark)**
- (iv) Comment on whether the sauce shows shear-thinning or shear-thickening behaviour. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

This is a direct reproduction of the **Derivation 2** in Section 3.2. Marks are distributed as follows:

- **Step 1–2** (force balance, expression for τ) → 1 mark
- **Step 3–4** (substitute Power Law, solve for velocity gradient) → 1.5 marks
- **Step 5–6** (integration with boundary condition) → 2 marks
- **Step 7–8** (mean velocity integration, final rearranged result) → 2 marks
- **Step 9** (Newtonian verification, $n=1$ check) → 0.5 marks

Final answer:
$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{4K}{D}\left(\dfrac{8V}{D}\right)^{n}}
$$

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Pressure drop per metre (2 marks):**

Using the generalized Hagen–Poiseuille equation:
$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{4K}{D}\left(\dfrac{8V}{D}\right)^{n}
$$

Substitute $D = 0.05$ m, $V = 0.5$ m/s, $K = 12$ Pa·sⁿ, $n = 0.45$:

$$
\dfrac{8V}{D} = \dfrac{8 \times 0.5}{0.05} = 80 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

$$
(80)^{0.45} = e^{0.45 \times \ln(80)} = e^{0.45 \times 4.382} = e^{1.972} \approx 7.185
$$

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{4 \times 12}{0.05} \times 7.185 = 960 \times 7.185 = 6897.6 \text{ Pa/m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \approx 6.90 \text{ kPa/m}}
$$

**(ii) Apparent viscosity at the wall (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{\text{app,wall}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{\text{wall}}^{\,n-1} = 12 \times (80)^{0.45-1} = 12 \times (80)^{-0.55}
$$

$$
(80)^{-0.55} = e^{-0.55 \times 4.382} = e^{-2.410} \approx 0.0898
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{app,wall}} = 12 \times 0.0898 \approx 1.077 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{app,wall}} \approx 1.08 \text{ Pa·s}}
$$

**(iii) Shear stress at the wall (1 mark):**

$$
\tau_{\text{wall}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{\text{wall}}^{\,n} = 12 \times (80)^{0.45} = 12 \times 7.185 \approx 86.2 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\text{wall}} \approx 86.2 \text{ Pa}}
$$

**(iv) Shear behaviour comment (1 mark):**

Since $n = 0.45 < 1$, the sauce exhibits **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) behaviour** — viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate. This is desirable for pumping and pouring applications.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Module 1 Pattern, Model Question B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the Maxwell and Kelvin–Voigt viscoelastic models. Derive the stress relaxation equation for the Maxwell model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dough sample is subjected to a stress relaxation test. The Maxwell model fits the data with $G = 1500$ Pa and $\eta = 900$ Pa·s. Calculate:
- (i) The relaxation time λ. **(1.5 marks)**
- (ii) The stress at $t = \lambda$, $t = 2\lambda$, and $t = 5\lambda$, given that the initial stress is 100 Pa. **(3 marks)**
- (iii) The percentage of stress remaining after 5 seconds. **(1.5 marks)**
- (iv) Briefly explain why the Maxwell model is not adequate to fully describe dough behaviour, and suggest one improvement. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Maxwell Model** = Spring ($G$) and Dashpot ($\eta$) in **series**. Under series loading, **stresses are equal** but **strains add**.

**Kelvin–Voigt Model** = Spring ($G$) and Dashpot ($\eta$) in **parallel**. Under parallel loading, **strains are equal** but **stresses add**.

*(1.5 marks for neat schematic description, 1.5 marks for explaining both models clearly)*

**Stress relaxation derivation** for Maxwell (as in Section 3.3):

- **Step 1–2** (Maxwell ODE, $\dot{\gamma} = 0$) → 1 mark
- **Step 3–4** (rearrange to first-order ODE) → 0.5 marks
- **Step 5–7** (separation, integration, exponentiation) → 1.5 marks
- **Step 8** (apply initial condition, final boxed result) → 1 mark

Final result:
$$
\boxed{\tau(t) = G \gamma_{0} \, e^{-t/\lambda} \quad \text{where } \lambda = \dfrac{\eta}{G}}
$$

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Relaxation time (1.5 marks):**
$$
\lambda = \dfrac{\eta}{G} = \dfrac{900 \text{ Pa·s}}{1500 \text{ Pa}} = 0.6 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = 0.6 \text{ s}}
$$

**(ii) Stress at various times (3 marks):**

Using $\tau(t) = \tau_0 \cdot e^{-t/\lambda}$ with $\tau_0 = 100$ Pa:

- At $t = \lambda = 0.6$ s:
$$
\tau(\lambda) = 100 \cdot e^{-1} = 100 \times 0.3679 = 36.79 \text{ Pa}
$$
$$
\boxed{\tau(\lambda) \approx 36.8 \text{ Pa}}
$$

- At $t = 2\lambda = 1.2$ s:
$$
\tau(2\lambda) = 100 \cdot e^{-2} = 100 \times 0.1353 = 13.53 \text{ Pa}
$$
$$
\boxed{\tau(2\lambda) \approx 13.5 \text{ Pa}}
$$

- At $t = 5\lambda = 3.0$ s:
$$
\tau(5\lambda) = 100 \cdot e^{-5} = 100 \times 0.00674 = 0.674 \text{ Pa}
$$
$$
\boxed{\tau(5\lambda) \approx 0.67 \text{ Pa}}
$$

**(iii) Percentage of stress remaining after 5 s (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\tau(5)}{\tau_0} = e^{-5/\lambda} = e^{-5/0.6} = e^{-8.333}
$$

$$
e^{-8.333} \approx 2.405 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\text{Percentage} = 2.405 \times 10^{-4} \times 100 = 0.0241 \%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Only } 0.024\% \text{ of initial stress remains}}
$$

**(iv) Adequacy of Maxwell model for dough (1 mark):**

The Maxwell model predicts **complete stress relaxation to zero**, but real dough retains a **residual elastic stress** due to its cross-linked gluten network. Furthermore, Maxwell describes only stress relaxation, not creep–recovery behaviour.

**Suggested improvement:** Use the **Burgers model** (Maxwell + Kelvin–Voigt in series), which captures both instantaneous elastic deformation, viscous flow, and delayed elastic recovery.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:**
> - **Unit errors** (1 mark lost per question): Always convert mm → m, cm → m, and minutes → seconds before substituting into equations. Forgetting this is the #1 cause of mark loss.
> - **Confusing τ₀ with τ(0)**: The yield stress τ₀ is a *material property*, not an initial condition. It is defined at the threshold of flow, not at $t=0$.
> - **Missing the Newtonian verification step**: In derivations, KTU examiners award a 0.5–1 mark bonus for verifying the limiting case $n=1$ recovers Newton's law.
> - **Forgetting to declare units** in the final answer: Always write "Pa/m", "Pa·s", "s" beside numerical answers.
> - **Mixing up G' and G''**: G' (storage, elastic) and G'' (loss, viscous) are often swapped. **Memory trick:** "Storage for the **e**lastic, **E**nergy **E**lastic" → G'.
> - **In Casson model**: Forgetting the square root on both sides — Casson plots $\sqrt{\tau}$ vs. $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ as a *straight line*, not $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY** — Tick each box after memorization.

- [ ] **Newtonian equation**: $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ — a straight line through origin (slope = η).
- [ ] **Power Law**: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$. **n = 1 → Newtonian**, **n < 1 → shear-thinning**, **n > 1 → shear-thickening**.
- [ ] **Apparent viscosity** (Power Law): $\eta_{\text{app}} = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$.
- [ ] **Bingham Plastic**: $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ — yield stress + linear post-yield flow.
- [ ] **Herschel–Bulkley**: $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ — the **most general** single-equation model for structured foods.
- [ ] **Casson equation**: $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ — **ISO standard for chocolate**.
- [ ] **Cross & Carreau**: Four-parameter models for fluids with **two Newtonian plateaus** (zero-shear + infinite-shear).
- [ ] **Hooke's Law**: $\tau = G \gamma$ — purely elastic.
- [ ] **Newton's Law**: $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ — purely viscous.
- [ ] **Maxwell Model** (series): $\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\tau}/G + \tau/\eta$ → $\tau(t) = G \gamma_0 e^{-t/\lambda}$, $\lambda = \eta/G$.
- [ ] **Kelvin–Voigt Model** (parallel): $\tau = G\gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$ → models creep recovery.
- [ ] **Burgers Model**: 4-element series combination of Maxwell + Kelvin–Voigt → models bread dough fully.
- [ ] **Dynamic (Oscillatory) testing**: $G^* = G' + iG''$, $\tan \delta = G''/G'$.
- [ ] **Generalized Hagen–Poiseuille (Power Law)**: $\Delta P/L = (4K/D)(8V/D)^n$.
- [ ] **Unit conversions**: 1 Pa·s = 1000 cP (centipoise); 1 mPa·s = 1 cP.
- [ ] **Time-independent models** depend only on instantaneous $\dot{\gamma}$.
- [ ] **Time-dependent models** include history (thixotropy = break-down, rheopexy = build-up).
- [ ] **Viscoelastic models** combine elastic (spring) and viscous (dashpot) elements.
- [ ] **Common food systems**: milk (Newtonian), ketchup (Power Law), chocolate (Casson), dough (Burgers).
- [ ] **Final rule**: For any derivation problem, **always verify the Newtonian limit** ($n=1$, $\tau_0 = 0$) to earn the bonus mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rheological Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Rheological Models # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Rheological Models** are idealized mathematical and mechanical constructs used to describe and predict the deformation and flow behavior of real materials under applied stress or strain. These models represent the combination of **ideal elastic (Hookean), ideal viscous (Newtonian), and plastic (Saint-Venant)** elements arranged in specific configurations to mimic the time-dependent mechanical response of food materials. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEFTT523):** > The Module 1 syllabus specifically requires students to understand the classification of **ideal bodies** (Hooke, Newton, Saint-Venant) and the derivation and application of **viscoelastic models** (Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, Burgers, Standard Linear Solid) used to characterize food systems. In rheology, the term **rheological model** broadly encompasses: 1. **Ideal mechanical analogs** – springs, dashpots, and friction sliders used to construct physical analogs. 2. **Constitutive equations** – differential or integral equations that mathematically describe stress–strain–time relationships. 3. **Viscoelastic response functions** – creep compliance $J(t)$ and relaxation modulus $G(t)$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Three Siblings of Matter"** > Imagine three siblings reacting to being pushed (force): > - **The Spring Child (Hookean Solid):** Push him and he instantly bounces back the exact moment you stop pushing. No delay. Perfect memory. > - **The Honey Jar (Newtonian Liquid):** Push him and he flows slowly, never bouncing back. Honey, water, and oils behave like this. > - **The Sand Pile (Plastic/Saint-Venant):** You can push him gently and nothing happens — until you exceed a critical force threshold. Then he gives way suddenly. > > **Real food materials** (like cheese, dough, ketchup, or yogurt) are mixtures of these three "siblings." **Rheological models** are recipes (series/parallel combinations) of these three elements that help engineers predict exactly how a food will deform, sag, stretch, or flow during processing, storage, or chewing. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | |--------|----------|--------------| | $G$ | Shear modulus (spring stiffness) | **Pa** | | $E$ | Young's modulus | **Pa** | | $\eta$ | Viscosity coefficient | **Pa·s** | | $\sigma_y$ | Yield stress | **Pa** | | $\lambda = \eta/G$ | Relaxation/retardation time | **s** | | $\tau$ | Shear stress | **Pa** | | $\dot{\gamma}$ | Shear rate | **s⁻¹** | | $\gamma$ | Shear strain | dimensionless | ## 1.4 Why Rheological Models Matter in Food Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Significance:** > - **Process Design:** Predicting pumping, mixing, extrusion, and pipe flow behavior of pastes, slurries, and melts. > - **Texture & Sensory Quality:** Correlating instrumental measurements with mouthfeel (firmness, chewiness, spreadability). > - **Shelf Life:** Modeling sag, creep, and relaxation in stored semi-solid foods like cheese blocks, chocolate bars, and bread loaves. > - **Quality Control:** Detecting batch-to-batch variation via fitting models to creep/recovery data. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Ideal Elements (Building Blocks) ### 2.1.1 Hookean Elastic Solid (Spring) - Represents **ideal solid** behavior: instantaneous, fully recoverable deformation. - Follows Hooke's Law: $$\sigma = G \cdot \gamma$$ where $G$ is the shear modulus. The strain is **instantaneous** upon loading and **fully recovered** upon unloading. > **Why this matters:** Foods like firm gelatin gels and hard cheese approximate this behavior at small strains. ### 2.1.2 Newtonian Viscous Liquid (Dashpot) - Represents **ideal fluid** behavior: irreversible, time-dependent flow. - Follows Newton's Law of Viscosity: $$\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ - Strain accumulates indefinitely under sustained load; no recovery on unloading. > **Why this matters:** Water, edible oils, and dilute solutions follow this behavior closely. ### 2.1.3 Saint-Venant Plastic Element (Friction Slider) - Represents a **yield phenomenon**: no deformation until stress exceeds a threshold $\sigma_y$. - The element is "locked" when $\sigma < \sigma_y$; once exceeded, it flows freely. - Mathematically described as: $$\sigma = \begin{cases} \sigma_y & \text{if } \gamma \text{ is changing} \\ \vert \sigma \vert < \sigma_y & \text{if } \gamma \text{ is constant} \end{cases}$$ > **Why this matters:** Critical for modeling tomato ketchup, mayonnaise, and molten chocolate — they do not flow until a minimum shear stress is applied. ## 2.2 Combined Viscoelastic Models ### 2.2.1 Maxwell Model (Spring + Dashpot in **Series**) - **Configuration:** Spring and dashpot connected end-to-end; same stress acts on both, strains add. - Represents **stress relaxation** behavior of viscoelastic liquids. - **Differential form:** $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ - **Stress relaxation** (constant strain $\gamma_0$): $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{G}{\eta}\,t\right) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda}\right)$$ - **Creep** (constant stress $\sigma_0$): $$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\,t$$ ### 2.2.2 Kelvin–Voigt Model (Spring + Dashpot in **Parallel**) - **Configuration:** Spring and dashpot side-by-side; same strain in both, stresses add. - Represents **creep and recovery** behavior of viscoelastic solids. - **Differential form:** $$\sigma(t) = G\,\gamma(t) + \eta\,\dot{\gamma}(t)$$ - **Creep compliance:** $$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda}\right)\right] \quad \text{with } \lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}$$ - The Maxwell model cannot represent recovery (permanent viscous flow); the Kelvin–Voigt model cannot represent stress relaxation in its pure form. ### 2.2.3 Standard Linear Solid (Zener Model) Two common sub-forms exist: - **Zener A (Maxwell arm in parallel with a spring):** $$\sigma + \frac{\eta_1}{G_1 + G_2}\dot{\sigma} = \frac{G_1 G_2}{G_1 + G_2}\,\gamma + \frac{G_2 \eta_1}{G_1 + G_2}\dot{\gamma}$$ - **Zener B (Kelvin–Voigt arm in series with a spring):** $$\sigma + \frac{\eta_1}{G_1}\dot{\sigma} = G_2 \gamma + \eta_1 \left(1 + \frac{G_2}{G_1}\right)\dot{\gamma}$$ ### 2.2.4 Burgers Model (Maxwell + Kelvin–Voigt in **Series**) - **Configuration:** A Maxwell element in series with a Kelvin–Voigt element — four parameters: $G_M$, $\eta_M$, $G_K$, $\eta_K$. - Capable of representing **instantaneous elasticity, delayed elasticity (retardation), viscous flow, and permanent deformation** — the most complete four-parameter model. - **Creep compliance:** $$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_M} + \frac{\sigma_0}{G_K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{G_K}{\eta_K}t\right)\right] + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M}t$$ ### 2.2.5 Generalized Models (Wiechert / Maxwell–Weichert Chain) - A **finite or infinite** number of Maxwell elements in parallel. - Captures a **spectrum of relaxation times** rather than a single one. - **Stress relaxation:** $$\sigma(t) = \gamma_0 \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda_i}\right)$$ > This is the basis of **Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)** and **small-amplitude oscillatory shear (SAOS)** fitting in modern food rheology. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Model / Element | Configuration | Constitutive Equation | Creep $\gamma(t)$ at $\sigma_0$ | Stress Relaxation $\sigma(t)$ at $\gamma_0$ | |---|----------------|---------------|------------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------------------| | 1 | Hooke (Spring) | Single spring | $\sigma = G\gamma$ | $\sigma_0 / G$ | $G\gamma_0$ | | 2 | Newton (Dashpot) | Single dashpot | $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $(\sigma_0/\eta)\,t$ | $0$ (instant decay) | | 3 | Saint-Venant | Friction slider | $\sigma = \sigma_y$ | $0$ (if $\sigma_0 < \sigma_y$) | $\sigma_y$ (after yield) | | 4 | Maxwell | Spring $\vert$ dashpot in series | $\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\sigma}/G + \sigma/\eta$ | $\sigma_0/G + (\sigma_0/\eta)\,t$ | $\sigma_0\exp(-t/\lambda)$, $\lambda = \eta/G$ | | 5 | Kelvin–Voigt | Spring $\vert$ dashpot in parallel | $\sigma = G\gamma + \eta\dot{\gamma}$ | $(\sigma_0/G)[1 - \exp(-t/\lambda)]$ | — (not applicable) | | 6 | Standard Linear Solid (Zener A) | Maxwell $\vert$ spring | $\sigma + (\eta/(G_1+G_2))\dot{\sigma} = \dots$ | $\sigma_0/G_1 + (\sigma_0/G_2)[1-\exp(-t/\lambda)]$ | $\sigma_\infty + (\sigma_0 - \sigma_\infty)\exp(-t/\lambda)$ | | 7 | Burgers (4-parameter) | Maxwell + Kelvin–Voigt in series | (sum of creep terms) | $\sigma_0/G_M + (\sigma_0/G_K)[1-\exp(-G_K t/\eta_K)] + (\sigma_0/\eta_M)t$ | $G_M\gamma_0\exp(-G_M t/\eta_M) + G_K\gamma_0\exp(-G_K t/\eta_K)$ | | 8 | Generalized Maxwell (Wiechert) | N Maxwell units in parallel | $\sigma(t) = \gamma_0 \sum G_i \exp(-t/\lambda_i)$ | — | Spectrum of $\lambda_i$ | > [!NOTE] > **LaTeX Note:** All absolute values inside this table are written using $\vert$ (e.g., $\vert \sigma \vert < \sigma_y$) to prevent markdown table column-breaking. This is in line with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 formatting rules. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Dough rheology:** Maxwell models fit the stress relaxation of bread dough during proofing. - **Chocolate tempering:** A yield-stress (Bingham/Herschel–Bulkley + elastic element) variant predicts snap and mouthfeel. - **Cheese texture:** Burgers model captures the combined elastic recoil and viscous flow during mastication. - **Yogurt and gels:** Kelvin–Voigt-type behavior is observed in creep tests of set yogurt under spoon pressure. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section presents **complete, line-by-line derivations** for the most exam-relevant models. Each algebraic transition is explicitly justified. ## 3.1 Derivation: Maxwell Model Constitutive Equation ### Step 1 — Define the model geometry The Maxwell element consists of a spring (modulus $G$) connected in **series** with a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$). ### Step 2 — Apply the series-additivity rule For elements in series, the **total strain is the sum** of the individual element strains, and the **stress is the same** in each element: $$\gamma_{\text{total}} = \gamma_{\text{spring}} + \gamma_{\text{dashpot}}$$ $$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_{\text{spring}} = \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}$$ ### Step 3 — Write the constitutive law for each element For the spring: $$\gamma_{\text{spring}} = \frac{\sigma}{G}$$ For the dashpot: $$\gamma_{\text{dashpot}} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta} \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ ### Step 4 — Differentiate the spring strain with respect to time $$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} = \frac{1}{G}\,\dot{\sigma}$$ ### Step 5 — Sum the strain rates Differentiating the series additivity rule: $$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{total}} = \dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} + \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}}$$ ### Step 6 — Substitute and rearrange Substituting the expressions from Steps 4 and 3: $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ > This is the **constitutive differential equation** of the Maxwell model. It expresses that the total deformation rate is the sum of an elastic-rate term and a viscous-rate term. ## 3.2 Derivation: Maxwell Model — Stress Relaxation Response ### Step 1 — Apply the boundary condition In a stress relaxation test, strain is held constant at $\gamma = \gamma_0$. Therefore: $$\dot{\gamma} = 0$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute into the constitutive equation $$0 = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ ### Step 3 — Rearrange into standard ODE form $$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{G}{\eta}\,dt$$ ### Step 4 — Integrate both sides $$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{G}{\eta}\int_{0}^{t} dt$$ ### Step 5 — Evaluate the integrals $$\ln\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\frac{G}{\eta}\,t$$ ### Step 6 — Exponentiate to obtain the final result $$\boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda}\right), \qquad \lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}}$$ > **Interpretation:** Stress decays exponentially from initial value $\sigma_0$ to zero with characteristic relaxation time $\lambda$. The longer $\lambda$ (high viscosity or low stiffness), the more "solid-like" the material feels during the test. ## 3.3 Derivation: Kelvin–Voigt Model — Creep Response ### Step 1 — Apply the parallel-additivity rule For elements in **parallel**, the **stress adds** and the **strain is identical** in both elements: $$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_{\text{spring}} + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}$$ $$\gamma_{\text{total}} = \gamma_{\text{spring}} = \gamma_{\text{dashpot}}$$ ### Step 2 — Write individual stresses $$\sigma_{\text{spring}} = G\gamma \qquad \sigma_{\text{dashpot}} = \eta\dot{\gamma}$$ ### Step 3 — Sum the stresses $$\sigma(t) = G\gamma(t) + \eta\dot{\gamma}(t)$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the creep boundary condition In a creep test, stress is held constant: $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$ for $t \geq 0$. Substituting: $$\sigma_0 = G\gamma + \eta\dot{\gamma}$$ ### Step 5 — Rearrange into standard first-order linear ODE $$\dot{\gamma} + \frac{G}{\eta}\gamma = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$ ### Step 6 — Solve using integrating factor The integrating factor is $e^{(G/\eta)t}$. Multiplying both sides: $$e^{(G/\eta)t}\dot{\gamma} + \frac{G}{\eta}e^{(G/\eta)t}\gamma = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}e^{(G/\eta)t}$$ The left-hand side is the derivative of $\left[\gamma e^{(G/\eta)t}\right]$: $$\frac{d}{dt}\left[\gamma\,e^{(G/\eta)t}\right] = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}e^{(G/\eta)t}$$ ### Step 7 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$ $$\gamma(t)\,e^{(G/\eta)t} - \gamma(0) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\int_0^t e^{(G/\eta)\tau}d\tau = \frac{\sigma_0}{G}\left[e^{(G/\eta)t} - 1\right]$$ ### Step 8 — Apply initial condition $\gamma(0) = 0$ $$\gamma(t)\,e^{(G/\eta)t} = \frac{\sigma_0}{G}\left[e^{(G/\eta)t} - 1\right]$$ ### Step 9 — Solve for $\gamma(t)$ $$\boxed{\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda}\right)\right], \qquad \lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}}$$ > **Interpretation:** Strain rises asymptotically toward the equilibrium value $\sigma_0 / G$, with retardation time $\lambda$. Upon unloading (at $t = t_1$), the strain recovers exponentially back to zero — a hallmark of **delayed elastic solid** behavior. ## 3.4 Derivation: Burgers Model — Creep Compliance (4-Parameter) ### Step 1 — Set up the model as a series combination A Burgers model is a Maxwell unit (spring $G_M$, dashpot $\eta_M$) **in series** with a Kelvin–Voigt unit (spring $G_K$, dashpot $\eta_K$). For series combinations, total strain is the sum of element strains: $$\gamma(t) = \gamma_{\text{Maxwell}}(t) + \gamma_{\text{Kelvin-Voigt}}(t)$$ ### Step 2 — Write the strain response of the Maxwell unit under creep From §3 of the Maxwell model, creep of a single spring + dashpot in series is: $$\gamma_{\text{Maxwell}}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_M} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M}\,t$$ ### Step 3 — Write the strain response of the Kelvin–Voigt unit under creep From §3.3 above: $$\gamma_{\text{Kelvin-Voigt}}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{G_K}{\eta_K}t\right)\right]$$ ### Step 4 — Sum the two responses $$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_M} + \frac{\sigma_0}{G_K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{G_K}{\eta_K}t\right)\right] + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M}t$$ ### Step 5 — Rearrange into the canonical Burgers creep form $$\boxed{\gamma(t) = \sigma_0\left\{\frac{1}{G_M} + \frac{1}{G_K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda_K}\right)\right] + \frac{t}{\eta_M}\right\}, \quad \lambda_K = \frac{\eta_K}{G_K}}$$ > **Physical interpretation of each term:** > - $\sigma_0 / G_M$ — **instantaneous elastic deformation** (springs immediately). > - $(\sigma_0 / G_K)[1 - \exp(-t/\lambda_K)]$ — **delayed viscoelastic deformation** (retarded dashpot). > - $(\sigma_0 / \eta_M)\,t$ — **irrecoverable viscous flow** (dashpot keeps flowing). ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Maxwell | Kelvin–Voigt | Burgers | Standard Linear Solid | |----------|---------|--------------|---------|------------------------| | No. of parameters | 2 | 2 | 4 | 3 | | Captures instantaneous elasticity | ✓ | ✗ (asymptotic) | ✓ | ✓ | | Captures delayed elasticity | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Captures viscous flow | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | | Captures stress relaxation | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | | Recovery on unloading | ✗ (permanent) | ✓ (full) | Partial | Partial / Full | | Best for food example | Molten dough | Yogurt gel | Cheese, bread | Solid chocolate | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Ideal Mechanical Elements (Building Blocks) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph INPUT["Applied Stress sigma"] A0[Applied Force or Stress] end A0 --> Hooke["HOOKEAN SPRING
sigma = G times gamma
Instant elastic recovery"] A0 --> Newton["NEWTONIAN DASHPOT
sigma = eta times dgamma dt
Viscous irreversible flow"] A0 --> Saint["SAINT-VENANT SLIDER
sigma = sigma_y
No flow below yield stress"] Hooke -->|Elastic strain| Out1[Recoverable Strain] Newton -->|Viscous strain| Out2[Permanent Strain] Saint -->|Threshold check| Out3[Yield Event] style A0 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937 style Hooke fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#064e3b style Newton fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0f172a style Saint fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#450a0a style Out1 fill:#ecfccb,stroke:#3f6212,color:#1f2937 style Out2 fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,color:#1f2937 style Out3 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#1f2937 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Combined Viscoelastic Model Architectures ```mermaid graph TB subgraph MaxwellUnit["MAXWELL MODEL - Series"] M1[Spring G] --- M2[Dashpot eta] end subgraph KelvinUnit["KELVIN VOIGT MODEL - Parallel"] K1[Spring G] K2[Dashpot eta] K1 --- K2 end subgraph ZenerUnit["STANDARD LINEAR SOLID - Zener A"] Z1[Spring G2] Z2[Spring G1 in series with Dashpot eta1] Z1 --- Z2 end subgraph BurgersUnit["BURGERS MODEL - Maxwell in series with Kelvin Voigt"] B1[Spring G_M] --- B2[Dashpot eta_M] --- B3[Spring G_K] --- B4[Dashpot eta_K] B3 --- B4 end M_Food[Food Example: Molten Dough
Stress Relaxation]:::food --> MaxwellUnit K_Food[Food Example: Set Yogurt
Creep Recovery]:::food --> KelvinUnit Z_Food[Food Example: Firm Cheese
Combined Response]:::food --> ZenerUnit B_Food[Food Example: Bread Crumb
Full Viscoelastic Response]:::food --> BurgersUnit classDef food fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Test Protocol Flow (Creep & Stress Relaxation) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Sample Mounted in Rheometer]):::start --> Q1{Type of Test?} Q1 -->|Constant Stress| Creep[Creep Test
sigma = sigma_0 applied]:::test Q1 -->|Constant Strain| Relax[Stress Relaxation Test
gamma = gamma_0 applied]:::test Creep --> C1[Record gamma t over time] C1 --> C2{Model Fit?} C2 -->|Instant + Delayed + Flow| CBurgers[Fit Burgers Model
4 parameters]:::fit C2 -->|Only Delayed| CKelvin[Fit Kelvin Voigt Model
2 parameters]:::fit Relax --> R1[Record sigma t over time] R1 --> R2{Model Fit?} R2 -->|Exponential Decay| RMaxwell[Fit Maxwell Model
2 parameters]:::fit R2 -->|Decay with Equilibrium| RZener[Fit Standard Linear Solid
3 parameters]:::fit CBurgers --> Output[Extract G eta and lambda]:::out CKelvin --> Output RMaxwell --> Output RZener --> Output Output --> Done([Predict Food Texture and Process Behavior]):::done classDef start fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#1f2937 classDef test fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0f172a classDef fit fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,color:#1f2937 classDef out fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#064e3b classDef done fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Hookean solid and write its constitutive equation. (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A Hookean solid is an **ideal elastic material** whose mechanical response obeys Hooke's Law. Under an applied shear stress $\sigma$, it deforms instantaneously and recovers completely upon removal of the stress. The constitutive equation is: $$\sigma = G \cdot \gamma$$ where $G$ is the **shear modulus (Pa)**, $\sigma$ is the shear stress (Pa), and $\gamma$ is the shear strain (dimensionless). Examples in food systems include firm gelatin gels and hard cheese at small strains. ### Question 2: Differentiate between a Maxwell element and a Kelvin–Voigt element. (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Maxwell Model | Kelvin–Voigt Model | |---------|---------------|---------------------| | Configuration | Spring and dashpot in **series** | Spring and dashpot in **parallel** | | Stress/Strain rule | Same stress, strains add | Same strain, stresses add | | Creep response | $\gamma = \sigma_0/G + (\sigma_0/\eta)t$ | $\gamma = (\sigma_0/G)[1 - \exp(-t/\lambda)]$ | | Stress relaxation | $\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp(-t/\lambda)$ | Not applicable in classical form | | Recovery on unloading | Permanent (viscous flow retained) | Full delayed recovery | | Best represents | Viscoelastic **liquid** | Viscoelastic **solid** | ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks):** #### (a) Derive the constitutive equation of the Maxwell model. (7 Marks) **Step-by-step model solution:** A Maxwell model is a spring (modulus $G$) connected in **series** with a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$). For a series arrangement: $$\gamma_{\text{total}} = \gamma_{\text{spring}} + \gamma_{\text{dashpot}}$$ $$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_{\text{spring}} = \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}$$ The spring obeys Hooke's law: $$\gamma_{\text{spring}} = \frac{\sigma}{G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G}$$ The dashpot obeys Newton's law: $$\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ Differentiating the strain additivity: $$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{total}} = \dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} + \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}}$$ Substituting: $$\boxed{\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}}$$ #### (b) Starting from the Maxwell constitutive equation, derive the stress relaxation modulus. (7 Marks) **Step-by-step model solution:** In a stress relaxation test, the strain is held constant at $\gamma_0$. Therefore: $$\dot{\gamma} = 0$$ Substituting into the Maxwell constitutive equation: $$0 = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{G}{\eta}\,dt$$ Integrating both sides with limits $\sigma_0$ at $t = 0$ to $\sigma(t)$ at time $t$: $$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{G}{\eta}\int_0^t dt$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\frac{G}{\eta}t$$ Defining the relaxation time $\lambda = \eta / G$ and exponentiating: $$\boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda}\right)}$$ > **Significance:** The relaxation modulus $G(t) = \sigma(t)/\gamma_0 = G\exp(-t/\lambda)$ provides the material's time-dependent elastic response after a sudden strain. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]:** #### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the four-parameter Burgers model and list its constitutive elements. (7 Marks) **Step-by-step model solution:** The **Burgers model** is a four-parameter viscoelastic model constructed by connecting a **Maxwell unit** (spring $G_M$ in series with dashpot $\eta_M$) **in series** with a **Kelvin–Voigt unit** (spring $G_K$ in parallel with dashpot $\eta_K$). **Mechanical representation (ASCII schematic):** ``` |---/\/\/\---[O O O]---|---/\/\\---[O O O]---| Spring GM Dashpot eta_M Spring GK Dashpot eta_K <-------- Maxwell unit -------> <--- Kelvin-Voigt unit ---> ``` **Parameters and physical meaning:** | Parameter | Symbol | Physical meaning | |-----------|--------|-------------------| | Spring modulus (Maxwell) | $G_M$ | Instantaneous elasticity | | Dashpot viscosity (Maxwell) | $\eta_M$ | Irreversible viscous flow | | Spring modulus (Kelvin) | $G_K$ | Equilibrium stiffness of retarded part | | Dashpot viscosity (Kelvin) | $\eta_K$ | Retardation viscosity | **Key applications:** Modeling cheese, bread dough, and other food materials that exhibit **all three** viscoelastic features simultaneously. #### (b) Derive the creep compliance expression for the Burgers model. (7 Marks) **Step-by-step model solution:** For two sub-units connected in **series**, total strain is the sum: $$\gamma(t) = \gamma_{\text{Maxwell}}(t) + \gamma_{\text{Kelvin-Voigt}}(t)$$ **Maxwell unit creep:** Under constant stress $\sigma_0$: $$\gamma_{\text{Maxwell}}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_M} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M}\,t$$ **Kelvin–Voigt unit creep:** Under constant stress $\sigma_0$: $$\gamma_{\text{Kelvin-Voigt}}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{G_K}{\eta_K}t\right)\right]$$ **Summing:** $$\boxed{\gamma(t) = \sigma_0\left\{\frac{1}{G_M} + \frac{1}{G_K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda_K}\right)\right] + \frac{t}{\eta_M}\right\}, \quad \lambda_K = \frac{\eta_K}{G_K}}$$ **Physical interpretation of each term:** 1. $\sigma_0/G_M$ — Instantaneous elastic deformation on loading. 2. $(\sigma_0/G_K)[1 - \exp(-t/\lambda_K)]$ — Retarded viscoelastic deformation, fully recoverable on unloading. 3. $(\sigma_0/\eta_M)\,t$ — Irrecoverable viscous flow. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - **Do not** confuse the spring in the Maxwell unit ($G_M$) with the spring in the Kelvin–Voigt unit ($G_K$). They serve **different physical roles** and have **different numerical values**. > - Many students **omit the $\lambda_K$ definition** or forget to specify the retardation time in the creep formula. Always include it. > - Students frequently **write the equation but fail to interpret** each term. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the physical interpretation of the three creep components. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - [ ] **Three ideal elements:** Hooke (spring, $G$), Newton (dashpot, $\eta$), Saint-Venant (friction slider, $\sigma_y$). - [ ] **Maxwell model:** Series spring–dashpot; **stress relaxes exponentially**; exhibits permanent viscous flow; cannot capture recovery. - [ ] **Kelvin–Voigt model:** Parallel spring–dashpot; **creep rises asymptotically** to $\sigma_0 / G$; **fully recovers** on unloading; cannot relax stress instantaneously. - [ ] **Relaxation time:** $\lambda = \eta / G$ — characterizes how quickly a material "forgets" a stress or "remembers" a strain. - [ ] **Standard Linear Solid (Zener model):** Three-parameter; combines elastic solid behavior with viscoelastic decay; bridges Maxwell and Kelvin–Voigt. - [ ] **Burgers model:** Four-parameter; captures **instantaneous elasticity + delayed elasticity + viscous flow**; best general-purpose model for solid foods. - [ ] **Generalized Maxwell (Wiechert) model:** Multiple Maxwell elements in parallel to capture a **spectrum of relaxation times** — used in dynamic oscillatory testing. - [ ] **Sign convention:** $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0[1 - \exp(-t/\lambda)]$ indicates retarded approach to equilibrium. - [ ] **Food applications:** Dough → Maxwell; Yogurt → Kelvin–Voigt; Cheese & Bread → Burgers; Chocolate → Zener/Plastic. - [ ] **Always pair** each equation with its **mechanical analog sketch** — KTU examiners frequently award 2 marks purely for a correctly labeled diagram. - [ ] **Numerical tip:** When asked for retardation/relaxation time, always compute $\lambda$ explicitly and quote its units (seconds).

topics.title: Types of Deformation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Deformation

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Deformation** in the context of food rheology is defined as the change in the shape, size, or relative position of particles within a food material when an external force (stress) is applied. Every food material — whether solid, semi-solid, or liquid — undergoes deformation when subjected to mechanical forces during processing (mixing, pumping, extrusion, chewing) or handling.

From the **KTU 2024 Scheme** perspective, deformation is broadly classified based on two fundamental criteria:

1. **Reversibility (Recovery behaviour)** — whether the material returns to its original shape after the force is removed.
2. **Time-dependence** — whether the deformation is instantaneous or progresses with time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to distinguish between **ideal elastic**, **ideal viscous (plastic/viscous flow)**, and **viscoelastic** deformation behaviours, and to identify real food systems that approximate each.

## 1.2 The Two Extreme Ideals

| Ideal Behaviour | Mechanical Model | Real Food Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Elastic (Hookean) Solid** | Spring | Apple flesh (short loading time), gelatin gel |
| **Ideal Viscous (Newtonian) Fluid** | Dashpot | Water, honey, refined oils |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Rubber Band vs. The Honey Jar:**
> - Pull a **rubber band** (Hookean solid) and release → it snaps back instantly. This is **elastic deformation**.
> - Push a spoon into a jar of **honey** (Newtonian fluid) → it flows continuously and does NOT recover. This is **viscous deformation**.
> - Most real foods (cheese, dough, chocolate, ketchup) sit *between* these two extremes — they are **viscoelastic**.

## 1.3 The Five Primary Types of Deformation

Based on the combination of recovery and time-dependence, KTU recognises five principal types:

1. **Elastic (Instantaneous, Fully Recoverable) Deformation**
2. **Plastic (Permanent, Time-Independent) Deformation**
3. **Viscous (Permanent, Time-Dependent) Deformation**
4. **Viscoelastic Deformation** (combination of viscous + elastic)
5. **Elastoviscoplastic Deformation** (combination of elastic + viscous + plastic — e.g., yield stress fluids)

The **stress (σ)** required to initiate and maintain deformation, and the resulting **strain (γ)** or **strain rate (γ̇)**, are the two governing rheological variables.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Elastic Deformation (Hookean Solid Behaviour)

**Mechanism:** When a stress is applied, atomic/molecular bonds stretch reversibly. Energy is **stored** in the material, not dissipated. Upon removal of stress, the stored elastic energy drives the material back to its original geometry.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Deformation is **instantaneous** (occurs the moment stress is applied).
- Strain is **fully recoverable** when stress is removed.
- The relationship between stress and strain is **linear** within the elastic limit (Hooke's Law).

**Hookean Spring Analogy:** A steel spring in a shock absorber — push it, it compresses; release, it returns.

**Real Food Examples:** Short-time compression of an apple, fresh bread crumb under quick thumb pressure, gelatin gel below its melting point.

## 2.2 Plastic Deformation (Time-Independent Permanent Flow)

**Mechanism:** Once the applied stress exceeds the **yield stress (σy)**, the material flows permanently. The deformation does **not recover** when the stress is removed, and importantly, it does **not depend on time** once yielding has occurred.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Below yield stress → behaves as a rigid solid (no deformation).
- Above yield stress → permanent deformation at constant rate.
- Time-independent strain rate.

**Real Food Examples:** Spreading cold butter on bread, moulding chocolate after tempering, pressing dough into a mould.

## 2.3 Viscous Deformation (Newtonian Fluid Behaviour)

**Mechanism:** Stress produces a continuous, irreversible rearrangement of molecules past one another. Energy is **dissipated as heat**, not stored.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Deformation is **time-dependent** (continuous flow under constant stress).
- Strain is **completely non-recoverable**.
- Stress is proportional to **strain rate**, not strain itself.

**Dashpot Analogy:** A piston moving through a cylinder filled with oil — the damper converts mechanical energy into heat and never returns to start.

**Real Food Examples:** Water, milk, fruit juices, refined edible oils, dilute sugar solutions.

## 2.4 Viscoelastic Deformation

**Mechanism:** The material exhibits **both** elastic energy storage **and** viscous energy dissipation simultaneously. The total deformation has a recoverable (elastic) component and an irrecoverable (viscous) component.

**Sub-classes:**
- **Viscoelastic solid** → finite recoverable strain (e.g., cheese, bread dough).
- **Viscoelastic liquid** → infinite recoverable strain (e.g., polymer solutions, molten chocolate at high T).

**Standard Linear Solid Model Analogy:** A spring and dashpot connected either in **Maxwell arrangement** (series) or **Kelvin–Voigt arrangement** (parallel).

## 2.5 Elastoviscoplastic Deformation

**Mechanism:** Combines **yield stress** (plastic threshold), **elastic recovery** below σy, and **viscous flow** above σy.

**Real Food Examples:** Tomato ketchup (must overcome σy before it pours), mayonnaise, margarine.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Deformation Type | Governing Equation | Symbol Meanings | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Elastic (Hookean) | $\sigma = E \cdot \gamma$ | $\sigma$ = stress, $E$ = elastic (Young's) modulus, $\gamma$ = strain | Pa |
| 2 | Viscous (Newtonian) | $\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta$ = viscosity, $\dot{\gamma}$ = strain rate | Pa·s |
| 3 | Plastic (Bingham) | $\sigma = \sigma_y + \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress | Pa |
| 4 | Power-law fluid | $\sigma = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $K$ = consistency index, $n$ = flow behaviour index | Pa·sⁿ |
| 5 | Hookean shear form | $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ | $\tau$ = shear stress, $G$ = shear modulus | Pa |
| 6 | Newtonian shear form | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | — | Pa·s |
| 7 | Strain definition | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | $\Delta L$ = change in length, $L_0$ = original length | dimensionless |
| 8 | Strain rate definition | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{d\gamma}{dt}$ | $t$ = time | s⁻¹ |
| 9 | Elastic strain energy | $W = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \sigma \cdot \gamma$ | energy stored per unit volume | J·m⁻³ |
| 10 | Critical yield criterion | $\sigma \geq \sigma_y \Rightarrow$ plastic flow begins | — | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition for plastic deformation:** Plastic flow occurs **only when** $\sigma > \sigma_y$. If $\sigma \leq \sigma_y$, the material behaves as a rigid elastic solid with **zero strain**.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding deformation types is essential for:

- **Process design**: sizing pumps and pipes (viscous), extruders (viscoelastic/plastic).
- **Texture engineering**: mouthfeel perception is dominated by viscoelastic response under chewing frequencies (~1–10 Hz).
- **Quality control**: hardness of cheese → elastic modulus; spreadability of butter → yield stress + viscosity.
- **Shelf-life prediction**: time-dependent deformation (creep) predicts sag/syneresis in puddings and yogurts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hookean (Elastic) Stress–Strain Relation

**Starting premise:** Within the elastic limit, the strain in a material is directly proportional to the applied stress.

**Step 1 — Define strain.**
The dimensionless strain under uniaxial tension or compression is given by the fractional change in length.

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Step 2 — Assume linear proportionality.**
Experimental observation (Robert Hooke, 1660) shows that within the elastic limit:

$$\sigma \propto \gamma$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the constant of proportionality.**
The constant $E$ is called the **elastic modulus** (or **Young's modulus** in tensile deformation; **shear modulus $G$** in shear deformation).

$$\sigma = E \cdot \gamma$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the strain definition.**

$$\sigma = E \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the elastic deformation (extension or compression).**

$$\Delta L = \frac{\sigma \cdot L_0}{E}$$

**Interpretation:** A higher modulus $E$ produces smaller deformation for the same applied stress — i.e., stiffer materials deform less.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Newtonian (Viscous) Stress–Strain-Rate Relation

**Starting premise:** For an ideal viscous fluid, stress is proportional to the rate of strain, not strain itself.

**Step 1 — Define strain rate.**
Strain rate is the time derivative of strain:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\gamma}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's postulate of viscosity (1687).**
Stress is linearly proportional to strain rate:

$$\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the viscosity coefficient $\eta$.**

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 4 — Express strain rate in differential form.**

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \frac{d\gamma}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate for a constant stress to obtain the time-dependent strain.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d\gamma &= \frac{\tau}{\eta} \, dt \\[4pt]
\int_{0}^{\gamma(t)} d\gamma &= \frac{\tau}{\eta} \int_{0}^{t} dt \\[4pt]
\gamma(t) &= \frac{\tau}{\eta} \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Strain grows **linearly with time** under constant viscous stress — characteristic of irreversible flow.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Bingham Plastic Equation

**Starting premise:** A Bingham material behaves as a rigid solid until the stress exceeds the yield stress $\sigma_y$, beyond which it flows viscously.

**Step 1 — Establish the two-regime condition.**

$$
\sigma \begin{cases} < \sigma_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\gamma} = 0 \\[4pt] \geq \sigma_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\gamma} > 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the yield threshold from the total stress to get the "excess" stress available for viscous flow.**

$$\sigma_{excess} = \sigma - \sigma_y$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's viscosity law to the excess stress.**

$$\sigma_{excess} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 4 — Combine Steps 2 and 3.**

$$\sigma - \sigma_y = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 5 — Final Bingham plastic equation.**

$$\sigma = \sigma_y + \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Interpretation:** The Bingham model is the superposition of a **friction-like yield threshold** plus **Newtonian viscous flow**. Many sauces (ketchup, mustard) and tomato concentrates obey this form.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Deformation Types

| Property | Elastic | Viscous | Plastic | Viscoelastic | Elastoviscoplastic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Recoverable on stress removal? | **Yes (full)** | **No** | **No** | **Partial** | **Partial** |
| Time-dependent? | No | **Yes** | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| Requires yield stress? | No | No | **Yes ($\sigma_y$)** | No | **Yes** |
| Stores energy? | **Yes** | No | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| Dissipates energy as heat? | No | **Yes** | Yes (during flow) | **Yes** | Yes |
| Model symbol | Spring | Dashpot | Friction slider | Spring + Dashpot | Spring + Dashpot + Slider |
| Governing law | Hooke | Newton | Bingham | Boltzmann superposition | Combined |
| Typical food | Apple flesh | Water | Cold butter | Cheese, dough | Ketchup |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Deformation Types

The following Mermaid block illustrates the hierarchical classification of deformation in food materials and how the three primitive mechanical elements (spring, dashpot, slider) combine to form real-food deformation models.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Stress sigma on Food Material] --> B{Does material recover on stress removal?}
    B -- Yes full --> C[Elastic Deformation]
    B -- No --> D{Is deformation time-dependent?}
    D -- No --> E[Plastic Deformation]
    D -- Yes --> F[Viscous Deformation]
    B -- Partial --> G[Viscoelastic Deformation]
    C --> H[Mechanical Model: SPRING]
    E --> I[Mechanical Model: FRICTION SLIDER]
    F --> J[Mechanical Model: DASHPOT]
    G --> K[Mechanical Model: SPRING + DASHPOT]
    A --> L{Does material have yield stress?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Elastoviscoplastic Deformation]
    L -- No --> N[Purely Viscous Newtonian Fluid]
    M --> O[Mechanical Model: SPRING + DASHPOT + SLIDER]
    H --> P[Real Foods: Apple Gelatin Bread Crumb]
    I --> Q[Real Foods: Cold Butter Moulded Chocolate]
    J --> R[Real Foods: Water Milk Refined Oil]
    K --> S[Real Foods: Cheese Bread Dough Yogurt]
    O --> T[Real Foods: Ketchup Mayonnaise Margarine]
```

```mermaid

        A3 --> A4[Strain recovers to zero]
    end

        B1[Stress applied] --> B2[Strain increases linearly with time]
        B2 --> B3[Stress removed]
        B3 --> B4[Strain remains permanent]
    end

```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each mechanical element maps to one mathematical operation in the constitutive equation. **Spring → multiplication by modulus $G$**; **Dashpot → multiplication by viscosity $\eta$ and time differentiation**; **Slider → enforces a yield threshold $\sigma_y$** before any motion occurs.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define the following with one food example each:**
(a) Elastic deformation
(b) Viscous deformation
(c) Plastic deformation

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Elastic deformation:** The instantaneous and fully recoverable change in shape of a food material upon application of stress, governed by Hooke's law $\sigma = E \cdot \gamma$. The material stores the deformation energy and releases it when the stress is removed. *Example: short-time compression of fresh apple flesh.*

**(b) Viscous deformation:** The time-dependent, irreversible flow of a food material in which stress is proportional to strain rate (Newton's law, $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$). Energy is dissipated as heat; no recovery occurs. *Example: flow of honey at room temperature.*

**(c) Plastic deformation:** Permanent, time-independent deformation that occurs only after the applied stress exceeds the yield stress $\sigma_y$. Below $\sigma_y$, the material does not deform. *Example: spreading cold butter with a knife.*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Distinguish between ideal Hookean and ideal Newtonian behaviour using their constitutive equations and recovery characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Ideal Hookean Solid | Ideal Newtonian Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Constitutive law | $\sigma = E \cdot \gamma$ | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ |
| Stress proportional to | Strain $\gamma$ | Strain rate $\dot{\gamma}$ |
| Recovery on stress removal | **Complete** | **None** |
| Energy fate | Stored elastically | Dissipated as heat |
| Mechanical analogue | Spring | Dashpot |
| Food example | Gelatin gel | Water, refined oil |

The Hookean model represents *instantaneous reversible deformation*, whereas the Newtonian model represents *continuous irreversible flow*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a stress–strain diagram and constitutive equation, derive the relationship governing **Hookean elastic deformation**. Define each term and give two food examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical gelatin gel of original height $L_0 = 5.0 \times 10^{-2}$ m is subjected to a compressive stress $\sigma = 1.2 \times 10^{4}$ Pa. The elastic modulus of the gel is $E = 3.0 \times 10^{4}$ Pa. Calculate the resulting strain $\gamma$ and the absolute deformation $\Delta L$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hookean Derivation** — Already derived in **Section 3.1**. Steps to be written in exam:

1. Strain definition: $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$
2. Linear proportionality: $\sigma \propto \gamma$
3. Constitutive equation: $\sigma = E \cdot \gamma$
4. Solve for deformation: $\Delta L = \dfrac{\sigma L_0}{E}$
5. Two food examples: apple flesh, gelatin gel.

Sketch: linear straight line through origin with slope $E$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute strain.**

$$\gamma = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{4}}{3.0 \times 10^{4}} = 0.40$$

**Step 2 — Compute deformation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= \gamma \cdot L_0 \\[4pt]
\Delta L &= 0.40 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-2} \\[4pt]
\Delta L &= 2.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in mm for clarity.**

$$\Delta L = 20 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** Strain $\gamma = 0.40$ (dimensionless, i.e., 40 %); absolute deformation $\Delta L = 2.0 \times 10^{-2}$ m = 20 mm.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Newtonian viscous deformation** equation from first principles. Explain why viscous strain grows linearly with time under constant stress.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A chocolate melt at 45 °C behaves as a Newtonian fluid with viscosity $\eta = 4.0$ Pa·s. It is sheared in a viscometer at a constant shear stress $\tau = 800$ Pa. Calculate:
(i) The shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$.
(ii) The strain developed after $t = 60$ s under this constant stress.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Newtonian Derivation** — Already derived in **Section 3.2**. Key points for the answer:

1. Define strain rate $\dot{\gamma} = d\gamma / dt$.
2. Apply Newton's postulate of viscosity $\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$.
3. Introduce viscosity $\eta$: $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$.
4. Integrate under constant $\tau$: $\gamma(t) = (\tau / \eta) \cdot t$.
5. Comment that linearity in time arises because $\eta$ is constant — any non-linearity indicates non-Newtonian (e.g., shear-thinning) behaviour.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Shear rate.**

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\tau}{\eta} = \frac{800}{4.0} = 200 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Strain after 60 s.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma(t) &= \frac{\tau}{\eta} \cdot t \\[4pt]
\gamma(60) &= \frac{800}{4.0} \times 60 \\[4pt]
\gamma(60) &= 200 \times 60 \\[4pt]
\gamma(60) &= 1.2 \times 10^{4} \text{ (dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Shear rate $\dot{\gamma} = 200$ s⁻¹; cumulative strain after 60 s is $\gamma = 1.2 \times 10^{4}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose marks here!**
> - **Forgetting units**: Always state units in the final numerical answer (Pa·s, s⁻¹, dimensionless). Marks are deducted if units are missing.
> - **Mixing up $E$ and $G$**: For tensile deformation, use Young's modulus $E$; for shear deformation, use shear modulus $G$. KTU examiners specifically check this distinction.
> - **Ignoring the yield-stress condition in plastic models**: Writing $\sigma = \sigma_y + \eta \dot{\gamma}$ is only valid when $\sigma > \sigma_y$. Always state the condition explicitly.
> - **Confusing strain and strain rate**: Strain $\gamma$ is dimensionless; strain rate $\dot{\gamma}$ has units s⁻¹. Many students interchange them in derivations.
> - **Skipping integration steps**: For viscous derivation, writing $\gamma = (\tau/\eta) \cdot t$ without showing the integration loses **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three primitive mechanical elements**: **Spring** (elastic), **Dashpot** (viscous), **Slider** (plastic).
- **Hooke's Law**: $\sigma = E \cdot \gamma$ — instantaneous and fully recoverable.
- **Newton's Law**: $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ — continuous, irreversible, time-dependent.
- **Bingham Plastic Law**: $\sigma = \sigma_y + \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ — flow only after yield.
- **Strain** is dimensionless; **strain rate** has units of s⁻¹.
- **Viscoelasticity** = elastic storage + viscous dissipation simultaneously.
- **Elastoviscoplasticity** = elastic + viscous + yield-threshold combined (ketchup, mayonnaise).
- **Food classification quick memory aid**:
  - *Apple / Gelatin / Bread crumb* → Elastic
  - *Water / Milk / Honey* → Viscous
  - *Cold butter / Moulded chocolate* → Plastic
  - *Cheese / Dough / Yogurt* → Viscoelastic
  - *Ketchup / Mayonnaise / Margarine* → Elastoviscoplastic
- Always state **whether recovery is full / partial / zero** when defining any deformation type.
- For numerical problems in KTU exams, **show all substitution steps and final units** — partial marks are awarded for correct intermediate algebra even if the final answer is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Newtonian Fluids — The Baseline of Rheology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A **Newtonian fluid** is a fluid for which the shear stress at any point is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (shear rate) at that point, and the constant of proportionality is the **viscosity (η)**. The fluid exhibits no memory of previous deformations, and viscosity is **independent** of shear rate, time of shearing, and prior shear history.

Mathematically, the constitutive equation is:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

### Real-World Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Classroom Board Eraser:** Imagine dragging a wet cloth across a blackboard. The *force per unit area* you feel is directly proportional to *how fast* you drag. Drag twice as fast → feel twice the resistance. This proportionality, with a constant "feel factor," is what defines a Newtonian fluid. Common food examples: **water, milk, fruit juices, vegetable oils, honey (at high temperature)**, and dilute sugar solutions.

### Key Physical Constants
- **Viscosity of water at 20 °C: η ≈ 1.002 mPa·s**
- **Viscosity of air at 20 °C: η ≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
- SI unit of dynamic viscosity: **Pa·s = kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹**
- Kinematic viscosity: $\nu = \eta / \rho$ (m²·s⁻¹)

---

## 1.2 Non-Newtonian Fluids — When Viscosity Behaves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A **Non-Newtonian fluid** is any fluid whose flow behaviour **deviates from Newton's law of viscosity**, i.e., the apparent viscosity is a function of shear rate, time of shearing, shear history, or a combination thereof.

### Real-World Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Ketchup Bottle:** When you shake or strike a ketchup bottle, it becomes more fluid (shear thinning — **pseudoplastic**). But tomato paste, when squeezed slowly, feels solid; only after a certain pressure threshold does it begin to flow (**Bingham plastic**). These fluids "remember" their deformation history — unlike water — and that memory is the essence of non-Newtonian behaviour.

### Classification of Non-Newtonian Fluids
| Family | Sub-type | Key Feature | Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time-Independent** | Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning) | η ↓ as $\dot{\gamma}$ ↑ | Ketchup, melted chocolate, banana puree |
| | Dilatant (Shear-thickening) | η ↑ as $\dot{\gamma}$ ↑ | Cornstarch slurry, wet sand |
| | Bingham Plastic | Requires yield stress σ_y | Tomato paste, margarine, peanut butter |
| | Casson Plastic | Shear-thinning after yield | Chocolate sauce, blood |
| **Time-Dependent** | Thixotropic | η ↓ with time at constant $\dot{\gamma}$ | Yogurt, gels, some batters |
| | Rheopectic | η ↑ with time at constant $\dot{\gamma}$ | Gypsum paste, certain starch suspensions |
| **Viscoelastic** | Linear Viscoelastic | Combines elastic & viscous | Bread dough, Mozzarella cheese, wheat flour dough |

---

## 1.3 Creep and Stress Relaxation — Probing Material Memory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Creep Test Definition:** A **creep test** applies a **constant stress (σ₀)** instantaneously and records the resulting **strain (γ(t))** as a function of time. The response reveals long-time viscous flow and elastic recovery.
>
> **Stress Relaxation Test Definition:** A **stress relaxation test** applies a **constant strain (γ₀)** instantaneously and records the **decaying stress (σ(t))** as a function of time.

### Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Creep = The Sagging Bookshelf Analogy:** Place a heavy box on a cheap wooden shelf. The shelf *instantly* deflects a little (elastic), then continues to slowly sag over minutes/hours (viscoelastic creep), and after removing the box, it partially recovers (retarded elasticity) but never returns fully (permanent viscous flow). This time-dependent strain response *is* a creep test.
>
> **Stress Relaxation = The Stretched Rubber Band Analogy:** Stretch a rubber band to a fixed length and hold it. You feel a strong pull initially; over time, that pull diminishes. The molecule chains are slowly rearranging to relieve the internal stress — the "memory" of the original unstrained state slowly reasserts itself.

---

## 1.4 Viscometry — Measuring Viscosity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** **Viscometry** is the experimental science of measuring viscosity and related rheological properties. Viscometers are classified as:
> 1. **Capillary (Tubular) Viscometers** — based on Hagen–Poiseuille flow
> 2. **Rotational Viscometers** — concentric cylinder, cone–plate, parallel plate
> 3. **Falling-Ball / Falling-Needle Viscometers** — based on Stokes' law
> 4. **Oscillatory Rheometers** — for viscoelastic characterization

---

## 1.5 Dynamic (Oscillatory) Tests — The Viscoelastic Probe

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** In **dynamic mechanical testing**, a small-amplitude sinusoidal strain (or stress) is imposed and the resulting stress (or strain) response is analysed. The sample experiences oscillatory shear and the material's **storage modulus (G′)** and **loss modulus (G″)** are obtained, characterising its solid-like and liquid-like components respectively.

### Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Jello Shaking Test:** Gently shake a bowl of set gelatin. The jello deforms and **recovers** — the energy you put in is partly **stored** (elastic) and partly **dissipated** as heat (viscous). The ratio of these two is a fingerprint of the material's viscoelasticity, captured by G′ and G″.

---

## 1.6 Force–Deformation & Stress–Strain Behaviour (Basic & Bulk)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Force–Deformation Behaviour** describes how a material responds to an applied **force** through **shape change (deformation)**. When normalised by area and original length, this becomes the **stress–strain relationship**.
>
> - **Basic (Engineering) Stress–Strain:** σ = F/A₀ vs ε = ΔL/L₀ (uniaxial)
> - **Bulk Stress–Strain:** describes **volumetric** deformation under **hydrostatic (isostatic) pressure** — relevant to compression, texture, and bulk modulus in foods.

### Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Sponge:** Pull a sponge in tension (basic stress–strain) — it stretches elastically. Now squeeze it in your fist (bulk stress–strain) — water is expelled and volume changes. Foods undergo both kinds of deformation during chewing, processing, and storage.

---

## 1.7 Elastic, Plastic, and Viscoelastic Behaviour

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Elastic (Hookean) Behaviour:** σ = E·ε — fully recoverable, instantaneous, energy stored.
> - **Plastic Behaviour:** Permanent, non-recoverable deformation once a **yield stress (σ_y)** is exceeded.
> - **Viscoelastic Behaviour:** Time-dependent combination of elastic and viscous responses — foods almost always fall here.

### Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Three Material Personalities:** Elastic = a **rubber ball** (always bounces back). Plastic = **wet clay** (stays where you push it). Viscoelastic = **bread dough** (springs back partially, but if you wait, it slowly flows). A food scientist's task is to measure the *proportion* of each personality in a real food.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constitutive Equations of Newtonian & Non-Newtonian Fluids

### 2.1.1 Newtonian Fluid (Recap)
The shear stress is **linearly** proportional to shear rate:

$$\tau_{xy} = -\eta \cdot \frac{dv_x}{dy}$$

For steady simple shear, this reduces to:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

- **Why linear?** In Newtonian fluids, momentum transfer between layers occurs purely through **molecular collisions**; no structural change with shear.
- **How to use:** A single number (η) completely characterises the fluid. Plot of τ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a **straight line through the origin**.

### 2.1.2 Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Model — Foundation of Shear-Dependent Viscosity
Many non-Newtonian foods are described by:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

- $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = flow behaviour index (dimensionless)
- **n = 1** → Newtonian (K = η)
- **n < 1** → **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**
- **n > 1** → **Dilatant (shear-thickening)**

The **apparent viscosity** is:

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does shear-thinning occur in food?** Long-chain polymers (starch, pectin, proteins) align with the flow direction as shear increases, reducing intermolecular entanglement drag → η falls. In contrast, dilatant suspensions have particles that **interlock and jam** under shear (e.g., dense cornstarch + water).

### 2.1.3 Bingham Plastic Model
A fluid that behaves as a rigid body below the **yield stress (σ_y)** and flows linearly above it:

$$\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}, \quad \text{when } \tau \geq \tau_y$$

- $\tau_y$ = yield stress (Pa)
- $\eta_p$ = plastic viscosity (Pa·s)

### 2.1.4 Herschel–Bulkley Model (Generalised Plastic)
Combines yield stress with power-law behaviour:

$$\tau = \tau_y + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** The Herschel–Bulkley model is the **most-used generalised equation** for foods exhibiting both a yield stress and shear-thinning (e.g., chocolate, margarine, mustard).

### 2.1.5 Casson Model
$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\eta_c \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$

Commonly used for **chocolate melts** and **blood**.

### 2.1.6 Time-Dependent Fluids

**Thixotropic (Structure Breakdown):**
- At constant $\dot{\gamma}$, $\eta_{app}(t)$ **decreases** with time (structure breaks down)
- At rest, structure rebuilds
- Loop: **up-curve** (increasing $\dot{\gamma}$) and **down-curve** (decreasing $\dot{\gamma}$) form a **hysteresis loop**

**Rheopectic (Structure Build-up):**
- At constant $\dot{\gamma}$, $\eta_{app}(t)$ **increases** with time
- Rare in foods; seen in some starch pastes and protein gels

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the hysteresis loop?** It represents the **kinetic energy barrier** for structural breakdown (thixotropy) or build-up (rheopexy). The loop area is a measure of the **time-dependent structural change**.

---

## 2.2 Creep Theory — Step-Stress Response

When a **constant shear stress σ₀** is applied at $t = 0$:

$$\gamma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot J(t)$$

where $J(t)$ = **creep compliance** (Pa⁻¹).

The classical 4-stage creep curve consists of:

1. **Instantaneous elastic strain** $\gamma_1 = \sigma_0 / G_0$ (glassy elastic response)
2. **Retarded elastic (delayed) strain** — Kelvin–Voigt-like recovery
3. **Steady-state viscous flow** $\dot{\gamma}_{ss} = \sigma_0 / \eta_0$
4. **Recovery (after unloading):** instantaneous recovery + retarded recovery + permanent viscous strain

The **Burgers model** captures all four stages:

$$J(t) = \frac{1}{G_0} + \frac{1}{G_1}\left(1 - e^{-t/\lambda}\right) + \frac{t}{\eta_0}$$

with retardation time $\lambda = \eta_1 / G_1$.

---

## 2.3 Stress Relaxation Theory — Step-Strain Response

When a **constant strain γ₀** is applied at $t = 0$:

$$\sigma(t) = \gamma_0 \cdot G(t)$$

where $G(t)$ = **stress relaxation modulus** (Pa).

A simple **Maxwell model** gives exponential decay:

$$G(t) = G_0 \cdot e^{-t/\lambda}, \quad \lambda = \eta / G_0$$

The **Generalised Maxwell (Wiechert) model** is a parallel sum:

$$G(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i \cdot e^{-t/\lambda_i}$$

where each branch has its own relaxation time $\lambda_i = \eta_i / G_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Creep and stress relaxation give *complementary* information but are **not interchangeable** for nonlinear materials. The **Boltzmann superposition principle** relates them through convolution integrals — but only for **linear viscoelastic** materials.

---

## 2.4 Dynamic (Oscillatory) Test Theory

A sinusoidal strain $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ produces a stress:

$$\sigma(t) = G'(\omega)\gamma_0 \sin(\omega t) + G''(\omega)\gamma_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

Equivalently, in complex form:

$$\sigma^*(t) = G^*(\omega) \cdot \gamma^*(t)$$

where $G^* = G' + iG''$ is the **complex modulus**.

| Quantity | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage modulus** | $G'(\omega) = \vert G^* \vert \cos\delta$ | Elastic (energy stored) |
| **Loss modulus** | $G''(\omega) = \vert G^* \vert \sin\delta$ | Viscous (energy dissipated) |
| **Complex modulus** | $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}$ | Total stiffness |
| **Loss tangent** | $\tan\delta = G'' / G'$ | Damping ratio |
| **Complex viscosity** | $\eta^* = G^* / (i\omega)$ | Frequency-dependent viscosity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in food:** A weak gel (e.g., yogurt) shows $G' > G''$ but both are frequency-dependent; a strong gel (firm tofu) shows $G' \gg G''$ with little frequency dependence.

---

## 2.5 Force–Deformation & Stress–Strain Theory

### Basic (Engineering) Stress–Strain
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}, \quad \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ (linear elastic)
**Newton's Law:** $\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$ (linear viscous)

**Elastic Limit / Yield Point:** stress above which permanent deformation occurs.

### Bulk (Volumetric) Stress–Strain
The **bulk modulus (K)** describes resistance to uniform compression:

$$K = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = \frac{\sigma_{hydro}}{\Delta V / V_0}$$

- **Compressibility:** $\beta = 1/K$
- Foods typically have $\beta$ in the range 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻¹⁰ Pa⁻¹
- Relevant to **high-pressure food processing (HPP)** of juices, meats, seafood.

### Elastic–Plastic Behaviour
Beyond yield stress $\sigma_y$, the material flows plastically. The **flow curve** has distinct regions:
1. **Elastic region** (σ < σ_y)
2. **Yield point** (σ = σ_y)
3. **Plastic flow region** (σ > σ_y, permanent deformation)

In food texture: biting into a cracker — elastic deformation, then brittle fracture (an extreme form of plasticity).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All key formulas in a single reference table. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ to denote absolute value (do **NOT** use the pipe `|` character that breaks markdown tables).

| # | Formula | Symbols | Unit | Applies to |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | τ: stress, η: viscosity | Pa, Pa·s, s⁻¹ | Newtonian fluids |
| 2 | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | K: consistency, n: flow index | Pa·sⁿ, — | Power-law fluids |
| 3 | $\eta_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Apparent viscosity | Pa·s | Power-law fluids |
| 4 | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | τ_y: yield, η_p: plastic visc. | Pa, Pa·s | Bingham plastic |
| 5 | $\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Herschel–Bulkley | Pa, Pa·sⁿ, — | Many food pastes |
| 6 | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\eta_c \dot{\gamma}}$ | Casson model | Pa, Pa·s | Chocolate, blood |
| 7 | $J(t) = \gamma(t)/\sigma_0$ | Creep compliance | Pa⁻¹ | Creep test |
| 8 | $G(t) = \sigma(t)/\gamma_0$ | Relaxation modulus | Pa | Stress relaxation |
| 9 | $G(t) = G_0 e^{-t/\lambda}$ | Maxwell decay | Pa, s | Single-mode Maxwell |
| 10 | $\lambda = \eta / G$ | Relaxation / retardation time | s | Viscoelastic models |
| 11 | $G' = \vert G^* \vert \cos\delta$ | Storage modulus | Pa | Dynamic test |
| 12 | $G'' = \vert G^* \vert \sin\delta$ | Loss modulus | Pa | Dynamic test |
| 13 | $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ | Loss tangent | — | Dynamic test |
| 14 | $\eta^* = G^*/(i\omega)$ | Complex viscosity | Pa·s | Dynamic test |
| 15 | $Q = \pi r^4 \Delta P \Delta t / (8 \eta L)$ | Capillary flow (Hagen–Poiseuille) | m³ | Viscometry |
| 16 | $\eta = \pi r^4 \Delta P t / (8 L V)$ | Viscosity from capillary | Pa·s | Capillary viscometer |
| 17 | $\sigma = F/A_0$ | Engineering stress | Pa | Force–deformation |
| 18 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering strain | — | Force–deformation |
| 19 | $K = -V \, dP/dV$ | Bulk modulus | Pa | Bulk stress–strain |
| 20 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Young's ↔ Shear modulus | Pa | Elasticity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Mooney–Rivlin** and **Ogden** hyperelastic models extend Hooke's law to large deformations; the **Drucker–Prager** yield criterion extends von Mises to granular foods. In food processing, K is critical for designing high-pressure pasteurisation, while G′/G″ characterise mouthfeel and spreadability.
