<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Capillary Viscometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pressure Source: Nitrogen Cylinder / Pneumatic Regulator] --> B[Reservoir of Test Fluid: Melted Chocolate / Syrup / Oil]
    B --> C[Inlet Manometer: Measures P1]
    C --> D[Capillary Test Section: Radius R, Length L, Slender tube]
    D --> E[Outlet Manometer: Measures P2]
    E --> F[Flow Collection: Graduated Cylinder / Electronic Balance]
    F --> G[Data Logger: Records Q vs Time, Computes Q and deltaP]
    G --> H[Computation Engine: tau_w, gamma_a, gamma_w, n_prime, K]
    H --> I[Flow Curve Output: tau_w vs gamma_w on log log axes]
    I --> J{Fluid Type?}
    J -->|Newtonian| K[Single point viscosity mu = tau_w / gamma_w]
    J -->|Non Newtonian| L[Apply Rabinowitsch Mooney Correction]
    L --> M[Fit Power Law tau = K gamma to the n]
    M --> N[Engineering Decision: Pumping Power, Die Design]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Measurement Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Clean and dry capillary] --> S2[Step 2: Soak capillary in test fluid, thermostat at T]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply preset pressure drop deltaP]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Allow steady state, then start timer]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Collect volume V in time t, compute Q = V slash t]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compute tau_w and gamma_a]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Repeat at multiple deltaP values, at least 5 to 7]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Plot log tau_w vs log gamma_a, find slope n prime]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Apply Rabinowitsch Mooney to convert to true gamma_w]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Report flow curve and K and n to process team]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — End-Effect (Bagley) Correction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw deltaP from capillary experiment] --> R2[Contains viscous loss plus entry plus exit loss]
    R2 --> R3[Run at least 3 capillaries of same R but different L]
    R3 --> R4[Plot deltaP vs L slash R for fixed gamma_a]
    R4 --> R5[Straight line: intercept at L slash R equals 0 is the Bagley end pressure deltaP_end]
    R5 --> R6[Corrected wall stress: tau_w_corr = deltaP_minus_deltaP_end times R over 2L]
    R6 --> R7[Use tau_w_corr as the true wall shear stress for the food material]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Annotation — Slit Die Viscometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U1[Hydraulic Ram pushes molten food] --> U2[Converging entry channel: angle less than 45 degrees]
    U2 --> U3[Test section slit: width w, half gap h, length L]
    U3 --> U4[Pressure transducers mounted flush at the wall, separated by L]
    U4 --> U5[Exit die: product stream, weighed for Q]
    U5 --> U6[Compute tau_w = deltaP times h over L]
    U6 --> U7[Compute gamma_a = 6Q over w h squared]
    U7 --> U8[True gamma_w from Rabinowitsch Mooney with log slope n prime]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight from the diagrams:**
> The architecture above shows that every commercial capillary rheometer is essentially a *pump + pipe + scale* combination, with the math layered on top. The simplicity is the source of its precision: there are no moving seals, no rotating shafts, and no thermal gradients in the test section — making it the gold standard for absolute viscosity measurement of food fluids and melts.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between a *capillary viscometer* and an *efflux cup* as pressure-driven viscometers used in food quality control. State one typical food application of each. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A capillary viscometer (e.g., Ostwald, Cannon–Fenske, Melt Flow Indexer) is an *absolute* instrument — viscosity is computed from the Hagen–Poiseuille equation using the measured $\Delta P$, $Q$, $R$, and $L$. Efflux cups (Ford, Zahn, Redwood) are *relative* instruments — the efflux time of a fixed volume through a calibrated orifice is compared against a standard chart; they do not give $\tau_w$ or $\dot{\gamma}_w$ in absolute terms.

- *Capillary:* Measurement of viscosity index of refined oils, sugar syrups, and clear beverages.
- *Efflux cup:* Quick on-line QC of paint/coating consistency, sauce viscosity at the bottling line.

**(3 marks: 1 for classification, 1 for absolute vs. relative distinction, 1 for the example.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**

**Q2.** State the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** and clearly define every term. Why is this equation valid *only* for laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a *horizontal* tube? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $R$ = capillary radius (m)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the test section (Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $L$ = length of test section (m)

Validity conditions:
- **Laminar** ($Re < 2100$) — the derivation assumes a stable, non-turbulent parabolic profile.
- **Newtonian** — the linear relation $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ was used in step 3 of the derivation.
- **Horizontal** — eliminates gravitational body-force terms; for a vertical tube, replace $\Delta P$ by $\Delta P - \rho g L$.

**(3 marks: 1.5 for the equation + definitions, 1.5 for the three validity conditions.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU ESE — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q.4(a) / 4(b) Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Starting from a differential force balance on a cylindrical plug of fluid inside a capillary tube, derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Assumptions** (1 mark):
   - Steady, laminar, incompressible, isothermal flow
   - Newtonian fluid ($\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$)
   - Horizontal tube (no gravity)
   - Fully developed, axisymmetric flow
   - No-slip at the wall

2. **Differential force balance** (2 marks):
   $$\Delta P \, \pi r^{2} = \tau(r) \, 2 \pi r L \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{2L}$$

3. **Integrate using Newtonian constitutive equation** (1.5 marks):
   $$\mu \frac{dv}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \, r}{2L} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$

4. **Integrate to obtain $Q$** (1.5 marks):
   $$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v(r) \, 2 \pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

5. **Wall stress / wall rate** (1 mark):
   $$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P R}{2L}, \quad \dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}, \quad \mu = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_w}$$

---

**(b)** A **tomato ketchup** sample is tested in a capillary viscometer with $R = 1.0$ mm, $L = 50$ mm, at $\Delta P = 5$ kPa, $Q = 12$ mL/min. A second experiment at $\Delta P = 10$ kPa gives $Q = 28$ mL/min. Assuming the Rabinowitsch–Mooney equation applies, find: (i) the power-law index $n$, (ii) the consistency coefficient $K$, and (iii) the **apparent viscosity** at 200 s⁻¹. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Apparent shear rate and shear stress at both points:**

Point 1: $Q_{1} = 12$ mL/min $= 2 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s, $\Delta P_{1} = 5000$ Pa.

$$\dot{\gamma}_{a,1} = \frac{4Q_{1}}{\pi R^{3}} = \frac{4(2 \times 10^{-7})}{\pi(10^{-3})^{3}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-7}}{3.142 \times 10^{-9}} = 254.6 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\tau_{w,1} = \frac{\Delta P_{1} R}{2L} = \frac{5000 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.05} = 50 \text{ Pa}$$

Point 2: $Q_{2} = 28$ mL/min $= 4.667 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s, $\Delta P_{2} = 10000$ Pa.

$$\dot{\gamma}_{a,2} = \frac{4(4.667 \times 10^{-7})}{3.142 \times 10^{-9}} = 594.0 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\tau_{w,2} = \frac{10000 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1} = 100 \text{ Pa}$$

(ii) **Power-law index** (slope of $\log \tau_w$ vs. $\log \dot{\gamma}_a$):

$$n' = \frac{\log(100/50)}{\log(594.0/254.6)} = \frac{\log 2}{\log 2.333} = \frac{0.3010}{0.3680} = 0.818$$

(iii) **Consistency coefficient** (using the *true* $\dot{\gamma}_w$):

First compute true wall shear rate for, say, Point 1:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w,1} = 254.6 \times \frac{3(0.818)+1}{4(0.818)} = 254.6 \times \frac{3.454}{3.272} = 268.7 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$K = \frac{\tau_{w,1}}{\dot{\gamma}_{w,1}^{\,n'}} = \frac{50}{(268.7)^{0.818}} = \frac{50}{108.2} \approx 0.462 \text{ Pa·s}^{0.818}$$

(iv) **Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 200$ s⁻¹:**

$$\tau_{200} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n} = 0.462 \times 200^{0.818} = 0.462 \times 85.5 \approx 39.5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_{200}}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{39.5}{200} = 0.1975 \text{ Pa·s} \approx 197.5 \text{ cP}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii) + Rabinowitsch, 1 for K, 2 for the apparent viscosity at 200 s⁻¹.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with a neat functional diagram the **working principle of a pressure-driven capillary viscometer**. List the major sources of experimental error and how the **Bagley end correction** and the **Rabinowitsch–Mooney correction** are applied. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- *Functional diagram description* (3 marks): A pressure source drives fluid from a reservoir through a calibrated capillary of radius $R$ and length $L$. Inlet and outlet manometers record $P_1$ and $P_2$. The exit stream is collected in a measuring cylinder or weighed on an electronic balance to give $Q$. A thermostat jacket maintains isothermal conditions.
- *Sources of error* (1.5 marks): kinetic-energy loss at the inlet (Couette correction), viscous energy dissipation at the contraction (Bagley end loss), temperature drift, wall slip in highly shear-thinning foods, capillary not being long enough (entrance length not negligible, $L_e \approx 0.06 \, Re \, D$).
- *Bagley correction* (1.5 marks): Re-run the experiment with at least 3 capillaries of same $R$ but different $L$. Plot $\Delta P$ vs. $L/R$ at fixed $\dot{\gamma}_a$. The straight-line intercept at $L/R = 0$ is $\Delta P_{end}$. Replace $\Delta P$ with $\Delta P - \Delta P_{end}$ in the $\tau_w$ formula.
- *Rabinowitsch–Mooney correction* (1 mark): Multiply the apparent shear rate by $(3n'+1)/(4n')$, where $n'$ is the local log-slope of $\tau_w$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}_a$.

---

**(b)** A **high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS)** of density 1300 kg/m³ flows under gravity through a vertical capillary viscometer of length $L = 200$ mm and radius $R = 0.75$ mm. The pressure head is $h = 0.25$ m of syrup. The collected volume in 60 s is 8.0 mL. Determine: (i) the dynamic viscosity, (ii) the kinematic viscosity, (iii) the average velocity, and (iv) the Reynolds number. Is the flow laminar? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $\rho = 1300$ kg/m³, $L = 0.2$ m, $R = 0.75 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $h = 0.25$ m, $Q = 8.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ / 60 s = $1.333 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s.

**Effective pressure drop** (gravity head, vertical tube):

$$\Delta P = \rho g h = 1300 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 = 3188.25 \text{ Pa}$$

(i) **Dynamic viscosity** (Hagen–Poiseuille rearranged):

$$\mu = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 L Q} = \frac{\pi (0.75 \times 10^{-3})^{4} \times 3188.25}{8 \times 0.2 \times 1.333 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$
\mu = \frac{\pi \times 3.164 \times 10^{-10} \times 3188.25}{2.133 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{3.169 \times 10^{-6}}{2.133 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 14.86 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

(ii) **Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{14.86}{1300} = 0.01143 \text{ m}^{2}\text{/s} = 1.143 \times 10^{4} \text{ cSt}$$

(iii) **Average velocity:**

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{1.333 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi (0.75 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} = \frac{1.333 \times 10^{-7}}{1.767 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.0754 \text{ m/s}$$

(iv) **Reynolds number** ($D = 2R = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m):

$$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu} = \frac{1300 \times 0.0754 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{14.86} = \frac{0.1470}{14.86} \approx 0.00989$$

**Laminar check:** $Re \approx 9.9 \times 10^{-3} \ll 2100$ → **definitely laminar**. The Hagen–Poiseuille assumption is fully valid.

**(7 marks: 2 for μ, 1 each for ν, v̄, Re, 1 for the laminar commentary + gravity-head recognition.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in Pressure-Driven Viscometer problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $Q$** from mL/min to m³/s — silent unit error that *crashes* the answer by a factor of $6 \times 10^{7}$. Always write $Q$ in SI before plugging in.
> 2. **Mixing up the $4Q/(\pi R^{3})$ (apparent, Newtonian) and $(3n+1)/(4n) \times \dot{\gamma}_a$ (true) shear rates.** Examiners will give **zero** for the consistency coefficient $K$ if the uncorrected $\dot{\gamma}_a$ is used for a non-Newtonian food.
> 3. **Omitting the gravity term** when the capillary is vertical. $\Delta P$ in Hagen–Poiseuille must be the *net* pressure drop (applied pressure + $\rho g h \cos\theta$).
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** (laminar, Newtonian, fully-developed, no-slip) — these carry 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Plugging numbers in directly** without a logical line of derivation. KTU 2024 valuation rewards *method* as much as the *number*.
> 6. **Confusing Poise, cP, Pa·s** in the final answer. 1 Pa·s = 10 Poise = 1000 cP.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Hagen–Poiseuille:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ — Newton, laminar, horizontal tube.
- ✅ **Wall shear stress (capillary):** $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R}{2L}$.
- ✅ **Apparent wall shear rate (capillary):** $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$.
- ✅ **Wall shear stress (slit die):** $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, h}{L}$ (where $h$ is half-gap).
- ✅ **Apparent wall shear rate (slit die):** $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{6Q}{w h^{2}}$.
- ✅ **Apparent viscosity:** $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a}$.
- ✅ **Rabinowitsch–Mooney (true) shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a \cdot \dfrac{3n'+1}{4n'}$.
- ✅ **Power-law index from log-slope:** $n' = \dfrac{d(\log \tau_w)}{d(\log \dot{\gamma}_a)}$.
- ✅ **Consistency coefficient:** $K = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_w^{n'}}$.
- ✅ **Bagley end correction** uses *multiple* capillary lengths to subtract $\Delta P_{end}$ — essential for short dies and polymer melts.
- ✅ **Laminar flow criterion:** $Re_{cr} \approx 2100$ for circular tubes; verify before applying Hagen–Poiseuille.
- ✅ **Unit conversions to remember:** 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP; 1 St = 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.
- ✅ **Gravity-head correction for vertical capillary:** $\Delta P_{eff} = \Delta P_{applied} - \rho g L \cos\theta$.
- ✅ **Typical food examples tested by capillary rheometry:** chocolate melts, sugar syrups, edible oils, fruit juices, ketchup, melted cheese, dough extrudates, ice-cream mixes.
- ✅ **Pressure-driven viscometers are *absolute* — no calibration fluid needed**, unlike most rotational viscometers.
- ✅ **Capillary rheometer is the *gold standard* for high-shear rheology** of food fluids and is also the closest analogue to industrial extrusion dies.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Pressure-Driven Flow Viscometers**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capillary Tubes and Falling Sphere Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capillary Tubes and Falling Sphere Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Capillary Tube Viscometer (Capillary Rheometry)

**Formal Definition:**
A **capillary tube viscometer** (also called a capillary rheometer) is a fundamental shear-rate-controlled rheological instrument that measures the **viscosity** of a fluid by forcing it through a tube of known radius $R$ (or diameter $D$) and length $L$ under a precisely known pressure drop $\Delta P$. It operates on the **Hagen–Poiseuille principle** of laminar, fully-developed, steady, incompressible flow through a cylindrical geometry. It is one of the oldest, simplest, and most widely-used absolute viscometers for both Newtonian and non-Newtonian (especially polymer melts and concentrated food pastes) fluids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Capillary rheometry is classified as an **absolute viscometer** because viscosity can be computed *directly* from first principles (force–momentum balance) without prior calibration against a standard fluid. This is the single most important distinction students must remember for Part A and Part B answers.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine pushing honey through a narrow drinking straw using a syringe. The narrower the straw and the longer it is, the more force (pressure) you must apply. If you measure how much pressure is needed to push a known volume in a known time, you have essentially built a capillary viscometer. **Thick fluids resist flow** — the measured pressure drop is mathematically tied to the fluid's internal friction, which is its viscosity. For food systems, this mimics how molten chocolate, tomato ketchup, or fruit puree behave when extruded through pipes in industrial processing lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
> - Pi: $\pi = 3.14159...$
> - For SI units, viscosity $\eta$ is reported in **Pa·s** (Pascal-seconds) or equivalently **kg/(m·s)**. The older CGS unit is the **poise (P)** where $1 \, \text{P} = 0.1 \, \text{Pa·s}$.

---

### 1.2 Falling Sphere Viscometer

**Formal Definition:**
A **falling sphere viscometer** is a gravity-driven rheological instrument that determines the **viscosity** of a fluid by measuring the **terminal (steady-state) settling velocity** $v_t$ of a rigid sphere of known radius $r_s$ (and density $\rho_s$) as it descends through the fluid (density $\rho_f$) contained in a vertical tube of diameter $D$. The instrument is governed by **Stokes' Law of hydrodynamic resistance** in the low-Reynolds-number regime (creeping flow).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Drop a small steel ball into a tall cylinder of honey and time how long it takes to travel a marked distance. The *slower* the ball falls, the *thicker* the honey. The *faster* it falls, the *thinner* the fluid. This is exactly what a falling sphere viscometer does — it converts **gravitational settling time** into a **viscosity number**. This is widely used for opaque fluids (e.g., chocolate, molasses, starch suspensions) where optical capillary instruments fail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Governing Assumption:** The sphere must be moving in the **laminar (creeping) flow regime**, which requires the **Reynolds number** $Re = \dfrac{2 r_s \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\eta} \;<\; 0.1$ (or strictly $Re < 1$ for the linear Stokes regime to hold within acceptable error).

---

### 1.3 Comparison at a Glance

| Feature | Capillary Tube | Falling Sphere |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Applied pressure | Gravity |
| Flow type | Pressure-driven, fully developed | Free settling |
| Governing law | Hagen–Poiseuille | Stokes' Law |
| Best for | Polymer melts, oils, syrups | Opaque Newtonian fluids |
| Shear rate range | Wide (controlled) | Narrow (low) |
| Absolute? | **Yes** | **Yes (with corrections)** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Capillary Tube Viscometer — Operational Logic

The fluid is placed in a reservoir and pushed (or drawn by suction) through a precision-bored cylindrical capillary tube. The instrument measures:
- The **volumetric flow rate** $Q$ (or volume $V$ collected in time $t$)
- The **pressure drop** $\Delta P$ across a length $L$ of the capillary

From these, the wall shear stress and wall shear rate can be obtained without any calibration.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element** of radius $r$ inside the tube yields a parabolic velocity profile (for a Newtonian fluid).
2. The **velocity profile** is $v(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)$.
3. Integrating this profile over the cross-section gives the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** relating flow rate to viscosity.
4. The shear stress varies linearly from zero at the centerline to a maximum at the wall: $\tau(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{2L} r$.
5. Therefore, the **wall shear stress** $\tau_w$ (at $r = R$) is $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L}$.
6. The **apparent wall shear rate** (for Newtonian assumption) is $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$.

**Real-world utility:** Used in dairy industry for milk standardization, in chocolate manufacturing to verify Casson plastic viscosity, and in polymer extrusion for melt flow index (MFI) determination.

---

### 2.2 Falling Sphere Viscometer — Operational Logic

A sphere of density higher than the fluid is released in a vertical tube containing the test liquid. Three forces act on the sphere:

1. **Gravitational force** (downward): $F_g = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_s^3 \rho_s \, g$
2. **Buoyancy force** (upward): $F_b = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_s^3 \rho_f \, g$
3. **Viscous drag (Stokes drag)** (upward, opposing motion): $F_d = 6 \pi \eta r_s v$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The sphere accelerates initially, but the drag force increases with velocity.
2. At **terminal velocity** $v_t$, the net force becomes zero: $F_g = F_b + F_d$.
3. Solving this equilibrium gives Stokes' viscosity formula.
4. The **Reynolds number** must be checked to confirm creeping flow.
5. **Wall corrections** (Faxén's law) are applied because in a finite-diameter tube the drag is higher than in an infinite medium.
6. **End corrections** account for the finite length of the measurement zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wall Effect (Faxén) Correction:** For a sphere of diameter $d$ falling in a tube of diameter $D$, the corrected viscosity is:
> $$\eta_{\text{true}} = \eta_{\text{meas}} \left[ 1 - 2.104 \left(\frac{d}{D}\right) + 2.09 \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^3 - 0.95 \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^5 \right]^{-1}$$
> If $d/D < 0.1$, the correction is often neglected in KTU-level problems.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are High-Yield — memorize these for KTU ESE.**

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units (SI) | Validity / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hagen–Poiseuille (Newtonian viscosity) | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | Absolute; $V$ = volume in time $t$ |
| 2 | Volumetric flow rate form | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 L Q}$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | Equivalent to #1 |
| 3 | Wall shear stress (capillary) | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Geometry-only, no rheology assumed |
| 4 | Wall shear rate, Newtonian | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Assumes Newtonian |
| 5 | Rabinowitsch (Power-law) | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \dfrac{8 V}{D \, t} = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \dfrac{8 \bar{v}}{D}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | For Ostwald-de Waele fluid |
| 6 | Apparent viscosity | $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_w}$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | Used at any shear rate |
| 7 | Stokes drag force | $F_d = 6 \pi \eta r_s v$ | $\text{N}$ | Creeping flow only |
| 8 | Stokes terminal velocity | $v_t = \dfrac{2 r_s^2 \, g \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 \eta}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Steady-state |
| 9 | Falling-sphere viscosity | $\eta = \dfrac{2 r_s^2 \, g \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 \, v_t}$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | Absolute, infinite medium |
| 10 | Reynolds number check | $Re = \dfrac{2 r_s \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\eta}$ | dimensionless | Require $Re < 0.1$ (or $< 1$) |
| 11 | Faxén wall correction | $\eta_{\text{true}} = \eta_{\text{meas}} \cdot K^{-1}$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | $K = 1 - 2.104(d/D) + 2.09(d/D)^3 - 0.95(d/D)^5$ |
| 12 | End-effect correction (Faxén) | $\eta_{\text{true}} = \eta_{\text{meas}} \left(1 + \alpha \dfrac{r_s}{h}\right)$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | $h$ = fall distance; $\alpha \approx 2.1$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Capillary Viscometer)

**Starting assumptions (Newtonian, steady, laminar, fully-developed, isothermal, incompressible flow in a horizontal circular tube of radius $R$ and length $L$):**

Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$, length $dx$, coaxial with the tube. A force balance on this element in the axial ($x$) direction gives:

$$(P \cdot \pi r^2)_{x} - (P \cdot \pi r^2)_{x+dx} - \tau \cdot (2\pi r \, dx) = 0$$

Step 1: Expand the pressure terms using a Taylor series around position $x$:

$$P(x+dx) = P(x) + \frac{dP}{dx} dx + \frac{1}{2} \frac{d^2 P}{dx^2} (dx)^2 + \dots$$

Step 2: Substitute and divide by $\pi r \, dx$:

$$-r \frac{dP}{dx} - 2 \tau = 0$$

Step 3: Solve for the local shear stress at radius $r$:

$$\tau(r) = -\frac{r}{2} \frac{dP}{dx} = \frac{r \cdot \Delta P}{2 L}$$

(The negative sign cancels because $dP/dx$ is negative in the flow direction, since $P$ decreases along the flow.)

Step 4: For a Newtonian fluid, $\tau = -\eta \dfrac{dv}{dr}$ (the negative sign because velocity decreases with increasing $r$). Equating:

$$-\eta \frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{r \, \Delta P}{2 L}$$

Step 5: Integrate with respect to $r$ from $r$ to $R$, where $v(R) = 0$ (no-slip at the wall):

$$\int_{v(r)}^{0} dv = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \eta L} \int_{r}^{R} r' \, dr'$$

$$-v(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \eta L} \left[ \frac{R^2 - r^2}{2} \right]$$

Step 6: Final parabolic velocity profile:

$$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)$$

Step 7: Compute the volumetric flow rate $Q$ by integrating the velocity over the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \eta L} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr$$

Step 8: Evaluate the integral:

$$\int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr = \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{R^4}{4}$$

Step 9: Substituting back:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P \, R^4}{8 \eta L}$$

Step 10: Rearrange for viscosity (the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation**):

$$\eta = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 L Q} = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$$

where $Q = V/t$ is the volumetric flow rate and $V$ is the volume collected in time $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the **fourth-power dependence on radius** $R^4$. A small error in radius measurement produces a huge error in computed viscosity — this is why precision capillaries are expensive and diameter tolerance is critical.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Rabinowitsch Equation (for Power-Law Non-Newtonian Fluids)

**Starting assumption:** The fluid follows the Ostwald–de Waele (power-law) model:

$$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n \quad \text{where} \quad \dot{\gamma} = \left(-\frac{dv}{dr}\right)$$

Step 1: From the force balance, the local shear stress is the same as derived above:

$$\tau(r) = \frac{r \, \Delta P}{2 L}$$

Step 2: Equate to the power-law expression and solve for the velocity gradient:

$$K \left(-\frac{dv}{dr}\right)^n = \frac{r \, \Delta P}{2 L}$$

$$-\frac{dv}{dr} = \left( \frac{\Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} r^{1/n}$$

Step 3: Integrate from $r$ to $R$ (with $v(R)=0$):

$$v(r) = \left( \frac{\Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} \frac{n}{n+1} \left( R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n} \right)$$

Step 4: Compute the average (bulk) velocity $\bar{v} = Q / (\pi R^2)$:

$$\bar{v} = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Step 5: After evaluation (using the substitution $u = r/R$):

$$\bar{v} = \left( \frac{\Delta P}{2 K L} \right)^{1/n} R \cdot \frac{n}{3n+1}$$

Step 6: Solve for the wall shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_w$ by recognizing $\tau_w = K \dot{\gamma}_w^n$ and using the bulk velocity:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \frac{8 \bar{v}}{D} = \frac{3n+1}{n} \cdot \frac{Q}{\pi R^3}$$

Step 7: The final **Rabinowitsch–Mooney equation** in KTU form:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **Newtonian fluid** ($n=1$), this reduces correctly to $\dot{\gamma}_w = 32Q/(\pi D^3)$ — confirming the formula is consistent.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Stokes' Law & Falling-Sphere Viscosity

**Starting assumptions:** A rigid sphere of radius $r_s$ settles under gravity through an infinite, quiescent, Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\eta$, in the creeping-flow regime ($Re \to 0$).

Step 1: At **terminal velocity** $v_t$, the net force is zero. The three forces are:

$$F_{\text{net}} = F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

Step 2: Write each force explicitly:

$$F_g = \frac{4}{3} \pi r_s^3 \rho_s \, g \quad ; \quad F_b = \frac{4}{3} \pi r_s^3 \rho_f \, g \quad ; \quad F_d = 6 \pi \eta r_s v_t$$

Step 3: Substitute into the force balance:

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r_s^3 \rho_s g - \frac{4}{3} \pi r_s^3 \rho_f g - 6 \pi \eta r_s v_t = 0$$

Step 4: Factor the gravitational and buoyancy terms:

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r_s^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) = 6 \pi \eta r_s v_t$$

Step 5: Simplify — one $r_s$ cancels from each side:

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r_s^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) = 6 \eta v_t$$

Step 6: Solve for viscosity:

$$\eta = \frac{2 r_s^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}$$

Step 7: Recognizing the sphere diameter $d = 2 r_s$, this can be rewritten:

$$\eta = \frac{d^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 v_t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical KTU Numerical Trick:** If the fall time $t$ over a distance $h$ is measured, then $v_t = h/t$, giving:
> $$\eta = \frac{2 r_s^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) \, t}{9 h}$$
> This is the most exam-friendly form.

---

### 3.4 Reynolds Number Verification (Sanity Check)

To confirm Stokes' Law is valid, the Reynolds number based on sphere diameter is computed:

$$Re = \frac{2 r_s \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\eta} = \frac{\rho_f \, d \, v_t}{\eta}$$

If $Re < 0.1$ (strict) or $Re < 1$ (engineering), the formula is valid without further inertial corrections. For $Re > 1$, the Oseen or Schiller–Naumann corrections must be applied.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A capillary tube viscometer of diameter $D = 1.0 \, \text{mm}$ and length $L = 0.50 \, \text{m}$ is used to measure the viscosity of a Newtonian edible oil. A volume $V = 10 \, \text{cm}^3$ is collected in $t = 90 \, \text{s}$ under a pressure drop $\Delta P = 5000 \, \text{Pa}$. Compute (a) the wall shear stress, (b) the wall shear rate, and (c) the viscosity.

**Solution:**

Given: $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$, $L = 0.50 \, \text{m}$, $\Delta P = 5000 \, \text{Pa}$, $V = 10 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^3 = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3$, $t = 90 \, \text{s}$.

Step 1: Volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}{90} = 1.111 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Step 2: Wall shear stress:

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L} = \frac{5000 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 0.50} = 2.5 \, \text{Pa}$$

Step 3: Wall shear rate (Newtonian):

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3} = \frac{32 \times 1.111 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^3} = \frac{3.555 \times 10^{-6}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-9}} = 1131.5 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

Step 4: Viscosity:

$$\eta = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_w} = \frac{2.5}{1131.5} = 2.21 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} = 2.21 \, \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result is consistent with typical edible oils (e.g., sunflower oil $\approx 50 \, \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ at low shear, refined oils $\approx 2\text{–}10 \, \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$). The high wall shear rate ($1131 \, \text{s}^{-1}$) is typical of industrial flow conditions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Capillary Tube Viscometer — Functional Architecture Flow

The following block diagram maps the operational flow of a capillary tube viscometer, including the data reduction pathway from raw measurements to viscosity output. This is the architecture you should be able to reproduce in a KTU diagram question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID RESERVOIR with test sample at controlled temperature T] --> B[PRESSURIZATION UNIT nitrogen gas or piston pump applies Delta P]
    B --> C[CAPILLARY TUBE of known diameter D and length L with no slip walls]
    C --> D[VOLUMETRIC COLLECTION measured volume V in time t]
    C --> E[PRESSURE TRANSDUCER measures Delta P across length L]
    D --> F[COMPUTE Q = V divided by t]
    E --> G[COMPUTE wall shear stress tau_w = Delta P times D divided by 4L]
    F --> H[COMPUTE wall shear rate gamma_w = 32 Q divided by pi D cubed]
    G --> I[CALCULATE apparent viscosity eta_app = tau_w divided by gamma_w]
    H --> I
    I --> J{RHEOLOGICAL MODEL}
    J -->|Newtonian| K[FINAL eta constant independent of shear rate]
    J -->|Power law| L[APPLY Rabinowitsch correction using flow index n]
    J -->|Bingham or Herschel Bulkley| M[APPLY yield stress and consistency corrections]
    K --> N[OUTPUT viscosity versus shear rate curve]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Falling Sphere Viscometer — Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram shows the complete measurement sequence of a falling sphere viscometer, including the wall and end correction loops that are critical for high-mark Part B answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VERTICAL GLASS TUBE of inner diameter D filled with test fluid at temperature T] --> B[RELEASE SPHERE of diameter d and density rho_s from top]
    B --> C[SPHERE ACCELERATES under gravity minus buoyancy minus drag]
    C --> D[TERMINAL VELOCITY REACHED when drag plus buoyancy equals gravity]
    D --> E[TIME MEASUREMENT over calibrated fall distance h gives t]
    E --> F[COMPUTE v_t = h divided by t]
    F --> G[CALCULATE eta = d squared times g times rho_s minus rho_f divided by 18 v_t]
    G --> H[REYNOLDS NUMBER CHECK Re = rho_f times d times v_t divided by eta]
    H --> I{Re less than 0.1 strict}
    I -->|YES| J[STOKES LAW VALID no inertial correction needed]
    I -->|NO| K[APPLY Oseen or Schiller Naumann correction]
    J --> L[WALL CORRECTION apply Faxen K factor using d divided by D]
    K --> L
    L --> M[END CORRECTION apply Faxen end factor using r_s divided by h]
    M --> N[FINAL corrected viscosity eta_true in Pa s]
```

### 4.3 Comparative System Architecture — Where Each Instrument Sits in the Rheometer Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ABSOLUTE VISCOMETERS derived from first principles] --> B[CAPILLARY TUBE Hagen Poiseuille law]
    A --> C[FALLING SPHERE Stokes law]
    A --> D[CONCENTRIC CYLINDER Couette flow]
    B --> E[FOOD APPLICATIONS chocolate extrusion molten sugar]
    C --> F[FOOD APPLICATIONS opaque syrups starch pastes]
    D --> G[FOOD APPLICATIONS sauces yogurts emulsions]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU:** Module 3 specifically asks students to *compare* the operating principles and identify which instrument suits which food system. The architecture above is a model answer for "Compare capillary and falling-sphere viscometers" type Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Module 3]**
*Define an absolute viscometer. State whether a capillary tube viscometer is absolute or relative, with justification.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **absolute viscometer** is one in which the viscosity of the fluid can be calculated directly from first principles of fluid mechanics using the instrument's geometric and operational parameters, *without the need for calibration* with a fluid of known viscosity.
A **capillary tube viscometer is absolute** because it uses the Hagen–Poiseuille equation, derived from a momentum balance on a cylindrical fluid element, to compute viscosity from the measured $\Delta P$, $Q$, $R$, and $L$ — all of which are primary physical quantities. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for classification, 1 for justification with the equation name.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
*What is the Rabinowitsch correction, and why is it needed in capillary rheometry of non-Newtonian fluids?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Rabinowitsch correction** adjusts the wall shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_w$ computed from the volumetric flow rate for the non-Newtonian (typically power-law) character of the fluid. It is given by $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \dfrac{8\bar{v}}{D}$, where $n$ is the power-law flow index.
It is needed because the **Newtonian formula** $\dot{\gamma}_w = 8\bar{v}/D$ underestimates the true wall shear rate for **shear-thinning fluids** ($n<1$) and overestimates it for **shear-thickening fluids** ($n>1$). (3 marks: 1 for the formula, 1 for the reason, 1 for shear-thinning/thickening distinction.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal capillary tube. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** A capillary tube of diameter $D = 1.5 \, \text{mm}$ and length $L = 0.4 \, \text{m}$ is used to measure the viscosity of a fruit concentrate. A pressure drop of $\Delta P = 8000 \, \text{Pa}$ drives a flow of $Q = 5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Compute (i) the wall shear stress, (ii) the wall shear rate, and (iii) the apparent viscosity. Is the flow laminar? Comment. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Full Derivation (7 marks):**
*(Students should write out the full derivation given in Section 3.1 of these notes. The seven marks are typically distributed as: 1 mark for the cylindrical element sketch + assumptions, 1 mark for the force balance equation, 1 mark for the velocity profile, 1 mark for the cross-sectional integration, 1 mark for evaluating the integral, 1 mark for the final Hagen–Poiseuille equation, and 1 mark for explicitly stating the assumptions list — laminar, steady, Newtonian, fully developed, isothermal, incompressible, no-slip.)*

Key equation to land on:

$$\eta = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 L Q}$$

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$, $L = 0.4 \, \text{m}$, $\Delta P = 8000 \, \text{Pa}$, $Q = 5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\rho_f \approx 1100 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (typical for fruit concentrate).

**(i) Wall shear stress (1 mark):**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L} = \frac{8000 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 0.4} = \frac{12}{1.6} = 7.5 \, \text{Pa}$$

**(ii) Wall shear rate (1 mark):**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3} = \frac{32 \times 5 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^3}$$

Compute denominator: $\pi \times 3.375 \times 10^{-9} = 1.0603 \times 10^{-8}$.

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0603 \times 10^{-8}} = 1509 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Apparent viscosity (1 mark):**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_w} = \frac{7.5}{1509} = 4.97 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} \approx 5.0 \, \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**Laminar flow check (3 marks):**
Compute Reynolds number:

$$Re = \frac{\rho_f \bar{v} D}{\eta_{app}}$$

Average velocity: $\bar{v} = Q / A = Q / (\pi D^2/4) = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 / 4} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{1.767 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.283 \, \text{m/s}$

$$Re = \frac{1100 \times 0.283 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4.97 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.467}{4.97 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 94$$

Since $Re \approx 94 < 2100$ (the critical Reynolds number for pipe flow), the flow is **laminar** and the Hagen–Poiseuille equation is valid. (3 marks: 1 for $\bar{v}$, 1 for $Re$ calculation, 1 for conclusion.)

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]*

**(a)** State and explain Stokes' Law. A steel sphere of diameter $d = 4 \, \text{mm}$ and density $\rho_s = 7800 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ is dropped into a vegetable oil of density $\rho_f = 920 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and viscosity $\eta = 0.08 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. Calculate the **terminal velocity** and the **Reynolds number** to verify the assumption of creeping flow. (7 marks)

**(b)** A glass sphere of diameter $d = 3.0 \, \text{mm}$ and density $\rho_s = 2500 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ falls through a syrup of density $\rho_f = 1300 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and viscosity $\eta = 0.5 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ inside a tube of diameter $D = 30 \, \text{mm}$. If the measured terminal velocity is $v_t = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}$, calculate (i) the apparent viscosity from Stokes' law, (ii) the Faxén wall correction factor $K$, and (iii) the **true viscosity**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Stokes' Law, terminal velocity, Re check (7 marks):**

**Stokes' Law statement (2 marks):**
For a rigid sphere moving at terminal velocity through a viscous Newtonian fluid in the creeping flow regime, the viscous drag force is given by:

$$F_d = 6 \pi \eta r_s v_t$$

This is the **Stokes drag force** and is valid for $Re < 0.1$ (or $<1$ in engineering practice).

**Terminal velocity calculation (3 marks):**

Equilibrium: $F_g = F_b + F_d$

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r_s^3 \rho_s g = \frac{4}{3} \pi r_s^3 \rho_f g + 6 \pi \eta r_s v_t$$

Solving for $v_t$ (with $r_s = d/2 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$):

$$v_t = \frac{2 r_s^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 \eta} = \frac{d^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \eta}$$

Substitute values:

$$v_t = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 9.81 \times (7800 - 920)}{18 \times 0.08}$$

$$v_t = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 9.81 \times 6880}{1.44} = \frac{1.080}{1.44} = 0.750 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Reynolds number check (2 marks):**

$$Re = \frac{2 r_s v_t \rho_f}{\eta} = \frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.750 \times 920}{0.08} = \frac{2.76}{0.08} = 34.5$$

Since $Re = 34.5 \gg 1$, the flow is **NOT** in the creeping regime! **Stokes' Law is invalid** for this case. The Oseen or Schiller–Naumann correction must be applied. *(This is a classic KTU trap-question — examiners reward students who spot and comment on the violation.)*

---

**(b) — Glass sphere, viscosity with wall correction (7 marks):**

**Given:** $d = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$, $\rho_s = 2500 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 1300 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\eta_{\text{meas}} = 0.5 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $D = 30 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$, $v_t = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}$.

**(i) Apparent viscosity from Stokes (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{d^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \, v_t} = \frac{(3.0 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 9.81 \times (2500 - 1300)}{18 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$= \frac{9 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 1200}{2.16 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.10595}{2.16 \times 10^{-3}} = 49.05 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**Re-check for Stokes validity (1 mark):**

$$Re = \frac{d \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\eta_{app}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 1300}{49.05} = 9.54 \times 10^{-6} \ll 1$$

Stokes' law is valid. (Note: the examiner provides a misleading $\eta = 0.5 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ as a distractor — always calculate $\eta$ from the given data.)

**(ii) Faxén wall correction factor (2 marks):**

Let $k = d/D = 3.0/30 = 0.1$.

$$K = 1 - 2.104(0.1) + 2.09(0.1)^3 - 0.95(0.1)^5$$

$$K = 1 - 0.2104 + 2.09 \times 10^{-3} - 9.5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.7917$$

**(iii) True viscosity (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{\text{true}} = \eta_{app} \times \frac{1}{K} = 49.05 \times \frac{1}{0.7917} = 61.96 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the wall correction:** Many students use Stokes' raw formula in a finite tube and lose 2–3 marks. The examiner expects you to know that the **infinite-medium** assumption is violated in practice, and to apply the Faxén factor when $d/D > 0.05$.
> 2. **Reynolds number neglect:** Always compute and report $Re$. If it exceeds 1, mention that the **Oseen correction** $F_d = 6\pi \eta r v (1 + \tfrac{3}{8}Re)$ is needed — this single comment often earns a full mark in valuation.
> 3. **Capillary vs. falling-sphere confusion:** Capillary uses $R^4$ (or $D^4$) dependence, falling sphere uses $r^2$ (or $d^2$). Mixing these will lead to absurd answers.
> 4. **Units:** Always convert mm to m, cm³ to m³, etc., *before* plugging into the formula. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit inconsistency.
> 5. **Rabinowitsch trap:** In capillary questions on *non-Newtonian* fluids, do not use $\dot{\gamma}_w = 32Q/(\pi D^3)$. Always multiply by $(3n+1)/(4n)$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- **Capillary viscometer is absolute** — uses Hagen–Poiseuille: $\eta = \pi R^4 \Delta P t / (8 L V)$.
- **Wall shear stress** in a capillary: $\tau_w = \Delta P \cdot D / (4L)$ — *no rheology assumed*.
- **Wall shear rate (Newtonian):** $\dot{\gamma}_w = 32 Q / (\pi D^3) = 8\bar{v}/D$.
- **Rabinowitsch correction** (for power-law fluids): $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \dfrac{8\bar{v}}{D}$.
- For $n=1$, Rabinowitsch reduces to the Newtonian form.
- **Falling sphere** uses **Stokes' Law** at terminal velocity: $\eta = d^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) / (18 v_t)$.
- **Three forces** at terminal velocity: gravity = buoyancy + Stokes drag.
- **Creeping flow** requirement: $Re < 0.1$ (strict) or $Re < 1$ (engineering). Check every time.
- **Faxén wall correction:** $\eta_{\text{true}} = \eta_{\text{meas}} \left[ 1 - 2.104(d/D) + 2.09(d/D)^3 - 0.95(d/D)^5 \right]^{-1}$.
- **End correction** (Faxén): $\eta_{\text{true}} = \eta_{\text{meas}} (1 + \alpha r_s/h)$ with $\alpha \approx 2.1$.
- **Capillary** is preferred for: polymer melts, edible oils, sugar syrups, chocolate (with Rabinowitsch).
- **Falling sphere** is preferred for: opaque Newtonian fluids (molasses, fruit concentrates, starch pastes).
- The **$R^4$ dependence** in Hagen–Poiseuille means radius accuracy dominates measurement error.
- The **$d^2$ dependence** in Stokes' law means sphere diameter accuracy is critical (calibrate spheres carefully).
- **Reynolds number check** is the single most important validation step in any absolute-viscometer calculation.
- Always report viscosity with correct **SI units** ($\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$); conversions: $1 \, \text{cP} = 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} = 1 \, \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$.
- **Bagley correction** and **Mooney correction** are *not* required for KTU Module 3 at the B.Tech level, but knowing they exist for entrance effects is bonus credit.
- Capillary rheometers are sometimes called "**melt flow indexers**" in polymer and chocolate industries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion Gelometer for Solid Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Torsion Gelometer for Solid Foods # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Torsion Gelometer (Torsion Wire Gelometer):** A precision rheological instrument designed to quantify the **gel strength** (firmness/hardness) and **shear modulus (modulus of rigidity, $G$)** of viscoelastic solid foods (gels, cheeses, jellies, bakery products) by applying a controlled **torsional (twisting) deformation** to a cylindrical or disc-shaped specimen and recording the resulting torque and angular deflection. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **Course Outcome CO3** (apply rheological principles to characterize food texture), the torsion gelometer falls under the family of **empirical fundamental tests** for solid-like foods. It works on the principle of **pure torsion of a cylindrical shaft**, where the elastic properties of the food gel are back-calculated from the measured torque–angle relationship. The instrument typically consists of a calibrated **tungsten or steel torsion wire**, a **fixed lower grip**, a **rotatable upper grip** connected to a graduated dial, and a **lever arm** through which a known weight or force is applied to generate the torque. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Twisting Spoon Test":** > Imagine you insert a spoon handle into a bowl of set jelly and try to twist it. The firmer the jelly, the more resistance you feel and the more force (torque) you need to twist the spoon by a certain angle. The torsion gelometer is essentially a scientific, instrumented version of this "twisting" test, but with a calibrated wire that tells you *exactly* how much torque was applied and *exactly* how many degrees the sample twisted before rupturing. **Key physical intuition:** - **Soft gel (e.g., unset gelatin):** Twists easily with low force → large angle, low torque. - **Firm gel (e.g., hard pectin jelly):** Resists twisting → small angle, high torque. - The **ratio of torque to angle** is directly related to the **shear modulus ($G$)** — a fundamental material property. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Converts applied weight to force | | Shear modulus of elasticity (modulus of rigidity) | $G$ | **Pa (N/m²)** | Quantifies gel firmness | | Torque | $T$ | **N·m** | Twisting moment applied | | Polar moment of inertia | $I_p$ | **m⁴** | Geometric resistance of cross-section | | Angular deflection | $\theta$ | **radians (rad)** | Angle of twist | > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The torsion gelometer is specifically covered because it is one of the *few* instruments that can measure **fundamental rheological parameters ($G$, $\tau_{max}$)** of *solid* foods — most other texture instruments (e.g., texture profile analyzer, penetrometer) only give **empirical force–distance** data, not true material properties. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The torsion gelometer operates on the **theory of torsion of a circular shaft** (Saint-Venant's torsion theory), assuming the food gel behaves as a **linear elastic solid** up to the point of fracture. **Step 1 — Sample Preparation:** A cylindrical specimen (length $L$, radius $R$) is cut from the gel using a sharpened cylindrical cutter. The ends must be flat and parallel to avoid stress concentration. **Step 2 — Mounting:** The specimen is clamped between two coaxial grips. The lower grip is fixed; the upper grip is attached to a calibrated torsion wire. **Step 3 — Application of Torque:** A known force $F$ is applied (usually through a pulley–weight system or a lever arm of length $r_{arm}$) at a right angle to the torsion wire. This generates a torque: $$T = F \cdot r_{arm}$$ **Step 4 — Measurement of Angular Deflection:** The upper grip rotates by an angle $\theta$ (in radians), measured by a graduated dial or optical lever. The corresponding twist in the torsion wire is also recorded (for calibration). **Step 5 — Calculation of Shear Stress and Strain:** The shear stress $\tau$ varies linearly from zero at the central axis to a maximum at the outer surface ($r = R$): $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{I_p}$$ where $I_p$ is the **polar moment of inertia** of the cross-section. The shear strain at radius $r$ is: $$\gamma(r) = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$ **Step 6 — Determination of Modulus of Rigidity ($G$):** For a linear elastic material, $G = \tau / \gamma$ at any given radius. The standard formula is: $$G = \frac{T \cdot L}{I_p \cdot \theta}$$ **Step 7 — Identification of Fracture Point:** The torque is increased gradually. The point at which the specimen fractures (visible by a sudden drop in the torque reading or a crack) gives the **gel strength** $\tau_{fracture}$. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below consolidates **all examinable formulas** for the torsion gelometer. Every formula has been verified against classical mechanics of materials (Timoshenko, *Mechanics of Materials*). | # | Formula | Description | Units | Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T = F \cdot r_{arm}$ | Torque from applied force | N·m | Lever arm perpendicular to wire | | 2 | $I_p = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{2}$ | Polar moment of inertia (solid cylinder) | m⁴ | Solid circular cross-section | | 3 | $\tau(r) = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{I_p}$ | Shear stress at radius $r$ | Pa | $0 \le r \le R$ | | 4 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{2T}{\pi R^3}$ | Maximum shear stress at surface | Pa | $r = R$, solid cylinder | | 5 | $\gamma(r) = \dfrac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$ | Shear strain at radius $r$ | dimensionless (rad) | Small angle, linear elastic | | 6 | $G = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{\gamma_{max}} = \dfrac{2 T L}{\pi R^4 \theta}$ | Shear modulus (modulus of rigidity) | Pa | Linear elastic, uniform $G$ | | 7 | $\phi = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot I_p}$ | Angle of twist (alternative form) | rad | Same as eq. 6 solved for $\theta$ | | 8 | $\tau_{fracture} = \dfrac{2 T_{fracture}}{\pi R^3}$ | Gel strength / fracture stress | Pa | At point of fracture | **Critical Note on Boundary Conditions:** - The formulas above assume **isotropic, homogeneous, linear elastic** behavior. - Food gels often **deviate from linearity**; therefore the **initial slope** of the torque–angle curve is used to determine $G$. - The **maximum** torque at fracture gives $\tau_{fracture}$ (gel strength). ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering | Application | Industry | Property Measured | |---|---|---| | Gelatin strength grading | Confectionery / Pharma | Bloom strength (related to $G$) | | Pectin gel quality | Jam / Jelly manufacturing | Gel firmness, setting point | | Cheese texture | Dairy (Cheddar, Gouda) | Modulus of rigidity vs. ripening time | | Agar gel standardization | Microbiology, Food | Gel strength at 1.5% concentration | | Starch gel retrogradation | Cereal / Bakery | $G$ vs. storage time (staling studies) | | Surimi (fish paste) gel | Seafood industry | Gel strength for kamaboko quality | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** The torsion test is preferred over compression tests for gels because **compression** can cause **barreling** and friction at the platens, while **torsion** produces a **pure shear** stress state with no friction artifacts — making $G$ a true material property. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Polar Moment of Inertia for a Solid Circular Cross-Section **Starting Definition:** The polar moment of inertia $I_p$ of a cross-section about its centroidal axis is defined as the integral of the squared radial distance over the area: $$I_p = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$ **Step 1 — Express the differential area:** For an annular ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$ in a circular cross-section, the differential area is: $$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$ *Explanation:* The circumference of the ring is $2\pi r$, and the radial thickness is $dr$, so the ring area is circumference $\times$ thickness. **Step 2 — Substitute $dA$ into the integral:** $$I_p = \int_0^R r^2 \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) = 2\pi \int_0^R r^3 \, dr$$ **Step 3 — Perform the integration:** $$I_p = 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}$$ **Final Result (boxed):** $$\boxed{I_p = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}}$$ *Note:* This is the geometric property that appears in **every** torsion gelometer formula. The strong $R^4$ dependence means a small error in measuring the sample radius causes a large error in $G$. ## 3.2 Derivation of Shear Stress Distribution in a Torsionally Loaded Cylinder **Starting Assumption:** Consider a long, straight, circular cylindrical specimen of radius $R$ and length $L$, fixed at one end ($x=0$) and subjected to a pure torque $T$ at the free end ($x=L$). **Step 1 — Equilibrium of an arbitrary cross-section:** If we make an imaginary cut at distance $x$ from the fixed end, the internal resisting moment must equal the applied torque. The shear stress $\tau(r)$ acting on element $dA$ at radius $r$ produces an elemental moment $\tau(r) \cdot dA \cdot r$ about the axis. Summing over the entire cross-section: $$T = \int_A \tau(r) \cdot r \, dA$$ **Step 2 — Assumption of linear stress distribution (Saint-Venant's hypothesis):** For a circular cross-section in torsion, the warping of cross-sections causes $\tau$ to vary linearly with $r$: $$\tau(r) = k \cdot r$$ where $k$ is a constant of proportionality (with units Pa/m). **Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $k$:** $$T = \int_0^R (k \cdot r) \cdot r \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) = 2\pi k \int_0^R r^3 \, dr = 2\pi k \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi k R^4}{2}$$ Solving for $k$: $$k = \frac{2T}{\pi R^4}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $k$ back into the stress equation:** $$\tau(r) = \frac{2T}{\pi R^4} \cdot r = \frac{T \cdot r}{I_p}$$ since $I_p = \pi R^4 / 2$. **Step 5 — Maximum shear stress at outer surface ($r = R$):** $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{2T}{\pi R^3}}$$ *Physical meaning:* The outer fibers of the cylinder experience the highest stress and will fail first — this is why gel specimens always fracture at the surface and propagate inward. ## 3.3 Derivation of Shear Strain and Modulus of Rigidity **Step 1 — Geometric definition of shear strain:** When the free end of the cylinder twists by an angle $\theta$ (in radians) relative to the fixed end, a longitudinal line on the surface (initially parallel to the axis) tilts and forms an angle $\gamma$ with the axis. For small angles: $$\gamma_{max} = \frac{R \cdot \theta}{L}$$ For a generic radius $r$: $$\gamma(r) = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$ **Step 2 — Hooke's law in shear (definition of $G$):** For a linear elastic solid, the shear modulus is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain at any point: $$G = \frac{\tau(r)}{\gamma(r)} = \frac{T \cdot r / I_p}{r \cdot \theta / L} = \frac{T \cdot L}{I_p \cdot \theta}$$ Note that the $r$ cancels, confirming that $G$ is a *material property*, independent of position. **Step 3 — Substitute $I_p = \pi R^4 / 2$:** $$G = \frac{T \cdot L}{(\pi R^4 / 2) \cdot \theta} = \frac{2 T L}{\pi R^4 \theta}$$ **Final Result (boxed):** $$\boxed{G = \frac{2TL}{\pi R^4 \theta}}$$ *This is the master equation* used to compute the modulus of rigidity of a food gel from torsion gelometer readings of torque $T$, length $L$, radius $R$, and angle of twist $\theta$. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A cylindrical gelatin gel specimen of radius $R = 10$ mm and length $L = 30$ mm is tested in a torsion gelometer. A torque of $T = 0.05$ N·m produces an angular twist of $\theta = 0.10$ rad. Calculate: > (a) The maximum shear stress at the surface. > (b) The shear modulus $G$ of the gel. ### Solution: **Given data (in SI units):** - $R = 10$ mm $= 0.010$ m - $L = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m - $T = 0.05$ N·m - $\theta = 0.10$ rad **Part (a) — Maximum Shear Stress:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{2T}{\pi R^3}$$ Substituting: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{2 \times 0.05}{\pi \times (0.010)^3} = \frac{0.10}{\pi \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.10}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\tau_{max} \approx 31{,}831 \text{ Pa} \approx 31.83 \text{ kPa}$$ **Part (b) — Shear Modulus:** $$G = \frac{2TL}{\pi R^4 \theta}$$ Substituting: $$G = \frac{2 \times 0.05 \times 0.030}{\pi \times (0.010)^4 \times 0.10} = \frac{0.003}{\pi \times 10^{-8} \times 0.10}$$ $$G = \frac{0.003}{3.1416 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 954{,}930 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.955 \text{ MPa}$$ **Interpretation:** This $G$ value is typical of a **medium-firm gelatin gel** (range 0.1–2 MPa). The result can be compared with published rheological data for benchmarking. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Torsion Gelometer Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a torsion gelometer, showing the interaction between the mechanical, measurement, and data-acquisition subsystems. ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["POWER SUPPLY UNIT
Stable DC source"] --> node2["TORSION WIRE
Calibrated Steel Wire"] node1 --> node3["UPPER GRIP ASSEMBLY
Rotates with twist angle"] node1 --> node4["ANGULAR ENCODER
Dial scale 0 to 360 deg"] node1 --> node5["LOADING LEVER ARM
Length rarm with pan"] node5 --> node6["APPLIED WEIGHT
Known mass in kg"] node5 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node7["FOOD GEL SPECIMEN
Cylindrical shape LR"] node7 --> node8["LOWER FIXED GRIP
Anchored to base plate"] node8 --> node9["RIGID BASE PLATE
Vibration isolated table"] node3 --> node4 node4 --> node10["DATA READOUT MODULE
Torque T and angle theta"] node10 --> node11["CALCULATION UNIT
G 2TL over pi R4 theta"] node11 --> node12["OUTPUT DISPLAY
Modulus of Rigidity in Pa"] node11 --> node13["FRACTURE DETECTION
Torque drop indicator"] node6 --> node5 node13 --> node10 ``` ## 4.2 Free-Body Schematic Description (Text-Based) For students who need to visualize the geometry during a derivation: ``` Force F (downward) │ ▼ ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐ │ Loading Pan + Weight (W) │ └───────────────┬───────────────┘ │ Cord over pulley ▼ ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Torsion Wire (vertical) │ ← Twist angle φ_wire │ Torque: T = W · r_arm │ └───────────────────┬───────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Upper Rotating Grip (clamp) │ ← Angular dial │ Angle of twist: θ (rad) │ └───────────────────┬───────────────────┘ │ ▼ ╔═══════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ FOOD GEL CYLINDER ║ ║ Length L, Radius R ║ ║ Modulus of Rigidity = G ║ ╚═══════════════════════════════════════╝ │ ▼ ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Lower Fixed Grip │ └───────────────────┬───────────────────┘ │ ▼ [ Rigid Base ] ``` ## 4.3 Shear Stress Distribution Across Cross-Section ``` Cross-section of cylindrical gel (looking down the axis) ● · · · · · · · τ=0 · · (neutral axis) · · · · · · · ● The shear stress τ(r) varies LINEARLY: - Zero at center (r = 0) - Maximum at outer surface (r = R) Linear profile: τ(r) = (2T/πR⁴) · r ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEFTT523]** **Define a torsion gelometer. Mention any two foods for which it is used to measure gel strength.** **Model Answer:** > A **torsion gelometer** is a rheological instrument used to measure the **gel strength (firmness)** and **shear modulus ($G$)** of solid food gels by applying a controlled torsional (twisting) deformation to a cylindrical specimen and recording the resulting **torque ($T$)** and **angular deflection ($\theta$)**. **Two food applications:** 1. **Gelatin desserts** — quality grading in confectionery. 2. **Pectin-based jams/jellies** — setting point and firmness control. *(Valid alternatives: agar gels, cheese, surimi/kamaboko, starch gels.)* **[3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for working principle + 1 for two examples]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEFTT523]** **Write the formula for the modulus of rigidity of a food gel as measured by a torsion gelometer, and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer:** $$G = \frac{2TL}{\pi R^4 \theta}$$ **Significance of each term:** | Symbol | Meaning | Significance | |---|---|---| | $G$ | Modulus of rigidity | Quantifies gel firmness; higher $G$ = stiffer gel | | $T$ | Applied torque | Twisting moment; measured via weight × lever arm | | $L$ | Length of specimen | Twist is proportional to length | | $R$ | Radius of specimen | Strong $R^4$ dependence — critical to measure accurately | | $\theta$ | Angular twist (rad) | Measured by graduated dial | > $G$ is a **fundamental material property**, independent of sample geometry (since both $L$ and $R$ are measured), making it superior to empirical firmness indices. **[3 marks: 1 for formula + 2 for term explanations]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEFTT523 Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **construction and working principle of a torsion gelometer**. Derive the expression for the **maximum shear stress** in a cylindrical food gel specimen under torsion. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] A cylindrical pectin gel of radius $R = 12$ mm and length $L = 25$ mm is twisted in a torsion gelometer. An applied weight of $W = 0.5$ kg hangs from a lever arm of length $r_{arm} = 0.10$ m. The recorded angular twist is $\theta = 0.08$ rad. Calculate: > - (i) The applied torque. > - (ii) The maximum shear stress. > - (iii) The shear modulus of the gel. > Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². --- ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Derivation **Construction (3 marks):** A torsion gelometer consists of the following components: 1. **Torsion wire** — A calibrated steel wire (usually tungsten) that provides a known torsional resistance. 2. **Upper grip (rotatable clamp)** — Holds the top of the cylindrical gel specimen; connected to the torsion wire and to a graduated angular dial. 3. **Lower grip (fixed clamp)** — Anchors the bottom of the specimen to a rigid base. 4. **Lever arm and loading pan** — A horizontal arm at the top of the torsion wire, with a pan to hold calibrated weights; the arm length $r_{arm}$ converts vertical weight into torque. 5. **Angular dial** — Graduated in degrees (must be converted to radians for $G$ calculation). 6. **Rigid base plate** — Vibration-isolated support. **Working Principle (2 marks):** 1. A cylindrical gel specimen of known dimensions ($L$, $R$) is mounted between the two grips. 2. A known weight $W$ is placed on the pan. The torque generated is $T = W \cdot r_{arm}$ (assuming the arm is horizontal). 3. The torque twists the torsion wire and the upper grip, applying a pure torsional load to the gel specimen. 4. The angular twist $\theta$ of the upper grip is read from the dial. 5. The torque is increased incrementally (by adding weights) until the specimen fractures. The fracture torque $T_{fracture}$ gives the **gel strength**. **Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress (2 marks):** For a cylindrical specimen of radius $R$ and length $L$ under pure torque $T$, the shear stress at radius $r$ is: $$\tau(r) = \frac{T \cdot r}{I_p}$$ where $I_p = \pi R^4 / 2$ is the polar moment of inertia. Substituting: $$\tau(r) = \frac{2T \cdot r}{\pi R^4}$$ At the outer surface, $r = R$, so: $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{2T}{\pi R^3}}$$ #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** - $R = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m - $L = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m - $W = 0.5$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s² - $r_{arm} = 0.10$ m - $\theta = 0.08$ rad **Step (i) — Applied Torque:** Force from weight: $$F = W \cdot g = 0.5 \times 9.81 = 4.905 \text{ N}$$ Torque: $$T = F \cdot r_{arm} = 4.905 \times 0.10 = 0.4905 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step (ii) — Maximum Shear Stress:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{2T}{\pi R^3} = \frac{2 \times 0.4905}{\pi \times (0.012)^3}$$ Denominator: $$\pi \times (0.012)^3 = 3.1416 \times 1.728 \times 10^{-6} = 5.429 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$ Therefore: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{0.981}{5.429 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.807 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 180.7 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step (iii) — Shear Modulus:** $$G = \frac{2TL}{\pi R^4 \theta}$$ Compute $R^4$: $$R^4 = (0.012)^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$ Numerator: $$2TL = 2 \times 0.4905 \times 0.025 = 0.024525$$ Denominator: $$\pi \times R^4 \times \theta = 3.1416 \times 2.0736 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.08 = 5.212 \times 10^{-9}$$ Therefore: $$G = \frac{0.024525}{5.212 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 4.706 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 4.71 \text{ MPa}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $T = 0.4905$ N·m - (ii) $\tau_{max} \approx 180.7$ kPa - (iii) $G \approx 4.71$ MPa **Interpretation:** $G \approx 4.7$ MPa is in the range of a **firm pectin gel** (typical range 1–10 MPa). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEFTT523 Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the **shear modulus of rigidity ($G$)** of a cylindrical food gel in terms of the torque ($T$), length ($L$), radius ($R$), and angle of twist ($\theta$) using torsion gelometer measurements. State clearly the assumptions made. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of the torsion gelometer compared to other texture-measuring devices. Give **two specific industrial applications** of torsion testing in food quality control. --- ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Derivation of Shear Modulus **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. The food gel behaves as a **homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic solid** within the tested range. 2. The cylindrical specimen has a **uniform circular cross-section** with flat, parallel ends. 3. The deformation is a **pure torsion** (no bending, no axial loading). 4. **Plane cross-sections remain plane** and rotate as rigid discs (Saint-Venant's principle). 5. **Small angle of twist** (so that $\tan \theta \approx \theta$ in radians). **Derivation (5 marks):** **Step 1 — Shear stress at radius $r$:** From the torsion equilibrium of a circular cross-section: $$\tau(r) = \frac{T \cdot r}{I_p} = \frac{2T \cdot r}{\pi R^4}$$ **Step 2 — Shear strain at radius $r$:** A longitudinal line on the cylinder tilts by angle $\gamma(r)$ when the free end rotates by $\theta$: $$\gamma(r) = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Hooke's law in shear:** $$G = \frac{\tau(r)}{\gamma(r)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{2Tr}{\pi R^4}}{\dfrac{r\theta}{L}} = \frac{2T \cdot r \cdot L}{\pi R^4 \cdot r \cdot \theta}$$ The $r$ cancels: $$\boxed{G = \frac{2TL}{\pi R^4 \theta}}$$ This expression shows that $G$ depends only on measurable quantities ($T$, $L$, $R$, $\theta$) and is **independent of the radial position** $r$ — confirming it is a true material constant. #### Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, and Industrial Applications **Advantages (3 marks):** | # | Advantage | Explanation | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Fundamental property measurement** | Yields true shear modulus $G$, not just empirical force values | | 2 | **Pure shear state** | No friction or barreling artifacts (unlike uniaxial compression) | | 3 | **Non-destructive within elastic range** | Allows repeated testing of the same sample | | 4 | **Wide range of stiffness** | Calibratable wire allows testing of soft gels to firm cheese | | 5 | **Fracture point clearly identifiable** | Sudden torque drop marks gel failure | **Limitations (2 marks):** | # | Limitation | Explanation | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Sample geometry critical** | $G$ is highly sensitive to $R$ (fourth-power dependence); small machining errors cause large $G$ errors | | 2 | **Assumes linear elasticity** | Real food gels are viscoelastic — $G$ varies with strain rate and time | | 3 | **Not suitable for very soft or brittle foods** | Soft gels may flow under own weight; brittle foods fracture before measurable twist | | 4 | **Slow and labor-intensive** | Manual loading and reading; not suited for high-throughput QC | | 5 | **End effects** | Stress concentration at grips can cause premature fracture | **Two Industrial Applications (2 marks):** 1. **Gelatin Industry:** Standardizing **bloom strength** of commercial gelatin — a 6.67% gelatin gel at 10°C is tested, and the force to depress a standard plunger 4 mm is reported as Bloom number. (Note: the Bloom gelometer uses penetration, but the *concept* of standardized gel strength testing originated from torsion testing principles.) 2. **Dairy Industry — Cheese Ripening Control:** The shear modulus $G$ of Cheddar or Gouda cheese is monitored as a function of ripening time. As proteolysis proceeds, $G$ decreases predictably, allowing manufacturers to determine the **optimal ripening duration** for desired texture. *(Other valid applications: surimi gel quality for kamaboko, agar gel standardization for microbiology, starch retrogradation studies.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks typical):** Forgetting to convert $R$ from **mm to m** and $\theta$ from **degrees to radians** is the #1 reason students get wrong numerical answers. **Always work in SI units.** > 2. **Using $R$ vs. $D$ in formulas (–1 mark):** The formulas use **radius**, not diameter. Mixing these up gives a $2^4 = 16$-fold error in $G$. > 3. **Forgetting to convert weight to force (–1 mark):** Torque = (mass × g) × arm length, NOT mass × arm length. $g = 9.81$ m/s² must appear. > 4. **Confusing shear stress with shear strain (–2 marks):** $\tau$ has units of Pa; $\gamma$ is dimensionless (radians). > 5. **Not stating assumptions in derivation (–1 mark):** KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for derivations without the "assumptions" preamble. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **The "Exam Eve" Summary — Read This 10 Minutes Before the Test** - ✅ **Torsion gelometer** = instrument for measuring **gel strength** and **modulus of rigidity ($G$)** of *solid* foods. - ✅ **Core principle:** Apply torque $T$, measure twist angle $\theta$, calculate $G = 2TL / (\pi R^4 \theta)$. - ✅ **Polar moment of inertia** for solid circular cross-section: $I_p = \pi R^4 / 2$. - ✅ **Shear stress varies linearly** with $r$: zero at center, **maximum at surface** $\tau_{max} = 2T/(\pi R^3)$. - ✅ **Shear strain** at radius $r$: $\gamma(r) = r\theta / L$. - ✅ **Gel strength / fracture stress** = maximum shear stress at the fracture point. - ✅ **Assumptions:** Homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic, small angles, circular cross-section. - ✅ **Working equation for $G$:** Always use **SI units** (m, N, rad) — conversion mistakes are the biggest mark-loser. - ✅ **Applications:** Gelatin, pectin, agar, cheese, surimi, starch gels. - ✅ **Advantage over compression:** Pure shear, no friction, gives fundamental property $G$. - ✅ **Key limitation:** $R^4$ sensitivity — sample must be machined precisely. - ✅ **Convert degrees → radians** by multiplying by $\pi / 180$ before plugging into $G$ formula. - ✅ **Convert mm → m** by dividing by 1000 (or by $10^3$) before all SI calculations. - ✅ **Final numerical answers** are typically reported in **kPa** (for $\tau$) or **MPa** (for $G$). - ✅ **The torsion wire** is a calibrated spring element — its own twist is subtracted to isolate the gel's twist, or the gel's twist is calculated as: $\theta_{gel} = \theta_{total} - \theta_{wire}$ where $\theta_{wire} = T \cdot L_{wire} / (G_{wire} \cdot I_{p,wire})$.

topics.title: Texture of Foods: Dough Testing Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Texture of Foods: Dough Testing Instruments ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Dough Texture Testing? **Dough Testing** refers to the instrumental measurement of the **mechanical, rheological, and textural properties of a dough mass** (typically wheat-based) under controlled deformation, mixing, and extension conditions. It is a critical subdomain of **Food Rheology** that bridges raw material (flour) quality with finished product (bread, biscuits, noodles) performance. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > *Dough rheology is the study of the flow and deformation behavior of a flour-water-ingredient system under applied stress, measured through standardized empirical and fundamental testing instruments to predict processing behavior and end-product quality.* ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Kneading Chef" vs. The "Lab Scientist" Imagine two bakers evaluating the same flour batch: 1. **The Home Chef** pinches the dough, rolls it between her fingers, and pulls it apart — judging "stickiness," "stretchiness," and "strength" purely by **feel** (subjective). 2. **The Industrial Food Scientist** loads the same dough into a **Farinograph** or **Extensograph** — a machine that kneads, stretches, and records the **exact force (in Brabender Units or Newtons)** needed to deform it, producing a **reproducible curve** (objective). The dough testing instruments are simply the **scientific, standardized, and quantifiable extension of the chef's hands**. They convert "feel" into numerical curves. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this important for KTU exams?** > The 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. You must link every instrument to its: > - **CO (Course Outcome):** Predicting processing performance from flour properties. > - **PO (Program Outcome):** Engineering knowledge applied to food systems. > - **PSO (Program Specific Outcome):** Quality control in bakery industries. ### 1.3 Key Textural Properties Measured in Dough | Property | Definition | Practical Significance | |---|---|---| | **Water Absorption (WA)** | Amount of water (%, 14% mb) flour can absorb to reach standard consistency | Predicts baking yield, dough handling | | **Development Time (DT)** | Time to reach maximum dough consistency | Indicates gluten strength | | **Stability** | Time dough maintains maximum consistency | Indicates mixing tolerance | | **Extensibility (E)** | Maximum stretch length before rupture | Predicts loaf volume, shaping behavior | | **Resistance to Extension (R)** | Force required to stretch dough | Indicates gluten network strength | | **Energy (W)** | Area under alveograph curve | Total baking strength | | **Hardness** | Peak force in TPA first compression | Dough firmness | ### 1.4 Classification of Dough Testing Instruments > [!NOTE] > **Three Broad Categories:** > > 1. **Mixing-Based Instruments** (measure dough during mixing) → Farinograph, Mixograph, Consistograph > 2. **Extension-Based Instruments** (measure dough extensibility) → Extensograph, Alveograph, Kieffer Rig > 3. **Fundamental/Empirical Instruments** (measure viscoelastic moduli) → Rheometer (oscillatory), DMTA, Texture Analyzer --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Farinograph (Brabender Farinograph — ICC 115, AACC 54-21) **Principle:** A fixed weight of flour (typically **300 g or 50 g** for the micro-farinograph) is mixed with water at a controlled temperature (30°C) in a thermostatted mixing bowl. Two sigma-shaped blades rotate at fixed speeds (60 rpm and 90 rpm counter-rotating). The **torque required to maintain constant blade speed** is recorded as a function of time. **Output:** The **Farinogram** — a characteristic curve of torque (Brabender Units, BU) vs. time (minutes). **Key Reading Points on the Farinogram:** - **Arrival Time (T₀):** Time to first reach 500 BU consistency - **Peak Time / Development Time (DT):** Time to reach maximum consistency - **Stability (S):** Time the curve remains above 500 BU - **Mixing Tolerance Index (MTI):** BU drop from peak at a fixed time after peak (typically 5 min) - **Time to Breakdown (Tb):** Drop below 500 BU after peak **Water Absorption Correction Formula:** $$WA_{corrected} = WA_{14\%} + 0.1 \cdot (T_{max} - T_{target})$$ where $T_{max}$ is the peak consistency achieved and $T_{target}$ is typically **500 BU**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked "What does the Farinograph measure?", the answer is **TORQUE** (not force, not viscosity directly). Torque ∝ Resistance to mixing. ### 2.2 The Mixograph (National Mfg., Lincoln, NE) **Principle:** A small flour sample (**10 g** for standard, **35 g** for large) is mixed with water by four vertical pins attached to a horizontal rotating head.
