The mixing torque is recorded on a **strip chart** (or digital output). **Output:** **Mixogram** — plots torque vs. time. **Key Differences from Farinograph:** - Smaller sample size (10 g vs. 300 g) - Faster mixing (~8–10 min total vs. 20+ min) - Records **total torque curve** (mid-line + envelope) - Measures **Mixing Tolerance** via curve width after peak ### 2.3 The Extensograph (Brabender Extensograph — ICC 114, AACC 54-10) **Principle:** A dough sample (150 g) is first mixed in the Farinograph, then **rounded**, **molded** in a specific shape, **rested (45 min, 90 min, or 135 min)** for gluten relaxation, and finally **stretched uniaxially** by a hook moving at constant speed (1.45 cm/s) until rupture. **Output:** **Extensogram** — Load (Extensograph Units, EU) vs. extension (cm). **Critical Parameters:** - **Extensibility (E):** Total length stretched before rupture (cm) - **Resistance to Extension (R₅₀):** Height of curve at 50 mm extension (EU) - **Maximum Resistance (Rₘₐₓ):** Peak height of curve - **Area Under Curve (A):** Energy input (cm²) **Ratio R₅₀/E** → Indicates **dough balance**: - **R₅₀/E ≈ 1.5–2.0:** Balanced dough (ideal for bread) - **R₅₀/E > 3.0:** "Short" or stiff dough (poor gas retention) - **R₅₀/E < 1.0:** "Slack" dough (sticky, hard to handle) ### 2.4 The Alveograph (Chopin Alveograph — ICC 121, AACC 54-30) **Principle:** A dough disk is **inflated by air pressure** from below (multiaxial/biaxial extension) until it ruptures into a bubble. The internal pressure is recorded. **Output:** **Alveogram** — Pressure (mm H₂O) vs. time. **Key Parameters (Chopin's Notation):** | Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **Over-pressure / Maximum Pressure** | **P** | Resistance to deformation | mm H₂O | | **Average Abscissa to Rupture** | **L** | Extensibility | mm | | **Index of Swelling** | **G** | $\sqrt{L \cdot 10}$ — geometric index | — | | **Configuration Ratio** | **P/L** | Balance | dimensionless | | **Deformation Energy / Strength** | **W** | Area under curve = $1.324 \cdot V \cdot L$ | $10^{-4}$ J | **The Alveograph W-value Formula (CRITICAL FOR KTU):** $$W = 1.324 \cdot V \cdot L$$ where $V$ is the **average abscissa volume** (cm³) read from the integrator, and $L$ is the length on the abscissa to rupture (mm). > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** Confusing the **Extensograph** (uniaxial) with the **Alveograph** (biaxial). They measure **different deformation modes**! Extensograph = stretching in one direction; Alveograph = inflating (like blowing a balloon). ### 2.5 Other Important Instruments #### 2.5.1 Kieffer Dough and Gluten Extensibility Rig - Micro-scale version of Extensograph - Uses ~0.5 g dough - Attached to **Texture Analyzer** (TA-XT2, TA-XTPlus) - Used for **small sample screening** (breeding programs) #### 2.5.2 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) for Dough — AACC 74-09 - Two-cycle compression using a cylindrical or conical probe - Parameters: **Hardness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Cohesiveness, Chewiness, Gumminess, Resilience** > [!IMPORTANT] > **TPA Imitation:** TPA does **NOT** measure fundamental properties — it is an **empirical double-bite test**. The shape of the probe (cylindrical vs. conical) critically affects results. #### 2.5.3 Dynamic Oscillatory Rheometer (Fundamental Rheometer) - Uses **parallel plate** or **serrated plate** geometry - **Strain sweep:** Determine Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVR) - **Frequency sweep:** Measure $G'$, $G''$, $\tan\delta$ - $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ — damping factor - Strong gluten dough: $\tan\delta < 0.3$ - Weak dough: $\tan\delta > 0.6$ #### 2.5.4 Amylograph (Brabender Amylograph — ICC 126) - Measures **pasting properties** of flour/water suspension (not dough) - Heating from 30°C → 95°C at 1.5°C/min - Records viscosity in **AU (Amylograph Units)** vs. temperature - Predicts **α-amylase activity** (Falling Number alternative) ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **PRINT THIS TABLE — HIGH-YIELD FOR EXAMS** | Formula | Definition | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | $WA_{corrected} = WA_{14\%} + 0.1(T_{max} - T_{target})$ | Water absorption adjustment to 500 BU | % | Farinograph | | $MTI = BU_{peak} - BU_{peak+5min}$ | Mixing tolerance index | BU | Farinograph | | $W = 1.324 \cdot V \cdot L$ | Alveograph deformation energy | $10^{-4}$ J | Alveograph | | $P/L$ | Configuration ratio | dimensionless | Alveograph | | $G = \sqrt{L \cdot 10}$ | Index of swelling | mm | Alveograph | | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's law of viscosity (ideal) | Pa | Fluid dough | | $G^* = G' + iG''$ | Complex modulus | Pa | Oscillatory | | $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ | Loss tangent | dimensionless | Oscillatory | | $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$ | Young's modulus (Hookean solid) | Pa | Extension | | $\eta^* = \sigma^*/\dot{\gamma}^*$ | Complex viscosity | Pa·s | Oscillatory | **⚠ Markdown Note:** All absolute values and vertical bars in formulas have been replaced with descriptive notation to preserve table integrity. ### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Industry 1. **Flour Milling & Baking Industries:** QC/QA, flour blending, predicting baking performance. 2. **Plant Breeding:** Screening thousands of wheat lines for gluten strength. 3. **Product Development:** Reformulating gluten-free doughs using hydrocolloids (xanthan, HPMC). 4. **Process Optimization:** Determining optimal mixing time, proof time, and baking conditions. 5. **Regulatory & Standards:** ICC (International Association for Cereal Science and Technology), AACC International (now AACCI), ISO standards. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Water Absorption (WA) Correction to 500 BU The Brabender Farinograph produces a torque curve that depends on flour:water ratio. To compare different flours, results are standardized to a target consistency of **500 BU** (the "500 line"). **Given:** - Experimental WA at peak consistency: $WA_{14\%}$ (%) - Peak consistency achieved: $T_{max}$ (BU) - Target consistency: $T_{target} = 500$ BU - Empirical sensitivity: 1% water change ≈ 10 BU change (hence the 0.1 factor) **Step 1: Establish the linear relationship between WA and consistency** For a small change in WA near the optimum: $$\Delta T = k \cdot \Delta WA$$ where $k = 10$ BU per 1% water (empirical constant for Brabender system). **Step 2: Express the deviation from target** $$\Delta T = T_{max} - T_{target}$$ **Step 3: Solve for the required WA correction** $$\Delta WA = \frac{\Delta T}{k} = \frac{T_{max} - T_{target}}{10}$$ $$\Delta WA = 0.1 \cdot (T_{max} - T_{target})$$ **Step 4: Apply correction to original WA** $$\begin{aligned} WA_{corrected} &= WA_{14\%} + \Delta WA \\ WA_{corrected} &= WA_{14\%} + 0.1 \cdot (T_{max} - T_{target}) \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** - If $T_{max} > 500$ BU → dough is too stiff → need MORE water → WA increases. - If $T_{max} < 500$ BU → dough is too soft → need LESS water → WA decreases. > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (KTU Board Style):** > A flour sample shows peak consistency of **540 BU** at **58.0% WA**. Calculate the corrected WA to 500 BU. > > **Solution:** > $WA_{corrected} = 58.0 + 0.1 \cdot (540 - 500) = 58.0 + 0.1 \cdot 40 = 58.0 + 4.0 = 62.0\%$ > > **Answer: 62.0%** (14% moisture basis) ### 3.2 Derivation of Alveograph W-Value (Chopin's Equation) The energy $W$ of deformation is the work done to inflate the dough disk until it ruptures. This equals the integral of pressure over volume. **Step 1: Express infinitesimal work** $$dW = P \, dV$$ where $P$ = instantaneous pressure (mm H₂O) and $dV$ = infinitesimal volume change. **Step 2: Integrate over the full inflation process** $$W = \int_{0}^{V_f} P(V) \, dV$$ **Step 3: Chopin's Empirical Simplification** Chopin assumed that the **average pressure** $\bar{P}$ (in mm H₂O) over the inflation cycle, combined with the total volume $V$ (in cm³) swept, approximates the energy. The empirical factor **1.324** converts mm H₂O·cm³ to $10^{-4}$ J, and accounts for the unit conversions. $$\begin{aligned} W &= k_{conv} \cdot \bar{P} \cdot V \\ \text{where } k_{conv} &= 1.324 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J per (mm H}_2\text{O} \cdot \text{cm}^3\text{)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Final Chopin's Formula** Since $\bar{P}$ is approximately proportional to $P$ (peak pressure) for typical wheat doughs, and $V$ is read directly from the integrator as the average abscissa volume: $$\boxed{W = 1.324 \cdot P \cdot V \text{ (energy in } 10^{-4} \text{ J)}}$$ Alternatively, expressed using **L** (length of the curve's abscissa at rupture, in mm): $$W = 1.324 \cdot V \cdot L$$ The relationship $L = 10 \cdot G^2$ where $G$ is the swelling index arises from the geometry of the inflating bubble. **Worked Example (KTU Board Style):** An alveograph test gives $V = 320$ cm³ and $L = 105$ mm. Calculate the deformation energy $W$. **Solution:** $$W = 1.324 \times 320 \times 105$$ $$\begin{aligned} W &= 1.324 \times 320 \times 105 \\ &= 1.324 \times 33600 \\ &= 44486.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \\ &= 4.45 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer: $W = 4.45$ J or equivalently $444.86 \times 10^{-4}$ J** ### 3.3 Derivation of Storage and Loss Moduli from Oscillatory Strain Sweep **Setup:** A dough disc is placed between parallel plates. The upper plate oscillates sinusoidally: $$\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 1: The resulting stress response (for viscoelastic material)** $$\sigma(t) = \gamma_0 \left[ G' \sin(\omega t) + G'' \cos(\omega t) \right]$$ **Step 2: Decompose into in-phase and out-of-phase components** - $G' \sin(\omega t)$ → **in-phase** (elastic, solid-like, recoverable) - $G'' \cos(\omega t)$ → **out-of-phase by 90°** (viscous, liquid-like, dissipated) **Step 3: Define the complex modulus** $$G^* = \frac{\sigma^*(t)}{\gamma^*(t)} = G' + iG''$$ **Step 4: Magnitude of complex modulus** $$\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$$ **Step 5: Loss tangent (damping factor)** $$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'}$$ **Step 6: LVR Identification** The **Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVR)** is the strain range where $G'$ is constant (within ±5%). The critical strain $\gamma_c$ marks the boundary: - Strong gluten doughs: $\gamma_c \approx 0.1\%$ - Weak/over-mixed doughs: $\gamma_c < 0.05\%$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical Significance:** Within the LVR, the dough structure is **undisturbed** by the test. Outside LVR, the test **destroys the gluten network** (irreversible damage). ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Instrument-to-Parameter Mapping | Instrument | Deformation Mode | Primary Output | Key Parameters | Standard | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Farinograph** | High-shear mixing | Torque vs. time (BU) | WA, DT, S, MTI, Tb | ICC 115 / AACC 54-21 | | **Mixograph** | High-shear mixing | Torque vs. time (arbitrary) | MT, Peak time, Curve width | AACC 54-40 | | **Extensograph** | Uniaxial extension | Load vs. extension (EU) | E, R₅₀, Rₘₐₓ, A | ICC 114 / AACC 54-10 | | **Alveograph** | Biaxial extension (inflation) | Pressure vs. time (mm H₂O) | P, L, W, P/L, G | ICC 121 / AACC 54-30 | | **Kieffer Rig** | Uniaxial micro-extension | Force vs. distance (N) | Peak force, Distance | AACC 54-61 | | **Texture Analyzer (TPA)** | Double compression | Force vs. time (N·s) | Hardness, Adhesiveness, etc. | AACC 74-09 | | **Oscillatory Rheometer** | Small-amplitude oscillatory shear | G', G'', tan δ | G', G'', η* | AACC 54-62 | | **Amylograph** | Heating + rotation | Viscosity vs. T (AU) | Peak viscosity, Breakdown | ICC 126 | --- ## 4.
