# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Casson Model from First Principles

The **Casson model** is the most historically important constitutive equation for fat rheology, particularly for chocolate. We derive it from **first principles of colloidal flocculation theory**.

### Step 1 — Starting Postulate

A fat crystal network can be idealized as a collection of **flocculated rigid rods** in a viscous matrix. The force needed to break a single rod–rod bond in shear is $F_{bond}$.

The shear stress required to **just initiate flow** (i.e., the yield stress) is therefore proportional to the number of bonds per unit area $N_{b}$:

$$\tau_{0} \;\propto\; F_{bond} \cdot N_{b}$$

This gives the basic proportionality:

$$\tau_{0} = C_{1} \cdot F_{bond} \cdot N_{b}$$

where $C_{1}$ is a geometric constant of order unity.

### Step 2 — Velocity Gradient After Yielding

Once the network breaks, the dispersed particles (fat crystals) move relative to the matrix. The relative velocity of a particle of size $a$ under a local shear stress $\tau$ scales as:

$$v_{rel} \;\propto\; \dfrac{\tau \cdot a}{\eta_{0}}$$

where $\eta_{0}$ is the viscosity of the continuous (oil) phase.

### Step 3 — Energy Dissipation Argument

The total viscous dissipation per unit volume is $\eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{2}$. Equating this to the work done by the applied stress minus the energy barrier of breaking bonds:

$$\tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} - \tau_{0} \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\tau - \tau_{0} = \eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

### Step 4 — Non-Linear Correction via Asymptotic Bond Breaking

Real fat networks show **shear-thinning**: as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases, more bonds break progressively. Casson proposed the following asymptotic form:

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}} + \sqrt{\eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\tau = \tau_{0} + 2\sqrt{\tau_{0} \cdot \eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}} + \eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

### Step 5 — Verification of Limiting Cases

**Case A: Very High Shear Rate** ($\dot{\gamma} \to \infty$)
The $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ term is dominated by the $\dot{\gamma}$ term:
$$\tau \approx \eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma} \quad \text{(Newtonian limit)}$$

**Case B: At Yield Point** ($\dot{\gamma} \to 0$)
$$\tau \to \tau_{0} \quad \text{(solid-like limit)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both limits are **physically correct**, confirming the Casson model is a **valid phenomenological constitutive equation** for fats.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Casson Parameters for Chocolate

A chocolate sample at 40°C is tested in a concentric cylinder rheometer. The following data are obtained:

| Shear Rate $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | Shear Stress $\tau$ (Pa) |
|---|---|
| 5 | 18.2 |
| 20 | 28.6 |
| 50 | 41.7 |
| 100 | 58.4 |
| 200 | 82.0 |

**Find the Casson yield stress $\tau_{0}$ and Casson viscosity $\eta_{c}$.**

### Solution

The Casson equation is:
$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}} + \sqrt{\eta_{c}} \cdot \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$$

This is **linear** in variables $Y = \sqrt{\tau}$ and $X = \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$, with slope $\sqrt{\eta_{c}}$ and intercept $\sqrt{\tau_{0}}$.

#### Step 1 — Transform the Data

| $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ (s⁻¹/²) | $\sqrt{\tau}$ (Pa¹/²) |
|---|---|
| $\sqrt{5} = 2.236$ | $\sqrt{18.2} = 4.266$ |
| $\sqrt{20} = 4.472$ | $\sqrt{28.6} = 5.348$ |
| $\sqrt{50} = 7.071$ | $\sqrt{41.7} = 6.458$ |
| $\sqrt{100} = 10.000$ | $\sqrt{58.4} = 7.642$ |
| $\sqrt{200} = 14.142$ | $\sqrt{82.0} = 9.055$ |

#### Step 2 — Linear Regression

Let slope $= m$ and intercept $= b$. Standard formulas:

$$m = \dfrac{N \sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{N \sum X^{2} - (\sum X)^{2}}$$

$$b = \dfrac{\sum Y - m \sum X}{N}$$

With $N = 5$:

$\sum X = 2.236 + 4.472 + 7.071 + 10.000 + 14.142 = 37.921$

$\sum Y = 4.266 + 5.348 + 6.458 + 7.642 + 9.055 = 32.769$

$\sum X^{2} = 5.000 + 20.000 + 50.000 + 100.000 + 200.000 = 375.000$

$\sum XY = (2.236)(4.266) + (4.472)(5.348) + (7.071)(6.458) + (10.000)(7.642) + (14.142)(9.055)$

Computing term by term:
- $(2.236)(4.266) = 9.539$
- $(4.472)(5.348) = 23.916$
- $(7.071)(6.458) = 45.667$
- $(10.000)(7.642) = 76.420$
- $(14.142)(9.055) = 128.061$

$\sum XY = 9.539 + 23.916 + 45.667 + 76.420 + 128.061 = 283.603$

#### Step 3 — Apply the Formulas

$$m = \dfrac{5 \times 283.603 - 37.921 \times 32.769}{5 \times 375.000 - (37.921)^{2}}$$

Numerator: $5 \times 283.603 = 1418.015$; $37.921 \times 32.769 = 1242.633$; difference = $175.382$

Denominator: $5 \times 375.000 = 1875.000$; $(37.921)^{2} = 1438.000$; difference = $437.000$

$$m = \dfrac{175.382}{437.000} = 0.4013 \; (\text{Pa}^{1/2} \cdot s^{1/2})$$

$$b = \dfrac{32.769 - 0.4013 \times 37.921}{5} = \dfrac{32.769 - 15.219}{5} = \dfrac{17.550}{5} = 3.510 \; (\text{Pa}^{1/2})$$

#### Step 4 — Extract Physical Parameters

$$\tau_{0} = b^{2} = (3.510)^{2} = 12.32 \; \text{Pa}$$

$$\eta_{c} = m^{2} = (0.4013)^{2} = 0.1610 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

#### Step 5 — Industrial Interpretation

- $\tau_{0} = 12.32$ Pa is **within the industrial specification for dark chocolate** (typically 5–25 Pa at 40°C) — the formulation is acceptable.
- $\eta_{c} = 0.161$ Pa·s is consistent with cocoa butter-based chocolate at 40°C.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Fat Product Rheology

| Product | $\tau_{0}$ (Pa) | Dominant Model | Key Textural Attribute | Critical Polymorph |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Butter (5°C)** | 800 – 1500 | Bingham | Spreadability vs. firmness | $\beta'$ |
| **Margarine (5°C)** | 500 – 1200 | Herschel–Bulkley | Spreadability, oil-off | $\beta'$ |
| **Shortening** | 200 – 600 | Power Law | Aeration, creaming | $\beta'$ |
| **Dark Chocolate (40°C)** | 5 – 25 | Casson | Mold-filling, snap | $\beta$ (Form V) |
| **Milk Chocolate (40°C)** | 10 – 30 | Casson | Gloss, snap | $\beta$ (Form V) |
| **Mayonnaise** | 30 – 80 | Herschel–Bulkley | Thixotropy, spoon-ability | N/A (emulsion) |
| **Ice Cream (–5°C)** | 2000 – 5000 | Bingham | Scoopability, meltdown | Partial coalescence |
| **Whipped Cream (5°C)** | 100 – 400 | Power Law ($n < 1$) | Stiffness, stability | $\beta'$ network + air |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define polymorphism in fats. List the three main polymorphic forms in order of increasing thermodynamic stability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Polymorphism is the ability of a triacylglycerol to crystallize into **multiple distinct crystal packing arrangements** with the same chemical composition but different physical properties.

Three forms in increasing stability:
1. **$\alpha$ (alpha)** — Hexagonal sub-cell, lowest melting point, least stable.
2. **$\beta'$ (beta prime)** — Orthorhombic sub-cell, metastable, gives fine texture; preferred in margarine and shortening.
3. **$\beta$ (beta)** — Triclinic sub-cell, most stable, coarse crystals; preferred in chocolate for snap and gloss.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correct order with sub-cell details.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**What is Solid Fat Content (SFC) and how is it measured?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Solid Fat Content (SFC) is the **percentage of fat that exists in the solid crystalline state** at a given temperature, expressed as:

$$SFC(\%) = \dfrac{\text{Mass of solid fat}}{\text{Total mass of fat}} \times 100$$

It is measured most commonly by **Pulsed Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (p-NMR)**, which exploits the difference in **spin-lattice relaxation times** between solid and liquid protons. Other methods include:
- **Dilatometry** (volume change on melting).
- **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** (heat of fusion).
- **Wide-line NMR**.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for p-NMR principle, 1 mark for alternative methods.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> In Part B numerical/model questions, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** for:
> 1. **Forgetting to report units** of $\tau_0$ (Pa) and $\eta$ (Pa·s).
> 2. **Using a wrong model** (e.g., applying Power Law when a yield stress is evident).
> 3. **Failing to linearize** the Casson equation (i.e., directly plotting $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$).
> 4. **Confusing $\alpha$, $\beta'$, $\beta$ polymorphs** with respect to stability and industrial preference.
> Always linearize Casson data by plotting $\sqrt{\tau}$ vs $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$.

---

### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **rheological behavior of butter and margarine** with suitable constitutive models. Explain why **polymorphic form $\beta'$ is industrially preferred** in these products. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fat sample at 25°C is tested in a rotational rheometer. Determine which constitutive model best fits the following data and compute the model parameters.

| $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | 1 | 5 | 20 | 50 | 100 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau$ (Pa) | 12.0 | 18.5 | 32.0 | 48.6 | 70.0 |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a) — Butter and Margarine Rheology**

**Butter Rheology:**
- A **water-in-oil emulsion** (≈ 80% fat, 16% water, 2% milk solids, 2% salt).
- The continuous phase is a **fat crystal network** in $\beta'$ form.
- Below yield stress: **elastic solid**; above yield stress: **plastic flow**.
- The fat crystal network is **destabilized by temperature abuse** → "oiling off."
- **Bingham model** is often a first approximation: $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$.
- Typical $\tau_0 \approx 800$–$1500$ Pa at 5°C.
- Butter exhibits **thixotropy** because the network is partially recoverable on resting.

**Margarine Rheology:**
- Also a **water-in-oil emulsion**, but with **vegetable oils** (sunflower, soy, palm) and hydrogenated fats.
- Industrial goal: maintain $\beta'$ polymorph → **fine crystals** → smooth texture, no graininess.
- Crystallization controlled in **scraped surface heat exchangers (SSHE)** with precise cooling profiles.
- **Herschel–Bulkley model** is more appropriate: $\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$, where $n < 1$ indicates **shear-thinning**.
- Spreadability correlates inversely with $\tau_0$ at refrigerator temperature.
- **Hardness** quantified by **cone penetrometry** (depth of cone penetration under standard load).

**Why $\beta'$ is preferred:**
- $\beta'$ crystals are **fine, needle-shaped (sub-micron)**, giving a **smooth, plastic texture**.
- $\beta$ crystals are **large, coarse, platelet-shaped**, producing **grainy mouthfeel**.
- $\beta' \to \beta$ transformation is **irreversible** in normal storage; avoiding it requires careful tempering.
- $\beta'$ is metastable — kinetically stable under proper conditions.

*(Marks: 2 for butter, 2 for margarine, 2 for $\beta'$ justification, 1 for constitutive models.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b) — Model Identification and Parameter Estimation**

**Step 1: Check for Yield Stress**

At $\dot{\gamma} = 1 \, s^{-1}$, $\tau = 12.0$ Pa $\neq 0$. The material has a non-zero stress at low shear, suggesting a **yield stress**. This rules out Power Law and Newtonian models. **Try Herschel–Bulkley**:

$$\tau - \tau_0 = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

We need to estimate $\tau_0$. Extrapolate linearly at low $\dot{\gamma}$:

Using first two points: slope $= (18.5 - 12.0)/(5 - 1) = 6.5/4 = 1.625$ Pa·s. Extrapolated to $\dot{\gamma} = 0$:

$$\tau_0 \approx 12.0 - 1.625 \times 1 = 10.375 \; \text{Pa}$$

Try $\tau_0 = 10$ Pa. Compute $\tau - \tau_0$:

| $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | $\tau$ (Pa) | $\tau - \tau_0$ (Pa) | $\log \dot{\gamma}$ | $\log(\tau - \tau_0)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.0 | 2.0 | 0.000 | 0.301 |
| 5 | 18.5 | 8.5 | 0.699 | 0.929 |
| 20 | 32.0 | 22.0 | 1.301 | 1.342 |
| 50 | 48.6 | 38.6 | 1.699 | 1.587 |
| 100 | 70.0 | 60.0 | 2.000 | 1.778 |

**Step 2: Power-Law Regression on Transformed Data**

Slope $n$ (from $\log(\tau - \tau_0)$ vs $\log \dot{\gamma}$):

$$n = \dfrac{N \sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{N \sum X^{2} - (\sum X)^{2}}$$

$X = \log \dot{\gamma}, \; Y = \log(\tau - \tau_0)$:

$\sum X = 0.000 + 0.699 + 1.301 + 1.699 + 2.000 = 5.699$

$\sum Y = 0.301 + 0.929 + 1.342 + 1.587 + 1.778 = 5.937$

$\sum X^{2} = 0.000 + 0.489 + 1.693 + 2.887 + 4.000 = 9.069$

$\sum XY = 0.000 + 0.649 + 1.746 + 2.696 + 3.556 = 8.647$

$$n = \dfrac{5 \times 8.647 - 5.699 \times 5.937}{5 \times 9.069 - (5.699)^{2}} = \dfrac{43.235 - 33.835}{45.345 - 32.479} = \dfrac{9.400}{12.866} = 0.731$$

Since $0 < n < 1$, the material is **shear-thinning** — consistent with Herschel–Bulkley.

**Step 3: Compute $K$**

$$\log K = \bar{Y} - n \bar{X} = \dfrac{5.937}{5} - 0.731 \times \dfrac{5.699}{5} = 1.1874 - 0.8331 = 0.3543$$

$$K = 10^{0.3543} = 2.263 \; \text{Pa} \cdot s^{n}$$

**Step 4: Final Model**

$$\boxed{\tau = 10.0 + 2.263 \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{0.731} \; \text{Pa}}$$

**Verification at $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \, s^{-1}$:**
$\tau = 10 + 2.263 \times 50^{0.731} = 10 + 2.263 \times 17.07 = 10 + 38.62 = 48.62$ Pa ✓ (matches 48.6 Pa).

*(Marks: 2 for model identification, 2 for transformation, 2 for regression, 1 for final model + verification.)*

---

### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **fat crystal network model** in detail. Explain the concepts of **flocculation, sintering, and partial coalescence** with their rheological significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dark chocolate is tested in a rotational rheometer at 40°C. Using the Casson model, determine the **yield stress and Casson viscosity** from the following data:

| $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | 2 | 10 | 30 | 80 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau$ (Pa) | 8.5 | 16.0 | 25.5 | 41.0 |

Comment on whether the chocolate is **industrially acceptable** for enrobing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a) — Fat Crystal Network Model**

**Network Architecture:**

The mechanical rigidity of plastic fats arises from a **three-dimensional network of fat crystals** dispersed in liquid oil. The network is described at three structural levels:

1. **Primary structure** — Molecular: polymorphism, fatty acid chain length, saturation, cis/trans geometry.
2. **Secondary structure** — Sub-micron: crystal habit (platelets, needles, spherulites), governed by cooling rate and polymorph.
3. **Tertiary structure** — Network level: how crystals aggregate, the bond density, and the mechanical rigidity.

**Flocculation:**
- Crystals in liquid oil experience **van der Waals attraction** and **depletion flocculation** from liquid oil minor components.
- Flocculation creates **clusters of crystals** held by weak physical bonds.
- Rheological effect: **increases $\tau_0$** dramatically (percolation threshold can be reached at low SFC).
- Reversible under shear.

**Sintering (Welding):**
- At temperatures close to the melting point, **liquid bridges** form between adjacent crystals.
- These bridges **weld** crystals into stronger aggregates.
- Rheological effect: **time-dependent hardening**; fat becomes firmer on storage.
- Irreversible on the timescale of interest; leads to "post-hardening" of butter and margarine.

**Partial Coalescence:**
- Occurs in **emulsions** (ice cream, whipped cream) where fat crystals **pierce the air–water or oil–water interface**.
- Crystals protrude from one droplet and **penetrate an adjacent droplet**, locking them.
- Rheological effect: **gel network** with very high $\tau_0$ at low fat content; basis of whipped cream and ice cream texture.

*(Marks: 3 for network levels, 2 for flocculation, 1 for sintering, 1 for partial coalescence.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b) — Casson Model for Chocolate**

**Step 1: Linearization**

The Casson equation in linear form:
$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_c} \cdot \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$$

Transform the data:

| $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ | $\sqrt{\tau}$ |
|---|---|
| $\sqrt{2} = 1.414$ | $\sqrt{8.5} = 2.915$ |
| $\sqrt{10} = 3.162$ | $\sqrt{16.0} = 4.000$ |
| $\sqrt{30} = 5.477$ | $\sqrt{25.5} = 5.050$ |
| $\sqrt{80} = 8.944$ | $\sqrt{41.0} = 6.403$ |

**Step 2: Linear Regression**

Let $X = \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$, $Y = \sqrt{\tau}$, $N = 4$:

$\sum X = 1.414 + 3.162 + 5.477 + 8.944 = 18.997$

$\sum Y = 2.915 + 4.000 + 5.050 + 6.403 = 18.368$

$\sum X^{2} = 2.000 + 10.000 + 30.000 + 80.000 = 122.000$

$\sum XY = (1.414)(2.915) + (3.162)(4.000) + (5.477)(5.050) + (8.944)(6.403)$

Computing term by term:
- $(1.414)(2.915) = 4.123$
- $(3.162)(4.000) = 12.648$
- $(5.477)(5.050) = 27.659$
- $(8.944)(6.403) = 57.270$

$\sum XY = 4.123 + 12.648 + 27.659 + 57.270 = 101.700$

**Slope $m = \sqrt{\eta_c}$:**

$$m = \dfrac{N \sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{N \sum X^{2} - (\sum X)^{2}}$$

$$m = \dfrac{4 \times 101.700 - 18.997 \times 18.368}{4 \times 122.000 - (18.997)^{2}}$$

Numerator: $406.800 - 348.937 = 57.863$

Denominator: $488.000 - 360.886 = 127.114$

$$m = \dfrac{57.863}{127.114} = 0.4553$$

**Intercept $b = \sqrt{\tau_0}$:**

$$b = \dfrac{\sum Y - m \sum X}{N} = \dfrac{18.368 - 0.4553 \times 18.997}{4} = \dfrac{18.368 - 8.649}{4} = \dfrac{9.719}{4} = 2.430$$

**Step 3: Extract Parameters**

$$\tau_0 = b^{2} = (2.430)^{2} = 5.905 \; \text{Pa}$$

$$\eta_c = m^{2} = (0.4553)^{2} = 0.2073 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step 4: Industrial Assessment**

For dark chocolate enrobing, industrial specifications at 40°C are:
- $\tau_0 = 5$–$15$ Pa
- $\eta_c = 0.1$–$0.4$ Pa·s

Our values: $\tau_0 = 5.91$ Pa (✓ within range) and $\eta_c = 0.207$ Pa·s (✓ within range).

**Conclusion: The chocolate is industrially acceptable for enrobing.** The yield stress is sufficient to prevent dripping, and the viscosity is low enough for smooth coating.

*(Marks: 2 for linearization, 2 for regression calculation, 1 for parameter extraction, 2 for industrial assessment.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Fat and Fat Products**

- ⭐ **Polymorphism:** $\alpha \to \beta' \to \beta$ (increasing stability, irreversible). $\beta'$ = margarine/shortening; $\beta$ = chocolate.
- ⭐ **Solid Fat Content (SFC)** is measured by **pulsed NMR**; reported at 10°C, 20°C, 30°C, 40°C.
- ⭐ **Yield stress $\tau_0$** is a **system property**, not a molecular one — it emerges from the network.
- ⭐ **Casson model** is the **industry standard for chocolate**; always linearize by plotting $\sqrt{\tau}$ vs $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$.
- ⭐ **Herschel–Bulkley** is the most general plastic model: $\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$ (use for margarine, butter).
- ⭐ **Bingham plastic** is a special case of Herschel–Bulkley with $n = 1$.
- ⭐ **Fat crystal network** has three structural levels: molecular, microscopic, macroscopic.
- ⭐ **Flocculation** $\to$ reversible aggregation; **sintering** $\to$ irreversible welding; **partial coalescence** $\to$ gel network in emulsions.
- ⭐ **Arrhenius equation** governs oil viscosity vs. temperature dependence.
- ⭐ **WLF equation** governs polymer/fat viscoelastic time–temperature superposition.
- ⭐ **Cone penetrometry** is the most common industrial test for fat hardness.
- ⭐ **Shear-thinning** ($n < 1$) is universally observed in structured fats.
- ⭐ Industrial chocolate target: $\tau_0 = 5$–$25$ Pa, $\eta_c = 0.1$–$0.4$ Pa·s at 40°C.
- ⭐ **Tempering** of chocolate is required to obtain the desired $\beta$ (Form V) polymorph.
- ⭐ **Avoid $\beta' \to \beta$ transition** in margarine — leads to graininess and oil-off.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumental Measurements of Textural Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Instrumental Measurements of Textural Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Topic

**Instrumental Measurement of Textural Properties** refers to the standardized, objective, and quantitative characterization of the mechanical, geometrical, and surface attributes of a food material using dedicated physical testing devices called **Texture Analyzers** (e.g., TA-XTPlus, TA.HDPlus, Instron Universal Testing Machine, Stevens-LFRA Texture Analyser). The measurement is performed by applying a precisely controlled **deformation** (compression, penetration, shear, cutting, extrusion, bending, or tension) to the food sample via a calibrated probe/cell and recording the resultant **force–time** or **force–deformation** response. This response is the instrumental fingerprint of texture.

According to the **International Standard ISO 11036 (Sensory analysis — Methodology — Texture profile)**, texture is a *"multiparametric attribute resulting from a combination of several mechanical, geometrical, and surface characteristics"* — and it is the role of instrumental methods to **emulate and quantify** these sensations using engineering principles of **force, displacement, velocity, and work**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The course outcome stresses the ability of the student to *"select, justify, and operate appropriate instruments for measuring texture in a wide range of food systems and to interpret the resulting force–deformation curves in terms of fundamental rheological parameters."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Food Detective's Toolkit"

Imagine you are a **detective** in a forensic lab, but instead of solving crimes, you are solving the mystery of *"What does this food feel like in the mouth?"* Each instrument is a different interrogation tool:

- **Compression test** → Like a **press squeezing a sponge** to see how much it squishes and bounces back. It tells you *hardness* and *springiness*.
- **Penetration test** → Like a **finger poking a ripe avocado** or a **needle testing cheese firmness**. It tells you how the surface resists localized indentation.
- **Shear test (Kramer / Warner-Bratzler)** → Like **scissors cutting through meat fibers** or a **guillotine slicing a sausage**. It measures *fibrousness* and *chewiness*.
- **Cutting test (Warner-Bratzler blade / Volodkevich bite)** → Mimics the **action of incisor teeth** during the very first bite.
- **Extrusion test (Back-extrusion / Ottawa cell)** → Like **squeezing a tube of toothpaste from the bottom** while measuring the force on a stationary piston. It measures *consistency* and *cohesiveness* of pastes/gels.
- **Tension test** → Like **pulling a piece of cheese apart** or stretching a noodle. It measures *tensile strength* and *extensibility*.
- **Bending test (3-point bend)** → Like **snapping a chocolate bar** over a fulcrum. It measures *brittleness* and *fracture force*.
- **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** → The **"double-squeeze interview"** — the food is compressed twice, mimicking the molar teeth action during one complete chew cycle, and seven parameters are extracted from a single curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:**
> Instrumental texture tests are broadly classified into three families:
>
> 1. **Imitative (or Sensory) tests** — they *imitate* the mouth action (TPA, Warner-Bratzler).
> 2. **Fundamental tests** — they *measure* a true engineering property (Young's modulus, true shear stress, viscosity).
> 3. **Empirical tests** — practical, product-specific, comparative tests (penetrometer, Brookfield Texture Probe for ketchup).
>
> The KTU examiner expects the student to **identify which family** a given test belongs to and **justify the probe/cell geometry** used.

---

## 1.3 Core Physical Quantities and Units

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Magnitude in Foods |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force | $F$ | **Newton (N)** | 0.01 – 500 N |
| Deformation / Penetration depth | $d$ | **metre (m)** | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-2}$ m |
| Engineering Stress | $\sigma$ | **Pascal (Pa, N·m⁻²)** | $10^{2}$ – $10^{6}$ Pa |
| Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon$ | **dimensionless (−)** | 0.05 – 1.0 |
| Strain rate | $\dot{\gamma}$ | **s⁻¹** | 0.01 – 100 s⁻¹ |
| Work / Energy of deformation | $W$ | **Joule (J)** | $10^{-4}$ – 10 J |
| Hardness (TPA) | $H$ | **N** | 1 – 100 N |
| Cross-head speed | $v$ | **m·s⁻¹** | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-1}$ m·s⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard reference cross-head speed for **TPA on a 1 cm cube** is **$v = 1.0 \text{ mm·s}^{-1}$** (or 60 mm·min⁻¹), as recommended by **Bourne (1978)** and adopted universally by KTU practical laboratories.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Classification of Instrumental Texture Tests

The instrumental measurement of food texture is a **rheo-mechanical** operation. The taxonomy of tests is built around two axes: **the type of stress applied** and **the deformation mode**.

### Step 1 — Identify the dominant stress mode

- **Compressive (uniaxial, between parallel plates)** → TPA, bulk compression.
- **Penetrative (localized)** → cone/needle penetrometer, puncture test.
- **Shear (multiaxial cutting)** → Kramer Shear Cell, Warner-Bratzler blade.
- **Extrusive (radial back-flow)** → Back-extrusion (Ottawa cell).
- **Tensile (uniaxial pulling)** → Tensile grips for noodles, gels, films.
- **Bending (3-point flexural)** → Snap test for chocolate, biscuits.
- **Torsional (rotational)** → Parallel-plate torsional rheometer (also used for small-amplitude oscillatory texture analysis).

### Step 2 — Identify the deformation mode

- **Large-deformation solid-like** → Foods fractured or permanently deformed.
- **Large-deformation liquid-like** → Foods flow under their own weight (extrusion).
- **Small-deformation linear viscoelastic** → Foods below the yield stress.

### Step 3 — Choose the parameter to be quantified

- *Hardness* (peak force in compression) → compression / penetration.
- *Shear strength / toughness* → Kramer, Warner-Bratzler.
- *Adhesiveness* → TPA negative area or pull-away test.
- *Consistency / firmness of pastes* → back-extrusion.
- *Brittleness* → bending or puncture.

---

## 2.2 The Force–Deformation / Force–Time Curve — The "Signature"

Almost every instrumental texture test yields a **force–deformation (F–d)** or **force–time (F–t)** curve. The shape of this curve encodes *all* the textural information. The student must read it like a cardiogram.

For a typical **TPA double-compression curve**, the following landmarks are critical:

1. **Peak A** — Maximum force during the first down-stroke → **Hardness**.
2. **Area under the first down-stroke (A1)** — Work of compression.
3. **Negative area (A2)** during the up-stroke → **Adhesiveness** (work needed to overcome the sticky contact between probe and food).
4. **Area under the second down-stroke (A3)** → Work of second compression.
5. **Time / distance at peak A** → $\varepsilon_{1}$.
6. **Time / distance at second peak B** → $\varepsilon_{2}$.
7. **Recovery ratio** → $\varepsilon_{2}/\varepsilon_{1}$ → **Springiness / Elasticity**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **exam-day cheat sheet**. Memorize the symbolic definitions and SI units. In equations, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value (do **not** write a vertical bar inside the table cells).

| # | Parameter | Defining Equation | Units | Typical Food |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Engineering Stress** | $\sigma \;=\; F / A_{0}$ | Pa | — |
| 2 | **Engineering Strain** | $\varepsilon \;=\; \Delta L / L_{0}$ | — | — |
| 3 | **Young's Modulus (E)** | $E \;=\; \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Pa | Apples, cheese |
| 4 | **Hardness, $H$ (TPA)** | $H \equiv F_{\text{peak,1}}$ | N | Most solids |
| 5 | **Cohesiveness, $C$ (TPA)** | $C \;=\; A_{3} / A_{1}$ | — | Gels, cheese |
| 6 | **Adhesiveness, $A_{d}$ (TPA)** | $A_{d} \;=\; \lvert A_{2} \rvert$ | N·s or J | Cheese, jam |
| 7 | **Springiness, $S$ (TPA)** | $S \;=\; \varepsilon_{2} / \varepsilon_{1}$ | — | Bread, sponge cake |
| 8 | **Gumminess, $G$ (TPA)** | $G \;=\; H \times C$ | N | Gummy candy, cheese |
| 9 | **Chewiness, $Ch$ (TPA)** | $Ch \;=\; G \times S \;=\; H \times C \times S$ | N | Meat, toffees |
| 10 | **Resilience, $R$ (TPA)** | $R \;=\; A_{r} / A_{\text{up-stroke,1}}$ | — | Aerated products |
| 11 | **Shear Stress (Kramer)** | $\tau \;=\; F / A_{\text{blades}}$ | Pa | Meat, vegetables |
| 12 | **Shear Strength (W-B)** | $\tau_{\text{WB}} \;=\; F_{\max} / A_{\text{cut}}$ | Pa | Cooked meat |
| 13 | **Penetration Index (PI)** | $PI \;=\; d^{3/2} / M^{1/2}$ | — | Cosmetic / food penetration |
| 14 | **Work of Penetration** | $W_{p} \;=\; \int_{0}^{d_{\max}} F\, dd$ | J | All penetrating tests |
| 15 | **Bending Modulus** | $E_{b} \;=\; F L^{3} / (4 b h^{3} \delta)$ | Pa | Chocolate bars |

Where:
- $A_{0}$ = original cross-sectional area of the sample.
- $L_{0}$ = original length; $\Delta L$ = change in length.
- $A_{1}$ = positive area of first compression; $A_{3}$ = positive area of second compression; $A_{2}$ = negative area (work of adhesion).
- $A_{r}$ = area of the up-stroke immediately following peak A (resilience numerator).
- $A_{\text{blades}}$ = total area of Kramer shear blades; $A_{\text{cut}}$ = cross-section of Warner-Bratzler cut.
- $F_{\max}$ = peak shear force in a Warner-Bratzler cut.
- $b, h, L$ = width, height, and support span of a 3-point bend specimen; $\delta$ = central deflection at break.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Process Design**: Hardness data inform the energy requirement for **cutting, milling, and chewing**; shear strength dictates the power draw of a **meat dicer** or **cheese shredder**.
- **Quality Control (QC)**: A standard Warner-Bratzler test at 24 h post-mortem is the **international gold standard** for cooked-beef tenderness grading.
- **Shelf-life Engineering**: Hardness decay curves of bread (crumb firmness) and jam (adhesiveness loss) drive **MAP and HPP** (high-pressure processing) shelf-life models.
- **Sensory-Instrumental Correlation**: TPA parameters are statistically correlated (Pearson / PCA) with trained-panel sensory scores to build **predictive texture models**.
- **3D Food Printing**: Tensile strength of printable gels sets the maximum overhang angle and printing speed.
- **R&D Reformulation**: When fat or sugar is reduced, *springiness* and *cohesiveness* collapse first — TPA quantifies this **compensatory hydrocolloid** requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering "Why" Summary:**
> Every instrumental texture test is, at its core, the **measurement of a force required to induce a prescribed deformation at a controlled rate, temperature, and geometry** — and the conversion of that force–deformation record into either a *sensory-mimicking index* (TPA) or a *fundamental material constant* ($E$, $\tau$, $\eta$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **mandatory** for full marks in KTU ESE numerical questions. The student is expected to write out **each algebraic transition** with a justification row.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the "Chewness" Identity in TPA

The TPA defines **Chewiness** as a derived parameter — it is *not* measured directly. It is constructed from the measured Hardness, Cohesiveness, and Springiness. The KTU examiner loves asking *"Show that $Ch = H \times C \times S$."*

### Step 1 — Define the three measured (primary) TPA parameters

- Hardness: $H \equiv F_{\text{peak,1}}$
- Cohesiveness: $C \equiv A_{3}/A_{1}$
- Springiness: $S \equiv \varepsilon_{2}/\varepsilon_{1}$

### Step 2 — Define Gumminess as the work-related property

In the **Szczesniak (1963) → Bourne (1978)** lineage, Gumminess is defined as *"the energy required to disintegrate a semi-solid food to a state ready for swallowing"*. For a non-brittle, non-elastic semi-solid (a gum), the strain energy is proportional to *Force × recoverable strain*. Dimensionally, $H$ has units of N (force), and $C$ is dimensionless — so multiplying $H$ by $C$ yields a quantity that scales with the *cohesive binding* of the matrix.

Therefore, the working definition adopted by the international standard is:

$$
G \;\equiv\; H \times C
$$

### Step 3 — Define Chewiness for a solid (springy) food

For a **solid food that must be chewed** (a chewy solid, e.g., meat or toffee), the energy required for swallowing is the Gumminess *scaled* by the recoverable strain (Springiness). The reasoning is that a more elastic chew returns energy back to the jaw, demanding *more* chews. Hence:

$$
Ch \;\equiv\; G \times S
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $G$ to obtain the final composite identity

$$
Ch \;=\; (H \times C) \times S
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Ch \;=\; H \times C \times S\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Dimensional check

$H$ has units of N; $C$ is dimensionless; $S$ is dimensionless. Therefore, $Ch$ has units of **N (Newton)**, which is the standard unit for Chewiness in TPA instrumentation. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kramer Shear Stress

The Kramer Shear Cell consists of a **rectangular box of $N$ parallel blades** (typically 8 or 10) that pass through slots in the bottom of the cell, shearing the food that is held in the cell. The total shearing surface is the **lateral surface of all blades combined**.

### Step 1 — Identify the geometry of one blade

Let:
- $L$ = length of one blade (m)
- $w$ = width of one blade (m)
- The blade has **two shearing faces** (top and bottom), and the food rests on a slotted base through which the blade travels.

### Step 2 — Write the shearing surface of one blade

For one blade, the two faces give an area $2Lw$. For $N$ blades, the total shearing surface area is:

$$
A_{\text{Kramer}} \;=\; 2 N L w
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Newton's definition of shear stress

Shear stress is the **tangential force per unit area**:

$$
\tau_{\text{Kramer}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\max}}{A_{\text{Kramer}}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\max}}{2 N L w}
$$

### Step 4 — Sanity check

For a typical Kramer cell with $N = 8$ blades of $L = 0.070$ m and $w = 0.005$ m:

$$
A_{\text{Kramer}} \;=\; 2 \times 8 \times 0.070 \times 0.005 \;=\; 5.6 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

For a peak force of, say, 200 N measured on cooked vegetables:

$$
\tau_{\text{Kramer}} \;=\; \frac{200}{5.6 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 3.57 \times 10^{4} \; \text{Pa} \;=\; 35.7 \; \text{kPa}
$$

This value is consistent with the published **shear-strength of cooked carrots** (≈ 20–80 kPa). ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the 3-Point Bending Stress for a Chocolate Bar

A 3-point bend test places a rectangular bar on two supports (span $L$) and loads it from above at the midpoint. The classical Euler-Bernoulli solution for the maximum bending stress at the mid-span is:

$$
\sigma_{\text{bend}} \;=\; \frac{3 F_{\text{break}} L}{2 b h^{2}}
$$

where:
- $F_{\text{break}}$ = force at fracture (N)
- $L$ = support span (m)
- $b$ = bar width (m)
- $h$ = bar thickness (m)

### Step 1 — Derive from beam theory (compact form)

For a centrally loaded simply-supported beam of rectangular cross-section, the **bending moment at mid-span** is $M = FL/4$. The **section modulus** of a rectangle is $Z = bh^{2}/6$. The maximum bending stress is $\sigma = M/Z$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{bend}} \;=\; \frac{M}{Z} \;=\; \frac{FL/4}{bh^{2}/6} \;=\; \frac{FL}{4} \cdot \frac{6}{bh^{2}} \;=\; \frac{6FL}{4 b h^{2}} \;=\; \frac{3FL}{2 b h^{2}}
$$

### Step 2 — Confirm units

Numerator: $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$. Denominator: $\text{m}\cdot\text{m}^{2} = \text{m}^{3}$. Ratio: $\text{N}\cdot\text{m} / \text{m}^{3} = \text{N/m}^{2} = \text{Pa}$. ✓

### Step 3 — Worked numerical example

A milk-chocolate bar of dimensions $L = 0.060$ m, $b = 0.025$ m, $h = 0.008$ m breaks at $F_{\text{break}} = 18$ N. Compute the bending stress.

$$
\sigma_{\text{bend}} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 18 \times 0.060}{2 \times 0.025 \times (0.008)^{2}}
$$

Compute denominator: $2 \times 0.025 \times 0.000064 = 3.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m³.
Compute numerator: $3 \times 18 \times 0.060 = 3.24$ N·m.

$$
\sigma_{\text{bend}} \;=\; \frac{3.24}{3.2 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 1.01 \times 10^{6} \;\text{Pa} \;=\; 1.01 \;\text{MPa}
$$

This is **physically realistic** for the flexural strength of milk chocolate (≈ 0.5 – 2 MPa). ✓

---

## 3.4 The Conversion of the Adhesiveness Area to Energy

The "negative area" $A_{2}$ in a TPA curve is the area of the portion of the curve that lies **below the time axis** during the up-stroke of the first cycle. It is the **work of adhesion** — the energy required to overcome the sticky contact between the probe and the food.

If the cross-head speed is $v$ (m·s⁻¹), then time $t$ relates to displacement $d$ by $d = vt$. The area under the $F$–$d$ curve has units of $\text{N}\cdot\text{m} = \text{J}$:

$$
A_{d} \;=\; \lvert A_{2} \rvert \;=\; \left\vert \int F \, dd \right\vert \;=\; \left\vert \int F \, v \, dt \right\vert
$$

Thus the unit of $A_{d}$ can be reported as **N·s** (if integrated against time) or **J** (if integrated against displacement) — they are equivalent because $v$ is constant.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Selecting a Test

| If the food is … | Dominant sensory attribute | Recommended test | Probe / Cell | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Solid gel (cheese, tofu) | Hardness, springiness, chewiness | **TPA** (2-cycle compression) | Flat plate (P/35, P/75) | $H, C, S, G, Ch, A_{d}, R$ |
| Fibrous solid (meat) | Fibrousness, tenderness | **Warner-Bratzler** | V-shaped blade, 1 mm thick | $F_{\max}$ and $\tau_{\text{WB}}$ |
| Particulate / chunky (stew, peas) | Multi-element shear | **Kramer Shear Cell** | 8–10 parallel blades | $\tau_{\text{Kramer}}$ |
| Surface skin (apple, sausage) | Skin strength, crispness | **Puncture** | Cylindrical / spherical probe | $F_{\text{puncture}}, d_{\text{fracture}}$ |
| Soft / semi-fluid (yogurt, ketchup) | Consistency, firmness | **Back-extrusion (Ottawa cell)** | Annular piston, slotted base | $F_{\text{steady-state}}$, consistency |
| Brittle sheet (chocolate, biscuit) | Snap, fracturability | **3-point bending** | 3-point bend rig | $F_{\text{break}}, \sigma_{\text{bend}}$ |
| Sticky / elastic (cheese, gum) | Stickiness, stringiness | **Adhesion / pull-away** | Flat plate, withdrawal | $F_{\text{pull-off}}, A_{d}$ |
| Thin / filamentous (noodle) | Tensile strength, elongation | **Tensile (uniaxial)** | Tensile grips (A/TG) | $\sigma_{\text{ten}}, \varepsilon_{\text{break}}$ |
| Filled / encapsulated (burst test) | Skin burst pressure | **Puncture-to-burst** | Spherical probe | $F_{\text{burst}}, d_{\text{burst}}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Master Taxonomy of Instrumental Texture Tests

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Instrumental Texture Tests]:::root
    A0 --> B1[Imitative Sensory Tests]
    A0 --> B2[Fundamental Engineering Tests]
    A0 --> B3[Empirical Practical Tests]

    B1 --> C1[Texture Profile Analysis TPA]
    B1 --> C2[Warner-Bratzler Cut]
    B1 --> C3[Volodkevich Bite]

    B2 --> C4[Uniaxial Compression]
    B2 --> C5[Back Extrusion]
    B2 --> C6[3-Point Bending]
    B2 --> C7[Uniaxial Tensile]
    B2 --> C8[Torsional Rheometry]

    B3 --> C9[Krame Shear Cell]
    B3 --> C10[Penetrometer Cone Needle]
    B3 --> C11[Puncture Probe]
    B3 --> C12[Ottawa Cell]

    C1 --> D1[Hardness H]
    C1 --> D2[Cohesiveness C]
    C1 --> D3[Springiness S]
    C1 --> D4[Adhesiveness Ad]
    C1 --> D5[Gumminess G]
    C1 --> D6[Chewiness Ch]
    C1 --> D7[Resilience R]

    C2 --> E1[Shear Force Fmax]
    C2 --> E2[WB Shear Stress tauWB]

    C4 --> F1[True Stress Sigma]
    C4 --> F2[True Strain Epsilon]
    C4 --> F3[Modulus E]

    C5 --> G1[Steady Force Fss]
    C5 --> G2[Consistency Index]

    C6 --> H1[Break Force Fbreak]

    C8 --> I1[G Prime G Double Prime]
    C8 --> I2[Loss Tan Delta]
```


## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — The TPA Double-Compression Curve

```
  Force (N)
    ^
    |         A
    |        /\
    |       /  \              B
    |      /    \            /\
    |     /      \          /  \
    |    /   A1   \        / A3 \
    |   /          \      /      \
    |  /            \    /        \
    | /              \  /          \
    |/                \/            \
----+-------------------------------------> Displacement (mm)
    |  <--->       <--->          <-->
    |    d1        d2
    |   (epsilon 1)  (epsilon 2)
    |                 A2 = negative area (adhesiveness, plotted below the axis)
```

**Annotations (ASCII safe, no markdown bolding inside Mermaid):**
- Peak A = HARDNESS (max force of first compression)
- Peak B = Hardness of second compression (used to compute Cohesiveness)
- Area A1 = Work of first compression
- Area A3 = Work of second compression
- Cohesiveness = A3 divided by A1
- Springiness = epsilon 2 divided by epsilon 1
- Area A2 = Adhesiveness (work done against the food stickiness on up-stroke)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight from the Schematic:**
> The ratio of **Peak B / Peak A** is called the **Hardness Recovery Ratio** and serves as a quick check on the *cohesiveness* of the food. A ratio close to 1.0 (e.g., a stiff cheese) indicates a brittle, low-cohesion sample; a ratio of 0.4–0.6 (e.g., a marshmallow) indicates a highly cohesive, rubbery structure.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define Texture Profile Analysis (TPA). List and briefly define the seven primary parameters derived from a TPA curve.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) is an **imitative, double-compression instrumental test** developed by Bourne (1978) and modelled on the Szczesniak (1963) sensory texture profile. The food sample (usually a cylinder or cube) is compressed to a prescribed strain (typically 25–75 %) twice in succession by a flat-plate probe, mimicking the action of the molar teeth during one complete chew. The resulting force–time (or force–deformation) curve is the **TPA fingerprint**, from which seven primary textural parameters are extracted.

**The Seven Primary TPA Parameters:**

1. **Hardness (H)** — the peak force recorded during the first compression cycle; represents the force required to attain a given deformation.
2. **Cohesiveness (C)** — the ratio of the positive area under the second compression curve to the positive area under the first compression curve; represents the strength of internal bonds.
3. **Adhesiveness (Ad)** — the magnitude of the *negative* area under the first up-stroke; represents the work needed to overcome sticky attractive forces between the food and the probe.
4. **Springiness (S)** — the ratio of the deformation at the second peak to the deformation at the first peak; represents the elastic recovery between chews.
5. **Gumminess (G)** — the product $H \times C$; represents the energy required to disintegrate a semi-solid food to a swallowable state.
6. **Chewiness (Ch)** — the product $H \times C \times S$; represents the energy required to chew a solid food to a swallowable state.
7. **Resilience (R)** — the ratio of the area of the up-stroke immediately following the first peak to the area of the down-stroke up to that peak; represents the instantaneous elastic recovery.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students confuse *Gumminess* with *Chewiness*. **Gumminess** is for **semi-solid** foods (gummy candy, soft cheese); **Chewiness** is for **solid** foods (meat, toffee, bread crust). Do not write either for a liquid — that is **Viscosity** (rheometer).

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
*Explain the principle and working of the Kramer Shear Cell. Mention the typical foods for which it is used.*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
The **Kramer Shear Cell** is a multi-bladed compression–shear test designed for **particulate, chunky, or multi-component foods** (e.g., peas, diced carrots, stew, sausages, fish balls) that cannot be characterised by a single-platen compression test. The food is placed in a **rectangular metal cell** fitted with a slotted base. A **set of 8–10 parallel blades** is driven vertically down through the slots, simultaneously **compressing and shearing** the food between the blades and the cell walls. The peak force and the force–deformation curve are recorded.

**Working:**
1. A known mass (typically 100 g) of the food is loaded uniformly into the cell.
2. The plunger carrying the blades descends at a constant cross-head speed (≈ 1.67 mm·s⁻¹).
3. The food is sheared by the edges of the blades as they pass through the slots.
4. The peak force $F_{\max}$ is recorded.
5. The **Kramer Shear Stress** is computed as $\tau_{\text{Kramer}} = F_{\max} / A_{\text{blades}}$ where $A_{\text{blades}} = 2NLw$ (with $N$ blades, each of length $L$ and width $w$).

**Typical foods:** frozen peas, cooked vegetables, canned meat, sausage crumbles, fish balls, corn kernels, and any particulate product.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The Kramer cell is **NOT** a pure shear instrument — it is a **combination of compression and shear**. Do not call it a "shear rheometer". The KTU examiner will deduct a mark if you do not clarify this.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose One

#### (a) Explain the principle, procedure, and instrument geometry of Texture Profile Analysis (TPA). Discuss the seven primary parameters and the derived (composite) parameters in detail, with the equations that interrelate them. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Principle:**
TPA is an **imitative instrumental test** that emulates the mechanical action of the human jaw on a food sample during a single complete chewing cycle. It is performed using a **Universal Texture Analyzer** (e.g., TA-XTPlus, TA.HDPlus, Instron) fitted with a **flat compression plate** (commonly P/35 — 35 mm dia, or P/75 — 75 mm dia). The sample is a regular geometric solid (cube, cylinder) of well-defined dimensions and is compressed **twice in succession** at a constant cross-head speed of **1.0 mm·s⁻¹** to a prescribed strain (commonly 25 %, 50 %, or 75 %, depending on the food and the standard being followed).

**2. Procedure:**
- Acclimate the sample to the test temperature (e.g., 20 ± 2 °C for most foods; 4 °C for butter).
- Place the sample centrally on the lower plate.
- Set the test parameters: pre-test speed 1.0 mm·s⁻¹, test speed 1.0 mm·s⁻¹, post-test speed 1.0 mm·s⁻¹, target strain 50 % of original height, trigger force 5 g, two compression cycles with a 5 s wait between them.
- Run the test; record the force–time curve.
- Use the instrument software to compute the seven TPA parameters.

**3. Instrument Geometry:**
- **Probe (upper):** flat-faced cylinder P/35 or P/75, made of polished stainless steel, attached to the moving crosshead.
- **Load cell:** 5 kg (50 N) or 30 kg (300 N) range, calibrated, mounted between the probe and the crosshead.
- **Sample stage (lower):** flat, polished, rigid metal platen.
- **Drive mechanism:** servo-motor driven screw, with closed-loop speed control.

**4. The Seven Primary Parameters (from the F–t curve):**

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Source on curve | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hardness | $H$ | Peak force of first compression | Peak A | N |
| 2 | Adhesiveness | $A_{d}$ | Negative area of first up-stroke | $A_{2}$ | N·s |
| 3 | Cohesiveness | $C$ | $A_{3}/A_{1}$ | Areas under cycles | — |
| 4 | Springiness | $S$ | $\varepsilon_{2}/\varepsilon_{1}$ | Time/distances at peaks | — |
| 5 | Resilience | $R$ | $A_{r}/A_{\text{up-1}}$ | Early up-stroke area ratio | — |
| 6 | Gumminess | $G$ | $H \times C$ | Derived | N |
| 7 | Chewiness | $Ch$ | $H \times C \times S$ | Derived | N |

**5. Equations that Interrelate the Parameters:**

$$
C \;=\; \frac{A_{3}}{A_{1}}
$$

$$
S \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{\varepsilon_{1}}
$$

$$
G \;=\; H \times C
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Ch \;=\; H \times C \times S\,}
$$

**6. Engineering utility:**
TPA is the **single most widely used** instrumental texture test in the food industry. It is used for **QC of cheese, bread, cereal bars, gummy candy, toffees, meat analogs, and yogurt gels**; for **reformulation studies** (fat/sugar replacement); and for **sensory-instrumental correlation** via PCA.

---

#### (b) Discuss in detail the various **penetration tests** used in the instrumental measurement of textural properties. Classify them and explain with a neat sketch the working of a **cone penetrometer** and a **puncture test**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Classification of Penetration Tests:**

Penetration tests apply a **localized deformation** to the food surface using a probe of well-defined geometry (cone, needle, sphere, cylinder). The test is essentially an **indentation** in which the force to push the probe to a fixed depth (or the depth reached under a fixed force) is measured.

| Class | Probe geometry | Measured quantity | Typical foods |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cone penetrometer** | Standard cone (ASTM D5, IP 49) | Penetration depth in 5 s under 100 g | Bitumen analogues, fats, margarines, ghee |
| **Needle / needle penetrometer** | Sharp needle (e.g., 1 mm dia, 30° apex) | Depth reached under fixed load | Soft cheese surface, butter, fruit skin |
| **Puncture test (cylindrical/spherical probe)** | Spherical or flat-ended cylindrical rod of finite radius | Peak force at fracture; depth at fracture | Apple, sausage skin, fish, cooked meat patties |
| **Profile penetrometer (Mecmesin / FTC)** | Matched load cell + probe | Continuous force–distance curve | Confectionery, gels |

**2. Working of a Standard Cone Penetrometer (ASTM/IP method):**

- **Components:** A standard cone (90° apex, 65 mm long, polished) is mounted on a release mechanism. The cone shaft carries a **penetration index needle** or **scale** for depth reading.
- **Procedure:** The cone is released and allowed to penetrate the food surface under its own **standard mass (100 g for foods, 150 g for very soft fats)** for a fixed time of **5 seconds**.
- **Measurement:** The depth of penetration (in 0.1 mm units) is read. The deeper the cone sinks, the *softer* the food.
- **Reporting:** The result is reported as *"penetration value"* (e.g., 75 × 0.1 mm = 7.5 mm) under defined temperature and time.
- **Engineering interpretation:** The penetration depth $d$ can be related to the **yield stress** $\sigma_{y}$ of the food via the classical cone-plasticity equation (Bishop, Hill, Mott):

$$
\sigma_{y} \;\approx\; \frac{F}{\pi \, d \, \tan(\alpha/2)}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the cone apex angle and $d$ is the penetration depth.

**3. Working of a Puncture Test:**

- **Components:** A texture analyzer with a **spherical or flat-ended cylindrical probe** (typical diameter 2–8 mm), load cell, and software for force–distance recording.
- **Procedure:** The probe descends at constant speed (≈ 1 mm·s⁻¹) into the food (often a whole fruit, sausage, or patty) until it **fractures the surface** and reaches a specified depth.
- **Measurement:** The **peak force** $F_{\text{puncture}}$ and the **depth at peak** $d_{\text{peak}}$ are recorded.
- **Outputs:**
  - *Skin strength* (force at first peak) for apples, tomatoes, sausages.
  - *Work of puncture* = $\int F \, dd$ from contact to $F_{\text{puncture}}$ — a measure of surface toughness.
  - *Crispness index* = number of force peaks per unit distance — a measure of brittleness/crispness (used in the study of snack foods).

**4. Practical importance:**
Penetration tests are **rapid, non-destructive of the bulk (only the surface is damaged)**, and require minimal sample preparation. They are the most common method in **on-line fruit sorting** (firmness) and in **QC of packaged sausages** (skin strength). They cannot, however, give the *internal cohesiveness* of the food — for that, TPA or compression testing is required.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the principle, working, and engineering analysis of the **Warner-Bratzler shear test** and the **Kramer Shear Cell**. Compare their suitability for different food systems. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Warner-Bratzler (W-B) Shear Test:**

**Principle:** The W-B test measures the **maximum shear force** required to cut a standard cylindrical core of food (typically a 1.27 cm diameter core of cooked meat) using a **V-shaped or flat blade of standard thickness (1.016 mm)**. The blade passes through a triangular slot in the support, ensuring a pure shearing cut across the fibers.

**Working:**
- A cylindrical core of the cooked meat (commonly 1.27 cm dia × 2–3 cm long) is cut using a cork borer.
- The core is placed in the **V-bottomed slot** of the W-B attachment.
- The blade descends at 1.0–2.5 mm·s⁻¹ and shears the core.
- The **peak force $F_{\max}$** is recorded (typically 10–100 N for cooked beef).
- The **W-B shear stress** is computed as:

$$
\tau_{\text{WB}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\max}}{A_{\text{cut}}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\max}}{\pi r^{2}}
$$

where $r$ is the radius of the core (typically 0.635 cm).

**Engineering analysis:**
- W-B is the **gold standard** for **meat tenderness** evaluation. A higher $F_{\max}$ (or $\tau_{\text{WB}}$) = tougher meat.
- It is *destructive* and *sample-shape-sensitive* (irregular core shapes skew the result).
- It is an **imitative** test (mimics the incisor teeth during the first bite).

**2. Kramer Shear Cell (Recap with Engineering Analysis):**

**Principle:** A multi-bladed plunger shears a bed of particulate food held in a slotted cell. The cell has typically 8 or 10 blades, each of which shears two faces (top and bottom).

**Working (recap):**
- 100 g (or specified mass) of food is loaded into the cell.
- The plunger descends at ≈ 1.67 mm·s⁻¹ and the **peak force $F_{\max}$** is recorded.
- The shear stress is:

$$
\tau_{\text{Kramer}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\max}}{2 N L w}
$$

**Engineering analysis:**
- Kramer is suitable for **particulate / mixed-matrix** foods that W-B cannot handle (whole peas, diced carrots, sausages, fish balls).
- The result is an **empirical composite** of compression, shear, and extrusion — it is NOT a fundamental material property.
- Kramer is more **representative of bulk processing** (e.g., extrusion cooking) than W-B.

**3. Comparison:**

| Attribute | Warner-Bratzler | Kramer Shear Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Probe | Single V-blade | 8–10 parallel blades |
| Sample form | Cylindrical core | Bulk particulate |
| Typical food | Cooked meat | Vegetables, sausages, peas |
| Stress type | Pure shear approximation | Combined compression + shear |
| Standardisation | High (USDA) | Moderate (industry QC) |
| Sensitivity to fiber direction | High (along-perpendicular to fibers) | Low |
| Empirical or fundamental | Empirical/imitative | Empirical |
| Typical result | $F_{\max}$ / $\tau_{\text{WB}}$ | $F_{\max}$ / $\tau_{\text{Kramer}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write that W-B measures "tenderness in N" — it actually measures **shear force in N**, and tenderness is the *inverse* (high force = tough = low tenderness). Always report and discuss both the force and the derived shear stress.

---

#### (b) Describe the **instrument geometry, measurement principle, and engineering analysis** of the **back-extrusion (Ottawa) cell** and the **3-point bending (snap) test** for food texture. State the typical applications. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Back-Extrusion Test (Ottawa Cell):**

**Geometry:** A cylindrical **container** (e.g., 35 mm dia, 70 mm long) and a **plunger** (annular, e.g., 30 mm dia × 20 mm tall) attached to the texture analyzer. The plunger fits **concentrically inside** the container, leaving a small annular gap (typically 1–2 mm). The container base has **slots or holes** to allow back-flow of the sample.

**Principle:** A **semi-fluid or paste-like food** (yogurt, ketchup, custard, pudding, sauce, melted cheese) fills the container. The plunger descends at constant speed (≈ 1 mm·s⁻¹). As the plunger compresses the sample, the food is **forced upward through the annular gap** (i.e., extruded *backwards* — hence "back-extrusion"). The force rises to a **peak** (associated with the initial yielding of the sample) and then settles to a **near-steady value** as continuous flow through the gap is established.

**Measured quantities:**
- $F_{\text{peak}}$ — force at initial yield (related to **hardness/firmness**).
- $F_{\text{steady}}$ — steady-state force (related to **consistency**).
- $W_{\text{back}}$ — total work of extrusion = $\int F \, dd$ (related to **cohesiveness and mouthfeel**).

**Engineering analysis:** The back-extrusion cell mimics the **compression and radial flow of food between the tongue and palate**. It is the most common test for **semi-fluid dairy and sauce** products. The geometry (annular gap width, plunger speed) must be standardized to allow cross-laboratory comparison.

**Typical applications:** yogurt, sour cream, ketchup, mayonnaise, melted processed cheese, custard, pudding, salad dressing, toothpaste, cosmetic pastes.

**2. 3-Point Bending (Snap) Test:**

**Geometry:** The sample (typically a rectangular bar of 4–10 cm length, 1–3 cm width, and 0.5–2 cm thickness) rests on **two cylindrical supports** separated by a span $L$. A **central loading nose** descends at constant speed (≈ 1 mm·s⁻¹) and bends the bar until it **fractures**.

**Principle:** The bending moment generated by the central load creates **tensile stress on the bottom face** and **compressive stress on the top face** of the bar. The bar fails when the tensile stress at the bottom face exceeds the **flexural strength** of the food.

**Measured quantities:**
- $F_{\text{break}}$ — force at fracture.
- $d_{\text{break}}$ — central deflection at fracture (a measure of **brittleness** — a small $d_{\text{break}}$ = brittle).
- The **bending stress** at fracture (see Section 3.3 derivation):

$$
\sigma_{\text{bend}} \;=\; \frac{3 F_{\text{break}} L}{2 b h^{2}}
$$

**Engineering analysis:** This test is the **definitive QC method for brittle food products**: chocolate bars, biscuits, crackers, snack bars, breakfast cereals (bars), and frozen desserts. The bending modulus can also be obtained from the **slope of the F–d curve in the linear region**:

$$
E_{b} \;=\; \frac{F L^{3}}{4 b h^{3} \delta}
$$

**Typical applications:** chocolate bars (snap test), biscuits (crispness), crackers, ice cream bars, snack bars, pasta rods, frozen foods.

**3. Combined Engineering Insight:**
The back-extrusion test is the **analogue of compression for fluids**; the 3-point bending test is the **analogue of compression for brittle sheets**. Together with TPA, Warner-Bratzler, and Kramer, they form the **"Big Five" of instrumental food texture measurement** — and a competent food rheologist should be able to select and justify the correct one for any given food system.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Examiner's Most-Cited Loss of Marks:**
> 1. Do not confuse **Adhesiveness** (N·s) with **Hardness** (N). The negative area below the F–t axis in a TPA curve is the work of adhesion; it is *not* a force.
> 2. Do not equate **Gumminess** with **Chewiness**. $G = H \times C$ is for semi-solids; $Ch = H \times C \times S$ is for solids. Marking scheme specifically tests this distinction.
> 3. Do not write the **Warner-Bratzler** test as a "Kramer cell with one blade" — the geometry and stress state are completely different.
> 4. In back-extrusion, **$F_{\text{steady}}$ is the consistency indicator**, not the peak. The peak is the *initial* force to start flow.
> 5. Always quote the **cross-head speed** (in mm·s⁻¹) and the **strain level** (in %) when describing a TPA test. These are mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Mile KTU 2024 Exam Revision — Read Twice, Then Close Your Eyes and Recall:**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] Instrumental texture measurement quantifies **mechanical response** to a controlled deformation.
- [ ] The **force–deformation (or force–time) curve** is the "fingerprint" of texture.
- [ ] Tests are classified into **imitative (TPA, W-B)**, **fundamental (uniaxial compression, tensile)**, and **empirical (Kramer, penetrometer, Ottawa cell, 3-pt bend)** families.

### TPA — The Headline Test
- [ ] **Two-cycle compression** at 1.0 mm·s⁻¹, target strain 25–75 %, 5 s pause between cycles.
- [ ] **Seven primary parameters:** $H, A_{d}, C, S, R, G, Ch$.
- [ ] **Composite identities:** $G = H \times C$ (semi-solids) and $Ch = H \times C \times S$ (solids).
- [ ] Adhesiveness is **the area below the F–t axis** in the up-stroke — report in N·s or J.
- [ ] Cohesiveness = $A_{3}/A_{1}$ — dimensionless.

### Shear & Cutting Tests
- [ ] **Warner-Bratzler:** single V-blade on a cylindrical core of cooked meat; output $F_{\max}$ and $\tau_{\text{WB}} = F_{\max} / (\pi r^{2})$.
- [ ] **Kramer cell:** multi-bladed (8–10) compression + shear; output $\tau_{\text{Kramer}} = F_{\max} / (2NLw)$.
- [ ] **Volodkevich bite test:** jaw-like clamp; used for soft fruits and gels.

### Penetration & Puncture
- [ ] **Cone penetrometer** = soft fats, ghee, margarine; depth reported in 0.1 mm units.
- [ ] **Puncture test** = apple, sausage skin; outputs $F_{\text{puncture}}$, depth at fracture, work of puncture.
- [ ] Cone yield stress formula: $\sigma_{y} = F / (\pi \, d \, \tan(\alpha/2))$.

### Back Extrusion (Ottawa Cell)
- [ ] Used for **pastes, gels, semi-fluids** (yogurt, ketchup, custard, sauce).
- [ ] Outputs: $F_{\text{peak}}$ (yield), $F_{\text{steady}}$ (consistency), $W_{\text{back}}$ (work).

### Bending (Snap) Test
- [ ] Used for **brittle sheets and bars** (chocolate, biscuits, crackers).
- [ ] Bending stress: $\sigma_{\text{bend}} = 3 F_{\text{break}} L / (2 b h^{2})$.
- [ ] Brittleness index = low $d_{\text{break}}$ (small deflection before fracture).

### Tensile Test
- [ ] Used for **noodles, films, gels**; output $\sigma_{\text{tensile}}$ and $\varepsilon_{\text{break}}$.

### Standard Cross-head Speeds to Remember
- [ ] TPA: 1.0 mm·s⁻¹ (sometimes 2.0 mm·s⁻¹ for harder samples).
- [ ] Kramer: 1.67 mm·s⁻¹.
- [ ] Warner-Bratzler: 1.0–2.5 mm·s⁻¹.
- [ ] Back-extrusion: 1.0 mm·s⁻¹.
- [ ] 3-point bend: 1.0 mm·s⁻¹.

### Must-Memorize SI Units
- [ ] $F$ in N, $E$ in Pa, $\tau$ in Pa, $H$ in N, $A_{d}$ in N·s (= J), $Ch$ in N, $C$ and $S$ dimensionless.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Do not call Gumminess and Chewiness interchangeable.
- [ ] Do not write $\sigma$ and $P$ (pressure) interchangeably; $\sigma$ here is uniaxial engineering stress, not bulk pressure.
- [ ] Always state **sample geometry, test speed, and strain level** when describing a test.
- [ ] Always **sketch the F–d curve** when explaining a TPA parameter — the KTU examiner awards 1–2 marks for the labelled curve alone.
- [ ] The **Kramer cell is NOT a pure shear instrument** — it is compression + shear. Always qualify.
- [ ] **Puncture ≠ Penetration**: puncture uses a blunt probe to fracture a surface; penetration uses a sharp cone/needle to sink into a soft material.

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — INSTRUMENTAL MEASUREMENTS OF TEXTURAL PROPERTIES**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chocolate rheology** is the branch of food rheology that studies the **flow and deformation behavior of chocolate mass** under applied stress, encompassing its viscosity, yield stress, thixotropy, and viscoelastic response. Chocolate is technically a **polydisperse suspension of solid particles (sugar, cocoa solids, milk powder) in a continuous fat phase (cocoa butter)**, optionally stabilized by surface-active agents such as **lecithin** and **polyglycerol polyricinoleate (PGPR)**.

From a KTU 2024 Scheme perspective, the rheology of chocolate is examined at three operational regimes:
1. **Processing rheology** – flow during mixing, conching, pumping, and pipe-line transport.
2. **Application rheology** – flow during tempering, depositing, enrobing, and shell formation in molding.
3. **Sensory/Perception rheology** – in-mouth melting, mouthfeel, and texture perception.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The International Confectionery Association (ICA) has standardized chocolate rheology testing at **40 °C** using a **rotational viscometer at shear rates 5, 10, 30, 50 s⁻¹**, and reports results in the form of **Casson model parameters** (Casson yield value and Casson plastic viscosity).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **crowded elevator (lift) full of passengers**:
- At rest, the passengers are **locked shoulder-to-shoulder** — no one can move. The elevator door opens but nobody steps out. This is the **yield stress (τ_y)**: chocolate will not flow until the applied stress exceeds a critical threshold.
- When the door is forced open with effort, passengers shuffle slowly — the system behaves like a **thick, sticky paste** (high apparent viscosity).
- As the people stream out, they accelerate, bumping into each other less. The crowd flows more freely — this is **shear-thinning** (pseudoplastic behavior).
- If you let the elevator doors close and reopen instantly, the passengers are **rearranged in a looser, more random pattern** — this is **thixotropy** (time-dependent structural recovery).

**Chocolate is essentially a "crowded elevator" of solid particles in liquid fat.** The more particles, the tighter the crowd, the higher the yield stress, and the harder it is to flow.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Typical Range (Dark Chocolate) | Industrial Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Casson Yield Value | **1 – 30 Pa** | Determines mold-filling and sag resistance |
| Casson Plastic Viscosity | **1 – 3 Pa·s** | Controls coating thickness during enrobing |
| Apparent Viscosity @ 40 °C, 40 s⁻¹ | **2 – 10 Pa·s** | Pumping/pipe-line design |
| Critical Particle Volume Fraction (φ_m) | **0.55 – 0.64** | Maximum packing limit of solids |
| Tempering Temperature (Dark) | **27 – 29 °C** | Polymorphic Form V stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Insight:** The "flow" in chocolate is governed not just by the fat phase but by the **inter-particle network** of sugar, cocoa, and milk solids. The fat is the *lubricant*, not the *load-bearer*.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Chocolate Behaves the Way It Does — Structural Logic

### 2.1.1 Chocolate as a Concentrated Suspension
A typical dark chocolate contains:
- **Cocoa mass** (40 – 70 %) → solid particles + cocoa butter
- **Sugar** (20 – 40 %) → crystalline sucrose particles
- **Cocoa butter** (25 – 35 %) → continuous fat phase
- **Lecithin / PGPR** (0.1 – 1 %) → emulsifiers
- **Moisture** (< 1 %)

The **total solids content** (non-fat) often exceeds **60 – 75 % by volume**, far above the dilute regime. Hence, hydrodynamic, Brownian, colloidal, and frictional forces all act simultaneously.

### 2.1.2 Origin of Yield Stress
A yield stress arises when particles form a **percolating network** that spans the entire volume of the suspension. In chocolate, this network is held together by:
1. **Van der Waals attraction** between closely-packed sugar/cocoa particles.
2. **Capillary bridges** of liquid fat forming menisci between particles.
3. **Mechanical interlocking** of irregularly-shaped particles.

If the applied stress is below the **Casson yield value (τ_y,C)**, this network remains intact and the chocolate deforms **elastically** (or remains rigid). Above τ_y,C, the network **breaks down irreversibly** and the chocolate flows.

### 2.1.3 Origin of Shear-Thinning
As shear rate (γ̇) increases:
- Particle aggregates **disaggregate**.
- Flocs align with the flow direction.
- Suspended particles form **anisotropic layers**, reducing viscous dissipation.
- The apparent viscosity (η_app = τ / γ̇) decreases monotonically.

### 2.1.4 Origin of Thixotropy
At constant high shear, viscosity **decreases with time** (structural breakdown). When shear is removed, viscosity **recovers gradually** as the network rebuilds. This is critical for:
- **Mold-filling** (need low viscosity during pouring).
- **Mold-retention** (need viscosity recovery to prevent sag).

### 2.1.5 Effect of Tempering on Rheology
Cocoa butter is **polymorphic** (Forms I – VI). Only **Form V (β₂)** gives the glossy snap and stable rheology. Untempered chocolate has **higher yield stress, higher viscosity**, and gives **fat bloom** on storage.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Model | Equation | Parameters | Use in Chocolate Rheology |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Newtonian** | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | η = viscosity | Melted cocoa butter (above 40 °C) |
| **Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele)** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | K, n | General shear-thinning fit |
| **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | τ_y, η_p | Threshold + linear flow |
| **Herschel–Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_y + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | τ_y, K, n | Generalized yield + power-law |
| **Casson Model** *(Industry Standard)* | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{y,C}} + \sqrt{\eta_{C} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$ | τ_y,C, η_C | **ICA standard for chocolate** |
| **Cross Model** | $\dfrac{\eta - \eta_\infty}{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (K \cdot \dot{\gamma})^{m}}$ | η₀, η_∞, K, m | Wide shear-rate range |
| **Carreau–Yasuda** | $\dfrac{\eta - \eta_\infty}{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty} = \left[1 + (\lambda \cdot \dot{\gamma})^{a}\right]^{(n-1)/a}$ | η₀, η_∞, λ, n, a | Smooth transition to Newtonian |
| **Mooney (Suspension)** | $\eta_r = \dfrac{\eta}{\eta_s} = \dfrac{\exp\!\left(\frac{2.5\phi}{1 - \phi/\phi_m}\right)}{1}$ | φ, φ_m | Effect of solids loading |
| **Krieger–Dougherty** | $\eta_r = \left(1 - \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_m}\right)^{-[\eta]\phi_m}$ | [η], φ_m | High-concentration regime |
| **Einstein (Dilute)** | $\eta_r = 1 + 2.5\phi$ | φ | Dilute limit (φ < 0.05) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for Chocolate:**
> - φ → φ_m ⇒ η_r → ∞ (the suspension becomes a **paste** with infinite viscosity).
> - At γ̇ → 0 ⇒ η → η₀ (zero-shear viscosity, controlled by network).
> - At γ̇ → ∞ ⇒ η → η_∞ (infinite-shear viscosity, controlled by fat phase only).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Pipe-line Design** — Pressure drop (ΔP) in chocolate pipelines is calculated using the **Metzner–Reed** correlation for non-Newtonian fluids, directly derived from the apparent viscosity.
2. **Enrobing Thickness Control** — The **Casson plastic viscosity** determines the **coating weight** of chocolate on biscuits/nuts; too low → dripping, too high → rough coat.
3. **Mold-Filling Simulation** — **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** simulations of mold-filling use the **Carreau** or **Cross** model to predict flow patterns and avoid air entrapment.
4. **Process Troubleshooting** — A sudden change in τ_y,C indicates **moisture pickup, particle agglomeration, or under-tempering**.
5. **Sensory Prediction** — In-mouth viscosity correlates with perceived **"smoothness"** and **"melt"** in sensory panels.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Casson Model from First Principles

The **Casson model (1959)** was originally developed for pigment suspensions, but has been adopted universally for chocolate because it captures both the yield stress and the shear-thinning behavior with just **two parameters**.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumptions
1. The suspension contains rigid, isometric (roughly spherical) particles in a Newtonian liquid.
2. Particles form a **flocculated network** at low shear.
3. As shear rate increases, the network breaks into progressively smaller aggregates.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Casson Equation

**Step 1 — Define the total stress as a sum of viscous and structural contributions.**

$$
\tau = \tau_{\text{struct}}(\dot{\gamma}) + \eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

*τ_struct* is the stress required to **break the inter-particle network**. *η_∞*·γ̇ is the **hydrodynamic viscous stress** in the fully disrupted state.

**Step 2 — Assume the structural stress scales with the square-root of shear rate.**

The empirical (and theoretically motivated) assumption is:

$$
\tau_{\text{struct}}(\dot{\gamma}) = \tau_y \left(1 - \frac{\dot{\gamma}}{\dot{\gamma} + \dot{\gamma}_0}\right) \approx \frac{\tau_y \cdot \dot{\gamma}_0}{\dot{\gamma} + \dot{\gamma}_0}
$$

For γ̇ ≫ γ̇₀, this decays as 1/γ̇.

**Step 3 — Combine hydrodynamic and structural contributions and take the square root.**

Casson observed empirically that for a wide range of suspensions, the **square-root of total stress** is **linear in the square-root of shear rate**:

$$
\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
\tau = \tau_y + \eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma} + 2\sqrt{\tau_y \cdot \eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 4 — Identification of Casson parameters.**

For chocolate, the industry defines:
- $\tau_{y,C}$ — **Casson yield value** (Pa): the extrapolated yield stress at γ̇ → 0.
- $\eta_C$ — **Casson plastic viscosity** (Pa·s): the viscosity when the network is fully broken down, i.e., the slope of √τ vs. √γ̇ plot squared.

Hence the canonical ICA form is:

$$
\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{y,C}} + \sqrt{\eta_C \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Example — Extracting Casson Parameters from ICA Data

> **Problem:** At 40 °C, the following data are obtained for a milk chocolate using a rotational viscometer:

| Shear rate γ̇ (s⁻¹) | 2 | 5 | 10 | 20 | 50 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shear stress τ (Pa) | 18.0 | 24.5 | 32.4 | 45.0 | 70.3 |

> **Find:** Casson yield value (τ_y,C) and Casson plastic viscosity (η_C).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute √γ̇ and √τ.**

| √γ̇ (s⁻¹)^(1/2) | 1.414 | 2.236 | 3.162 | 4.472 | 7.071 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| √τ (Pa)^(1/2) | 4.243 | 4.950 | 5.692 | 6.708 | 8.385 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression of √τ vs. √γ̇.**

Let $y = \sqrt{\tau}$ and $x = \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$. Linear fit: $y = a + b \cdot x$.

Mean of x:
$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1.414 + 2.236 + 3.162 + 4.472 + 7.071}{5} = 3.671
$$

Mean of y:
$$
\bar{y} = \frac{4.243 + 4.950 + 5.692 + 6.708 + 8.385}{5} = 5.996
$$

Slope b:
$$
b = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

Numerator (numerical):
- (1.414 − 3.671)(4.243 − 5.996) = (−2.257)(−1.753) = 3.957
- (2.236 − 3.671)(4.950 − 5.996) = (−1.435)(−1.046) = 1.501
- (3.162 − 3.671)(5.692 − 5.996) = (−0.509)(−0.304) = 0.155
- (4.472 − 3.671)(6.708 − 5.996) = (0.801)(0.712) = 0.570
- (7.071 − 3.671)(8.385 − 5.996) = (3.400)(2.389) = 8.123

$$
\text{Numerator} = 3.957 + 1.501 + 0.155 + 0.570 + 8.123 = 14.306
$$

Denominator (numerical):
- (1.414 − 3.671)² = 5.094
- (2.236 − 3.671)² = 2.059
- (3.162 − 3.671)² = 0.259
- (4.472 − 3.671)² = 0.642
- (7.071 − 3.671)² = 11.560

$$
\text{Denominator} = 5.094 + 2.059 + 0.259 + 0.642 + 11.560 = 19.614
$$

$$
b = \frac{14.306}{19.614} = 0.729 \;\text{Pa}^{1/2} \cdot \text{s}^{1/2}
$$

Intercept a:
$$
a = \bar{y} - b \bar{x} = 5.996 - (0.729)(3.671) = 5.996 - 2.676 = 3.320 \;\text{Pa}^{1/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert back to Casson parameters.**

Casson plastic viscosity:
$$
\eta_C = b^2 = (0.729)^2 = 0.531 \;\text{Pa·s}
$$

Casson yield value:
$$
\tau_{y,C} = a^2 = (3.320)^2 = 11.02 \;\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

- τ_y,C ≈ 11 Pa — typical for a well-formulated dark chocolate.
- η_C ≈ 0.53 Pa·s — slightly low; may need slight increase in lecithin or moisture control.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Packing Fraction from Mooney Equation

The **Mooney equation** predicts the relative viscosity of a monodisperse suspension as a function of solid volume fraction φ:

$$
\eta_r = \frac{\eta}{\eta_s} = \exp\!\left( \frac{2.5 \phi}{1 - \phi/\phi_m} \right)
$$

**Step 1 — Limiting case φ → 0:**

Expand the exponential:
$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{2.5\phi}{1 - \phi/\phi_m}\right) \approx 1 + \frac{2.5\phi}{1 - \phi/\phi_m} + \mathcal{O}(\phi^2)
$$

For φ ≪ φ_m:
$$
\eta_r \approx 1 + 2.5\phi
$$

This recovers the **Einstein equation** for dilute suspensions.

**Step 2 — Limiting case φ → φ_m:**

The denominator $(1 - \phi/\phi_m)$ → 0, hence the exponential diverges:
$$
\eta_r \to \infty
$$

**Physical meaning:** at the maximum packing fraction φ_m, particles are in contact and the suspension can no longer flow.

**Step 3 — Application to chocolate:**

For a typical dark chocolate with φ ≈ 0.55 and φ_m ≈ 0.62:
$$
\eta_r = \exp\!\left(\frac{2.5 \times 0.55}{1 - 0.55/0.62}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{1.375}{1 - 0.887}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{1.375}{0.113}\right) = \exp(12.17) \approx 194{,}000
$$

This enormous relative viscosity (~ 2 × 10⁵) explains why chocolate processing requires careful control — small increases in solids can cause viscosity to **explode exponentially**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Rule:** Chocolate manufacturers aim to keep φ **≤ 0.85 × φ_m** to leave a margin for safe processing.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Chocolate Rheology

| Parameter | Effect on τ_y,C | Effect on η_C | Engineering Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Total solids (φ) | ↑↑ strongly | ↑↑ strongly | Recipe balance, particle size distribution |
| ↑ Cocoa butter content | ↓↓ strongly | ↓↓ strongly | Cocoa butter addition |
| ↑ Lecithin (0.1 – 0.5 %) | ↓ moderately | ↓ moderately | Emulsifier dosing |
| ↑ PGPR (replaces lecithin) | ↓↓↓ strongly | ↓ moderately | Used in molding chocolate |
| ↓ Particle size (d) | ↑ slightly | ↑ slightly | Refining/Conching time |
| ↑ Temperature (T) | ↓↓ strongly | ↓↓ strongly | Tempering & enrobing temperature |
| ↑ Moisture (> 1 %) | ↑↑ severely | ↑↑ severely | Strict humidity control |
| ↑ Fat bloom / under-tempering | ↑↑ strongly | ↑ strongly | Proper Form V seeding |

```mermaid
    H[Tempering Form V] --> A
    I[Untempered Form IV] --> A
    H --> H1[Glossy snap low yield stress]
    I --> I1[Dull surface fat bloom high yield stress]
```
