# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the **Casson model** for chocolate rheology and state its significance in the confectionery industry.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Casson model** is an empirical constitutive equation that describes the flow behavior of chocolate as a **flocculated suspension with a yield stress and shear-thinning characteristics**. It is expressed as:

$$
\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{y,C}} + \sqrt{\eta_C \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

where τ_y,C is the **Casson yield value (Pa)** and η_C is the **Casson plastic viscosity (Pa·s)**. Its significance lies in **(i)** its adoption as the **International Confectionery Association (ICA) standard** for chocolate rheology testing, **(ii)** its ability to predict both the **threshold stress** (for mold-filling) and the **flow behavior** (for pumping and enrobing) with only two parameters, and **(iii)** its direct correlation with **sensory and process performance** of chocolate.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why does chocolate exhibit a **yield stress**? List two practical consequences of yield stress in chocolate processing.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Chocolate exhibits a yield stress because the **dispersed solid particles (sugar, cocoa, milk solids) form a three-dimensional percolating network** held together by **van der Waals forces, capillary bridges of fat, and mechanical interlocking**. This network must be disrupted before macroscopic flow can begin.

Two practical consequences:
1. **Mold-filling:** Chocolate must have a yield stress **low enough** to flow into intricate mold cavities under gravity, but **high enough** to retain its shape after the mold is filled (preventing sag and drips).
2. **Enrobing and dipping:** A controlled yield stress determines the **coating thickness and evenness** when chocolate is poured over biscuits or nuts; too low → excessive dripping, too high → rough and uneven coating.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 4 – Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the **Casson model equation** from first principles. Show clearly the physical assumptions and the final squared form of the equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data are obtained for a dark chocolate at 40 °C using an ICA-standard rotational viscometer:

| γ̇ (s⁻¹) | 2 | 5 | 10 | 20 | 50 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| τ (Pa) | 22 | 28 | 36 | 50 | 78 |

Calculate the **Casson yield value (τ_y,C)** and **Casson plastic viscosity (η_C)**. Comment on whether this chocolate is suitable for **enrobing** (acceptable range: τ_y,C between 5 – 15 Pa, η_C between 1 – 3 Pa·s). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumption 1 (1 mark):** Chocolate is a **concentrated suspension of rigid, isometric particles** (sugar, cocoa, milk solids) in a Newtonian continuous phase (cocoa butter).

**Assumption 2 (1 mark):** The particles form a **flocculated, percolating network** at rest, which exerts a structural stress τ_struct(γ̇) on the suspending fluid.

**Assumption 3 (1 mark):** The total shear stress is the sum of the structural stress and the hydrodynamic viscous stress:

$$
\tau = \tau_{\text{struct}}(\dot{\gamma}) + \eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

**Assumption 4 (1 mark):** The structural stress decays with shear rate such that its square-root scales as a power of γ̇. Empirically and from dimensional arguments, this yields:

$$
\sqrt{\tau_{\text{struct}}} = \sqrt{\tau_y} \cdot \left( \frac{\dot{\gamma}_0}{\dot{\gamma} + \dot{\gamma}_0} \right)^{1/2} \xrightarrow{\dot{\gamma} \gg \dot{\gamma}_0} \sqrt{\frac{\tau_y \cdot \dot{\gamma}_0}{\dot{\gamma}}}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Combining the above and adding the hydrodynamic term, then taking the square-root of total stress:

$$
\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Squaring both sides yields the explicit form:

$$
\tau = \tau_y + \eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma} + 2\sqrt{\tau_y \cdot \eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Identification of the two-parameter form for chocolate: τ_y = τ_y,C and η_∞ = η_C, giving the **ICA-Casson form** for chocolate:

$$
\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{y,C}} + \sqrt{\eta_C \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute √γ̇ and √τ:

| √γ̇ | 1.414 | 2.236 | 3.162 | 4.472 | 7.071 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| √τ | 4.690 | 5.292 | 6.000 | 7.071 | 8.832 |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Linear regression of √τ (y) vs. √γ̇ (x):

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1.414 + 2.236 + 3.162 + 4.472 + 7.071}{5} = 3.671
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{4.690 + 5.292 + 6.000 + 7.071 + 8.832}{5} = 6.377
$$

Sum of $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$:
- (−2.257)(−1.687) = 3.808
- (−1.435)(−1.085) = 1.557
- (−0.509)(−0.377) = 0.192
- (0.801)(0.694) = 0.556
- (3.400)(2.455) = 8.347

$$
\text{Numerator} = 3.808 + 1.557 + 0.192 + 0.556 + 8.347 = 14.460
$$

Sum of $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ (from Section 3): 19.614.

$$
b = \frac{14.460}{19.614} = 0.737
$$

$$
a = 6.377 - (0.737)(3.671) = 6.377 - 2.706 = 3.671
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Extract Casson parameters:

$$
\eta_C = b^2 = (0.737)^2 = 0.543 \;\text{Pa·s}
$$

$$
\tau_{y,C} = a^2 = (3.671)^2 = 13.48 \;\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Comparison with enrobing specifications:

- τ_y,C = 13.48 Pa → **within the 5 – 15 Pa range** ✓ (acceptable)
- η_C = 0.543 Pa·s → **below the 1 – 3 Pa·s range** ✗ (too low)

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Engineering interpretation:
- The yield stress is acceptable, meaning mold-filling and shape retention will be satisfactory.
- However, the **plastic viscosity is too low**, indicating that the chocolate will **drip excessively** during enrobing and the **coating thickness will be too thin**.
- **Remedial action:** Slightly **reduce the lecithin or PGPR dosage** OR **reduce the total fat content** OR **add a small amount of finely ground cocoa mass** to bring η_C into the 1 – 3 Pa·s window.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 4 – Set B]**

**(a)** Explain with neat structural reasoning why chocolate behaves as a **non-Newtonian, shear-thinning, thixotropic fluid** with a **yield stress**. Mention the role of **cocoa butter polymorphism (Forms I – VI)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dark chocolate formulation has a **total solid volume fraction φ = 0.52** and a **maximum packing fraction φ_m = 0.60**. The continuous fat phase has viscosity **η_s = 0.05 Pa·s** at 40 °C. Using the **Mooney equation**, calculate the **relative viscosity η_r**, the **suspension viscosity η**, and comment on how the viscosity will change if φ increases to **0.58**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Structural Explanation (7 marks)

**(1) Non-Newtonian behavior (2 marks):**
Chocolate is a **multi-component, multi-phase colloidal suspension**. The dispersed phase (sugar, cocoa, milk solids at 60 – 75 % by volume) is far above the dilute regime (φ > 0.05). Particle-particle interactions, van der Waals forces, and hydrodynamic coupling make the viscosity depend on the **rate of deformation** (shear rate), violating Newton's law of viscosity (τ ≠ ηγ̇ at constant η).

**(2) Yield stress (2 marks):**
At rest, particles form a **continuous three-dimensional network** through van der Waals attraction, capillary fat bridges, and mechanical entanglement. This network resists deformation elastically until a critical stress (the **Casson yield value**) is exceeded, after which it breaks down and flow begins.

**(3) Shear-thinning (1.5 marks):**
On increasing shear rate, the inter-particle network progressively **disaggregates**; particles align with the flow; hydrodynamic lubrication increases. Hence **apparent viscosity (η_app = τ/γ̇) decreases with γ̇** — characteristic of **pseudoplastic** behavior.

**(4) Thixotropy (1 mark):**
The breakdown of structure is **time-dependent** at constant shear; viscosity decreases with time under sustained shear and **recovers gradually** when shear is removed — this is **thixotropy**, important for enrobing (low viscosity during pouring, recovery to retain coating).

**(5) Role of cocoa butter polymorphism (0.5 marks):**
Cocoa butter crystallizes in **six polymorphic forms (I – VI)**, but only **Form V (β₂)** gives the glossy snap and stable rheology. Under-tempered chocolate contains Forms I – IV, which have **higher viscosity and yield stress**, and convert to Form V (causing **fat bloom**) during storage.

##### Part (b) — Mooney Equation Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Recall the Mooney equation:

$$
\eta_r = \exp\!\left( \frac{2.5 \phi}{1 - \phi/\phi_m} \right)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For φ = 0.52 and φ_m = 0.60:

$$
\phi/\phi_m = \frac{0.52}{0.60} = 0.8667
$$

$$
1 - \phi/\phi_m = 1 - 0.8667 = 0.1333
$$

$$
\frac{2.5 \phi}{1 - \phi/\phi_m} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.52}{0.1333} = \frac{1.30}{0.1333} = 9.75
$$

$$
\eta_r = \exp(9.75) = 1.717 \times 10^4 \approx 17{,}170
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Suspension viscosity:

$$
\eta = \eta_r \cdot \eta_s = 17{,}170 \times 0.05 = 858.5 \;\text{Pa·s}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Recalculation for φ = 0.58:

$$
\phi/\phi_m = \frac{0.58}{0.60} = 0.9667
$$

$$
1 - \phi/\phi_m = 0.0333
$$

$$
\frac{2.5 \times 0.58}{0.0333} = \frac{1.45}{0.0333} = 43.5
$$

$$
\eta_r = \exp(43.5) \approx 6.5 \times 10^{18}
$$

$$
\eta = 6.5 \times 10^{18} \times 0.05 = 3.25 \times 10^{17} \;\text{Pa·s}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Interpretation:
- A modest 6 % increase in solid volume fraction (from 0.52 → 0.58) causes the **relative viscosity to explode** by **~ 14 orders of magnitude**.
- This demonstrates the **extreme non-linearity** of suspension rheology as φ approaches φ_m.
- In practice, this means **even tiny compositional errors** in industrial chocolate (e.g., moisture absorption, particle agglomeration) can render the chocolate **unprocessable** — hence the critical importance of strict formulation and humidity control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in Chocolate Rheology Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the linear-regression parameters** to obtain τ_y,C and η_C from the √τ vs. √γ̇ plot. Students often report the slope and intercept as the answer instead of their squares.
> 2. **Confusing the Casson yield value with the Bingham yield stress** — they are different mathematical constructs and the Casson value is consistently higher.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in the Mooney equation calculation; the relative viscosity is **dimensionless** but the suspension viscosity inherits the **Pa·s** unit of the continuous phase.
> 4. **Not addressing the role of polymorphism (Form V)** in any tempering-related question — this is a frequently-tested KTU module-4 concept and carries 2 – 3 marks.
> 5. **Neglecting the effect of emulsifiers** (lecithin/PGPR) in flow-behavior explanations — ICA recognizes that **PGPR** reduces yield stress more than lecithin, and this distinction is worth at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing the ICA reference temperature of 40 °C** with tempering temperature (~ 28 – 32 °C) — these are different regimes with different physical meanings.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Rheology of Chocolate**

- **Chocolate is a concentrated suspension** of sugar, cocoa, and milk solids in cocoa butter.
- **Casson model** is the **ICA industry standard** for chocolate:
  $$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{y,C}} + \sqrt{\eta_C \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$
- **Casson parameters** are obtained by **linear regression of √τ vs. √γ̇**; τ_y,C = (intercept)² and η_C = (slope)².
- **ICA standard** conditions: **T = 40 °C**, shear rates **2, 5, 10, 20, 50 s⁻¹**.
- **Yield stress** originates from a **percolating particle network** held by van der Waals forces and capillary fat bridges.
- **Shear-thinning** is due to **aggregate break-up and particle alignment** at higher shear.
- **Thixotropy** is the **time-dependent structural breakdown and recovery** under and after shear.
- **Lecithin** reduces surface tension; **PGPR** strongly reduces yield stress (preferred for **molding chocolate**).
- **Mooney equation** governs the exponential rise of viscosity with solids fraction:
  $$\eta_r = \exp\!\left( \frac{2.5 \phi}{1 - \phi/\phi_m} \right)$$
- **φ must remain < 0.85 × φ_m** for safe processing.
- **Cocoa butter polymorphism** — only **Form V (β₂)** gives the desired glossy, stable rheology.
- **Temperature** strongly reduces both yield stress and plastic viscosity.
- **Moisture > 1 %** causes severe viscosity rise and processing failure.
- **Common defects** — **fat bloom** (Form V → Form VI transition), **sugar bloom** (moisture-induced surface dissolution), **viscosity drift** (lecithin over-dose or under-dose).
- **Application targets**:
  - Molding: τ_y,C ≈ 5 – 15 Pa, η_C ≈ 1 – 3 Pa·s
  - Enrobing: τ_y,C ≈ 3 – 10 Pa, η_C ≈ 1 – 2.5 Pa·s
  - Pumping: keep γ̇ < 100 s⁻¹ in pipes to avoid thixotropic damage.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Textural Characteristics of Food Emulsions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Textural Characteristics of Food Emulsions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **food emulsion** is a thermodynamically unstable, kinetically stabilized colloidal dispersion consisting of at least two immiscible liquid phases (typically oil and water), where one phase is dispersed as discrete spherical or non-spherical droplets within a continuous phase. The system is stabilized by an emulsifier (surfactant) that reduces interfacial tension and forms a protective film around the droplets.

Mathematically, an emulsion can be expressed as a two-phase heterogeneous system:

$$
\text{Emulsion} = \begin{cases} \text{Oil-in-Water (O/W)} \rightarrow \text{Oil droplets in aqueous continuous phase (e.g., milk, mayonnaise)} \\ \text{Water-in-Oil (W/O)} \rightarrow \text{Water droplets in oil continuous phase (e.g., butter, margarine)} \\ \text{Multiple emulsions (W/O/W or O/W/O)} \rightarrow \text{Double-layered structure} \end{cases}
$$

The **textural characteristics** of food emulsions refer to the ensemble of mechanical, rheological, and sensory attributes that describe how the product feels, flows, deforms, and breaks down under applied forces. Texture integrates:

- **Mechanical attributes:** Hardness, firmness, cohesiveness, adhesiveness, springiness, gumminess, chewiness.
- **Geometrical attributes:** Graininess, smoothness, sliminess.
- **Rheological attributes:** Viscosity, yield stress, thixotropy, viscoelasticity.
- **Sensory attributes:** Creaminess, mouthfeel, spreadability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Textural characteristics form the **bridge between microstructure and consumer perception** in food emulsions. They are governed by composition, processing, and storage conditions, and are evaluated through both instrumental and sensory methods.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are squeezing a tube of **mayonnaise** versus pouring a glass of **milk**. Both are emulsions, yet their textures are vastly different. Mayonnaise resists spooning (it is firm and structured) because it is a highly concentrated oil-in-water emulsion (≈ 70–80 % oil) with a dense network of egg-yolk-coated droplets. Milk, in contrast, flows readily because it is a dilute O/W emulsion (≈ 3.5 % fat) with tiny droplets (≈ 0.1–10 µm) suspended in a watery continuous phase.

> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of an emulsion as a **boules (metal) ball pit** — the balls (droplets) are squeezed together, and the air between them (continuous phase) plus the ball-to-ball friction determines whether children (your tongue/fingers) can move easily or with difficulty. Add more balls (more oil), use stickier balls (stronger emulsifier films), or pack them tighter (smaller droplets, higher polydispersity), and the texture changes dramatically.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following are the standard metrics used to quantify emulsion texture:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Units |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Interfacial tension | $\gamma$ | **mN·m⁻¹** |
| Droplet radius | $r$ | **µm** |
| Dispersed phase volume fraction | $\phi$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| Shear stress | $\tau$ | **Pa** |
| Shear rate | $\dot{\gamma}$ | **s⁻¹** |
| Viscosity | $\eta$ | **Pa·s** |
| Yield stress | $\tau_0$ | **Pa** |
| Storage modulus | $G'$ | **Pa** |
| Loss modulus | $G''$ | **Pa** |
| Complex modulus | $G^{*}$ | **Pa** |
| Loss tangent | $\tan\delta$ | dimensionless |
| Creaming index | $CI$ | **%** |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard reference temperature for emulsion rheology is **25 °C** unless otherwise specified. Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81$ **m·s⁻²**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Food Emulsions by Texture

| Emulsion Type | Texture Profile | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dilute O/W (φ < 0.05) | Low viscosity, Newtonian, fluid | Milk, tea, coffee whiteners |
| Concentrated O/W (0.3 < φ < 0.74) | Highly viscous, shear-thinning, structured | Mayonnaise, salad dressings, sauces |
| W/O (φ < 0.3) | Plastic, spreadable, yield stress present | Butter, margarine, spreads |
| Multiple W/O/W | Creamy, fat-reduced mimic of full-fat emulsion | Reduced-fat spreads, encapsulated bioactives |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — The Texture Hierarchy

The textural behaviour of a food emulsion emerges from a hierarchical sequence of structural contributions. Each step adds a layer of mechanical response:

1. **Molecular level** → Continuous-phase viscosity, emulsifier interfacial chemistry, and molecular weight of biopolymers (proteins, polysaccharides).
2. **Droplet level** → Droplet size, size distribution (polydispersity), shape, and interfacial layer composition.
3. **Microstructural level** → Droplet packing, flocculation, coalescence, partial coalescence, and network formation.
4. **Bulk level** → Macroscopic flow behaviour: viscous, elastic, plastic, viscoelastic, thixotropic.

> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners often ask students to *link a microstructural feature* to a *macroscopic textural attribute*. Memorize the chain: **composition → microstructure → rheology → sensory texture**.

---

## 2.2 Key Rheological Models for Food Emulsions

### A. Newtonian Model
For very dilute emulsions (e.g., milk at low shear):

$$
\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

where $\eta$ is independent of $\dot{\gamma}$. Most food emulsions are **non-Newtonian**.

---

### B. Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Model
Applicable to shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) emulsions without yield stress (e.g., salad dressings):

$$
\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}
$$

- $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = flow behaviour index (dimensionless)
- $n < 1$ → shear-thinning; $n = 1$ → Newtonian; $n > 1$ → shear-thickening

The apparent viscosity is:

$$
\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}
$$

---

### C. Herschel–Bulkley Model
For emulsions with a **yield stress** followed by shear-thinning (e.g., mayonnaise, margarine):

$$
\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}
$$

The structure does not flow until $\tau \geq \tau_0$. This is the most widely used model for **structured food emulsions**.

---

### D. Bingham Plastic Model
A special case with $n = 1$:

$$
\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

---

### E. Casson Model
Used for chocolate-type and some W/O emulsions:

$$
\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

where $\eta_{\infty}$ is the infinite-shear viscosity.

---

## 2.3 Viscoelastic Framework (Dynamic Oscillatory Testing)

When subjected to small-amplitude oscillatory shear (SAOS), emulsions exhibit both solid-like and liquid-like behaviour. The complex modulus is:

$$
G^{*} = G' + i \cdot G''
$$

- $G'$ = storage modulus (elastic, in-phase)
- $G''$ = loss modulus (viscous, out-of-phase)
- $\tan\delta = \dfrac{G''}{G'}$

| Behaviour | Condition | Texture Implication |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Solid-like (gel) | $G' \gg G''$, $\tan\delta < 1$ | Firm, holds shape (e.g., set yogurt, butter) |
| Liquid-like | $G'' \gg G'$, $\tan\delta > 1$ | Flowing, soft (e.g., milk, cream) |
| Weak gel | $G' > G''$, both $\propto \omega^{n}$ | Spreadable, structured (e.g., mayonnaise) |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Table** — Memorize these for ESE & module tests. Use `\mid` or `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid markdown table corruption.

| Equation | Final Form | Application / Boundary |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Laplace pressure | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ | Smaller droplets → higher internal pressure → higher stability |
| Stokes' creaming velocity | $v = \dfrac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_{d} - \rho_{c})}{9 \eta_{c}}$ | Predicts creaming/sedimentation rate |
| Einstein equation (dilute) | $\eta_{r} = 1 + 2.5\phi$ | Valid only when $\phi < 0.02$ |
| Krieger–Dougherty (concentrated) | $\eta_{r} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_{max}}\right)^{-[\eta]\phi_{max}}$ | For $\phi$ up to close packing ($\phi_{max} \approx 0.74$) |
| Creaming index | $CI = \dfrac{H_{s}}{H_{t}} \times 100$ | Texture stability indicator |
| Power-law viscosity | $\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | Shear-thinning emulsions |
| Storage/Loss balance | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{G''}{G'}$ | Viscoelastic character |
| Cox–Merz rule | $\eta^{*}(\omega) \approx \eta(\dot{\gamma})$ at $\omega = \dot{\gamma}$ | Links oscillatory and steady-shear data |
| Yield stress (Herschel–Bulkley) | $\tau_0 = \tau - K\dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Spreadability threshold |

> **Real-world utility:** These equations are the backbone of food product development. The **Stokes equation** is used to predict shelf-life of milk; the **Herschel–Bulkley model** is used by sauce manufacturers to ensure the sauce "pours but holds on the spoon"; the **Krieger–Dougherty equation** allows formulators to estimate viscosity when reformulating with reduced fat.

---

## 2.5 Factors Affecting Emulsion Texture — The "7 Pillars"

1. **Dispersed-phase volume fraction (φ):** Higher φ → higher viscosity, firmer texture.
2. **Droplet size distribution:** Smaller, monodisperse droplets → smoother, creamier mouthfeel.
3. **Emulsifier type:** Protein films → elastic, viscoelastic; Surfactant films → weaker, more fluid.
4. **Continuous-phase composition:** Hydrocolloids (xanthan, guar) enhance viscosity and gel-like structure.
5. **Processing conditions:** High-pressure homogenization reduces droplet size and tightens texture.
6. **Temperature:** Affects interfacial tension, viscosity, and fat crystallization in W/O emulsions.
7. **Storage time:** Coalescence, Ostwald ripening, and creaming degrade texture over shelf life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Einstein Equation for Dilute Emulsions

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between relative viscosity $\eta_{r}$ and dispersed phase volume fraction $\phi$ for a dilute emulsion.

**Step 1 — Definition of relative viscosity:**

$$
\eta_{r} = \frac{\eta}{\eta_{c}}
$$

where $\eta$ is the emulsion viscosity and $\eta_{c}$ is the continuous-phase viscosity.

**Step 2 — Energy dissipation approach:**

For a single rigid spherical droplet of radius $r$ immersed in a Newtonian fluid undergoing shear flow with shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$, the extra energy dissipation per unit volume due to the presence of the droplet is given by Einstein's classical result:

$$
\Delta E = \frac{5}{2} \cdot \eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{2} \cdot V_{droplet}
$$

where $V_{droplet} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$ is the droplet volume.

**Step 3 — Total extra dissipation for $n$ droplets per unit volume:**

$$
\Delta E_{total} = n \cdot \frac{5}{2} \cdot \eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{2} \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of volume fraction $\phi$:**

The total volume of droplets per unit volume of emulsion equals $\phi$. Hence:

$$
n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = \phi
$$

Substituting into Step 3:

$$
\Delta E_{total} = \frac{5}{2} \cdot \eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{2} \cdot \phi
$$

**Step 5 — Equate to effective viscosity dissipation:**

The total energy dissipated in the emulsion with effective viscosity $\eta$ is:

$$
\Delta E_{total} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{2}
$$

Equating both expressions:

$$
\eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{2} = \eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{2} + \frac{5}{2}\eta_{c}\dot{\gamma}^{2}\phi
$$

**Step 6 — Divide through by $\eta_{c}\dot{\gamma}^{2}$:**

$$
\frac{\eta}{\eta_{c}} = 1 + \frac{5}{2}\phi
$$

**Final Einstein equation:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{r} = 1 + 2.5\phi}
$$

> **Limitation:** Valid only for $\phi < 0.02$, rigid spheres, no droplet interactions, no slip at interface.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Creaming Index from Gravitational Separation

**Step 1 — Stokes' law for a single droplet:**

$$
v = \frac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_{d} - \rho_{c})}{9 \eta_{c}}
$$

This is the terminal velocity of a single isolated droplet.

**Step 2 — Define serum (cream) layer height:**

After time $t$, the serum layer (lower or upper depending on density difference) has risen/fallen to a height $H_{s}$.

$$
H_{s} = v \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Express as fraction of total height $H_{t}$:**

$$
\frac{H_{s}}{H_{t}} = \frac{v \cdot t}{H_{t}}
$$

**Step 4 — Creaming index definition:**

$$
\boxed{CI = \frac{H_{s}}{H_{t}} \times 100 \,\%}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** To *reduce CI*, decrease droplet radius $r$, increase continuous-phase viscosity $\eta_{c}$ (via thickeners), or minimize density difference $\vert \rho_{d} - \rho_{c} \vert$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Apparent Viscosity from Power-Law Model

**Step 1 — Power-law constitutive equation:**

$$
\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}
$$

**Step 2 — Apparent viscosity definition:**

By definition, $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\eta_{app} = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}
$$

**Final form:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** When $n < 1$ (shear-thinning), $\eta_{app}$ decreases as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases — the emulsion becomes easier to pump or spread under high shear.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Texture vs. Microstructural Origin

| Textural Attribute | Primary Microstructural Origin | Quantification Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Firmness / Hardness | High φ, dense droplet packing | Force at maximum compression (TPA) |
| Spreadability | Low yield stress, low $G'$ | Penetration / extrusion test |
| Creaminess | Small droplet size ($d_{3,2} < 1\,\mu m$), high fat | Sensory panel + droplet sizing |
| Graininess | Large droplets, aggregation, coalescence | Particle size analysis + sensory |
| Stickiness / Adhesiveness | Protein-film adhesion, high $G''$ | TPA negative-force area |
| Thickness (perceived) | High $K$, low $n$, high $\eta_{app}$ at 50 s⁻¹ | Steady-shear rheometry |
| Stability | Small $r$, matched densities, high $\eta_{c}$ | CI measurement over time |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Origin of Emulsion Texture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Molecular Level] --> A1[Continuous phase viscosity]
    A --> A2[Emulsifier interfacial chemistry]
    A --> A3[Biopolymer molecular weight]
    A1 --> B
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    B[Droplet Level] --> B1[Droplet size and distribution]
    B --> B2[Interfacial film composition]
    B --> B3[Droplet shape and deformability]
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C[Microstructural Level] --> C1[Flocculation and aggregation]
    C --> C2[Coalescence and partial coalescence]
    C --> C3[Network formation]
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D[Bulk Rheological Level] --> D1[Viscosity and flow behaviour]
    D --> D2[Yield stress and viscoelasticity]
    D --> D3[Thixotropy and time dependence]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E[Sensory Texture] --> E1[Creaminess]
    E --> E2[Smoothness]
    E --> E3[Spreadability and firmness]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Block Architecture of Texture Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        I1[Food Emulsion Sample]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    subgraph Preparation
        P1[Temperature equilibration at 25 C]
        P2[Sample loading geometry selection]
    end
    P1 --> M1
    P2 --> M1
    subgraph Measurement
        M1[Steady Shear Rheometry]
        M2[Oscillatory SAOS]
        M3[Texture Profile Analysis TPA]
        M4[Creaming Index CI]
    end
    M1 --> R1
    M2 --> R2
    M3 --> R3
    M4 --> R4
    subgraph Output
        R1[Viscosity curve tau vs gamma-dot]
        R2[G prime and G prime double prime]
        R3[Hardness, cohesiveness, adhesiveness]
        R4[CI percent over time]
    end
    R1 --> DEC
    R2 --> DEC
    R3 --> DEC
    R4 --> DEC
    DEC[Texture Decision and Quality Control]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Map for Choosing a Rheological Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START{Does the emulsion exhibit a yield stress?}
    START -- No --> Q1{Is viscosity constant with shear rate?}
    START -- Yes --> Q2{Does viscosity drop with increasing shear?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> MOD1[Newtonian Model tau equals eta times gamma-dot]
    Q1 -- No --> MOD2[Power Law Model tau equals K gamma-dot to the power n]
    Q2 -- Yes --> MOD3[Herschel Bulkley Model tau equals tau-zero plus K gamma-dot to the power n]
    Q2 -- No --> MOD4[Bingham Plastic tau equals tau-zero plus eta-p times gamma-dot]
    MOD1 --> OUT[Apply to milk, very dilute systems]
    MOD2 --> OUT2[Apply to salad dressings, sauces]
    MOD3 --> OUT3[Apply to mayonnaise, margarine, spreads]
    MOD4 --> OUT4[Apply to tomato ketchup, certain W/O emulsions]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1: Define "food emulsion" and classify with examples. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **food emulsion** is a colloidal dispersion of two immiscible liquids (typically oil and water), where one phase is dispersed as droplets within the other, stabilized by an emulsifier that reduces interfacial tension and forms a protective film.

Classification:

| Type | Continuous Phase | Examples |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| O/W | Water | Milk, mayonnaise, ice cream, salad dressings |
| W/O | Oil | Butter, margarine, spreads |
| Multiple (W/O/W) | Water | Reduced-fat products, encapsulated ingredients |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for classification with examples.)*

---

### Question A2: What is the "Creaming Index" and how is it used to evaluate emulsion stability? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Creaming Index (CI)** is a quantitative measure of the extent of gravitational separation in an emulsion, expressed as the percentage of the total emulsion height occupied by the separated serum (or cream) layer:

$$
CI = \frac{H_{s}}{H_{t}} \times 100 \,\%
$$

- $H_{s}$ = height of separated serum layer
- $H_{t}$ = total height of emulsion in the storage tube

**Use:** A **lower CI** indicates better kinetic stability against creaming. CI is monitored over time as a shelf-life indicator. Industry often accepts $CI < 10\%$ for stable products.

*(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for explanation, 1 for application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Discuss the rheological models used to characterize the textural behaviour of food emulsions. Explain the role of dispersed phase volume fraction and droplet size distribution on texture. **[14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Rheological Models for Food Emulsions **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Newtonian Model:**

$$
\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

Applies to very dilute emulsions (e.g., skim milk with φ < 0.05). Viscosity is constant regardless of shear rate.

**Step 2 — Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Model:**

$$
\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}
$$

- Used for shear-thinning emulsions without yield stress (e.g., light salad dressings, fruit juice emulsions).
- $K$ = consistency index; $n$ = flow behaviour index.
- When $n < 1$: shear-thinning (viscosity decreases with shear).

**Step 3 — Herschel–Bulkley Model:**

$$
\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}
$$

- Most appropriate for **structured food emulsions** like mayonnaise and margarine.
- The emulsion behaves as a solid below yield stress $\tau_{0}$ and flows plastically above it.

**Step 4 — Bingham Plastic Model:**

$$
\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

- A linear special case of Herschel–Bulkley with $n = 1$.
- Used for ketchup and certain W/O emulsions.

**Step 5 — Casson Model:**

$$
\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

- Empirical model used for chocolate-type suspensions and some W/O emulsions.
- $\eta_{\infty}$ = infinite-shear viscosity.

**Step 6 — Viscoelastic Models (SAOS):**

For dynamic testing:

$$
G^{*} = G' + i G'', \quad \tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'}
$$

- $G' > G''$ (tan δ < 1) → gel-like structured emulsion (e.g., set mayonnaise).
- $G'' > G'$ (tan δ > 1) → liquid-like emulsion (e.g., cream).

---

#### Part (b) — Effect of φ and Droplet Size on Texture **[7 Marks]**

**Dispersed Phase Volume Fraction (φ):**

1. **Dilute regime (φ < 0.02):** Einstein equation applies: $\eta_{r} = 1 + 2.5\phi$. Texture is fluid, Newtonian-like.
2. **Moderate regime (0.02 < φ < 0.3):** Droplet interactions begin. Modified models (Mooney, Krieger–Dougherty) are required.
3. **Concentrated regime (φ > 0.3):** Droplets are close-packed, deformation occurs, and the emulsion becomes highly viscous, shear-thinning, and may exhibit yield stress. Texture becomes firm and structured (e.g., mayonnaise at φ ≈ 0.74).

The **Krieger–Dougherty equation** captures this:

$$
\eta_{r} = \left(1 - \frac{\phi}{\phi_{max}}\right)^{-[\eta]\phi_{max}}
$$

where $\phi_{max} \approx 0.74$ (random close packing of spheres) and $[\eta] = 2.5$ (intrinsic viscosity).

**Droplet Size Distribution (DSD):**

1. **Mean droplet diameter ($d_{3,2}$ — Sauter mean):** Smaller $d_{3,2}$ increases viscosity (more interfacial area, more friction).
2. **Polydispersity:** A broad distribution packs more efficiently than a monodisperse one, slightly lowering viscosity but may cause textural non-uniformity.
3. **Creaminess:** Sensory creaminess correlates with sub-micron droplets ($d < 1\,\mu m$) — used in premium ice cream and beverages.
4. **Graininess / Sandiness:** Large droplets ($d > 30\,\mu m$) cause gritty mouthfeel — undesirable in most emulsions.

**Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Effect on Viscosity | Effect on Texture |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ↑ φ | Strong increase (Krieger–Dougherty) | Firmer, thicker, possibly pasty |
| ↓ $d_{3,2}$ | Moderate increase | Smoother, creamier |
| ↑ Polydispersity | Slight decrease | Variable, less uniform |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often write only the **Power-Law model** and forget Herschel–Bulkley — for *structured* emulsions like mayonnaise, **H-B is the correct primary model**.
> 2. When asked about φ effects, writing only "viscosity increases" without citing the **Krieger–Dougherty** equation loses 2 marks.
> 3. Confusing **$d_{3,2}$** (Sauter mean) with **$d_{4,3}$** (volume mean) — for texture, $d_{3,2}$ is more relevant.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Describe the instrumental methods for measuring textural characteristics of food emulsions. Explain the concept of texture profile analysis (TPA) and the parameters obtained. **[14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Instrumental Texture Measurement Methods **[7 Marks]**

**1. Rotational Rheometry:**

- **Steady-shear test:** Measures viscosity $\eta$ as a function of shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ over a range (e.g., 0.01 to 1000 s⁻¹). Determines flow behaviour and model fit (Power-law, Herschel–Bulkley).
- **Creep test:** Applies a constant small stress; monitors strain over time. Separates elastic, viscoelastic, and viscous responses.
- **Oscillatory test (SAOS):** Applies sinusoidal strain; measures $G'$, $G''$, and $\tan\delta$. Identifies gel-strength, crossover points, and structure.

**2. Texture Profile Analysis (TPA):**

- A **double-compression test** that imitates the action of the jaw during mastication.
- Uses a probe (typically cylindrical or conical) to compress the sample to a defined strain (e.g., 25–75 %) twice in succession.
- Generates a **force–time curve** from which textural parameters are extracted.

**3. Penetration / Extrusion Tests:**

- Measure force required to push a probe into or extrude the emulsion through an orifice.
- Used for spreadability and tube-flow assessment.

**4. Particle / Droplet Sizing:**

- Laser diffraction, light scattering, or microscopy.
- Determines $d_{3,2}$, $d_{4,3}$, and size distribution — critical for correlating microstructure to texture.

**5. Creaming Index (CI) Test:**

- Long-term storage in graduated tubes; visual measurement of serum-layer height as a function of time.
- Indirect textural indicator for stability.

**6. Tribology:**

- Emerging technique using soft-tribometer contact surfaces to mimic oral lubrication.
- Measures friction coefficient as a function of entrainment speed — relevant to "mouthfeel" and creaminess perception.

---

#### Part (b) — Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) — Parameters **[7 Marks]`

A TPA curve has two compression cycles. The standard parameters extracted are:

| Parameter | Definition | Sensory Correlate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hardness** | Peak force during first compression | Firmness, force to bite through |
| **Cohesiveness** | Ratio of positive-force area of 2nd compression to 1st compression | Degree to which sample holds together |
| **Adhesiveness** | Negative-force area after first compression | Stickiness to mouth/palate |
| **Springiness** | Distance recovered between end of 1st and start of 2nd compression | Elasticity, bounce-back |
| **Gumminess** | Hardness × Cohesiveness | Energy required to disintegrate a semi-solid |
| **Chewiness** | Gumminess × Springiness | Work to chew a solid-like sample |
| **Resilience** | Area of withdrawal in first compression / area of penetration | Ability to recover from deformation |

**Typical TPA Curve (textual sketch):**

The curve shows two peaks corresponding to two compressions. The first peak height = Hardness. The negative area after the first bite = Adhesiveness. The areas under the upstroke of each bite are used to calculate Cohesiveness and Gumminess.

**For food emulsions:**

- **Mayonnaise:** High hardness, moderate cohesiveness, low adhesiveness — firm and well-structured.
- **Yogurt (set):** Low hardness, high cohesiveness, low adhesiveness — smooth and sliceable.
- **Cream cheese:** Medium hardness, high cohesiveness, medium adhesiveness — spreadable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — TPA Mistakes:**
> 1. Students frequently **omit the definitions** of parameters (e.g., gumminess = hardness × cohesiveness is *not* enough — must define *each* term).
> 2. **Do not confuse Adhesiveness with Cohesiveness** — cohesiveness = internal binding; adhesiveness = sticking to surfaces (mouth, probe).
> 3. Many students forget to mention the **probe geometry** (cylindrical, conical, spherical) — examiners award a mark for specifying it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Textural Characteristics of Food Emulsions:**

- **Definition:** Emulsion = dispersion of two immiscible liquids; O/W (milk, mayo) and W/O (butter) are the two primary types.
- **Microstructure rules texture:** φ (volume fraction), droplet size distribution, emulsifier type, and continuous-phase viscosity are the four master levers.
- **Herschel–Bulkley model** is the *default model* for structured food emulsions (mayonnaise, margarine) — memorize:
  $$\tau = \tau_{0} + K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$
- **Einstein equation** applies only in the **dilute regime** ($\phi < 0.02$):
  $$\eta_{r} = 1 + 2.5\phi$$
- **Krieger–Dougherty** handles concentrated emulsions up to random close packing ($\phi_{max} \approx 0.74$).
- **Stokes' law** governs creaming rate — to reduce creaming, *decrease r*, *increase $\eta_{c}$*, or *match densities*.
- **Creaming Index:** $CI = (H_{s}/H_{t}) \times 100\,\%$ — lower is better for stability.
- **SAOS interpretation:** $G' > G''$ → gel-like; $G'' > G'$ → liquid-like; weak gels dominate in spreadable emulsions.
- **TPA parameters:** Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness, Resilience — know both definitions and sensory correlates.
- **Cox–Merz rule** links oscillatory and steady-shear data: $\eta^{*}(\omega) \approx \eta(\dot{\gamma})$ at $\omega = \dot{\gamma}$ — useful when sample is fragile.
- **Sensory–rheology link:** Creaminess ↔ small $d_{3,2}$ (< 1 µm) + high $G'$ + low $\tan\delta$; Graininess ↔ $d > 30$ µm + flocculation.
- **Standard temperature** for emulsion rheology: **25 °C**; gravitational constant $g = 9.81$ m·s⁻².
- **Tribology** is the modern frontier for correlating instrumental texture with mouthfeel perception.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of Emulsifiers in Relation to Food Texture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Functions of Emulsifiers in Relation to Food Texture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emulsifier (Food Science Definition):** A surface-active agent (surfactant) possessing both hydrophilic (polar) and lipophilic (non-polar) moieties in the same molecule, capable of reducing interfacial tension between two immiscible phases (oil and water) and stabilizing the dispersed system against coalescence, flocculation, and creaming. In the context of food texture, emulsifiers are functional ingredients that actively modify the structural, mechanical, and sensory properties of food matrices by interacting with proteins, starches, lipids, and crystalline phases.

From a **rheological** perspective, emulsifiers are **rheology-modifying additives** that govern the viscoelastic behaviour, yield stress, gel strength, and textural perception of food products. They function at the molecular, microstructural, and macroscopic levels.

### 1.2 Key Categories of Food Emulsifiers

| Category | Examples | Source | Typical Use |
|----------|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Lecithins** | Soy lecithin, egg lecithin | Soybean, egg yolk | Chocolate, bakery, margarine |
| **Mono- and Diglycerides** | GMS (Glycerol Monostearate) | Partial hydrolysis of fats | Bread, ice cream, whipped toppings |
| **Polyglycerol Esters (PGE)** | PGE oleate | Polymerized glycerol | Bakery, sauces |
| **Sorbitan Esters (Spans/Tweens)** | Span 80, Tween 80 | Sorbitol derivatives | Ice cream, dressings |
| **Sucrose Esters** | SE of fatty acids | Sucrose + fatty acid | Bakery, beverages |
| **Proteins (Amphiphilic Biopolymers)** | Casein, WPI, soy protein | Dairy/plant | All emulsion systems |
| **Lactylates** | Sodium stearoyl lactylate (SSL) | Lactic acid + fatty acid | Bread, cakes |
| **Amphoteric Emulsifiers** | DATEM | Diacetyl tartaric acid esters | Bread, bakery |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Matchmaker" Analogy**
> Imagine **oil and water** as two people from completely different cultural backgrounds who refuse to mix at a party. An emulsifier acts like a **bilingual matchmaker** — one arm of the molecule "speaks oil" (lipophilic tail, e.g., a long fatty acid chain), while the other arm "speaks water" (hydrophilic head, e.g., an OH or COO⁻ group). The matchmaker introduces oil to water, holds hands (orbits at the interface), and prevents the two from drifting apart. **Without the matchmaker**, the oil droplets coalesce, separate, and ruin the texture. **With the matchmaker**, the emulsion remains stable, smooth, and creamy.

A more **rheology-flavoured analogy** is the **bridge-builder between phases**: the emulsifier builds a *molecular bridge* across the oil–water interface, and the *strength, density, and elasticity* of these bridges determine the viscosity, yield stress, and mouthfeel of the food.

### 1.4 The HLB Concept (Foundation for All Emulsifier Function)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HLB (Hydrophilic–Lipophilic Balance)** is a dimensionless scale (0–20, originally; 0–40 in some extensions) developed by **William C. Griffin (1949)** to quantify the balance between the hydrophilic and lipophilic portions of a surfactant molecule.
> - **Low HLB (3–6):** Water-in-Oil (W/O) emulsifiers (lipophilic)
> - **Mid HLB (7–9):** Wetting agents
> - **High HLB (10–18):** Oil-in-Water (O/W) emulsifiers (hydrophilic)

The HLB scale is the **single most important tool** in KTU examinations for selecting an emulsifier for a specific food application.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Why Emulsifiers Work

The seven core functions of emulsifiers in food texture modification operate through distinct physicochemical mechanisms:

**1. Reduction of Interfacial Tension (γ)**
- Pure oil–water interfacial tension is **~30–50 mN/m**.
- Emulsifiers adsorb at the interface and reduce γ to **~1–10 mN/m**.
- Thermodynamic driving force: Gibbs free energy of emulsification.
- Equation: $\Delta G = \gamma \cdot \Delta A$
- Lower γ → lower energy required to create new interface → smaller droplets → smoother texture.

**2. Formation of a Mechanical Barrier (Steric/Electrostatic Stabilization)**
- Adsorbed emulsifier layer physically prevents droplet–droplet contact.
- Provides **steric hindrance** (large molecules) and/or **electrostatic repulsion** (charged groups).
- Directly influences the **creaming index** and **rheological stability** of emulsions.

**3. Protein Displacement from Interfaces (Competitive Adsorption)**
- In dairy/egg-based systems, proteins are the natural emulsifiers.
- Small-molecule surfactants (e.g., monoglycerides) can **displace proteins** from the oil–water interface.
- This causes **partial coalescence** in ice cream → critical for **aerated structure and overrun**.

**4. Complexation with Starch (Anti-Staling Action)**
- Monoglycerides and SSL form **inclusion complexes (V-amylose helices)** with amylose.
- Prevents **retrogradation** of amylopectin → delays bread staling.
- Modifies crumb firmness and shelf-life of bakery products.

**5. Modification of Fat Crystallization**
- Emulsifiers adsorb onto fat crystal surfaces, controlling **polymorphism, crystal size, and network structure**.
- Affects the **plasticity, spreadability, and mouthfeel** of margarine, chocolate, and shortening.

**6. Aeration and Foam Stabilization**
- Emulsifiers lower surface tension at the air–water interface.
- Stabilize gas cells in whipped products (meringue, mousses, ice cream).
- Control **overrun, foam stability, and bubble size distribution** — direct rheological parameters.

**7. Modification of Bulk Phase Rheology**
- Emulsifiers can form **liquid crystalline mesophases** (lamellar, hexagonal, cubic) in the continuous phase.
- These mesophases dramatically increase viscosity and impart **yield stress** → texturizing function.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Symbol Meaning & Units** | **Application** |
|-------------|------------------------|----------------------------|-----------------|
| Interfacial free energy of emulsification | $\Delta G = \gamma \cdot \Delta A$ | $\gamma$ = interfacial tension (N/m or mN/m), $\Delta A$ = new area created (m²) | Predicts thermodynamic stability |
| Gibbs adsorption isotherm | $\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT} \cdot \dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}$ | $\Gamma$ = surface excess (mol/m²), $C$ = bulk concentration (mol/L), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | Surfactant adsorption at interface |
| Laplace pressure (droplet) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ | $r$ = droplet radius (m) | Pressure inside droplet |
| Griffin's HLB (non-ionic) | $\text{HLB} = 20 \times \dfrac{M_h}{M_t}$ | $M_h$ = mass of hydrophilic portion, $M_t$ = total molecular mass | HLB for fatty acid esters, alcohols |
| Davies' group contribution HLB | $\text{HLB} = 7 + \sum(\text{hydrophilic groups}) - \sum(\text{lipophilic groups})$ | Group numbers from Davies' table | HLB for complex surfactants |
| Bancroft's rule | Emulsion type determined by phase where emulsifier is **more soluble** | — | Predicts O/W vs W/O |
| Required HLB for oil phase | HLB of emulsifier blend = $\sum_i \phi_i \cdot \text{HLB}_i$ | $\phi_i$ = mass fraction of emulsifier $i$ | Formulation design |
| Stokes' law (creaming) | $v = \dfrac{2r^2 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g}{9\eta}$ | $r$ = radius, $\rho$ = densities, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\eta$ = viscosity | Predicts emulsion stability |
| Creaming index | $\text{CI} = 100 \times \dfrac{H_s}{H_t}$ | $H_s$ = serum layer height, $H_t$ = total height | Quantifies instability |
| Power-law fluid viscosity | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | $K$ = consistency index, $n$ = flow behaviour index | Emulsion flow behaviour |
| Yield stress (Herschel–Bulkley) | $\sigma = \sigma_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress, $K$, $n$ = constants | Structured emulsions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems**
> - For an **ideal emulsion**, $\gamma$ is treated as a constant at equilibrium.
> - For **Newtonian emulsions** with droplet size below 1 µm, viscosity often follows the **Krieger–Dougherty equation**:
> $$\eta_r = \left(1 - \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_m}\right)^{-[\eta]\phi_m}$$
> where $\phi$ = dispersed phase volume fraction, $\phi_m$ = maximum packing fraction (~0.64 for monodisperse spheres), $[\eta]$ = intrinsic viscosity (2.5 for hard spheres).

### 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in Food Engineering

- The **HLB equations** allow a formulator to mathematically select the optimal emulsifier system.
- **Laplace pressure** governs droplet breakup in high-pressure homogenizers.
- **Stokes' law** dictates shelf-life design — the only ways to reduce creaming rate are: (a) reduce $r$ (homogenization), (b) increase $\eta$ (thickening agents), or (c) match densities.
- **Herschel–Bulkley** parameters allow engineers to **engineer mouthfeel** numerically (e.g., spoonable yogurt vs. drinkable yogurt).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Interfacial Tension and Droplet Size Reduction

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 1 L oil-in-water emulsion contains 100 mL of oil as droplets of average diameter 10 µm. An emulsifier reduces the interfacial tension from 35 mN/m to 5 mN/m, and the homogenizer breaks the droplets to 1 µm diameter. Calculate the **ratio of new surface area to old surface area**, the **change in interfacial free energy**, and comment on the role of the emulsifier.

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{oil}} = 100 \text{ mL} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
- $d_1 = 10 \, \mu m = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$ (initial diameter)
- $d_2 = 1 \, \mu m = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$ (final diameter)
- $\gamma_{\text{initial}} = 35 \text{ mN/m} = 35 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}$
- $\gamma_{\text{final}} = 5 \text{ mN/m} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}$

**Step 1 — Number of initial droplets:**
Volume per droplet:
$$V_1 = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_1^3 = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (5 \times 10^{-6})^3$$

$$V_1 = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (125 \times 10^{-18})$$

$$V_1 = 523.6 \times 10^{-18} \text{ m}^3 = 5.236 \times 10^{-16} \text{ m}^3$$

Number of droplets:
$$N_1 = \dfrac{V_{\text{oil}}}{V_1} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{5.236 \times 10^{-16}} = 1.91 \times 10^{11}$$

**Step 2 — Number of final droplets (same oil volume):**
$$V_2 = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (0.5 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 5.236 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^3$$

$$N_2 = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{5.236 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.91 \times 10^{14}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of new area to old area:**
Area per droplet is $4\pi r^2$. Since $N \propto 1/r^3$ and $A_{\text{total}} = N \cdot 4\pi r^2 \propto 1/r$:

$$\dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{d_1}{d_2} = \dfrac{10 \, \mu m}{1 \, \mu m} = 10$$

So the new area is **10× larger**.

**Step 4 — Total initial interfacial area:**
$$A_1 = N_1 \cdot 4\pi r_1^2 = 1.91 \times 10^{11} \cdot 4\pi (5 \times 10^{-6})^2$$

$$A_1 = 1.91 \times 10^{11} \cdot 4\pi \cdot 25 \times 10^{-12} = 1.91 \times 10^{11} \cdot 3.142 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$A_1 = 60.0 \times 10^{1} = 0.0600 \text{ m}^2$$

Wait — let me recompute carefully:
$A_1 = 1.91 \times 10^{11} \times 3.1416 \times 25 \times 10^{-12}$
$= 1.91 \times 10^{11} \times 78.54 \times 10^{-12}$
$= 150.0 \times 10^{-1} = 15.0 \text{ m}^2$ (approximately)

**Step 5 — Total final interfacial area:**
$$A_2 = 10 \cdot A_1 = 150 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Change in interfacial free energy (ΔG):**
$$\Delta G_{\text{initial}} = \gamma_1 A_1 = 35 \times 10^{-3} \times 15.0 = 0.525 \text{ J}$$

$$\Delta G_{\text{final}} = \gamma_2 A_2 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 150 = 0.750 \text{ J}$$

$$\Delta G_{\text{net}} = 0.750 - 0.525 = +0.225 \text{ J}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU:** Even with the emulsifier (lower γ), the *net* free energy increases because the **enormous increase in interfacial area (10×)** outweighs the 7× reduction in γ. This is precisely why **kinetic stability** (emulsifier adsorption barrier) is required — the system is thermodynamically unstable but kinetically trapped.

### 3.2 Derivation: Calculating Required HLB of an Oil Phase

**Problem:** A salad dressing contains 60% sunflower oil, 30% groundnut oil, and 10% castor oil (by mass). Determine the **required HLB** of the emulsifier blend needed to stabilize this O/W emulsion. Given required HLB values: sunflower = 7, groundnut = 6, castor = 14.

**Step 1 — Apply weighted average formula:**
$$\text{HLB}_{\text{required}} = \sum_i m_i \cdot \text{HLB}_i$$

where $m_i$ is the mass fraction of each oil.

$$\text{HLB}_{\text{required}} = (0.60)(7) + (0.30)(6) + (0.10)(14)$$

$$\text{HLB}_{\text{required}} = 4.20 + 1.80 + 1.40 = 7.40$$

**Step 2 — Select emulsifier:**
Choose an emulsifier with HLB ≈ 7.40, e.g., a **blend of Tween 60 (HLB 14.9) and Span 60 (HLB 4.7)**.

Let $x$ be the mass fraction of Tween 60. Then:
$$7.40 = x(14.9) + (1-x)(4.7)$$

$$7.40 = 14.9x + 4.7 - 4.7x$$

$$7.40 - 4.7 = 10.2x$$

$$2.70 = 10.2x \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 0.265$$

So **26.5% Tween 60 + 73.5% Span 60** matches the required HLB.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Emulsifier Function vs. Food Texture Outcome

| **Emulsifier Function** | **Mechanism** | **Food System** | **Texture Outcome** | **CO Mapping (KTU)** |
|--------------------------|----------------|------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Reduce γ, form barrier | Adsorption at O/W interface | Mayonnaise, milk | Creamy, smooth mouthfeel | CO2, CO3 |
| Protein displacement | Displaces casein from interface | Ice cream | Controlled partial coalescence → overrun | CO3, CO4 |
| Starch complexation | V-amylose inclusion | Bread, cake | Softer crumb, anti-staling | CO2, CO4 |
| Fat crystal modification | Adsorbs on crystal faces | Chocolate, margarine | Bloom control, snap, gloss | CO4, CO5 |
| Aeration stabilization | Lowers air–water γ | Whipped cream, meringue | Stable foam, light texture | CO3, CO5 |
| Mesophase formation | Liquid crystals in continuous phase | Whipped toppings, sauces | Yield stress, spoonable texture | CO4, CO5 |
| Viscosity enhancement | Hydration, network formation | Dressings, dips | Body and cling | CO3 |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Predicting Emulsion Type via Bancroft's Rule + HLB

**Decision Tree (Numerical Logic):**

Step 1 — Identify the **continuous phase** requirement.
Step 2 — For an O/W emulsion: choose emulsifier with **HLB > 10**.
Step 3 — For a W/O emulsion: choose emulsifier with **HLB < 6**.
Step 4 — Check Bancroft's rule: the emulsifier must be **more soluble in the continuous phase**.
Step 5 — Calculate the **required HLB** of the oil phase (weighted average).
Step 6 — Match the emulsifier or blend to that HLB value.
Step 7 — Validate with a small-scale lab trial (centrifuge test for creaming).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization shortcut (KTU):** HLB ≤ 6 → W/O; HLB 7–9 → Wetting; HLB ≥ 10 → O/W.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Emulsifier Action Map in Food Texture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Food Matrix] --> B[Emulsifier Added]
    B --> C{Mode of Action}
    C --> D[Interfacial Tension Reduction]
    C --> E[Protein Displacement]
    C --> F[Starch Complexation]
    C --> G[Fat Crystal Modification]
    C --> H[Aeration Stabilization]
    C --> I[Mesophase Formation]
    D --> D1[Smooth, Fine Droplets]
    D1 --> T1[Creamy Mouthfeel]
    E --> E1[Partial Coalescence in Ice Cream]
    E1 --> T2[Stable Overrun and Air Cells]
    F --> F1[Anti-Staling of Bread]
    F1 --> T3[Soft Crumb, Extended Shelf Life]
    G --> G1[Controlled Polymorphism]
    G1 --> T4[Gloss, Snap in Chocolate]
    H --> H1[Stable Foam Structure]
    H1 --> T5[Light, Airy Texture]
    I --> I1[Liquid Crystal Network]
    I1 --> T6[Yield Stress, Spoonable Body]
    T1 --> Z[Final Sensory Texture]
    T2 --> Z
    T3 --> Z
    T4 --> Z
    T5 --> Z
    T6 --> Z
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Emulsifier Selection Logic (HLB Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Food System] --> B{Continuous Phase?}
    B -->|Water| C[Target: O or W Emulsion]
    B -->|Oil| D[Target: W or O Emulsion]
    C -->|O in W| E[Required HLB Greater than 10]
    C -->|W in O| F[Required HLB Less than 6]
    D -->|W in O| F
    D -->|O in W| E
    E --> G[Compute Weighted HLB of Oil Phase]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Emulsifier Available?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Use Directly if HLB Matches]
    H -->|No| J[Blend Two Emulsifiers]
    J --> K[Solve for Mass Fractions]
    K --> L[Verify with Lab Test]
    I --> L
    L --> M[End: Validated Emulsifier System]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Multi-Level Texture Modulation

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Molecular_Level
        m1[Amphiphilic Molecule]
        m2[HLB Value]
        m3[CMC Threshold]
    end
    subgraph Microstructural_Level
        mi1[Droplet Size Distribution]
        mi2[Crystal Polymorph]
        mi3[Liquid Crystal Phase]
    end
    subgraph Macroscopic_Level
        ma1[Viscosity]
        ma2[Yield Stress]
        ma3[Mouthfeel and Sensory]
    end
    m1 --> mi1
    m2 --> mi1
    m3 --> mi3
    mi1 --> ma1
    mi2 --> ma2
    mi3 --> ma2
    ma1 --> ma3
    ma2 --> ma3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term HLB. List the HLB range suitable for (a) oil-in-water emulsifier, (b) water-in-oil emulsifier.*

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **HLB (Hydrophilic–Lipophilic Balance)** is a numerical scale (0–20) developed by Griffin to express the balance between the hydrophilic and lipophilic groups of a surfactant.
> - **(a) O/W emulsifier:** HLB range **10–18** (hydrophilic dominant)
> - **(b) W/O emulsifier:** HLB range **3–6** (lipophilic dominant)
> - HLB 7–9: Wetting agents.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]**
*Explain the role of emulsifiers in the formation of ice cream texture.*

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> In ice cream, emulsifiers (e.g., glycerol monostearate, Tween 80) perform three texture-critical functions:
> 1. **Partial coalescence** — they displace milk proteins (casein) from the fat droplet interface during freezing, enabling fat globules to partially coalesce and form a **fat network** that traps air cells.
> 2. **Stabilization of air cells** — they lower the air–water interfacial tension, producing **fine, uniform air cells**, increasing overrun.
> 3. **Prevention of ice crystal growth** — they interact with fat and protein, restricting recrystallization during storage, giving a **smooth, creamy texture**.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style 1]**
*(a)* Define an emulsifier. Explain the **seven major functions of emulsifiers** in food texture with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A food formulation requires an O/W emulsion. The oil phase has the composition: 50% soybean oil, 30% palm oil, 20% coconut oil. Required HLB values: soybean = 7, palm = 8, coconut = 10. Calculate the **required HLB**. If the available emulsifiers are Span 80 (HLB = 4.3) and Tween 80 (HLB = 15.0), determine the **mass fractions** of each to be used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
Refer to Section 2.1 of these notes for the seven functions (interfacial tension reduction, steric barrier, protein displacement, starch complexation, fat crystal modification, aeration stabilization, mesophase formation). Students must provide at least **one food example per function** for full marks (e.g., bread for starch complexation, chocolate for fat crystal modification, ice cream for protein displacement, mayonnaise for emulsification).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Required HLB of oil phase (weighted average):**
$$\text{HLB}_{\text{req}} = (0.50)(7) + (0.30)(8) + (0.20)(10)$$
$$\text{HLB}_{\text{req}} = 3.50 + 2.40 + 2.00 = 7.90$$

**Step 2 — Set up equation for blend:**
Let $x$ = mass fraction of Tween 80.
$$7.90 = x(15.0) + (1-x)(4.3)$$
$$7.90 = 15x + 4.3 - 4.3x = 10.7x + 4.3$$
$$3.60 = 10.7x \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 0.3365$$

**Result:**
- **Tween 80:** 33.65 % (by mass)
- **Span 80:** 66.35 % (by mass)

Since required HLB (7.90) is < 10, this is in the borderline wetting/weak O/W region; for a robust O/W emulsion, the **target HLB of the oil phase** would be re-evaluated. However, mathematically, this is the matched blend.

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style 2]**
*(a)* With a neat diagram, explain the **mechanism of emulsifier action at the oil–water interface**. Discuss **Bancroft's rule** and its significance in emulsion type prediction. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Derive the expression for **Stokes' law of creaming** in an emulsion. A 200 mL O/W emulsion contains 30 mL of oil phase dispersed as droplets of 5 µm diameter. Density of oil = 900 kg/m³, density of water = 1000 kg/m³, viscosity of water = 1 mPa·s. Calculate the **creaming velocity** in m/s and the **time required** for the top of the serum layer to reach a height of 2 cm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
Draw a labelled diagram showing an emulsifier molecule with a hydrophilic head (circle) and lipophilic tail (zig-zag line) oriented at the oil–water interface. The hydrophilic head dissolves in water, the lipophilic tail dissolves in oil. This orientation stabilizes the interface.

**Bancroft's Rule:** "The phase in which the emulsifier is **more soluble** tends to be the **continuous phase**."
- If emulsifier is water-soluble (high HLB) → O/W emulsion.
- If emulsifier is oil-soluble (low HLB) → W/O emulsion.
- Significance: provides a fundamental physicochemical basis for choosing the right emulsifier.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Recall Stokes' law derivation:**
A spherical droplet of radius $r$ and density $\rho_1$ falling/rising through a continuous phase of density $\rho_2$ and viscosity $\eta$ under gravity $g$ reaches terminal velocity when:
$$F_{\text{gravity}} = F_{\text{buoyancy}} + F_{\text{drag}}$$

$$\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_1 g = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_2 g + 6 \pi \eta r v$$

Solving for $v$:
$$v = \dfrac{2 r^2 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g}{9 \eta}$$

For oil droplets in water, $\rho_1 < \rho_2$, so the oil rises (creaming).

**Step 2 — Numerical calculation:**
- $r = 2.5 \, \mu m = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $\rho_1 = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_2 = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\eta = 1 \text{ mPa·s} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

$$v = \dfrac{2 (2.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 (900 - 1000)(9.81)}{9 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v = \dfrac{2 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-12} \times (-100) \times 9.81}{9 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v = \dfrac{-1.226 \times 10^{-8}}{9 \times 10^{-3}} = -1.36 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$$

The negative sign indicates **upward motion** (creaming).
$$|v| = 1.36 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s} = 1.36 \, \mu m/s$$

**Step 3 — Time for serum layer to reach 2 cm:**
$$t = \dfrac{h}{|v|} = \dfrac{0.02}{1.36 \times 10^{-6}} = 14{,}706 \text{ s} \approx 4.08 \text{ hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign convention mistake**: Forgetting to interpret the negative sign of $v$ in Stokes' law. *Examiner will deduct 1 mark* if the direction of motion is not mentioned.
> 2. **Unit conversion error**: Mixing µm with m, or mPa·s with Pa·s. Always convert all quantities to **SI units (m, kg, Pa·s, m/s)** before substituting.
> 3. **HLB calculation step-skipping**: Students often write the final blend fractions without showing the algebraic solving step. **Always show the substitution and the linear equation explicitly** for full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring Bancroft's rule** when asked to predict emulsion type. A common KTU trap is to ask "which emulsifier for butter (W/O)?" — students guess based on HLB alone. Mention Bancroft's rule.
> 5. **Confusing overrun with air cell stability**: Overrun = (volume of air)/(volume of mix) × 100, not the air cell size. Do not interchange these terms.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):**
>
> - **HLB scale:** 0–20 (Griffin); W/O: 3–6, Wetting: 7–9, O/W: 10–18.
> - **Griffin's HLB formula:** $\text{HLB} = 20 \cdot M_h / M_t$ (non-ionic surfactants).
> - **Davies' HLB:** Group contribution method; $\text{HLB} = 7 + \Sigma(\text{hydrophilic}) - \Sigma(\text{lipophilic})$.
> - **Bancroft's rule:** Emulsifier is more soluble in the **continuous phase**.
> - **Interfacial tension reduction:** Pure oil/water γ = 30–50 mN/m; with emulsifier = 1–10 mN/m.
> - **Stokes' law:** $v = \dfrac{2r^2 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g}{9\eta}$ — direct control on creaming rate.
> - **Seven functions of emulsifiers in food texture:** (1) γ reduction, (2) mechanical barrier, (3) protein displacement, (4) starch complexation (anti-staling), (5) fat crystal modification, (6) aeration, (7) mesophase formation.
> - **Ice cream mechanism:** Partial coalescence via protein displacement → fat network → air cell trapping.
> - **Bread anti-staling:** Monoglycerides/SSL form V-amylose complexes → retarded retrogradation.
> - **Chocolate texture:** Emulsifiers (lecithin, PGE) control fat crystal polymorphism → gloss, snap, bloom resistance.
> - **Required HLB** of an oil mixture = mass-fraction-weighted average of component HLBs.
> - **Formulating an emulsifier blend:** Solve a linear equation for mass fractions $x$ and $(1-x)$.
> - **Rheology equations to remember:** $\Delta G = \gamma \cdot \Delta A$, $\Delta P = 2\gamma / r$, Herschel–Bulkley $\sigma = \sigma_y + K\dot{\gamma}^n$.
> - **Always state units** in numerical answers (mN/m, µm, m/s, Pa·s).
> - **Always interpret signs** in Stokes' law (negative = upward = creaming).
> - **Crystalline fat and emulsifier interaction** is a frequent KTU 14-mark question topic — know the role of sorbitan tristearate in chocolate and PGE in margarine.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensory Measurement of Food Texture and Texture Profile
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT523 - FOOD RHEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sensory Measurement of Food Texture and Texture Profile

---

# 🧪 Sensory Measurement of Food Texture and Texture Profile

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensory Texture Measurement** is the scientific discipline of quantifying, qualifying, and profiling the textural attributes of food by trained human panelists using controlled psychological and physiological response scales. It is the **only method that directly measures the human perception** of food texture, since texture is fundamentally a multi-modal sensory experience (tactile, auditory, visual, kinesthetic).

### 📘 Formal KTU Definition

> **Sensory Measurement of Texture** may be defined as the systematic, statistically-validated, and reproducible human-panellist evaluation of a food's mechanical, geometrical, and surface (mouthfeel) properties, as perceived during biting, chewing, manipulation in the mouth, and swallowing, using standardized scoring scales, reference standards, and environmental protocols (ISO 13299 / ASTM MNL 26).

The associated concept, **Texture Profile (TP)**, is a **multi-dimensional descriptive snapshot** of how a food behaves in the mouth — analogous to a "fingerprint" — capturing the *sequence*, *intensity*, and *duration* of textural sensations from first bite to swallow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Szczesniak (1963)** defined texture as *"the sensory manifestation of the structural and mechanical properties of food, as perceived by the sense of touch, hearing and sight."* This is the KTU-prescribed foundational definition.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy — The "Food Sommelier" Concept

Imagine you are a **wine sommelier**. You do not just say "this wine is good or bad." You describe *body*, *tannin*, *finish*, *aroma*, *acidity*, and *length on the palate* — using a precise vocabulary, a rating scale, and reference samples.

A **sensory texture analyst is the sommelier of food texture**. They do not just say "this yogurt is thick." They describe:
- *How thick?* (viscosity)
- *Does it hold its shape?* (firmness)
- *Does it stick to the spoon?* (adhesiveness)
- *How does it melt in the mouth?* (mouth-coating, rate of breakdown)
- *How quickly does it disappear when chewed?* (rate of structure breakdown)

The **Texture Profile** is therefore the *"tasting note"* of food texture — a structured, descriptive, time-resolved map of mouthfeel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics** used in KTU-evaluated texture profile work:
> - **15 cm unstructured line scale** (generic)
> - **9-point hedonic scale** (preference)
> - **15-point Spectrum™ intensity scale** (descriptive)
> - **Texture category reference foods** (e.g., cream cheese = 1.0 hardness reference)

### 🧬 Why Sensory Texture Measurement Cannot Be Replaced by Instruments

Although instrumental rheometers (TPA, penetration, extrusion) measure *force–distance–time* with high precision, they measure **proxy mechanical properties**, not perception. The human oral cavity is a unique, soft-tissue, salivation-controlled, temperature-modulated, multi-axial **biomechanical reactor** that no machine fully replicates. Sensory methods are therefore *indispensable* in KTU 2024-scheme food rheology and product development.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 2.1 The Szczesniak Classification of Texture Attributes (1963, 1979, 2002)

Szczesniak's classification is the **gold standard KTU-evaluated taxonomy** of food texture. It organizes texture attributes into **three hierarchical groups**:

| Level | Group | Nature of Attributes | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary (Mechanical)** | Geometric | Related to shape, size, particle characteristics | *Graininess, Fibrousness, Cellularity, Crystallinity* |
| | Mechanical | Force/displacement responses | *Hardness, Cohesiveness, Viscosity, Elasticity, Adhesiveness, Brittleness, Chewiness, Gumminess* |
| | Other (Mouthfeel) | Moistness, fat-related, chemical | *Wetness, Oiliness, Grittiness, Smoothness, Slipperiness* |
| **Secondary** | Derived from primaries | Multi-sensory | *Crunchiness (audiory + mechanical), Juiciness (chemical + mechanical), Meltability* |
| **Tertiary** | Time-related | How attributes evolve during mastication | *Rate of breakdown, Rate of melting, Length of time until swallow* |

### 🔬 2.2 The Texture Profile Method (TPM) — Brandt, Skinner & Sherman (1963)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Texture Profile Method (TPM)** is the **original sensory Texture Profile Analysis technique**, developed at **General Foods Corporation in 1963** by Brandt, Skinner, and Sherman. It is *the* foundational human-TPA method on which modern Spectrum™ and QDA methods are based, and is a **high-yield KTU topic**.

**The TPM Procedure (Step Logic):**

1. **Sample preparation** — Uniform size, shape, and temperature (commonly 1.5 cm cube; 20 ± 2 °C for solids; 4 ± 1 °C for refrigerated).
2. **Pre-test bite** — Place sample between molars; compress *once* at controlled rate; record perception of first-bite mechanical properties.
3. **Mastication cycle** — Chew at a *natural* rhythm (typically 1 chew/sec); note sequential textural changes.
4. **Swallow threshold** — Record the number of chews before the bolus is ready to swallow.
5. **Post-swallow residue** — Note the mouthfeel after swallowing (oily, drying, sticky, clean).
6. **Scoring on standardized scales** — Rate each attribute on a defined intensity scale (originally 1–7; later 1–15 in Spectrum™).
7. **Repetition** — At least **6–10 trained panelists**; each evaluates the sample **at least 2 times** in randomized order.
8. **Statistical analysis** — Means, ANOVA, panelist consistency (CV ≤ 25 %), and significance testing (p < 0.05).

### 🔬 2.3 Definitions of the Eight Primary Mechanical Texture Attributes

These are the **must-memorize KTU definitions**:

| Attribute | KTU-Prescribed Definition | Reference Material | Example Food |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | Force required to *compress* a food between molars (or between tongue and palate) | Cream cheese = 1.0 | Carrot, hard candy |
| **Cohesiveness** | Degree to which a food *holds together* (deforms without rupturing) | Soft cheese = 1.0 | Egg white, soft bread |
| **Elasticity** | Degree to which a food returns to its *original shape* after compression | Marshmallow = 1.0 | Sponge cake, pizza |
| **Adhesiveness** | Force required to *remove* food that sticks to oral surfaces (teeth, palate) | — | Peanut butter, caramel |
| **Viscosity** | Resistance to *flow* under an applied force (perceived during swallowing) | Honey = 1.0 | Yogurt, ketchup, syrup |
| **Brittleness (Fracturability)** | Force with which a food *breaks* / crumbles / fractures | Shortbread = 1.0 | Crackers, chips |
| **Chewiness (Mastication energy)** | Energy required to *chew* a solid food until ready to swallow | Beef jerky = 1.0 | Steak, gum, fudge |
| **Gumminess (Sticky-solid behavior)** | Energy required to *disintegrate* a semi-solid food to a state ready for swallowing | Cold fudge = 1.0 | Gummy candy, fudge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two higher-order (composite) attributes** derived from primaries (KTU 2024 Scheme high-yield):
> - **Chewiness** = Hardness × Cohesiveness × Elasticity (in TPM)
> - **Gumminess** = Hardness × Cohesiveness (in TPM)

### 🔬 2.4 Modern Descendants of TPM

| Method | Year | Key Feature | Scale | Reference Foods |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TPM (Brandt et al.)** | 1963 | First standardized profile | 1–7 | Cream cheese, etc. |
| **Spectrum™ Method** | 1980s (Meilgaard) | Universal reference scales, anchored to physical references | 0–15 | Universal |
| **QDA™ (Stone & Sidel)** | 1974 | Free-choice vocabulary generation; trained panel | 15-cm line | Product-specific |
| **Free-Choice Profiling** | 1980s (Williams & Arnold) | Each panelist generates own vocabulary | Individual | — |
| **Temporal Dominance of Sensations (TDS)** | 1999 (Pineau, Schlich) | Time-resolved dominant attribute | Multiple | — |

### 🧮 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically derived from the Texture Profile force–time trace** (instrumental or sensory-equivalent), and form the KTU 2024 formula-banked equations for Module 4.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardness } (H) &= F_{\text{peak, first bite}} \quad [\text{N}] \\[4pt]
\text{Cohesiveness } (C) &= \frac{A_2}{A_1} \quad [\text{dimensionless}] \\[4pt]
\text{Springiness } (S) &= \frac{t_2}{t_1} \quad [\text{dimensionless}] \\[4pt]
\text{Adhesiveness } (A_d) &= -A_3 \quad [\text{N·s}] \\[4pt]
\text{Chewiness } (Ch) &= H \times C \times S \quad [\text{N}] \\[4pt]
\text{Gumminess } (G) &= H \times C \quad [\text{N}] \\[4pt]
\text{Resilience } (R) &= \frac{A_4}{A_5} \quad [\text{dimensionless}] \\[4pt]
\text{Cohesiveness Ratio (sensory analog)} &= \frac{\text{Work of 2nd compression}}{\text{Work of 1st compression}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $A_1$ = positive force–time area of first compression
- $A_2$ = positive force–time area of second compression
- $A_3$ = negative force–time area (adhesion trough)
- $A_4, A_5$ = areas under the upstroke and downstroke of the first compression
- $t_1$ = time at peak force (first compression)
- $t_2$ = time at peak force (second compression)

### 📊 KTU-Prescribed Sensory Texture Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Symbolic Form | Unit | Physical Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hardness | $H = F_{\text{max, 1st}}$ | **N** | Peak biting force | Biscuit, cheese, meat |
| Cohesiveness | $C = A_2 / A_1$ | **—** | Internal binding strength | Bread, cake, cheese |
| Springiness | $S = t_2 / t_1$ | **—** | Elastic recovery | Sponge, marshmallow |
| Adhesiveness | $A_d = -A_3$ | **N·s** | Sticking work | Caramel, peanut butter |
| Chewiness | $Ch = H \cdot C \cdot S$ | **N** | Mastication energy | Steak, jerky, toffee |
| Gumminess | $G = H \cdot C$ | **N** | Sticky-solid disintegration | Fudge, gummy candy |
| Resilience | $R = A_4 / A_5$ | **—** | Instantaneous recovery | Chewy candy |
| Viscosity (sensory) | $V = F_{\text{shear, oral}} / \dot{\gamma}_{\text{oral}}$ | **Pa·s** | Resistance to swallowing flow | Yogurt, soup, sauce |
| Rate of Breakdown | $ROB = dT/dt \big\vert_{\text{chew}}$ | **units/s** | Texture decay during chewing | Crackers, chips |
| Number of Chews to Swallow | $N_c$ | **count** | Composite mastication effort | All solid foods |

> [!WARNING]
> Do **NOT** confuse the **Sensory** Chewiness definition (Hardness × Cohesiveness × Elasticity) with the **Instrumental TPA** Chewiness (Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Product Development (R&D)**: Texture profile guides formulation of *similar* and *substitute* products (e.g., plant-based meats mimicking chicken).
- **Quality Control (QC)**: Batch-to-batch sensory texture acceptance windows.
- **Shelf-life studies**: Texture decay kinetics (e.g., crispness loss in crackers).
- **Consumer Science**: Linking descriptive texture to hedonic liking (preference mapping — external/Internal Preference Mapping).
